7  Post-Training: SFT, Preferences, RLHF/DPO, and Behavior Specs [F+S]

title: 7  Post-Training: SFT, Preferences, RLHF/DPO, and Behavior Specs [F+S]
section: 7.10 DPO removes the explicit reward-model loop
text:
7.10 DPO removes the explicit reward-model loop
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) starts from the same pairwise preference model and KL-regularized policy objective, then algebraically removes the explicit reward model. The result is an offline classification-like loss over preference pairs. There is no reward-model training stage, value model, or online sampling loop in ordinary DPO.
For one prompt x, consider the optimization over policies

\max_\pi\;
\mathbb{E}_{y\sim\pi(\cdot\mid x)}[r(x,y)]
-\beta D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(\pi(\cdot\mid x)\,|\,
\pi_{\text{ref}}(\cdot\mid x)\right).

Introduce a normalization constant Z(x) so probabilities sum to one. Solving this constrained problem yields

\pi^*(y\mid x)
=\frac{1}{Z(x)}\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)
\exp\!\left(\frac{r(x,y)}{\beta}\right).

Rearrange for the reward:

r(x,y)=\beta\log\frac{\pi^*(y\mid x)}
{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)}+\beta\log Z(x).

Now substitute this expression into the Bradley–Terry reward difference. The prompt-only term \beta\log Z(x) appears for both completions and cancels. Replacing the unknown optimum with the trainable policy gives the DPO loss

\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\theta)
=-\mathbb{E}_{(x,y_w,y_l)}
\left[
\log\sigma\!\left(
\beta\left[
\log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_w\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w\mid x)}
-
\log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_l\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l\mid x)}
\right]
\right)
\right].

Every term has an operational meaning. The policy sequence log-probability is the sum of completion-token log-probabilities under the same chat template and response mask. The reference is usually the frozen SFT policy. The inner difference asks whether the trainable policy has increased the chosen response relative to the reference more than it increased the rejected response. The outer log-sigmoid is binary preference likelihood. The coefficient \beta sets the scale of departure from the reference under this parameterization and must be tuned with evaluation, not interpreted as a universal preference strength.
For two candidates under the same prompt, the fixture’s softmax normalizer cancels inside each chosen-minus-rejected log-probability. Its implementation therefore uses logit gaps directly:
# code/ch07/posttrain_lab.py — changed region: train_dpo
policy_gap = logits[prompt][chosen] - logits[prompt][rejected]
reference_gap = reference[prompt][chosen] - reference[prompt][rejected]
margin = beta * (policy_gap - reference_gap)
probability = 1.0 / (1.0 + math.exp(-margin))
loss -= math.log(max(probability, 1e-12))
derivative = beta * (probability - 1.0)
gradient[prompt][chosen] += derivative
gradient[prompt][rejected] -= derivative









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




posttrain_lab.py after train_dpo
25 lines
the chosen-vs-rejected policy log-ratio margin increases relative to a frozen SFT reference




DPO is attractive when a fixed preference corpus covers the behavior you want and an offline training job is operationally preferable. It is not automatically the better objective. If the dataset was generated by an older or much weaker policy, it may contain little information about errors made by the current policy. DPO cannot explore outcomes absent from its corpus. Online RL can sample those outcomes, ask a reward or verifier to score them, and update on the induced distribution.
Sequence length deserves explicit attention. A completion log-probability is normally a token sum, so its magnitude and gradient composition depend on length. Pair collection may also prefer longer answers, as our fixture does. Do not “fix” this by silently changing sums to means; that changes the objective. Instead, report length-conditioned preference accuracy, expected response length, and task quality at matched lengths. Then choose data balancing, objective variants, or decoding controls based on measured product behavior.

7  Post-Training: SFT, Preferences, RLHF/DPO, and Behavior Specs [F+S]

title: 7  Post-Training: SFT, Preferences, RLHF/DPO, and Behavior Specs [F+S]
section: 7.11 RLAIF and constitutional training scale the feedback
text:
7.11 RLAIF and constitutional training scale the feedback
Human comparison is slow and expensive. Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) uses a model to produce some preference labels or critiques, usually against an explicit rubric or set of principles. The resulting labels can train a reward model for online RL or feed a direct preference objective. The optimization math does not become different because the labeler is a model; the measurement risks do.
An AI labeler can be applied consistently, cheaply, and at high volume. It can also share blind spots with the policy, prefer its own style, follow an injected instruction inside a candidate, or turn a vague rubric into confident but unstable labels. Separate policy and evaluator contexts. Treat candidate text as data rather than instructions. Randomize positions, swap and remap pairs, include human-labeled calibration sets, and stratify agreement by rule. When consequences are high or the rubric itself is contested, humans remain the authority for the specification and adjudication.
Constitutional AI is a more structured use of written principles. In the original pipeline, a supervised phase has a model critique and revise responses according to a constitution, producing safer demonstrations. A reinforcement phase obtains AI-generated preference feedback under those principles and trains toward it. The durable idea is not a particular list of principles. It is the traceable transformation

\text{written rule}\rightarrow\text{critique}\rightarrow
\text{revision or preference}\rightarrow\text{training example}\rightarrow
\text{rule-linked evaluation}.

This is why behavior specifications came first in the chapter. A constitution, model spec, or product policy becomes useful to training only after conflicts, applicability, and examples are operationalized. Natural-language principles do not enforce themselves, and a model-generated critique is evidence to evaluate rather than proof that the response complies.
Choose the pipeline by the feedback interface:









Available signal
First method to consider
What it buys
Main residual




Expert can write the target
SFT
simple, stable imitation
weak coverage outside demonstrations


Expert can rank fixed candidates
DPO or another direct preference loss
offline preference learning without RM/PPO
limited by candidate support and label bias


Current policy must explore; learned judgment is usable
RM + PPO-style RLHF
fresh on-policy data and optimization
operational complexity and proxy reward


Outcome is programmatically verifiable
online RL with verifier
scalable objective feedback
specification gaps in the verifier; Chapter 8 develops this case


Human labels are scarce; rubric is explicit
RLAIF with human calibration
label scale and consistency
common-mode evaluator bias



These methods can be staged. A common logic is SFT for format and basic behavior, preference tuning for subjective comparisons, then online RL only where exploration or verifiable outcomes justify it. More stages are not automatically better. Every stage should earn its place through a held-out, specification-linked delta.

text:
7.12 Landscape 2026 (dated)






Note



Verified 2026-07-19. Hugging Face TRL exposes maintained trainers for SFT, reward modeling, DPO, PPO-family and other post-training objectives; PEFT supplies adapter-based tuning; torchtune supplies PyTorch-native recipes. Config schemas, supported objectives, default loss normalizations, chat-template handling, and integrations continue to change. Public behavior artifacts also evolve: OpenAI maintains its Model Spec in an official repository, while Anthropic published a revised Claude constitution in January 2026. These are current implementation and governance profiles, not the chapter’s durable mechanism.
Verify live: check the TRL documentation, PEFT documentation, torchtune documentation, OpenAI Model Spec source, and Anthropic’s constitution before choosing APIs, defaults, or adopting any public behavior text. Appendix C owns the annual audit.

title: 7  Post-Training: SFT, Preferences, RLHF/DPO, and Behavior Specs [F+S]
text:
7.13 Build
The post-training microscope isolates the losses from model scale. Each prompt has four candidate completions represented by three observable features: task success, safety, and normalized length. The base policy begins with logits that favor fluent but specification-inferior candidates. SFT receives one demonstrated target per prompt. A seeded synthetic annotator creates 512 preference pairs with a hidden left-position offset and an excessive preference for length. A linear reward model fits those pairs, and DPO updates the SFT policy against its frozen copy.
Run the fast path from the newbook directory:
python code/ch07/posttrain_lab.py --svg code/ch07/posttrain_metrics.svg
python -m unittest discover -s code/ch07 -p "test_*.py" -v
The program uses only the Python standard library. It prints JSON and regenerates the SVG embedded below. The important deterministic values at seed 7 are:








Measurement
Result
Interpretation




SFT loss, first → last checkpoint
1.729 → 0.136
demonstrations become likely


reward-model loss, first → last checkpoint
0.693 → 0.448
the model learns the annotator’s pairwise choices


DPO loss, first → last checkpoint
0.693 → 0.469
preference margins increase relative to SFT


chosen answer appeared left
61.7%
position bias is present


chosen answer was longer
66.4%
preference labels overvalue length


learned length reward weight
+1.133
the reward model reproduces that bias


rewarded action probability, one REINFORCE step
0.250 → 0.327
the policy-gradient sign is correct



The integrated stage comparison answers two questions at once: did behavior move toward the specification, and what collateral property moved with it?






Figure 7.3: How do specification utility and response length change across the base, SFT, and DPO stages? The deterministic fixture raises expected utility from 2.986 to 4.111 to 4.268; expected normalized length falls from 0.576 to 0.326 under SFT and rises to 0.366 after preference tuning.



Figure Figure 7.3 shows why one aggregate is insufficient. SFT makes all four demonstrated answers top-1 and moves expected specification utility from 2.986 to 4.111. DPO raises utility again to 4.268. Yet expected normalized length also rises from 0.326 to 0.366 after DPO because the preference instrument overvalues verbosity. DPO is optimizing the dataset it received. The correct response is to repair and re-audit the measurement process, not to claim that a falling DPO loss certifies the behavior specification.
Deliberately break the run in two ways. First, set the position offset in collect_preferences from 1.0 to 0.0; predict the left-choice rate before running, then check it. Second, set the annotator length weight from 1.5 to the specification’s -0.3; the reward model’s learned length weight and the DPO length delta should change sign or shrink. These interventions connect label collection to learned behavior.
The optional real-model adapter is a boundary test, not a second training stack. Install optional dependencies in a separate environment and invoke it with a model whose tokenizer publishes a chat template:
python code/ch07/hf_logprob_adapter.py MODEL_ID `
  --candidate "Concise candidate." `
  --candidate "A longer alternative candidate."
It renders the user message through the tokenizer’s chat template, appends each candidate, and sums only completion-token log-probabilities. That is the quantity used in the DPO derivation. Download, network access, model code, and accelerator placement are opt-in; the default test suite never accesses them. A production trainer should use a maintained implementation, but this adapter lets you inspect template and sequence-score semantics before trusting one.
Honesty note. The fixture is a microscope, not evidence that a linear four-action policy predicts large-model quality. Its claims are narrower and executable: the gradient signs, loss reductions, reference-relative DPO margin, and propagation of measured label bias. Real post-training additionally requires held-out prompts, multiple seeds, distributed training, model-specific templates, safety evaluation, and slice-level human review.

7  Post-Training: SFT, Preferences, RLHF/DPO, and Behavior Specs [F+S]

title: 7  Post-Training: SFT, Preferences, RLHF/DPO, and Behavior Specs [F+S]
text:
7.14 What endures, what changes
What endures. Desired behavior must exist as a versioned, testable artifact before it becomes data. SFT is masked next-token likelihood on demonstrations. Pairwise reward modeling is logistic learning on reward differences. REINFORCE uses the log-derivative trick; baselines reduce variance without changing its expected gradient. PPO limits on-policy update size and usually uses a critic. DPO substitutes the KL-regularized policy–reward relationship into the preference model. None of these objectives can recover requirements absent from their measurement process.
What changes. Trainer APIs, recommended objective variants, template formats, model families, adapter methods, preferred labelers, and frontier post-training sequences will change. Keep them in dated profiles and re-run specification-linked evaluations. Public constitutions and model specifications are living documents. The method choice should follow the signal, coverage, and operational constraints rather than the popularity of a library or acronym.

title: 7  Post-Training: SFT, Preferences, RLHF/DPO, and Behavior Specs [F+S]
text:
7.15 Exercises

Derive the SFT gradient. For a four-action softmax policy with target action 0, derive \partial\mathcal{L}/\partial l_j=p_j-\mathbb{1}[j=0]. Use it to calculate the first update from uniform logits by hand, then compare with train_sft.
Verify the baseline identity. Replace the baseline in reinforce_check with 0, 0.25, and 0.9. Predict the sign of the sampled update in each single run. Then explain why a valid baseline preserves the expected gradient even though an individual sample can change sign.
Audit position bias. Extend each preference record with candidate identities and collect every content pair in both orders. Report left-choice rate and swap consistency after remapping verdicts to candidate identity. Define a release threshold and justify it.
Repair length bias. Create a length-matched evaluation slice and one data intervention without changing the reward loss. Predict the learned length weight and DPO expected length before running. Report utility and length together.
Test masking and packing. Construct two serialized chat examples with sentinel tokens. Implement response-only labels and a packed attention mask. Assert that user tokens have label -100 and that the second example cannot attend to the first under isolated packing.
Compare feedback interfaces. For a customer-support behavior of your choice, write one behavior-spec rule and construct: an SFT demonstration, a pairwise preference item, and a deterministic verifier. State what each representation captures and what it loses.
Defend a pipeline. You have 50,000 fixed human preference pairs from an older policy, a strong SFT checkpoint, no trustworthy online reward service, and a strict two-week deadline. Choose between SFT-only, DPO, and PPO-based RLHF. Name the evaluation gates, the largest residual risk, and the evidence that would cause you to revisit the choice.

7.16 Notes and sources
The canonical three-stage SFT → reward model → PPO pipeline is documented in Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback. Learning to Summarize from Human Feedback is an earlier language-model RLHF treatment. The policy-gradient estimator follows Williams’s REINFORCE paper, and the clipped objective follows Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms.
The direct objective and its KL-regularized derivation come from Direct Preference Optimization. The high-quality-small-SFT-data result discussed here comes from LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment. AI feedback and position-bias evidence are treated in RLAIF: Scaling Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. The critique/revision and AI-feedback pipeline comes from Constitutional AI. Current public behavior-artifact examples are linked in the dated landscape box and should be read as first-party documents, not universal specifications.

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
section: 8.3 A correct answer can be generated and still be lost
text:
8.3 A correct answer can be generated and still be lost
Suppose a model solves a hard algebra problem correctly in one of eight sampled responses. The other seven disagree. A plurality vote returns the most common wrong answer. A learned judge prefers a polished but invalid derivation. Asking the model to “check again” changes the right answer into a wrong one. The generator had enough capability to produce success, yet the surrounding inference procedure failed to find and preserve it.
Test-time compute is computation spent after the weights are fixed to improve an answer. It can be spent in three distinct ways. Serial compute extends one trajectory with more intermediate tokens, revisions, or backtracking. Parallel compute samples independent trajectories. Search compute branches on partial trajectories, evaluates them, and decides which branches deserve expansion. These mechanisms are often grouped under “thinking longer,” but they make different bets.
Why can serial reasoning help at all? A transformer applies a fixed number of layers per generated token. When it emits an intermediate token and conditions on that token next, the autoregressive loop creates another serial computation step. A chain of thought is therefore an external scratch sequence that lets a fixed-depth network perform a longer computation. The trace may help the model reach an answer; it is not automatically a faithful explanation of how the answer arose. Chapter 25 owns that monitoring and faithfulness question.
Parallel compute exploits a different fact: the model defines a distribution over possible paths. One sample may miss a low-probability correct region; repeated sampling can cover it. Search combines both ideas by preserving promising prefixes and abandoning others. Every method eventually needs a selection signal. This creates the generator–verifier gap: in some domains, checking a candidate is cheaper and more reliable than generating one. A compiler can reject invalid code much faster than a model can invent a correct program. Substitution can check an arithmetic answer. A proof assistant can validate a formal certificate.
The gap is conditional, not universal. Judging an essay may be as difficult as writing it. A learned verifier can share the generator’s errors. A test suite can omit the behavior an optimizer exploits. More candidate compute helps only when the selection mechanism can recognize improvement.
Figure Figure 8.1 makes the allocation decision explicit. The next unit of compute should buy a new path, a deeper path, or a better check—not merely more tokens by habit.

flowchart TD
    Q["Problem + current candidate set"] --> V{"Reliable external check available?"}
    V -->|"yes"| G{"Any candidate passes?"}
    G -->|"yes"| STOP["Return verified candidate"]
    G -->|"no"| CHOOSE{"Current failure is coverage or depth?"}
    V -->|"no"| UNCERTAIN["Use a calibrated selection rule; retain uncertainty"]
    CHOOSE -->|"coverage"| PAR["Sample another independent trajectory"]
    CHOOSE -->|"depth"| SER["Extend or revise a promising trajectory"]
    CHOOSE -->|"branch uncertainty"| SEARCH["Expand and prune partial thoughts"]
    PAR --> Q
    SER --> Q
    SEARCH --> Q





flowchart TD
    Q["Problem + current candidate set"] --> V{"Reliable external check available?"}
    V -->|"yes"| G{"Any candidate passes?"}
    G -->|"yes"| STOP["Return verified candidate"]
    G -->|"no"| CHOOSE{"Current failure is coverage or depth?"}
    V -->|"no"| UNCERTAIN["Use a calibrated selection rule; retain uncertainty"]
    CHOOSE -->|"coverage"| PAR["Sample another independent trajectory"]
    CHOOSE -->|"depth"| SER["Extend or revise a promising trajectory"]
    CHOOSE -->|"branch uncertainty"| SEARCH["Expand and prune partial thoughts"]
    PAR --> Q
    SER --> Q
    SEARCH --> Q




Figure 8.1: Where can the next unit of test-time compute produce new information?





The invariant is that extra compute enters a measured loop. Without a verifier, confidence rule, or stopping condition, “think longer” is only an instruction to spend more.

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.4 What you will build






TipThe reasoning-and-RLVR microscope



You will run one engine-free, standard-library CPU build rooted at code/ch08/reason_rl.py. A seeded arithmetic fixture exposes five reasoning strategies across easy, medium, and hard prompts. The same exact-answer verifier first ranks repeated samples and then becomes the reward for a clipped GRPO update.
The build measures coverage, plurality accuracy, noisy-verifier best-of-n, true pass@1, normalized policy entropy, expected response length, and objective reward. A deliberately misspecified length proxy runs through the same GRPO core to show Goodhart’s law. render_metrics.py generates the chapter’s print-safe SVG. Success means exact RLVR raises true accuracy, group advantages are zero-mean, and the broken proxy run visibly separates measured reward from true utility.
hf_generate_adapter.py is an optional transformers adapter that supplies real sampled completions and extracted numeric answers. It requires an explicit model download and is never imported by the deterministic build.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.5 Coverage turns samples into opportunity
Let a prompt x have per-sample probability p_x of producing a response that passes an exact verifier. If k samples are conditionally independent, the probability that at least one succeeds is

\operatorname{pass@}k(x)=1-(1-p_x)^k.

This is coverage: whether the candidate set contains a success. The marginal gain from sample k+1 is p_x(1-p_x)^k, so returns diminish but remain positive whenever p_x\in(0,1). Dataset pass@k averages this quantity over prompts. Real samples are correlated, and p_x varies widely, so the equation is a mental model rather than an estimator to apply blindly. Empirical work has nevertheless found smooth inference-scaling relationships over large sample ranges in verifiable domains.
Coverage is not selection accuracy. Self-consistency samples reasoning traces, extracts their final answers, and returns the plurality answer:

\hat a=\arg\max_a\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathbb{1}[a_i=a].

Plurality does not require more than half the votes. It works asymptotically when the correct answer a^* is the unique most probable answer bucket:

P(A=a^*)>\max_{a\ne a^*}P(A=a).

The commonly repeated “each sample must be more than 50% accurate” condition applies to binary majority voting, not general plurality. If wrong mass is diffuse across many answers, a correct bucket with probability 0.35 can still win. If one systematic mistake has probability 0.45, repeated sampling makes the system more confidently wrong even when coverage approaches one.
The reference fixture gives two correct strategies the same arithmetic answer, one wrong strategy a concentrated off-by-one answer, and other wrong strategies diffuse answers. The core sampling loop records all three selection outcomes:
# code/ch08/reason_rl.py — changed region: ttc_curve
actions = [sample_index(probabilities, rng) for _ in range(samples)]
answers = [
    candidate_answer(gold, action, trial + i)
    for i, action in enumerate(actions)
]
correct = [is_correct(action) for action in actions]
coverage += any(correct)
winner = max(dict.fromkeys(answers), key=answers.count)
plurality += winner == gold
marked = [i for i, hit in enumerate(correct) if hit != (rng.random() < .18)]
selected = rng.choice(marked) if marked else 0
noisy_bon += correct[selected]









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




reason_rl.py after ttc_curve
38 lines
coverage, plurality, and noisy-verifier selection are measured separately on the same candidates




At one sample, all three metrics equal the fixture’s 0.397 pass@1. At 32 samples, exact-verifier coverage reaches 1.000, plurality reaches only 0.583, and noisy selection reaches 0.732. The gap between coverage and selected accuracy is not unused model capability in the abstract; it is an engineering opportunity whose value depends on verifier quality.
Serial and parallel compute can also interact. One can sample k short traces or fewer long traces under the same token budget. If the task fails because early paths are diverse but shallow, extra length may help. If one long trajectory repeatedly commits to the same early mistake, parallel restarts may help. Measure success against total sampled tokens, not sample count alone, and stratify by difficulty.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.6 Search chooses where reasoning branches
Independent samples throw away partial work. Search keeps a frontier of partial reasoning states and allocates expansions selectively. To make this useful, define four interfaces: a state representation, an expansion operator, a partial-state score, and a termination check. “Ask the model for another thought” is only the expansion operator.
Beam search retains the best B prefixes at each depth. Standard token beam search scores cumulative log-probability, which measures model likelihood rather than correctness. Reasoning beam search instead branches on semantic steps and may combine policy likelihood with a process score. It is simple and parallel, but pruning is irreversible: a temporarily low-scored branch cannot recover.
Tree of Thoughts (ToT) treats a coherent intermediate thought as a node, expands several child thoughts, scores them, and permits backtracking. The tree preserves distinct histories. Graph of Thoughts (GoT) also merges equivalent or reusable intermediate states, which can avoid recomputation and combine partial results. Merging requires canonical state identity. If two states look similar in prose but carry different assumptions, an incorrect merge corrupts every descendant.
Verifier-guided search uses a process reward model or deterministic partial checker to rank prefixes. It earns its cost when bad branches can be rejected before they consume full generations. Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) balances exploitation of high-value branches and exploration of uncertain branches. A common selection score is

a^*=\arg\max_a\left[
Q(s,a)+c\sqrt{\frac{\log N(s)}{1+N(s,a)}}
\right],

where Q(s,a) is estimated value after choosing thought a from state s, N(s) is the parent visit count, N(s,a) is the edge visit count, and c controls exploration. Expansion adds a candidate thought; evaluation or simulation estimates downstream value; backup updates ancestor statistics. Language-model search must decide what constitutes a state, because raw prose creates an enormous, weakly canonicalized tree.
Figure Figure 8.2 compares what the algorithms retain. The arrows encode ancestry; the graph merge and MCTS backup are the operations unavailable to a simple beam.

flowchart LR
    subgraph B["Beam: fixed frontier"]
        B0["prompt"] --> B1["top thought A"]
        B0 --> B2["top thought B"]
        B1 --> B3["top-B next states"]
        B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph T["Tree of Thoughts: branch and backtrack"]
        T0["prompt"] --> T1["thought A"]
        T0 --> T2["thought B"]
        T1 --> T3["A1"]
        T1 --> T4["A2"]
        T2 --> T5["B1"]
    end
    subgraph G["Graph of Thoughts: merge reusable state"]
        G0["prompt"] --> G1["route A"]
        G0 --> G2["route B"]
        G1 --> G3["canonical intermediate"]
        G2 --> G3
    end
    subgraph M["MCTS: selection and value backup"]
        M0["visited root N,Q"] --> M1["selected branch"]
        M1 --> M2["new expansion"]
        M2 -. "value backup" .-> M0
    end





flowchart LR
    subgraph B["Beam: fixed frontier"]
        B0["prompt"] --> B1["top thought A"]
        B0 --> B2["top thought B"]
        B1 --> B3["top-B next states"]
        B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph T["Tree of Thoughts: branch and backtrack"]
        T0["prompt"] --> T1["thought A"]
        T0 --> T2["thought B"]
        T1 --> T3["A1"]
        T1 --> T4["A2"]
        T2 --> T5["B1"]
    end
    subgraph G["Graph of Thoughts: merge reusable state"]
        G0["prompt"] --> G1["route A"]
        G0 --> G2["route B"]
        G1 --> G3["canonical intermediate"]
        G2 --> G3
    end
    subgraph M["MCTS: selection and value backup"]
        M0["visited root N,Q"] --> M1["selected branch"]
        M1 --> M2["new expansion"]
        M2 -. "value backup" .-> M0
    end




Figure 8.2: What information does each reasoning-search structure retain?





The selection rule, not the topology name, determines whether search helps. Model likelihood can favor familiar errors. A learned process score can be confidently wrong off distribution. A deterministic partial checker may be incomplete. Track expansions, prunes, verifier calls, recovered branches, and final verified success so extra search has an accountable marginal return.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.7 Verifiers set the ceiling
An outcome reward model (ORM) scores a complete response or final answer. It is cheap to call once per candidate and matches tasks whose final result is sufficient. Exact answer matching, unit tests, and a simulator terminal condition are programmatic ORMs. A learned sequence classifier is also an ORM, but its score is a proxy rather than a proof.
A process reward model (PRM) scores intermediate steps or prefixes. It can identify the first invalid transformation, prune a branch early, and provide denser credit during training. Its supervision is much harder. Annotators need consistent step boundaries and must distinguish a locally unusual but valid path from an error. Monte Carlo labels—continue a prefix many times and use downstream success as its value—replace human labels with sampling assumptions and can be noisy on rare but recoverable states.
The operational choice follows the information available:









Verifier
Observes
Useful when
Residual risk




exact ORM
final artifact
answer or program has an executable truth condition
parser or tests omit cases


learned ORM
full response
quality is holistic but labeled comparisons exist
style and distribution biases


deterministic process check
partial state
invariants can reject a branch early
checker is incomplete


learned PRM
intermediate reasoning steps
early pruning saves substantial generation
local labels may penalize valid alternatives



A noisy verifier places a mathematical ceiling on best-of-n. Suppose a sampled candidate is correct with probability p. A binary verifier has symmetric error rate q: it marks a correct candidate positive with probability (1-q) and a wrong one positive with probability q. Among candidates marked positive, its precision is

\pi_v=
\frac{p(1-q)}{p(1-q)+(1-p)q}.

As the candidate pool grows, the probability of finding some marked candidate approaches one, but selecting uniformly among marked candidates approaches precision \pi_v, not perfect accuracy. A ranker has a different formula, yet the same constraint: increasing n creates more correct candidates and more false positives. The tail behavior of the verifier determines which wins.
Verification must therefore be evaluated under the candidate distribution induced by search. Measure false positives at the top of the score distribution, not only average classification accuracy on random pairs. Hold out adversarial candidates. For programmatic checks, mutate correct and incorrect solutions and verify that the parser rejects malformed, ambiguous, and multiple-answer outputs.
This also explains the limits of intrinsic self-correction. Asking the same model to reconsider without new evidence reuses correlated knowledge and biases. Empirical work has found that such prompts can leave reasoning unchanged or flip correct answers to wrong ones. This is not a claim that revision never works. It separates revision with new information—compiler errors, test failures, retrieved facts, a PRM signal—from revision that only restates the original uncertainty. Log right-to-wrong and wrong-to-right transitions separately; a net score can hide destructive correction.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.8 Allocate compute by marginal value
There is no globally optimal number of reasoning tokens or samples. Let m denote a strategy—serial extension, parallel sampling, or guided search—and b its compute budget. A decision rule can be written

(m^*,b^*)=\arg\max_{m,b}
\left(\mathbb{E}[U\mid x,m,b]-\lambda C(m,b)\right),

where U is task utility, C is compute measured consistently, and \lambda expresses how much utility justifies another unit of compute. The expectation is conditional on the prompt x because difficulty changes the curve.
Easy prompts may already pass a cheap check, so more compute adds little. Medium prompts often benefit most: the generator has nonzero coverage and the verifier can discriminate candidates. On extremely hard prompts, all candidates may fail and group rewards may have zero variance. Search then spends heavily without a useful gradient or selection signal. A stronger base policy, better decomposition, or new external information may be required before test-time scaling pays.
Compute-optimal studies confirm the need to condition strategy on prompt difficulty and verifier behavior rather than applying one fixed best-of-n. In practice, build per-slice curves of utility against total generated tokens and verifier calls. The slope

\frac{\Delta\text{verified utility}}{\Delta\text{sampled tokens}}

is more actionable than a single benchmark maximum. Stop when the slope falls below the product’s threshold or when the verifier cannot distinguish additional candidates.
This chapter owns the allocation mechanism, not production serving controls. Provider-specific reasoning settings, request budgets, cache interaction, length forcing, and the latency economics of long traces belong in Chapter 10. Here, compute is an abstract measured budget so the decision survives changes in API surface.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
section: 8.9 GRPO replaces the critic with siblings
text:
8.9 GRPO replaces the critic with siblings
Chapter 7 introduced PPO’s value model as a learned baseline. Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), introduced in DeepSeekMath, removes that critic. For each prompt x, sample a group of G responses y_1,\ldots,y_G from the old policy and score them with rewards r_1,\ldots,r_G. Define

\bar r=\frac{1}{G}\sum_{i=1}^{G}r_i,
\qquad
s_r=\sqrt{\frac{1}{G}\sum_{i=1}^{G}(r_i-\bar r)^2},

and the group-relative advantage

\hat A_i=\frac{r_i-\bar r}{s_r+\varepsilon}.

The siblings supply the baseline. A response better than its group receives positive advantage; one worse receives negative advantage. If all rewards are equal, every advantage is zero and the prompt supplies no learning signal. The reference fixture implements exactly this population-standardized calculation:
# code/ch08/reason_rl.py — changed region: grpo_advantages
mean = sum(rewards) / len(rewards)
variance = sum((reward - mean) ** 2 for reward in rewards) / len(rewards)
if variance == 0.0:
    return [0.0] * len(rewards)
scale = math.sqrt(variance)
return [(reward - mean) / scale for reward in rewards]
For token t of response i, let

\rho_{i,t}(\theta)=
\frac{\pi_\theta(y_{i,t}\mid x,y_{i,<t})}
{\pi_{\theta_{\mathrm{old}}}(y_{i,t}\mid x,y_{i,<t})}.

A common GRPO loss applies the same sequence advantage to each response token:

\mathcal{L}_{\text{GRPO}}(\theta)=
-\frac{1}{G}\sum_{i=1}^{G}\frac{1}{|y_i|}
\sum_{t=1}^{|y_i|}
\min\!\left(
\rho_{i,t}\hat A_i,
\operatorname{clip}(\rho_{i,t},1-\epsilon,1+\epsilon)\hat A_i
\right)
+\beta D_{\mathrm{KL}}(\pi_\theta\|\pi_{\text{ref}}).

Here |y_i| is response length, \epsilon is PPO’s clipping width, and \beta controls an optional reference KL. Implementations vary in normalization, ratio granularity, and whether a reference penalty is retained for a programmatic reward. Those choices are part of the algorithm, not harmless reductions.
The fixture uses one discrete strategy as a sequence-level action. It reuses each sampled group for three clipped epochs and applies a small reference KL. The active gradient is the exact softmax score gradient:
# code/ch08/reason_rl.py — changed region: train_grpo
ratio = probabilities[action] / old[action]
clipped = (advantage >= 0 and ratio > 1 + clip) or (
    advantage < 0 and ratio < 1 - clip
)
if not clipped:
    for index in range(5):
        score = float(index == action) - probabilities[index]
        gradient[index] -= advantage * ratio * score / group_size









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




reason_rl.py after train_grpo
58 lines
sibling rewards replace the critic, zero-variance groups produce zero signal, and clipped ratios bound sample reuse




GRPO removes the learned value network; it does not remove online sampling, old-policy bookkeeping, reward computation, or the need to monitor exploration. The memory saving matters, but we do not repeat Chapter 6’s bytes-per-parameter arithmetic. The conceptual delta from PPO is the baseline source.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.10 RLVR turns verification into training
Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) uses an executable check as the reward instead of a learned human-preference model. Math equivalence, unit tests, a theorem prover, a simulator, or a constrained optimizer can produce the signal. The reward may still include formatting and other terms, but only the programmatically checked portion deserves the word verifiable.
The connection to test-time compute is direct. Repeated sampling uses a verifier to select a response without changing weights. RLVR uses the same verifier to increase the probability of successful responses in future samples. The fixture’s is_correct() function first computes exact-verifier coverage and then supplies the exact GRPO reward vector. This is one mechanism promoted from ranker to trainer.
The R1 family made a multi-stage version of this pipeline prominent. R1-Zero applied large-scale RL directly to a base policy and reported the emergence of longer reasoning and self-revision-like behavior, alongside readability and language-mixing problems. The R1 pipeline added cold-start long-reasoning examples before RL, then used rejection sampling to create supervised data and a further RL stage to improve reasoning while restoring broader behavior. Trace distillation into smaller models is a customization mechanism owned by Chapter 11.
Figure Figure 8.3 answers what each stage contributes. It should not be read as a universal mandatory recipe.

flowchart LR
    B(["Base policy"]) --> C["Cold-start SFT<br/>readable reasoning format"]
    C --> R["RLVR<br/>explore and reinforce verified outcomes"]
    R --> S["Rejection-sampling SFT<br/>retain strong traces and broad data"]
    S --> F["Final RL<br/>reasoning plus behavior objectives"]
    V["Programmatic verifier"] -->|"outcome reward"| R
    V -->|"filter candidates"| S





flowchart LR
    B(["Base policy"]) --> C["Cold-start SFT<br/>readable reasoning format"]
    C --> R["RLVR<br/>explore and reinforce verified outcomes"]
    R --> S["Rejection-sampling SFT<br/>retain strong traces and broad data"]
    S --> F["Final RL<br/>reasoning plus behavior objectives"]
    V["Programmatic verifier"] -->|"outcome reward"| R
    V -->|"filter candidates"| S




Figure 8.3: What distinct problem does each stage of an R1-style pipeline address?





The durable pattern is staged error correction. SFT establishes a usable support and format; online RL changes the distribution using fresh outcomes; rejection sampling converts selected successes back into stable supervised data. Skip a stage when its measured delta does not justify its cost.
An exact-looking verifier can still be incomplete. Numeric extraction may accept a correct number embedded in contradictory text. Unit tests may miss forbidden I/O. A proof checker validates the formal statement it receives, which may not match the natural-language task. Verifier tests and specification review remain part of RLVR.
crumbs: Part II — Training and Post-Training
8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
section: 8.11 Entropy, length, and Goodhart break naive RL
text:
8.11 Entropy, length, and Goodhart break naive RL
The training reward can rise while the policy becomes less useful. Three mechanisms must be separated because they require different repairs.

8.11.1 Entropy collapse removes exploration
For a token or discrete action distribution, entropy is

H(\pi)=-\sum_a\pi(a)\log\pi(a).

Policy-gradient updates increase actions that happened to earn positive advantage and decrease alternatives. Across many updates, probability concentrates and entropy falls. Some concentration is the goal: pass@1 improves when correct paths become more likely. Entropy collapse is the failure regime in which diversity disappears before the policy has explored enough of the solution space. Training loss can look healthy while pass@k coverage and novel strategy discovery stagnate.
Track entropy by position and prompt difficulty, distinct-answer count, pass@1, and high-k coverage. Wider upper clipping for positive-advantage tokens, explicit entropy control, reference regularization, and richer prompt curricula can preserve exploration. These are interventions to validate, not guarantees.


8.11.2 Length can be rewarded accidentally by loss or data
Vanilla GRPO’s per-response division by |y_i| changes token weighting. For positive-advantage responses it can favor shorter correct responses; for negative-advantage responses it penalizes each token of a long wrong response less, creating pressure toward longer wrong answers. Group standard-deviation normalization can also overweight prompt groups according to their reward variance.
Dr. GRPO removes response-length and within-group standard-deviation normalization to target these biases. DAPO uses batch-level token averaging, asymmetric clipping that permits more upward movement, dynamic sampling that removes all-equal reward groups, and soft overlength shaping. These proposals show that “GRPO” is a family of objectives whose reduction and normalization must be written down.








Variant or intervention
Changes
Intended failure addressed




Dr. GRPO
removes response-length and group-std normalization
long incorrect-response and difficulty weighting bias


DAPO token-level loss
averages active tokens across the batch
per-response length bias


DAPO clip-higher
separates lower and upper clip widths
premature entropy collapse


DAPO dynamic sampling
drops groups with zero reward variance
rollout compute with zero gradient


overlength shaping
penalizes approaching truncation gradually
hard cutoff and runaway response length



The chapter fixture does not claim to reproduce vanilla GRPO’s token-normalization bias: its action is an entire synthetic response. Instead, it creates a separate and more severe length problem in the reward itself.


8.11.3 Goodhart’s law separates proxy reward from utility
Goodhart’s law is the warning that a measure stops behaving like the intended goal when optimization pressure targets the measure. Let U(x,y) be true specification utility and \hat R(x,y) the implemented reward. On the original candidate distribution they may correlate. GRPO changes that distribution toward regions with high \hat R. If the verifier or shaping term has a blind spot, the policy can find responses where \hat R is high and U is low.
The fixture’s exact mode rewards only correct arithmetic. Its broken mode adds an excessive response-length bonus. The five action rewards are produced through the same core:
# code/ch08/reason_rl.py — changed region: reward_values
if mode == "exact":
    return [1.0, 1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0]
if mode == "proxy":
    return [
        float(is_correct(action)) + 2.2 * TOKEN_LENGTHS[action] / 30
        + .1 * float(action != 3)
        for action in range(5)
    ]
The proxy run initially raises true accuracy from 0.393 to 0.670 as it reinforces a verbose correct strategy. Continued optimization discovers a longer incorrect strategy with higher proxy reward. By step 120, normalized proxy objective reaches 0.992, true accuracy falls to 0.018, expected length rises from 8.49 to 29.49 fixture tokens, and normalized entropy falls to 0.087. This is the canonical signature: measured reward improves, independent utility reverses, and exploration disappears.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




reason_rl.py with exact and proxy reward adapters
14 lines
the same optimizer exposes divergence between optimized reward and independently measured correctness




Mitigation begins with separation of measurements. Keep outcome quality, length, format, and verifier score as distinct time series. Evaluate on hidden tests and adversarial candidates not used by the reward. Constrain cheap-to-game shaping terms, inspect top-reward failures, and stop or roll back when reward and independent utility decouple. An ensemble of correlated weak judges is not an independent check.
Sequence-level outcomes also create a credit-assignment problem: every token receives the same \hat A_i, although only a few steps caused success or failure. PRMs and executable intermediate checks can densify credit, but they import their own label and verifier errors. Multi-turn agent trajectories add state, tools, and delayed effects; Chapter 23 develops that distinct problem.

8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
section: 8.12 Capability claims require pass@k and proof
text:
8.12 Capability claims require pass@k and proof
When RLVR raises pass@1, two explanations are possible. It may shift probability toward reasoning paths already present in the base policy, or it may make previously inaccessible strategies reachable. A pass@1 score cannot distinguish them.
Compare full pass@k curves before and after training. If the tuned model wins at small k but the base model catches up or exceeds it at large k, RL has sharpened sampling while possibly reducing coverage. One 2025 study reported this pattern across its settings. ProRL reported counter-evidence that prolonged RL can uncover strategies its sampled base models did not reach. These results use finite budgets, particular base models, prompts, and verifiers; neither establishes a universal theorem about capability creation.
A defensible claim reports pass@k over a wide disclosed range, semantic diversity of successful traces, held-out task families, and whether a purportedly novel strategy can be elicited from the base under matched search. Pretraining and post-training data lineage matter: an “emergent” behavior may already be latent in a base model or template. Capability is not established by a longer trace or an “aha” phrase.
Final-answer exact matching is also insufficient for proof tasks. A correct numeric destination can follow invalid reasoning. Proof-graded evaluation has three increasingly strict forms:

expert grading against a problem-specific marking scheme, with partial credit and identified invalid steps;
a validated proof grader calibrated against expert judgments;
formal verification, where a proof assistant checks a machine-readable certificate against the intended theorem and axioms.

Formal verification has the strongest local correctness contract but requires successful translation into the formal statement. Human and learned grading handle natural-language arguments but need disagreement measurement and adversarial validation. Chapter 22 owns judge calibration and statistical evaluation; the reporting minimum here is to disclose benchmark version, prompt/template, sample count, temperature, generated-token budget, selection rule, verifier, and whether the number is pass@k, average sample accuracy, or selected accuracy.
A saturated benchmark can still be a regression test, but it cannot separate frontier systems. Maintain difficulty slices and retire capability claims when ceiling effects or contamination dominate. Never compare a one-sample score to a selected best-of-many score without labeling the compute and selection procedure.

8.13 Landscape 2026 (dated)






Note



Verified 2026-07-19. Reasoning evaluation is moving from short final-answer sets toward continuously refreshed problems, natural-language proof grading, research-level questions, and formal Lean artifacts. MathArena’s 2026 platform includes proof-based competitions, research-level arXiv problems, and formal proof generation. FrontierMath released a major v2 update on 2026-06-12 after revising a substantial portion of its items, an unusually sharp reminder that benchmark version is part of every score. AdvancedMathBench appeared in July 2026 with advanced proof-generation and verification tasks. Benchmark contents, model coverage, graders, and leaderboards are volatile; deleting this box leaves the chapter’s pass@k and proof-verification mechanisms intact.
Verify live: consult the MathArena platform paper, MathArena site, FrontierMath versioned benchmark page, and AdvancedMathBench paper before citing task counts, versions, scoring procedures, saturation, or model results. Appendix C owns the annual audit.

8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.14 Build
The reasoning-and-RLVR microscope uses 60 synthetic arithmetic tasks split across three difficulty classes. Five discrete strategies stand in for a response distribution: concise correct, verbose correct, concentrated off-by-one, diffuse wrong, and long reward-hacking. The representation is intentionally small enough that policy probabilities, entropy, and expected length can be computed exactly. It does not pretend that five actions reproduce token-level language modeling.
Run the deterministic path from the newbook directory:
python code/ch08/reason_rl.py
python code/ch08/render_metrics.py code/ch08/reasoning_metrics.svg
python -m unittest discover -s code/ch08 -p "test_*.py" -v
The sampler evaluates six budgets from one to 32 samples. The trainer draws groups of eight, computes exact group-relative advantages, reuses each group for three clipped updates, and retains a small reference KL. Both exact and broken reward modes call the same trainer. The generated figure contains three different questions: what repeated sampling buys, whether objective and true accuracy agree, and how exploration changes.






Figure 8.4: How do repeated sampling, exact RLVR, and a misspecified proxy change accuracy and exploration? Exact-verifier coverage reaches 1.000 at about 271 sampled fixture tokens while plurality reaches 0.583 and a noisy verifier reaches 0.732. Exact RLVR reaches 0.982 true accuracy; the proxy objective reaches 0.992 while true accuracy collapses to 0.018.



Figure Figure 8.4 separates three conclusions. In the left panel, more candidate tokens continue to increase coverage, but selection quality limits realized accuracy. In the middle panel, exact reward and true utility agree, while the proxy run crosses from useful shaping into Goodhart failure after step 30. In the right panel, both policies lose entropy; the proxy run collapses almost completely as it concentrates on the exploit.
The exact checkpoints are:









Measurement
Initial
Final or peak
Interpretation




exact-RLVR true accuracy
0.393
0.982 final
verified strategies become probable


exact-RLVR normalized entropy
0.918
0.335 final
exploitation rises; some diversity remains


proxy-run true accuracy
0.393
0.670 peak, 0.018 final
reward first helps, then selects an exploit


proxy normalized objective
0.476
0.992 final
optimized measure continues improving


proxy expected length
8.49
29.49 final
length bonus dominates behavior



Break the fixture deliberately. Change the concentrated wrong answer in candidate_answer to a diffuse wrong answer and predict how plurality changes. Set verifier flip rate from 0.18 to zero and confirm noisy best-of-n becomes exact coverage. Reduce the proxy length coefficient until objective and correctness no longer reverse. Every modification tests a causal mechanism rather than merely changing a score.
The optional real-model adapter replaces only candidate generation. With torch and transformers installed separately, run:
python code/ch08/hf_generate_adapter.py MODEL_ID `
  "Compute 37 * 14 and put the final integer last." --count 8
It renders a chat template when the tokenizer provides one, samples without vLLM or a rollout engine, returns completion token counts, and extracts each final integer. Model download, network access, and device placement are opt-in. Hardware kernels may remain nondeterministic even with a seed, so live results should be stored as a versioned distribution rather than snapshot-tested prose.
Honesty note. The deterministic lab proves objective mechanics, selection ceilings, and a constructed reward failure. It does not establish a benchmark result, the sample efficiency of GRPO on a neural language model, or the universal behavior of any named model. A real run needs held-out tasks, multiple seeds, strict verifier tests, token-level log-probabilities, and independent evaluation.

8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.15 What endures, what changes
What endures. Serial reasoning extends computation through the autoregressive loop. Parallel samples create coverage, not automatic accuracy. Search needs state, expansion, value, and stopping interfaces. Verifier false positives cap best-of-n. External feedback is qualitatively different from unsupported self-revision. GRPO replaces PPO’s critic with a group baseline; all-equal groups contain no learning signal. Entropy, objective normalization, credit assignment, and reward misspecification must be measured independently. Pass@1 alone cannot establish new capability.
What changes. Search recipes, GRPO-family variants, reasoning curricula, benchmark difficulty, proof graders, model traces, and recommended training stacks will continue to move. Provider inference controls and their economics belong to Chapter 10’s dated serving profile. Keep model names, benchmark scores, and trainer defaults outside the durable mechanism.

title: 8  Reasoning: Test-Time Compute and RLVR [F+S]
text:
8.16 Exercises

Reverse plurality. Change the fixture so one wrong answer is the unique mode while correct samples still have nonzero probability. Sweep to k=256 and show coverage approaching one while plurality approaches the wrong answer. State the violated unique-mode condition.
Verify the ceiling. For a fixed p and symmetric verifier error q, compute \pi_v by hand. Modify the fixture to use a single difficulty and sweep k until empirical noisy best-of-n approaches that precision.
Implement one search. Add a beam over partial arithmetic operations using one shared expansion function and one exact partial-state checker. Report expansions and verified accuracy against independent sampling at equal expansion count.
Remove zero-signal groups. Add DAPO-style dynamic sampling that skips all-equal reward groups. Measure the fraction of skipped groups at the beginning and end of exact RLVR, then explain when resampling saves compute and when it biases the prompt distribution.
Repair length normalization. Extend the fixture to token-level actions and compare per-response normalization with a fixed-budget denominator. Predict which incorrect strategy receives less penalty and test the prediction.
Attack the verifier. Replace exact integer equality with “gold digits appear anywhere.” Construct the shortest incorrect response that earns reward, train the proxy run, and write the verifier test that would have prevented it.
Defend an allocation. A proof generator has 20% pass@1, 70% pass@32, and a learned verifier with 75% precision in its selected tail. Choose among longer single traces, parallel best-of-n, PRM-guided search, or stopping. Specify the measurement that would reverse your choice.

8.17 Notes and sources
The serial-reasoning and sampling foundations are Chain-of-Thought Prompting, Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning, and Large Language Monkeys. Difficulty-conditioned allocation follows Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally. Search mechanisms are developed in Tree of Thoughts, Graph of Thoughts, and the MCTS-based Reasoning via Planning.
Process supervision and automated process labels are treated in Let’s Verify Step by Step and Math-Shepherd. The intrinsic-correction warning comes from Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet; its claim boundaries should be preserved rather than generalized to every feedback-assisted revision method.
GRPO originated in DeepSeekMath and was popularized for RLVR by DeepSeek-R1. Objective corrections and scaling variants are documented in Understanding R1-Zero-Like Training / Dr. GRPO, DAPO, and The Entropy Mechanism of Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning Language Models. The competing capability interpretations are represented by Does RL Really Incentivize Reasoning Capacity Beyond the Base Model? and ProRL. Current proof-evaluation sources are kept in the dated landscape box.

9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
section: 9.3 One request has two latency phases
text:
9.3 One request has two latency phases
A user sends a 2,000-token contract and asks for a 100-token answer. The first visible token arrives slowly. The remaining tokens stream at a steadier cadence. A dashboard reports one latency number, so an engineer shortens the output limit. Total latency falls, but the long initial pause remains. The intervention changed the wrong phase.
Autoregressive inference has two mechanically different phases. During prefill, the model processes the prompt positions in parallel and constructs a key–value cache for every layer. During decode, it produces one new position, appends that position’s keys and values to the cache, and repeats. Prefill can expose large matrix multiplications to the accelerator. Decode has a serial dependency: token t+1 cannot be chosen before token t becomes context.
Three request-level measurements keep the distinction visible:

Time to first token (TTFT) starts when the caller submits a request and ends when the first output token is available. End-to-end TTFT can include network transit, queueing, tokenization, prefill, and the first decode decision. A model-only TTFT excludes some of those terms, so every report must state its boundary.
Time per output token (TPOT) is the average time between output tokens after the first. Inter-token latency (ITL) is each individual gap; its tail exposes stalls hidden by a mean.
End-to-end latency for n_{out} output tokens is approximately


L \approx \mathrm{TTFT} + (n_{out}-1)\,\mathrm{TPOT}.

The approximation is useful only when accompanied by its units and scope. Stop detection, detokenization, network flushing, variable step time as the cache grows, and scheduling can make individual gaps differ. Still, it immediately explains why shortening a response mostly affects the second term and shortening a long prompt mostly affects the first.
Figure Figure 9.1 asks where a millisecond can enter one request. It deliberately ends at the boundary of a single request. Continuous batching, queue disciplines, prefix caching, speculative decoding, goodput, and production service objectives belong to Chapter 10.

sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Caller
    participant Q as Queue and tokenizer
    participant M as Model runtime
    participant D as Detokenizer
    C->>Q: prompt + decoding policy
    Q->>M: prompt token IDs
    Note over Q,M: queueing + tokenization
    M->>M: prefill all prompt positions; build KV
    M->>M: decode first output token
    M-->>D: first token ID
    D-->>C: first safe text chunk
    Note over C,M: TTFT ends
    loop remaining output positions
        M->>M: one cached decode step
        M-->>D: next token ID
        D-->>C: zero or more safe characters
    end
    Note over C,M: gaps are ITL; their mean is TPOT





sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Caller
    participant Q as Queue and tokenizer
    participant M as Model runtime
    participant D as Detokenizer
    C->>Q: prompt + decoding policy
    Q->>M: prompt token IDs
    Note over Q,M: queueing + tokenization
    M->>M: prefill all prompt positions; build KV
    M->>M: decode first output token
    M-->>D: first token ID
    D-->>C: first safe text chunk
    Note over C,M: TTFT ends
    loop remaining output positions
        M->>M: one cached decode step
        M-->>D: next token ID
        D-->>C: zero or more safe characters
    end
    Note over C,M: gaps are ITL; their mean is TPOT




Figure 9.1: Where does elapsed time enter one autoregressive request?





The durable measurement rule is to preserve both phases. A single percentile for total latency cannot tell whether prompt work, serial generation, queueing, or streaming is responsible.

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.4 What you will build






TipThe inference-behavior microscope



You will run one integrated, engine-free CPU build rooted at code/ch09/inference_lab.py. The facade composes three focused mechanisms: request_profile.py, decoding_runtime.py, and selective_prediction.py. Its deterministic fixture follows one request through an illustrative roofline model, accounts for KV bytes, applies temperature and four truncation policies, demonstrates processor ordering, creates a greedy flip from two legal floating-point reduction orders, calibrates answer confidence, constructs split-conformal answer sets, applies conformal risk control, and buffers a split UTF-8 stream safely.
The build emits TTFT, TPOT, total latency, KV MiB, sampler support and entropy, replay equality, probability drift, calibration scores, semantic entropy, risk–coverage points, conformal coverage, and streamed text. render_metrics.py turns the same values into the chapter’s print-safe SVG. Success means every formula matches the fixture, seed replay is exact while the batch-order probe still flips, calibration improves proper scores, and the abstention report distinguishes marginal error from error among answered queries.
hf_inference_adapter.py is an optional torch/transformers seam. It times a real cached greedy loop and compares a prompt alone with the same prompt in a larger batch. It may download weights and is never imported by the default build.

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
section: 9.5 KV bytes place the phases on a roofline
text:
9.5 KV bytes place the phases on a roofline
The KV cache prevents decode from recomputing every earlier key and value. This chapter needs one result from that mechanism: its storage grows linearly with sequence length. For a conventional decoder cache,

M_{KV}=2LBTH_{KV}d_hs \quad \text{bytes}.

Here L is the number of layers, B the batch size, T the cached sequence length, H_{KV} the number of key/value heads, d_h the head dimension, and s the bytes per stored scalar. The leading 2 accounts for keys and values. Query heads do not appear. In multi-head attention, H_{KV} usually equals the number of query heads. Grouped-query attention uses fewer KV heads, and multi-query attention uses one. A latent-attention architecture stores a compressed representation with architecture-specific additions, so substituting its query-head count into this formula is wrong.








Attention cache form
Width term
Cache ratio against H_Q-head MHA




Multi-head attention
H_{KV}=H_Q
1


Grouped-query attention
H_Q > H_{KV} > 1
H_{KV}/H_Q


Multi-query attention
H_{KV}=1
H_Q^{-1}


Latent/compressed attention
compressed latent plus model-specific state
derive from that architecture



The fixture uses 32 layers, eight KV heads, head dimension 128, batch one, and two-byte cache values. It therefore stores 128 KiB per cached token: 16 MiB at 128 tokens, 64 MiB at 512, and 256 MiB at 2,048. These are configuration arithmetic, not measurements of a named model.
Storage explains growth; the roofline model explains why prefill and decode often hit different hardware limits. Arithmetic intensity is

I=\frac{\text{floating-point operations}}{\text{bytes moved from the limiting memory level}}.

If hardware sustains peak compute P_{max} FLOP/s and memory bandwidth W_{max} byte/s, attainable performance is bounded by

P(I)\leq \min(P_{max},\;I W_{max}).

The ridge point I^*=P_{max}/W_{max} separates the bandwidth-limited slope from the compute ceiling. For a weight matrix W\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times k} multiplied by b activation vectors, the dominant work is about (2mkb) FLOPs. If the weights dominate traffic and each weight occupies s bytes, intensity is approximately (2b/s). With two-byte weights, I\approx b. A decode matrix multiplication has a small effective b; prefill exposes many prompt positions and has a much larger one.
This is a placement model, not a latency oracle. Attention reads growing KV state. Fused kernels change traffic. Caches above HBM, quantization, sparsity, and tensor parallel communication add ceilings. The fixture intentionally uses an illustrative 80 TFLOP/s compute ceiling and 2 TB/s memory ceiling, giving a ridge at 40 FLOP/byte. Its decode point at intensity one is bandwidth-bound; all three prefill points are compute-bound. Chapter 10 turns these corollaries into batching and serving decisions.
# code/ch09/request_profile.py — changed region: request_profiles
def kv_bytes(tokens: int, batch: int = 1) -> int:
    return (2 * MODEL["layers"] * MODEL["kv_heads"] * MODEL["head_dim"]
            * tokens * batch * HARDWARE["dtype_bytes"])

def request_profiles(output_tokens: int = 128):
    parameter_bytes = MODEL["parameters"] * HARDWARE["dtype_bytes"]
    for tokens in (128, 512, 2048):
        prefill_ms = max(2 * MODEL["parameters"] * tokens / HARDWARE["peak_flops"],
                         parameter_bytes / HARDWARE["memory_bandwidth"]) * 1000
        decode_bytes = parameter_bytes + kv_bytes(tokens)
        tpot_ms = max(2 * MODEL["parameters"] / HARDWARE["peak_flops"],
                      decode_bytes / HARDWARE["memory_bandwidth"]) * 1000
        yield prefill_ms + tpot_ms, tpot_ms, kv_bytes(tokens)









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




request_profile.py after request profiling
31 lines
KV storage is linear in cached tokens; prompt length raises TTFT far more than TPOT in the declared illustrative roofline.

9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
section: 9.6 Decoding turns logits into a choice
text:
9.6 Decoding turns logits into a choice
At output position t, the model produces one logit z_i for every vocabulary token i. A decoding policy turns that vector into a token. Greedy decoding returns an index with maximum logit. Sampling first defines probabilities and draws from them. Both choices are caller policy layered over the same model output.
Temperature rescales logits before softmax:

p_i(T)=\frac{\exp(z_i/T)}{\sum_j\exp(z_j/T)}, \qquad T>0.

Equivalently, if p_i(1) is the ordinary softmax probability, then p_i(T) is proportional to p_i(1)^{1/T}. Lower T sharpens the distribution; higher T flattens it. Dividing all logits by the same positive value preserves their order. Temperature changes gaps and entropy, not which token has the highest logit.
Implement the small-T limit as a branch to argmax rather than evaluating exp(z/T) with a near-zero divisor. Subtracting the maximum scaled logit before exponentiation prevents overflow without changing probabilities. The fixture’s softmax does both. At temperature=0, it returns a one-hot greedy distribution with deterministic tie-breaking by index. That makes the choice rule deterministic for a fixed logit vector. Section Section 9.9 will show why the vector itself may still move.
Greedy decoding optimizes one local decision at each step, not the probability or usefulness of a complete sequence. Beam search retains several high-scoring prefixes and usually normalizes sequence scores to reduce a short-output bias. It is search, not stochastic sampling; maximizing likelihood can also produce generic or repetitive text. Chapter 8 owns search and verifier-driven selection. This chapter keeps beam search as an exercise so the sampler remains inspectable.
The bounded Route-B backfill for Chapter 12 begins here. A reader who understands the map logits → policy → token, the temperature equation, and the determinism contract in Section Section 9.9 has the sampling foundation needed to call a model API without treating its parameters as magic.
crumbs: Part III — Inference, Serving, and Customization
9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.7 Truncation shapes the candidate support
Sampling from the full softmax preserves every token with nonzero probability. A large vocabulary can leave a long tail of locally implausible tokens. Truncation masks part of the distribution and renormalizes the survivors before drawing. It is a bias–diversity decision: remove too little and tail events can damage coherence; remove too much and useful alternatives disappear.
Three policies express different invariants:

Top-k retains exactly the k highest-probability tokens, except that a defensive implementation always retains at least one. Its support size is fixed even when the distribution is flat or sharply peaked.
Nucleus or top-p sorts tokens by descending probability and retains the smallest prefix whose cumulative mass reaches p. Its support adapts to distribution shape. The token that crosses the threshold belongs in the set; applying the mask before including it creates an off-by-one error. For any threshold, the top token keeps the set nonempty.
Min-p retains token i when p_i\geq \alpha p_{max}, where p_{max} is the largest probability and alpha is a relative cutoff. Its threshold rises and falls with the leading token. This can preserve more candidates at high temperature than a fixed cumulative-mass rule while still removing probabilities small relative to the mode.

Locally typical sampling offers another view: retain tokens whose information content -\log p_i lies near the distribution’s entropy, then stop when their mass reaches a budget. It can reduce degenerate repetition on some open-ended tasks, but no truncation policy dominates across workloads.
The order temperature → probabilities → truncation → renormalization matters because top-p, min-p, and typicality inspect probabilities after temperature has reshaped them. On the fixture vector at temperature 1.2, full sampling retains five tokens, top-2 retains two, top-p at 0.8 retains three, min-p at 0.15 retains four, and typical sampling at 0.8 retains three. These support counts are local facts about one vector, not universal recommended settings.
# code/ch09/decoding_runtime.py — changed region: top-p and min-p in truncate
elif method == "top_p":
    keep, mass = set(), 0.0
    for i in order:
        keep.add(i)
        mass += probs[i]
        if mass >= value:
            break
elif method == "min_p":
    cutoff = value * probs[order[0]]
    keep = {i for i in order if probs[i] >= cutoff} or {order[0]}
total = sum(probs[i] for i in keep)
return [probs[i] / total if i in keep else 0.0 for i in range(len(probs))]

New code
Invariant now verified

Every filter returns a normalized, nonempty distribution; top-p includes its threshold-crossing token.

9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.8 Processor order changes the policy
Real generation applies more than temperature and a support mask. A logit processor changes logits using token history or caller policy. A logit bias adds a token-specific offset. A presence penalty acts once after a token has appeared. A frequency penalty grows with its count. A repetition penalty often uses a sign-aware multiplicative rule: divide a positive seen-token logit by r>1, but multiply a negative one by r. Applying the same division to negative logits would make them less negative and accidentally reward repetition.
Let c_i be the count of token i, a_i an explicit bias, f a frequency penalty, and u a presence penalty. One additive form is

z'_i=z_i+a_i-fc_i-u\mathbf{1}[c_i>0].

If the result is then divided by temperature, an additive offset’s effective log-probability strength scales as (1/T). Moving the same penalty after temperature changes the policy. Hard grammar masks, banned tokens, forced tokens, EOS rules, and provider-specific processors add further ordering choices. Record the effective pipeline, not merely parameter names.
Figure Figure 9.2 shows the conceptual order used by the fixture. A serving library may combine stages or use a documented different order; that variation belongs in a versioned adapter test.

flowchart LR
    Z(["Model logits z"]) --> P["History processors: repetition, frequency, presence"]
    P --> B["Policy bias and hard token masks"]
    B --> T["Divide by temperature T"]
    T --> S(["Stable softmax probabilities"])
    S --> M["Top-k, top-p, min-p, or typical mask"]
    M --> R["Renormalize surviving mass"]
    R --> C{"Choice rule"}
    C -->|"sample"| RNG(["Seeded categorical draw"])
    C -->|"greedy"| ARG(["Argmax with tie rule"])





flowchart LR
    Z(["Model logits z"]) --> P["History processors: repetition, frequency, presence"]
    P --> B["Policy bias and hard token masks"]
    B --> T["Divide by temperature T"]
    T --> S(["Stable softmax probabilities"])
    S --> M["Top-k, top-p, min-p, or typical mask"]
    M --> R["Renormalize surviving mass"]
    R --> C{"Choice rule"}
    C -->|"sample"| RNG(["Seeded categorical draw"])
    C -->|"greedy"| ARG(["Argmax with tie rule"])




Figure 9.2: In what order does a decoding configuration become one categorical draw?





Penalties are behavioral tools, not semantic constraints. Source code may need the same closing bracket, variable name, or indentation token many times. Aggressive repetition suppression can make valid syntax unreachable. Logit bias can ban a surface token while a synonym or alternate tokenization expresses the same content. If a property must hold, use a parser, schema, or deterministic validator at the appropriate boundary; Chapter 10 covers production structured decoding.








Workload
Useful starting policy
Measure before changing it




Extract one label from a closed set
greedy or constrained choice
label accuracy, invalid-output rate


Generate varied creative candidates
moderate sampling with adaptive truncation
human preference, diversity, repetition


Produce executable code
low-entropy candidates plus tests
test-pass rate, syntax, repeated-run reliability


Answer a consequential factual query
sampling policy is secondary to evidence and abstention
supported-claim accuracy, coverage, escalation load



The last row prevents a category error. Lower temperature can make a false answer more repeatable. It cannot supply missing evidence.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




decoding_runtime.py after processors
14 lines
Seen positive logits shrink, seen negative logits become more negative, and additive controls precede temperature.
