9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.9 Seeded is not batch invariant
Set temperature to zero, fix a seed, send the same prompt twice, and receive different text under load. Nothing requires sampling to have caused the difference. Greedy output changes if a tiny numerical difference changes the ordering of two nearly tied logits.
Floating-point addition is not associative. For finite-precision values, (a+b)+c may differ from a+(b+c). GPU kernels partition reductions across threads and tiles. A runtime may choose a different kernel or split when batch shape, sequence packing, quantization path, or hardware changes. Those legal execution paths can produce slightly different logits. Once an argmax flips, the new token changes every later autoregressive input, so a minute drift can become a different completion.
A seed controls draws from pseudorandom generators. It does not pin weights, tokenizer revision, prompt serialization, kernel selection, reduction order, request batching, model replica, quantization, or postprocessing. Use four distinct reproducibility claims:

Seed replay: the same probability vectors and random-number stream yield the same sampled indices.
Run-to-run numerical repeatability: the same request and execution environment yield the same intermediate values.
Batch invariance: one request yields the same result regardless of which other requests share the batch or how its sequence is partitioned.
Cross-environment bitwise identity: results remain identical across hardware, driver, library, model, and tokenizer revisions. This is the strongest and least commonly available claim.

The lab’s narrated failure is intentionally small. Token A’s logit contribution is reduced from [1e16, 1, -1e16, 0]. Serial summation loses the 1 and returns zero. A partitioned order cancels the large terms first and returns one. Token B stays at 0.5. The two legal orders flip the greedy winner and move the maximum probability by 0.244919. This is a pedagogical construction, not a measured estimate of drift in a real model.
# code/ch09/request_profile.py — changed region: batch_invariance_probe
parts = [1e16, 1.0, -1e16, 0.0]
serial = sum(parts)
partitioned = (parts[0] + parts[2]) + (parts[1] + parts[3])
single = softmax([serial, 0.5])
batched = softmax([partitioned, 0.5])
greedy_flip = single.index(max(single)) != batched.index(max(batched))
max_drift = max(abs(a - b) for a, b in zip(single, batched))
Figure Figure 9.3 turns “deterministic” into an auditable envelope. Pinning only the seed closes one of several doors.

flowchart LR
    I(["Canonical request bytes"]) --> W["Pinned weights + tokenizer + template"]
    W --> N["Pinned numerical path: dtype, kernels, reductions"]
    N --> B["Batch- and partition-invariant scheduling"]
    B --> R["Pinned RNG stream and tie rule"]
    R --> D["Buffered detokenization + stop policy"]
    D --> O(["Reproducible output bytes"])
    U["Unpinned replica, load, version, or hardware"] -.-> N
    U -.-> B





flowchart LR
    I(["Canonical request bytes"]) --> W["Pinned weights + tokenizer + template"]
    W --> N["Pinned numerical path: dtype, kernels, reductions"]
    N --> B["Batch- and partition-invariant scheduling"]
    B --> R["Pinned RNG stream and tie rule"]
    R --> D["Buffered detokenization + stop policy"]
    D --> O(["Reproducible output bytes"])
    U["Unpinned replica, load, version, or hardware"] -.-> N
    U -.-> B




Figure 9.3: Which surfaces must be invariant before a repeated request can promise the same output?





Test the property you need under the concurrency pattern you will operate. Save prompt bytes, all decoding controls, model and tokenizer revisions, runtime and kernel versions, device type, batch context when exposed, token IDs, and token-level scores. If the product invariant is semantic equivalence rather than byte identity, define and evaluate that separately. Chapter 22 owns the statistical comparison; this section owns the causal surfaces that must be recorded.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




request_profile.py after the batch probe
12 lines
Exact seed replay does not imply batch invariance; a changed reduction order can flip greedy output without randomness.

9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.10 Hallucination is a task-relative failure
Now consider a different failure. A user asks, “Which treaty created the Aerolith Maritime Council in 1987?” The entity is fabricated. A model produces a fluent treaty name, date, membership list, and citation. Lowering temperature makes the same invention appear more consistently. The inference loop is behaving as configured; the answer fails against the world.
Hallucination is useful only after the evaluation contract says what the output should be faithful to. The term otherwise collapses different defects with different remedies:









Failure
Reference violated
Example
Appropriate first measurement




Factual error
external world state
wrong capital or date
atomic factual correctness


Fabrication
existence in the world or source set
invented entity or paper
existence/provenance check


Unsupported claim
supplied evidence
true-sounding claim absent from sources
claim-level support


Source contradiction
supplied evidence
answer reverses a policy clause
entailment/contradiction


Stale knowledge
required snapshot time
names an earlier office holder
time-stamped correctness


Task misunderstanding
user intent or schema
answers a related question
intent/task success


Unfaithful explanation
claimed causal reasoning
rationale does not support answer
process/faithfulness study



Long-form factuality should be decomposed into atomic claims before scoring; one correct paragraph can contain one consequential false sentence. A citation is not evidence until the cited source exists, is authoritative for the claim, and entails it. Core retrieval, source selection, citation entailment, and attribution mechanics belong to Chapter 14. This chapter owns the prior decision: whether the system has enough calibrated support to answer at all.
Why does the failure occur? Next-token training rewards predictions that match text distributions. A false premise can resemble a legitimate historical question, and likely continuations include treaty vocabulary. Finite training data, ambiguous or conflicting sources, model capacity, distribution shift, and lossy parametric storage all create error. Post-training can reward helpful, decisive answers. Evaluations that give zero for abstention but some chance of credit for guessing reinforce the same behavior. The 2025 analysis Why Language Models Hallucinate formalizes this guessing incentive; TruthfulQA earlier demonstrated that imitating human text can reproduce popular false beliefs.
No decoding rule repairs a knowledge gap in general. Greedy decoding can select the most probable false continuation. Sampling may occasionally find a correct answer but also expands the tail. Self-consistency can concentrate a shared misconception. A model’s critique can share its first answer’s missing knowledge. External evidence and independent checks can change the information state; abstention can prevent an unsupported guess from being presented as fact.
This section begins the bounded Chapter 14 backfill cluster. The durable sequence is: define the reference, decompose the failure, measure a confidence signal against correctness, and enforce an action below threshold. “Hallucination rate” without those choices is not a reproducible metric.

9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.11 Confidence must forecast correctness
A confidence score q(x) is calibrated for a binary event Y when cases assigned confidence near c are correct about a fraction c of the time:

\Pr(Y=1\mid q(X)\approx c)\approx c.

Calibration is population- and event-specific. A score calibrated for exact short-answer correctness may not be calibrated for every atomic claim in a long response, for policy compliance, or after a domain shift.
Token log probability is one possible signal, not a probability that a world claim is true. Summing token log probabilities gives a sequence log probability, which usually becomes more negative with length. Averaging removes that direct length scaling but changes the quantity and can favor verbose continuations differently. A probability assigned to “True” after showing the model its own answer, often called P(True), asks a more relevant event; Kadavath et al. found useful calibration in studied settings and weaker cross-task generalization for some self-knowledge signals. Verbalized confidence can outperform raw conditional probabilities for some post-trained models, as Tian et al. reported, but it remains a learned utterance that must be calibrated on the deployment task.
Open-ended answers introduce a symmetry: “Paris” and “The capital is Paris” are different strings with the same meaning. Surface-form entropy counts them separately. Semantic entropy first groups mutually equivalent answers into meaning clusters c, sums probability mass inside each cluster, and computes

P(c\mid x)=\sum_{y\in c}p(y\mid x), \qquad
H_{sem}(x)=-\sum_c P(c\mid x)\log P(c\mid x).

In the fixture, surface probabilities [0.36, 0.24, 0.20, 0.12, 0.08] have entropy 1.489 nats. The first two are paraphrases, so merging them lowers semantic entropy to 1.085 nats. Real semantic-entropy systems sample responses and use bidirectional entailment or another semantic equivalence model. The clustering model can make mistakes, the samples are correlated, and entropy may reflect legitimate ambiguity rather than ignorance. Kuhn, Gal, and Farquhar introduced the linguistic-invariance formulation; later work demonstrated hallucination detection with semantic entropy in open-ended generation.
Calibration needs a loss, not only a diagram. For binary correctness y\in\{0,1\} and score q:

\mathrm{Brier}(q,y)=(q-y)^2,

and log loss is -y\log q-(1-y)\log(1-q). Both are proper scoring rules: in expectation, truthful probabilities minimize the score. Expected calibration error (ECE) bins predictions, compares mean confidence with bin accuracy, and weights the gaps. ECE is readable but depends on bin boundaries and sample size; report a proper score and a reliability diagram with it.
The lab fits one scalar temperature on a calibration split, then evaluates a disjoint deterministic fixture. It moves ECE from 0.060 to 0.024 and Brier score from 0.2015 to 0.1983. Temperature calibration is monotone, so it changes the meaning of a threshold but not the ranking of examples. A risk–coverage curve and its area can therefore remain unchanged even when ECE improves. Calibration measures probability fidelity; discrimination measures whether safer cases rank above riskier ones.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




selective_prediction.py after calibration
64 lines
Confidence is evaluated against correctness on held-out items; semantic grouping removes paraphrase-only uncertainty.

9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
section: 9.12 Abstention spends coverage to buy reliability
text:
9.12 Abstention spends coverage to buy reliability
An abstention policy maps uncertainty to an action. Below a threshold, the system might say it lacks support, ask a clarifying question, retrieve evidence, narrow the claim, route to a qualified reviewer, or refuse a request. The model may propose a confidence score; deterministic caller code must enforce the threshold and record the branch.
Let g_t(x)=\mathbf{1}[q(x)\geq t] mean “answer at threshold t,” and let loss \ell(x,y) be one for a wrong answer and zero for a correct one. Coverage is the fraction answered,

C(t)=\mathbb{E}[g_t(X)].

Two quantities are often both called risk:

R_{selective}(t)=\frac{\mathbb{E}[\ell g_t]}{\mathbb{E}[g_t]},
\qquad
R_{marginal}(t)=\mathbb{E}[\ell g_t].

Selective risk is the error rate among answered queries. Marginal error is the fraction of all incoming queries that receive a wrong answer; abstentions count as zero loss. They differ by coverage: R_{marginal}=C\,R_{selective}. A requirement saying “ten percent risk” is incomplete until it names one.
Sweeping t creates a risk–coverage curve. A useful confidence ranking lowers selective risk as coverage falls. Area under the risk–coverage curve (AURC) summarizes that ranking, but important operating regions and slices should still be reported. Chapter 22 owns interval estimates and release comparisons; here the curve is the mechanism that turns a threshold into an explicit trade.
Split conformal prediction adds a finite-sample coverage statement without assuming that raw model probabilities are calibrated. On a held-out calibration set, define nonconformity s_i=1-p_i(y_i) using the probability assigned to the true answer. Sort the n scores and choose the order statistic at rank \lceil(n+1)(1-\alpha)\rceil. For a new input, return all answers whose nonconformity is at most that threshold. Under exchangeability of calibration and future examples, the set contains the true answer with marginal probability at least (1-\alpha).
One safe interface answers only when this conformal set is a singleton and abstains otherwise. On the synthetic fixture with \alpha=0.10, the threshold is 0.8625. Test set coverage of the true label is 0.93; 0.66 of queries produce singleton sets; singleton error is 0.106. The guarantee concerns true-label inclusion in the set. It does not guarantee that each singleton is correct or that the conditional error among answered cases is below ten percent.
Conformal risk control (CRC) extends the idea to a bounded loss that changes monotonically with a threshold. For the lab’s marginal wrong-answer loss, a simple binary correction accepts a threshold only when

\frac{e_t+1}{n+1}\leq \alpha,

where e_t is the number of wrong answers that the threshold would release on n calibration examples. Choose the lowest accepted threshold to maximize coverage. Under the method’s exchangeability and loss assumptions, this controls expected marginal loss up to the conformal correction. With \alpha=0.10, the fixture selects confidence 0.85, answers 40 percent of queries, and incurs marginal error 0.07. Yet its selective risk is 0.175. The build is designed to make that distinction impossible to miss.
# code/ch09/selective_prediction.py — changed region: crc_threshold
def crc_threshold(items, alpha):
    for threshold in sorted({item["confidence"] for item in items}):
        errors = sum(item["confidence"] >= threshold and not item["correct"]
                     for item in items)
        if (errors + 1) / (len(items) + 1) <= alpha:
            return threshold
    return math.inf
A conformal guarantee is not permanent certification. Exchangeability can fail when users, prompts, languages, evidence availability, or models change. Calibration data can be contaminated or too small for critical slices. A learned confidence model can be attacked. Monitor coverage and outcomes, freeze the policy version used for each decision, and fall back to a conservative route when drift invalidates the evidence. For consequential applications, also price the cost of abstention: delayed care, inaccessible service, and overloaded human queues are real harms.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




selective_prediction.py after selective output
58 lines
Split conformal controls set coverage, while CRC targets a declared marginal loss; neither is mislabeled as per-answer certainty.

9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
section: 9.13 Streaming tokens are not text chunks
text:
9.13 Streaming tokens are not text chunks
An inference server emits token IDs. A user interface displays Unicode text. Those boundaries need not align. UTF-8 represents one Unicode scalar value with one to four bytes, and byte-level tokenizers can expose a piece that ends inside a multibyte character. Decoding each event independently may emit replacement characters or raise an error. A token can also decode to an empty string until combined with neighbors.
Stop sequences create the same boundary problem at a different level. If the requested stop is STOP, one chunk may end in ST and the next begin with OP. Checking each chunk separately leaks the stop marker and may stream text that should have remained hidden. Buffer at least the longest possible stop-prefix suffix, run an incremental decoder, and emit only text that can no longer participate in a stop.
# code/ch09/decoding_runtime.py — changed region: stream_until
decoder = codecs.getincrementaldecoder("utf-8")()
pending, emitted = "", []
for chunk in chunks:
    pending += decoder.decode(chunk)
    if stop in pending:
        emitted.append(pending.split(stop, 1)[0])
        return "".join(emitted)
    keep = max(0, len(stop) - 1)
    if len(pending) > keep:
        emitted.append(pending[:-keep] if keep else pending)
        pending = pending[-keep:] if keep else ""
The lab splits the UTF-8 bytes for 🙂 across three events and splits STOP across two. The emitted text is exactly A🙂; neither a replacement character nor stop text leaks. A production implementation must additionally handle several stop strings, normalization policy, end-of-sequence tokens, cancellation, maximum-token termination, client disconnects, and an explicit finish reason. Never infer “the model chose to finish” when the server actually hit a length limit.
This edge is where byte-level reproducibility meets user-visible behavior. Comparing only token IDs misses detokenizer and stop-policy changes. Comparing only final text misses token-score drift and hidden stop leakage. Preserve both representations when debugging.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




Integrated inference lab complete
Four modules; largest 112 lines
A partial Unicode scalar and a cross-chunk stop are buffered into exactly one safe user-visible string.

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.14 Landscape 2026 (dated)

Verified on 2026-07-19. Current Hugging Face generation documentation exposes top-p, min-p, typical sampling, penalties, stopping criteria, and many additional processors. Names, defaults, and composition order have changed across releases; pin and inspect the effective GenerationConfig instead of copying a default into the durable design.
The PyTorch reproducibility note states that complete reproducibility is not guaranteed across releases or platforms and documents deterministic-algorithm controls with possible performance cost. Thinking Machines Lab’s 2025 batch-invariance analysis identified batch-dependent reduction strategies as a request-level source of inference variation. As of this verification date, vLLM’s batch-invariance mode is documented as beta, has hardware/model coverage limits, and can trade performance for invariant scheduling; its separate reproducibility page limits guarantees to the same hardware and vLLM version.
Uncertainty research continues beyond scalar log probabilities. 2026 work includes adaptive Bayesian estimation of semantic entropy and geometry-calibrated conformal abstention. These are research directions, not default production guarantees; their estimators, tasks, and assumptions require independent validation.
Verify live: sampler parameter names and defaults; processor order; log-probability availability; batch-invariance feature status and supported kernels/models; deterministic-mode performance; model/tokenizer revisions; and the assumptions and benchmark status of named uncertainty methods.

text:
9.15 Build
Run the deterministic build from the newbook directory:
python code/ch09/inference_lab.py
python code/ch09/render_metrics.py
python -m unittest discover -s code/ch09 -p "test_*.py" -v
The first command prints the complete measurement record. The second regenerates inference_metrics.svg from that same record. The six tests exercise temperature and truncation invariants, sign-aware processors, KV accounting, roofline placement, seed replay versus batch invariance, calibration, both conformal paths, semantic entropy, streaming, and the optional adapter’s import boundary.
At the pinned fixture, request anatomy is:

The decode probe reproduces 20 seeded draws exactly, then flips one greedy token when its reduction order changes; maximum probability drift is 0.244919. Calibration temperature 1.49 lowers ECE from 0.060 to 0.024 and Brier score from 0.2015 to 0.1983. Semantic grouping lowers entropy from 1.489 to 1.085 nats. Split conformal reaches 0.93 set coverage and answers with a singleton on 0.66 of items. CRC answers 0.40 of items at marginal error 0.07, while selective risk remains 0.175.
The three panels in Figure 9.4 connect those results. Panel A explains phase placement, Panel B distinguishes calibration from accuracy, and Panel C places marginal and conditional error on the same coverage axis.






Figure 9.4: How do request phase, calibration, and abstention describe different inference risks? The illustrative roofline places decode below the ridge and prefill above it; temperature calibration moves fixture confidence toward observed accuracy; and the CRC point reaches marginal error 0.07 at coverage 0.40 while selective risk is 0.175. All values are deterministic analytical or synthetic fixtures, not deployed-model measurements.



To replace only the model seam, install compatible torch and transformers packages and run:
python code/ch09/hf_inference_adapter.py MODEL_ID "The prompt to profile" --max-new-tokens 16 --batch-size 8
Record the exact model revision, tokenizer, dtype, device, library versions, prompt bytes, warm-up policy, and whether synchronization is included in each timer. The adapter uses a hand-rolled cached greedy loop to expose TTFT, mean TPOT, token IDs, text, and measured cache storage. Its batch probe compares a request alone with an identical request in a larger batch. A zero flip on one prompt is not proof of batch invariance; sweep prompts, near ties, batch shapes, and load.






WarningHonesty boundary



The default timing values are analytical outputs of a declared seven-billion-parameter configuration and illustrative hardware ceilings. The QA labels, confidence distribution, batch-order flip, and semantic clusters are synthetic. They teach equations and failure distinctions; they do not estimate any model, GPU, domain, or production error rate. The optional adapter supplies measurements, not a benchmark protocol. Chapter 10 owns serving experiments, and Chapter 22 owns statistical evaluation.

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.16 What endures, what changes
What endures. Prefill and decode create different latency terms. KV storage depends on KV heads and grows linearly with cached tokens. Roofline placement follows arithmetic intensity. Temperature rescales probability gaps without reordering logits; truncation changes support; processor order changes the final policy. A seed controls randomness, not every numerical or scheduling path. Hallucination is defined against a reference and task. Token likelihood, semantic uncertainty, calibration, discrimination, set coverage, marginal error, and selective risk are different quantities. Abstention is a caller-enforced action with an explicit coverage cost. Token events require buffered detokenization and stop handling.
What changes. Model and tokenizer revisions, cache layouts, accelerator ceilings, kernel selection, deterministic-mode support, sampler names/defaults, processor order, log-probability APIs, uncertainty estimators, benchmark results, and provider guarantees all move. Keep those facts in adapters, measurement metadata, and the dated landscape. Removing the dated block leaves the request, decoding, calibration, and abstention mechanisms intact.
Chapter 10 now takes the same prefill/decode anatomy into a multi-request serving system, where batching, caching, scheduling, and capacity turn per-request mechanisms into production trade-offs.

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.17 Exercises

Temperature and adaptive support. Sweep top-p and min-p over temperatures 0.7, 1.0, and 1.5 on at least 100 fixture draws per setting. Predict support and entropy first. Add repetition and distinct-n measurements, then state why a winner on this vector is not a universal default.
Break a necessary repetition. Add no_repeat_ngram to the shared processor pipeline. Construct a tiny code or JSON continuation in which a repeated bracket or key token is necessary. Demonstrate the failure at penalty 2.0 and name the deterministic constraint that should own syntax instead.
Compare beam search without moving its boundary. Implement width-B beam search with length normalization against the same logits interface. On one closed task and one open-ended prompt, compare complete-sequence likelihood, diversity, and task quality with sampling. Explain why verifier-driven search remains Chapter 8’s mechanism.
Probe batch dependence. Extend the optional adapter over batch sizes 1, 2, 8, and 32 and at least 100 prompts. Record max log-probability drift and greedy flip rate with full environment metadata. Repeat with a documented deterministic or batch-invariant mode and report its performance cost.
Separate calibration from ranking. Replace scalar temperature scaling with another held-out calibrator. Show ECE and Brier score before and after, then verify whether AURC changes. Explain why a monotone calibrator can improve threshold meaning without changing which examples are answered first.
Stress the calibration split. Repeat split conformal and CRC with calibration sizes 50, 200, and 1,000 over many random splits. Plot set coverage, marginal error, selective risk, and participation. Identify which statement each method guarantees and what exchangeability violation would invalidate it.
Design a consequential abstention contract. A medical-information endpoint has a stated wrong-answer budget. Define the event, whether the budget is marginal or conditional, the confidence signal, calibration population, conformal target, decoding policy, clarification/retrieval/escalation branches, drift trigger, and user-visible message. Defend the residual harm from both wrong answers and abstentions.

9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]

title: 9  Inference Anatomy and Model Behavior: Sampling, Hallucination, and Abstention [F]
text:
9.18 Notes and sources
The decoding foundation is Holtzman et al., The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration, which motivates nucleus sampling and analyzes high-likelihood degeneration. Locally Typical Sampling defines information-content typicality. Nguyen et al., Turning Up the Heat, gives the min-p rule and its evaluated high-temperature regime. Current parameter surfaces are documented in the official Hugging Face generation reference.
The performance model follows Williams, Waterman, and Patterson, Roofline: An Insightful Visual Performance Model. Reproducibility claims are grounded in the official PyTorch reproducibility note, the primary batch-invariance analysis, and current vLLM reproducibility and batch-invariance documentation. UTF-8’s variable-width encoding is specified by the Unicode Standard.
Hallucination and factuality sources include TruthfulQA, Why Language Models Hallucinate, FActScore for atomic long-form factual precision, and ALCE for citation-grounded generation. These works study different reference contracts; their scores should not be collapsed into one universal hallucination number.
Confidence mechanisms follow Kadavath et al., Language Models (Mostly) Know What They Know; Tian et al., Just Ask for Calibration; and Kuhn, Gal, and Farquhar, Semantic Uncertainty. Selective risk follows Geifman and El-Yaniv, Selective Classification for Deep Neural Networks. Finite-sample mechanisms follow A Gentle Introduction to Conformal Prediction, Conformal Risk Control, and Quach et al., Conformal Language Modeling. The guarantee statements in this chapter intentionally distinguish prediction-set coverage, marginal loss control, and conditional selective risk.

Not required: access to an accelerator, Kubernetes, an inference engine, a quantized checkpoint, or prior experience operating a model server. The default build is a deterministic CPU simulation; an optional adapter measures a real OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
section: 10.3 Goodput: the objective a serving system optimizes
text:
10.3 Goodput: the objective a serving system optimizes
A model endpoint reports record token throughput after a deployment change. At the same time, interactive users see more long pauses before the first token, and streaming occasionally freezes behind large prompts. Both observations can be true. The server completed more work per second by accepting larger batches, but too many individual requests missed the latency contract. Throughput rewarded work that users considered late.
This chapter builds the ability to explain and control that failure. The objective is goodput: the rate of requests that complete while satisfying every declared service-level objective (SLO). Let a measurement window have duration W seconds and contain requests indexed by i. Let T_i^{first} be request i’s time to first token (TTFT), T_i^{token} its time per output token (TPOT), and let S^{first} and S^{token} be their respective limits. A two-SLO goodput is

G(S^{first},S^{token})=
\frac{1}{W}\sum_i
\mathbf{1}\!\left[T_i^{first}\le S^{first}
\;\land\;
T_i^{token}\le S^{token}\right]
\quad\text{requests/second}.

The indicator contributes one only when both conditions hold. A production contract might add availability, output validity, or task quality. Reporting each pass rate separately can hide that different requests fail each objective. The window and workload distribution are part of the definition.
TTFT and TPOT retain the boundaries from Chapter 9. End-to-end TTFT includes admission, queueing, tokenization, prefill, the first decode step, detokenization, and network delivery unless a narrower boundary is stated. TPOT describes gaps after the first token; tail inter-token latency is often more revealing than an average. The serving layer adds queueing and interference, so a fast isolated forward pass does not imply a fast service.
Goodput is a function of offered load. Batching initially improves useful work per invocation. Past a knee, queues grow, KV memory fills, preemption rises, and the qualified fraction collapses. Capacity is therefore a goodput-versus-arrival-rate curve for a pinned workload, accompanied by token lengths, concurrency, cancellations, cache pattern, and decoding policy.
Figure Figure 10.1 locates each decision. Components may share a process, but authentication, routing, scheduling, execution, KV ownership, streaming, and measurement remain distinct responsibilities.

flowchart LR
    U(["Caller request"]) -->|"identity, prompt, SLO"| G["Gateway: authenticate and admit"]
    G -->|"model and tenant route"| R["KV- and load-aware router"]
    R -->|"queued request"| S["Iteration scheduler"]
    S -->|"token budget and block table"| E(["Inference engine and kernels"])
    E <-->|"allocate, read, append"| K[("Paged KV pool")]
    E -->|"token IDs and timing"| D["Safe detokenizer and streamer"]
    D -->|"text events"| U
    S -.->|"queue, preemption, SLO counters"| M[["Measurements"]]
    K -.->|"hits, evictions, bytes"| M
    D -.->|"TTFT and token gaps"| M





flowchart LR
    U(["Caller request"]) -->|"identity, prompt, SLO"| G["Gateway: authenticate and admit"]
    G -->|"model and tenant route"| R["KV- and load-aware router"]
    R -->|"queued request"| S["Iteration scheduler"]
    S -->|"token budget and block table"| E(["Inference engine and kernels"])
    E <-->|"allocate, read, append"| K[("Paged KV pool")]
    E -->|"token IDs and timing"| D["Safe detokenizer and streamer"]
    D -->|"text events"| U
    S -.->|"queue, preemption, SLO counters"| M[["Measurements"]]
    K -.->|"hits, evictions, bytes"| M
    D -.->|"TTFT and token gaps"| M




Figure 10.1: Which serving component can change a request’s latency or state?





Locate every optimization on this path and name its invariant. Batching can damage TTFT, cache affinity can overload a replica, and speculation can consume contested compute. Chapter 26 owns monetary cost and fleet provisioning; this chapter supplies their goodput mechanics.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.4 What you will build






TipThe serving decision lab



You will run one integrated, engine-free build rooted at code/ch10/serving_lab.py. Its deterministic fixture schedules one burst of variable-length requests with fixed and continuous batching, constructs tenant-scoped prefix-cache keys, calculates cache break-even, verifies speculative accept/reject sampling, measures grammar-mask distortion, quantizes an outlier-heavy vector at three block sizes, evaluates prefill/decode disaggregation, and allocates a synthetic reasoning-token budget.
The build emits makespan, slot utilization, SLO-qualified goodput, cache blocks and tail waste, reuse break-even, speculative acceptance and target-distribution error, removed probability mass, quantization RMSE and effective bits per value, transfer-versus-interference decisions, and a reasoning quality/cost curve. render_metrics.py generates the chapter’s numeric SVG from the same record. Success means all six mechanism tests pass and every displayed number is reproducible.
openai_compatible_adapter.py is an optional network seam. It measures first-content latency, stream-event gaps, total time, and endpoint-reported output tokens without becoming a second build. The deterministic path never imports it for network access.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.5 Continuous batching and paged KV
A fixed batch of four decode requests has remaining lengths 2, 12, 4, and 10 tokens. After the two-token request finishes, its slot sits unused until the twelve-token request finishes. If every batch member reserves its maximum sequence-sized KV region, short requests also leave holes in device memory. Compute capacity and memory capacity are stranded for different versions of the same reason: variable-length requests are treated as fixed rectangles.
For a static batch of size B with output lengths L_1,\ldots,L_B, a simple slot utilization is

U_{static}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{B}L_i}{B\max_i L_i}.

The numerator is useful request-steps. The denominator is the slots available while the longest member runs. This equation ignores kernel efficiency and prefill, but it exposes length skew. Grouping similar lengths improves the ratio only when arrival rate and waiting-time budgets permit delaying requests to form those groups.
Continuous batching, also called iteration-level or in-flight batching, makes admission a per-iteration decision. After each decode step, the scheduler removes completed or cancelled requests and admits waiting work into the freed capacity. Orca established iteration-level scheduling with selective batching; its durable contribution is the scheduling boundary, not one queue policy. A production scheduler usually enforces a token budget because one long prefill and many one-token decode steps have very different work despite being the same number of requests.
The scheduler also needs a KV admission bound, fairness policy, cancellation path, and response when unknown output outgrows its reservation. Preemption may swap KV, discard it for recomputation, or reject work. Count preemptions, recomputed tokens, swapped bytes, queue age, and deadline misses; repeatedly evicting long requests can produce high utilization and poor goodput.
Long prefills create another head-of-line problem. Chunked prefill divides a prompt into bounded chunks interleaved with decode. Smaller chunks protect TPOT but add overhead and may delay that prompt’s TTFT. Chunk size is an SLO dial, not a copied constant.
Memory becomes schedulable when the KV cache is paged. A request sees logical KV blocks in token order; a block table maps them to noncontiguous physical frames. Frames are allocated only as tokens arrive. External fragmentation from requiring one contiguous maximum-length region disappears, while internal waste is bounded by less than one block per live sequence for a conventional tail block. The attention kernel follows the block table when reading keys and values. This is device-memory management inspired by virtual memory; it does not imply that an operating system is faulting model state to disk.
Paged blocks support copy-on-write: branches share immutable full prefix blocks but receive private frames when appending to a partial tail. Figure Figure 10.2 separates logical order from physical placement.

flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Sequence A logical blocks"]
        A0["prefix 0"] --> A1["prefix 1"] --> A2["private tail A"]
    end
    subgraph B["Sequence B logical blocks"]
        B0["prefix 0"] --> B1["prefix 1"] --> B2["private tail B"]
    end
    subgraph P["Physical KV frames"]
        F7[("frame 7: prefix 0")]
        F2[("frame 2: tail B")]
        F9[("frame 9: prefix 1")]
        F4[("frame 4: tail A")]
    end
    A0 -->|"block-table entry"| F7
    B0 -->|"shared read-only entry"| F7
    A1 -->|"block-table entry"| F9
    B1 -->|"shared read-only entry"| F9
    A2 -->|"copy on append"| F4
    B2 -->|"copy on append"| F2





flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Sequence A logical blocks"]
        A0["prefix 0"] --> A1["prefix 1"] --> A2["private tail A"]
    end
    subgraph B["Sequence B logical blocks"]
        B0["prefix 0"] --> B1["prefix 1"] --> B2["private tail B"]
    end
    subgraph P["Physical KV frames"]
        F7[("frame 7: prefix 0")]
        F2[("frame 2: tail B")]
        F9[("frame 9: prefix 1")]
        F4[("frame 4: tail A")]
    end
    A0 -->|"block-table entry"| F7
    B0 -->|"shared read-only entry"| F7
    A1 -->|"block-table entry"| F9
    B1 -->|"shared read-only entry"| F9
    A2 -->|"copy on append"| F4
    B2 -->|"copy on append"| F2




Figure 10.2: How can two sequences share an immutable prefix without requiring contiguous KV memory?





Block size trades metadata and kernel locality against tail waste and reuse granularity. Small blocks reduce waste but enlarge tables; large blocks do the reverse. Select with goodput over realistic length distributions, not memory utilization alone.
# code/ch10/serving_lab.py — changed regions: static_schedule, continuous_schedule
static = static_schedule([2, 12, 4, 10, 3, 9, 5, 8, 6, 7, 2, 11], 4)
continuous = continuous_schedule([2, 12, 4, 10, 3, 9, 5, 8, 6, 7, 2, 11], 4)

assert continuous["utilization"] > static["utilization"]
assert continuous["makespan_steps"] < static["makespan_steps"]

Invariant now verified

Freed slots are refilled at iteration boundaries; full prefix blocks may be shared, but each branch owns its partial tail.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.6 Prefix caching and cache economics
An agent sends the same system instructions, tool schemas, and policy text on every turn. The service recomputes their keys and values each time because one request changed a timestamp near the front of the prompt. A different deployment gets frequent cache hits, but accidentally lets two tenants share a prefix entry. Prefix caching is valuable only when identity, placement, and economics are all correct.
A prefix cache retains KV blocks after their original request no longer needs them, allowing a later request with the exact same token prefix and model state to skip the corresponding prefill. “Exact” is mechanical. Semantically equivalent text with different token IDs is a miss. Equal token IDs computed under different model weights, tokenizer behavior, position treatment, prompt template, multimodal input, or adapter are not interchangeable.
The cache key must therefore cover every input that can change KV values. The lab uses

K=(tenant,model,tokenizer,template,adapter,H(token\ IDs)).

The tenant component is policy, not mathematics. Explicit cross-tenant sharing of public immutable prefixes needs its own trust domain and auditable opt-in. Default isolation prevents a hit from becoming a presence oracle or state leak.
Caching has a break-even point. Let C be the cost to recompute a prefix, C_w the cost of the first computation plus writing the cache entry, C_r the cost of one later read, and r the number of future reuses. Without caching, total cost is (1+r)C. With caching, it is C_w+rC_r. When C_r<C, caching wins if

r>\frac{C_w-C}{C-C_r}.

“Cost” may be milliseconds on the critical path, accelerator-seconds, energy, or an internal price, but every term must use the same unit. If a remote read is slower than recomputation, no reuse count repairs the critical-path latency unless the read can be overlapped or frees a more constrained resource. The fixture assigns C=1, C_w=1.25, and C_r=0.10 cost units, producing a threshold of 0.278 future reuses. Because reuse arrives in whole events, the first reuse pays back the write in that fixture.
For eviction, approximate value density as expected reuse probability times recomputation saved, divided by retained bytes. Least-recently-used is cheap when recency predicts reuse but fails on periodic jobs, scans, and large popular prefixes. Size- and recomputation-aware policies protect denser entries; pinned bytes need quotas.
A radix tree exposes shared paths; parent-linked block hashes expose equivalent reuse. Neither places work across replicas. A KV-aware router balances reusable length against queue depth, free frames, and deadline. Always choosing the largest hit can create a hot replica, so route on predicted completion or SLO risk.
Hierarchical KV caches extend the working set to host memory, peers, storage, or a shared service, adding transfer and coordination. Track usable-hit bytes, read latency, transferred bytes, recomputed tokens, and TTFT; lookup hit rate alone can mislead. Compressed or low-precision KV also enters Section Section 10.8’s validation ladder.
Prompt ordering affects reuse, but Chapter 13 owns that caller-side layout and compaction. The server hashes what it receives and never assumes semantic equivalence.
# code/ch10/serving_lab.py — changed regions: make_cache_key, cache_breakeven
key_a = make_cache_key("tenant-a", list(range(45)))
key_b = make_cache_key("tenant-b", list(range(45)))

assert key_a != key_b
assert abs(cache_breakeven(1.0, 1.25, 0.10) - 5 / 18) < 1e-12









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




serving_lab.py after prefix economics
40 lines
Reuse requires complete cache identity, and a cache write is admitted only against comparable compute, read, and expected-reuse costs.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
section: 10.7 Speculation and constraints trade compute for serial steps
text:
10.7 Speculation and constraints trade compute for serial steps
Autoregressive decode waits for one target-model decision before constructing the next input. Two serving techniques reduce the cost of that serial boundary in different ways. Speculative decoding guesses several future tokens and verifies them together. Constrained decoding removes illegal next tokens before sampling. Both can improve a target metric, and both can waste work when their assumptions are wrong.
In exact speculative sampling, a fast draft distribution q proposes a token x, and the target distribution p accepts it with probability

a(x)=\min\!\left(1,\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\right).

On rejection, sample from normalized residual (p-q)_+, where (v)_+=\max(v,0). This correction emits distribution p. The expected one-token acceptance is

\sum_x q(x)a(x)=\sum_x\min(p(x),q(x))=1-\operatorname{TV}(p,q),

where \operatorname{TV} is total-variation distance. For a block, the target scores several proposals in parallel, accepts the valid prefix, then corrects if needed. Exactness covers the target distribution under compatible processors, not identical random traces or latency.
Speedup is accepted tokens per verification divided by all added work. Draft compute, KV, shape-dependent verification, and rejected proposals consume resources. At saturation, an isolated latency win can reduce fleet goodput. Measure proposal acceptance, draft/verification time, memory, and goodput. Draft models, prediction heads, prompt lookup, and n-grams share this accounting boundary.
Constrained decoding starts from a language recognizer rather than a predictor. A finite-state machine (FSM) represents regular constraints such as fixed choices and many lexical patterns. A context-free grammar (CFG) adds a stack, allowing nested structures such as balanced arrays and objects. At each step, the runtime advances a parser state, determines which token byte strings can continue a valid parse, masks every other token, renormalizes, and samples. Tokenization makes this harder than checking characters after generation: one token can contain several characters, split a delimiter, or cross a grammar transition.
Masks can be compiled and cached by parser state; deterministic literals may jump forward without a model call. An empty allowed set indicates an unsatisfiable schema or implementation error. Dropping the constraint changes the contract.
A grammar guarantees syntax, not truth, authorization, or business validity. Chapters 17 and 24 validate values and authorize effects. If illegal tokens hold mass m, masking removes m and renormalizes; the lab removes 0.60. This is exact for the constrained distribution, not unconstrained generation. Because format restrictions can impair reasoning, evaluate designs that separate deliberation from a small validated final object.
# code/ch10/serving_lab.py — changed regions: speculative_draw, constrained_distribution
target, draft = [0.52, 0.28, 0.15, 0.05], [0.42, 0.32, 0.18, 0.08]
token, accepted = speculative_draw(target, draft, random.Random(731))
masked = constrained_distribution([0.60, 0.25, 0.15], {1, 2})

assert abs(sum(masked["distribution"]) - 1) < 1e-12









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




serving_lab.py after fast decoding
62 lines
Speculative rejection restores the target distribution; grammar masking returns a normalized nonempty constrained distribution and reports removed mass.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.8 Quantization is a validation problem
A four-bit checkpoint occupies less storage than its sixteen-bit parent, yet serves fewer requests per second. Its engine dequantizes weights through an unfused fallback kernel, while an important tool-selection task regresses even though perplexity barely moves. “Four bit” described storage, not the complete numerical and execution contract.
Quantization approximates high-precision values with a smaller representation. Describe a recipe along several independent axes:

what is quantized: weights, activations, KV cache, or communication buffers;
numerical family and width: integer, floating point, or another codebook;
scale granularity: tensor, channel, group, row, or block;
when scales are selected: offline/static or per-input/dynamic;
accumulator and output precision; and
kernel, hardware, and model-architecture support.

W4A16 means four-bit weights and sixteen-bit activations; W4A4 compresses both. Weight-only recipes target decode weight bandwidth. Activation quantization enables lower-precision matrix multiplication but faces input-dependent outliers. KV error may accumulate with context length.
For symmetric integer quantization of a block x, choose scale s>0 and signed integer bound Q. The lab uses

q_i=\operatorname{clip}\!\left(\operatorname{round}(x_i/s),-Q,Q\right),
\qquad \hat{x}_i=sq_i.

With b bits, a common symmetric bound is Q=2^{b-1}-1. Smaller blocks give each local range its own scale and reduce damage from distant outliers. They also store more scale metadata. If each block contains g values, value width is b bits, and each scale uses b_s bits, a basic storage estimate is

b_{effective}=b+\frac{b_s}{g}

bits per value, before alignment, zero points, global scales, and container overhead. “Four bit” can therefore mean 4.06, 4.25, 5.0, or another effective width depending on block size and metadata.
Outliers motivate several recipes. Weight-only methods use approximate second-order information or protect activation-salient channels. Activation-aware methods can migrate scale difficulty: for invertible diagonal D,

XW=(XD^{-1})(DW).

Rescaling eases activation range while moving it into offline-calibrated weights. Other recipes isolate, decompose, or rotate outliers. The full-precision algebra may be exact; quantization creates the approximation. Calibration must match deployed data and shapes.
Validate from representation to service: loading and scale layout; optimized kernels for real shapes and feature combinations; layer error and perplexity diagnostics; task, tool, structured-output, long-context, safety, and calibration metrics; then cold/warm latency, memory, energy, and goodput. Predeclare detectable quality regressions.
The lab places two outliers in 256 synthetic values and applies symmetric four-bit quantization. Blocks of 16, 64, and 256 yield RMSE 0.455, 0.978, and 1.415 fixture units, while their simple effective widths are 5.00, 4.25, and 4.06 bits per value. The smaller block buys fidelity with metadata. These values teach the trade-off; they do not rank production formats.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




serving_lab.py after block quantization
26 lines
A finer scale block reduces this outlier fixture’s RMSE while increasing scale metadata per value.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.9 Disaggregate when interference exceeds transfer
A decode stream produces a token every few milliseconds until a long prompt enters the same worker. The prompt’s prefill turns one scheduler iteration into a large compute job, and every active stream stalls. Chunking reduces the stall but cannot give prefill and decode different hardware shapes or independent scaling. Separating the phases removes interference, then adds a KV transfer to the critical path.
In prefill/decode disaggregation, a prefill worker processes the prompt and materializes its KV state. That state moves to a decode worker, which generates output and streams it to the caller. Prefill is commonly more compute-oriented; decode is commonly more memory-bandwidth- and KV-capacity-oriented. Separate pools can use different parallel widths, batch policies, and replica counts, and can scale according to input-token and output-token demand.
The split is not automatically faster. Let \Delta I be latency or capacity recovered by removing prefill/decode interference and \Delta S the benefit of phase-specialized hardware or batching. Let T_{KV} be nonoverlapped KV-transfer time, T_R routing and coordination, T_Q extra queue imbalance, and T_F expected failure/retry cost. A useful admission inequality is

\Delta I+\Delta S>T_{KV}+T_R+T_Q+T_F.

If M_{KV} bytes must cross a path with effective bandwidth B_{eff} bytes per second, then T_{KV}\ge M_{KV}/B_{eff} before protocol overhead. Long prompts create more interference to remove but also more KV state to transfer. Layer-wise streaming can overlap transfer with prefill; shared memory or a remote cache may avoid a full copy; congestion can make effective bandwidth much lower than a link’s headline. Measure the critical path rather than substituting a fabric specification.
Figure Figure 10.3 makes ownership transitions explicit. The router needs cancellation semantics spanning both pools. A decode failure after successful prefill should not automatically recompute the prompt if a valid, authorized KV object remains available. Conversely, a stale or partial KV object must never be treated as complete.

sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Caller
    participant R as SLO-aware router
    participant P as Prefill pool
    participant K as KV transport or shared tier
    participant D as Decode pool
    C->>R: prompt, identity, deadline
    R->>P: admit prefill
    P->>P: chunked or full prefill
    P->>K: publish complete KV blocks + identity
    K->>D: transfer or expose authorized KV
    D-->>R: ready only after KV validation
    D-->>C: first and subsequent tokens
    alt cancellation or deadline expiry
        R-->>P: cancel unfinished prefill
        R-->>D: cancel decode and release frames
        R-->>K: expire request-scoped handle
    end





sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Caller
    participant R as SLO-aware router
    participant P as Prefill pool
    participant K as KV transport or shared tier
    participant D as Decode pool
    C->>R: prompt, identity, deadline
    R->>P: admit prefill
    P->>P: chunked or full prefill
    P->>K: publish complete KV blocks + identity
    K->>D: transfer or expose authorized KV
    D-->>R: ready only after KV validation
    D-->>C: first and subsequent tokens
    alt cancellation or deadline expiry
        R-->>P: cancel unfinished prefill
        R-->>D: cancel decode and release frames
        R-->>K: expire request-scoped handle
    end




Figure 10.3: Where does a disaggregated request pay coordination and KV-transfer cost?





Chunked prefill and disaggregation are complementary controls. An aggregated worker can chunk to cap decode stalls. A prefill pool can chunk for fairness among long prompts and to overlap KV transfer. Tiny chunks increase launch and scheduling overhead; huge chunks recreate head-of-line blocking. Tune against TTFT and TPOT together.
Capacity planning often describes xP yD: x prefill replicas feeding y decode replicas. The ratio depends on input/output lengths, prefix-hit rates, reasoning budgets, parallel topology, and SLOs. Static ratios fail when traffic composition changes, so a controller may reassign roles or route selected short prompts through aggregated workers. The lab’s simple inequality rejects disaggregation for a 0.25 GiB KV fixture that saves 3 ms but pays 4.05 ms, and accepts it for a 4 GiB fixture that saves 35 ms while paying 22.8 ms. Both results depend on declared synthetic bandwidth and coordination costs.

New code
Invariant now verified




serving_lab.py after phase splitting
13 lines
A request is disaggregated only when measured interference saved exceeds KV transfer and coordination in the same latency unit.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
section: 10.10 Parallelism and multi-tenancy share more than weights
text:
10.10 Parallelism and multi-tenancy share more than weights
When one model does not fit or one replica cannot meet demand, “use more GPUs” leaves the important choice unspecified. Parallel strategies move different data at different frequencies. Serving magnifies their communication costs because decode repeatedly runs small, latency-sensitive steps rather than large training batches.









Strategy
What is divided
Serving advantage
Dominant serving risk




Data parallel replicas
requests, with complete model copies
independent scaling and failure domains
load and prefix-cache imbalance


Tensor parallelism (TP)
tensors within each layer
makes each layer fit; combines device bandwidth
collective communication on every layer and decode step


Pipeline parallelism (PP)
consecutive layer groups
crosses nodes with fewer, larger transfers than TP
pipeline bubbles, stage imbalance, and per-request latency


Expert parallelism (EP)
mixture-of-experts expert weights
distributes a sparse model’s expert memory and work
token all-to-all, hot experts, and synchronization at imbalance



TP shards matrix work and reduces partial results with collectives, favoring fast scale-up links; communication eventually dominates small decode steps. PP sends activations between layer stages but irregular microbatches leave bubbles. EP routes tokens to experts; sparse activation lowers arithmetic, not expert storage or all-to-all cost, and hot experts set batch time.
Serving carries KV state and deadlines instead of optimizer state. Parallel width changes KV placement, cache reuse, verification shapes, and failure radius. Benchmark the actual topology and collectives; an oversubscribed boundary can erase arithmetic gains.
Multi-LoRA shares expensive xW while applying adapter-specific low-rank xAB terms on the fly, avoiding a merged W per tenant. Adapters can page from host, and grouped kernels batch heterogeneous terms, as Punica and S-LoRA show. Account for rank, load latency, residency, and TP compatibility.
Sharing a base model, process, accelerator, or cache expands the security boundary. A minimum serving contract includes:

cache keys containing tenant policy and every model/tokenizer/template/adapter revision;
authenticated adapter identity, integrity verification, and an authorization check before load;
quotas for KV bytes, prefix entries, active sequences, speculative work, and reasoning tokens;
explicit release and zeroization behavior for host buffers and accelerator allocations;
isolation tests for local/scratch memory, not only global device memory;
constant-work or coarse-bucket mitigations where cache hits, adapter loads, or queue timing reveal sensitive presence; and
attestation when the threat model requires proof of the loaded software/firmware measurement.

Attestation establishes a measured starting stack, not correct authorization or absence of memory and timing leaks. Zeroization must cover cancellation, crashes, and eviction. Stronger tenants may require separate devices, nodes, encryption domains, or deployments.
LeftoverLocals recovered data across GPU process boundaries on affected implementations. A container boundary is not evidence that device-local memory is scrubbed. Chapter 24 owns the full threat model; serving owns these shared-state facts.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
section: 10.11 Choose an engine by its feature lattice
text:
10.11 Choose an engine by its feature lattice
An engine wins a public throughput benchmark, so a team selects it for a service that needs one quantized mixture-of-experts model, two LoRA adapters, grammar constraints, prefix caching, tensor parallelism, and speculative decoding at the same time. Each feature appears in documentation. Their combination falls back to an unfused path or is unsupported. Feature checklists are one-dimensional; deployments occupy intersections.
A feature lattice is the set of combinations that work for a pinned model, hardware topology, engine revision, and request shape. Engine selection begins with a workload card:







Workload dimension
Values to pin before comparison




Model
architecture, exact revision, tokenizer/template, dense or MoE, modalities


Numbers
weight, activation, accumulator, and KV formats; quantization recipe


Requests
input/output length distributions, concurrency, arrival process, priorities


Semantics
sampler/processors, grammar/schema, stop handling, determinism requirement


State
prefix reuse, session affinity, remote KV, adapters, maximum live KV


Scale
accelerator type, memory, interconnect, TP/PP/EP/replica layout, disaggregation


Operations
metrics, traces, cancellation, draining, rollout, failure recovery, support horizon



The PagedAttention work associated with vLLM supplies a useful reference architecture: iteration scheduling plus block-managed KV allows high-concurrency autoregressive serving without contiguous per-sequence reservations. That mechanism is durable. An engine comparison still needs to verify model-specific attention kernels, graph capture, compilation, quantization layouts, distributed collectives, grammar integration, speculative strategy, and adapter kernel support.
Optimized engines specialize by shape. Kernel fusion avoids round trips to device memory. CUDA graphs or analogous capture mechanisms reduce launch overhead for supported static patterns. Compilers specialize operations and layouts. These optimizations create warm-up time and shape coverage boundaries. A rare batch size, long context, adapter rank, or processor can trigger recompilation or fallback. Record warm-up policy and count fallback paths. An OpenAI-compatible HTTP route provides interface portability, not identical tokenization, sampler order, stop behavior, usage accounting, or streaming events.
Not every language model has the autoregressive execution assumed so far. A diffusion language model may iteratively revise masked positions with bidirectional attention and commit several positions in parallel. A conventional causal KV cache cannot be reused unchanged when previously hidden states can change. Scheduling units become denoising steps or blocks rather than one appended token. Approximate caches and confidence-guided commits introduce quality/throughput controls. Treat such models as a different engine capability, not as a new checkpoint behind an autoregressive server flag.
Cluster orchestration sits above the model engine. A multi-node replica needs gang placement and topology awareness so all shards start on compatible links. Readiness begins after weights, kernels, and communication groups are usable, not when a container starts. Scale-down drains streams and releases or migrates KV state. Rolling a model, tokenizer, template, quantization recipe, or adapter changes cache identity. Autoscaling signals should include queued input/output tokens, active KV bytes, cache hits, preemptions, and SLO risk; CPU utilization misses the constrained resource.
The evaluation procedure is direct: create a matrix whose rows are workload slices and whose columns are required feature combinations. Run correctness gates first, then sweep offered load and measure goodput, memory, warm-up, and failure recovery. Headline benchmark results can shortlist engines. Only the pinned lattice can select one.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
section: 10.12 Production test-time compute needs a budget
text:
10.12 Production test-time compute needs a budget
A reasoning model improves a difficult planning task when allowed to think longer, so a service enables the highest reasoning setting for every request. Simple requests now occupy decode slots and KV frames for many more tokens. Queueing pushes hard requests past their deadlines, and total successful work falls. More compute improved isolated accuracy while reducing production utility.
Chapter 8 treated test-time compute as search and verification. In serving, reasoning budget is a reservation: reasoning tokens, effort tier, deadline, candidates, or tool calls. These are not interchangeable. Completion limits can truncate answers, effort can be opaque, and verifier stopping does not bound each attempted trajectory.
Hidden reasoning still consumes decode steps and holds state when only a final answer or summary returns. Use reported reasoning usage or empirical latency/token distributions by tier. Do not log private chain of thought for observability: summaries are generated output, not execution proof. Chapter 25 owns faithfulness; telemetry should prefer budgets, timings, tool events, verifier outcomes, and evidence.
A budget policy compares marginal value with marginal system cost. For one more reasoning allocation, let \Delta p be the estimated increase in task success, V the value of success, \Delta n additional generated tokens, c_n their direct unit cost, \Delta h added KV/slot holding time, c_h its capacity shadow price, and D expected harm from delay. Continue only while

\Delta p\,V > \Delta n\,c_n+\Delta h\,c_h+D.

The shadow price c_h rises near saturation, making budgets load- and difficulty-aware. Start cheap, escalate when a classifier or verifier predicts value, and enforce hard deadlines and token ceilings. Record tier, stop reason, outcome, and queue conditions; lower budgets under overload before rejecting all traffic.
Tools expose holding cost. Keeping KV through a tool wait avoids recomputation but pins memory; eviction requires re-prefill; a slower tier pays transfer. Decide from expected wait, state size, reuse, and queue pressure. Cancellation spans tool, model, and KV lease. Stable tool definitions also create reusable prefixes.
Inference-time budget forcing stops or extends a trace; s1 used it as an experimental control. Training-time Length Controlled Policy Optimization teaches requested lengths. Truncation can cut solutions, continuation can overthink, and trained controllers need separate evaluation. None guarantees monotonic quality.
The lab’s synthetic curve rises from score 0.631 at 32 reasoning tokens to 0.774 at 256, then declines to 0.771 at 512 while score per thousand tokens falls throughout. It represents saturation and an overthinking tail, not a model measurement. A production curve must be estimated by task slice, outcome severity, and load. The serving decision is to purchase marginal success, not to maximize visible reasoning length.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




serving_lab.py after reasoning allocation
10 lines
The budget sweep exposes diminishing marginal quality and permits a higher token budget to reduce synthetic score.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.13 Landscape 2026 (dated)

Verified on 2026-07-19. Current vLLM prefix-caching documentation describes block hashing with LoRA, multimodal, and cache-salt identity. SGLang’s paper describes radix-tree reuse and cache-aware scheduling. The TensorRT-LLM model-feature matrix exposes combination support. llama.cpp server documentation covers portable local CPU/GPU serving. Treat them as peers with different targets and inspect pinned matrices.
The OCP MX specification defines MXFP4 with an eight-bit scale per 32 FP4 values: nominally 4.25 bits/value before packing. NVIDIA NVFP4 documentation uses an FP8 scale per 16 FP4 values plus a tensor FP32 scale: nominally 4.5 bits/value plus amortized global scale. TensorRT-LLM’s matrix shows hardware/model dependence. Nominal width predicts neither quality nor speed.
LeaderWorkerSet represents multi-host replicas, and the Gateway API Inference Extension supplies inference-aware selection. llm-d and NVIDIA Dynamo compose routing, tiered KV, disaggregation, and orchestration around engines. APIs and maturity require live audit.
Current official OpenAI model documentation exposes model-specific reasoning-effort controls and notes that higher effort can increase latency and output use. Levels, defaults, visibility, cache behavior, and prices vary. Open-weight controls differ; exposed reasoning is not evidence of faithfulness.
Diffusion-language-model serving remains an emerging, separate execution path. Fast-dLLM studies approximate KV reuse and confidence-aware parallel decoding for bidirectional diffusion models. Its reported results are paper-specific, not a general replacement for autoregressive engine measurements.
Verify live: engine/model/kernel combination support; quantization scale layouts and hardware requirements; prefix-cache isolation and hash inputs; speculative strategies; grammar coverage; multi-LoRA and parallelism combinations; disaggregated KV protocols; Kubernetes API maturity; reasoning-effort names, usage accounting, and caching; local-server hardware backends; versions, licenses, security advisories, and benchmark configurations.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.14 Build
Run the deterministic build from the newbook directory:
python code/ch10/serving_lab.py
python code/ch10/render_metrics.py
python -m unittest discover -s code/ch10 -p "test_*.py" -v
All twelve requests arrive at step zero into four slots. Synthetic SLOs are TTFT at most 16 steps and TPOT at most 1.05; steps are abstract units. Fixed batches await their longest member, while continuous scheduling refills completed slots.










Policy
Makespan (steps)
Slot utilization
Requests satisfying both SLOs
Goodput (requests/step)




Fixed batches
32
0.617
8
0.250


Continuous refill
26
0.760
12
0.462



For 45 tokens in blocks of 16, two branches share two blocks and own thirteen-token tails, using four frames instead of six. Cross-tenant reuse fails. Cache break-even is 0.278 future reuses.
Over 40,000 deterministic speculative draws, theoretical/empirical acceptance is 0.900/0.901 and target total variation is 0.00365. Synthetic low-load and saturated rates are 3.413 and 0.931 times baseline: exactness does not imply speedup. Grammar masking removes 0.60 mass.








Quantization block
RMSE (fixture units)
Simple effective bits/value




16
0.455
5.000


64
0.978
4.250


256
1.415
4.062



Figure Figure 10.4 separates occupied capacity from qualified work, exposes scale granularity, and shows why reasoning needs a marginal-value stop.






Figure 10.4: How do scheduling, scale granularity, and reasoning budget change a serving decision? Continuous refill improves utilization and SLO-qualified goodput on the fixed request burst; finer four-bit blocks reduce RMSE while increasing metadata; and the synthetic reasoning curve saturates before its largest token budget. All values are deterministic analytical or synthetic fixtures.



To measure a real local or remote engine through an OpenAI-compatible route, run the optional seam:
python code/ch10/openai_compatible_adapter.py \
  http://127.0.0.1:8000 MODEL_ID "Explain why goodput can fall while throughput rises" \
  --max-tokens 64
The adapter reports first content event, because an SSE event need not equal one token, plus endpoint usage when supplied. Real experiments need controlled arrival rates and pinned revisions, hardware, topology, numbers, cache state, warm-up, request distributions, policy, and SLO boundaries. Verify output and cancellation semantics before speed.






WarningHonesty boundary



The default scheduler has unit-cost token steps and no real kernels. Its cache costs, bandwidth, coordination delays, draft contention, quantization vector, grammar distribution, and reasoning scores are declared analytical or synthetic fixtures. They validate equations and decision logic; they estimate no model, accelerator, engine, provider, tenant, or production workload. The optional adapter is a measurement seam, not a load generator or benchmark protocol.

10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.15 What endures, what changes
What endures. Goodput counts work that satisfies the complete user-visible contract. Continuous scheduling converts length variation into iteration-level admission; paged KV converts sequence growth into block allocation. Prefix reuse requires exact state identity and must beat recomputation after read, write, placement, and eviction costs. Exact speculative sampling preserves a target distribution through accept/reject correction but can lose under contention. Grammar constraints guarantee syntax, not semantics or authority. Quantization is specified by object, format, scale, accumulator, kernel, and validation—not bit width alone. Disaggregation wins only after subtracting KV movement and coordination. Parallelism exchanges memory and arithmetic for communication. Shared weights, adapters, caches, and accelerators are security boundaries. Reasoning consumes tokens, KV, slots, and deadlines even when its trace is hidden.
What changes. Engine versions, supported model-feature combinations, kernel coverage, accelerator formats, graph and compiler behavior, quantization recipes, speculative strategies, diffusion-model runtimes, remote-KV protocols, Kubernetes APIs, orchestration projects, provider effort controls, prices, and benchmark results all move. Keep these in pinned adapters, compatibility tests, and the dated landscape. Removing the landscape leaves every decision inequality and invariant above intact.
Chapter 11 moves from serving many fixed model variants to creating and governing customized ones; the serving lattice here becomes the deployment constraint for those artifacts.

title: 10  Serving at Scale: Engines, Caching, Quantization, and Production Test-Time Compute [F+S]
text:
10.16 Exercises

Verify the scheduler by hand. Draw the four slots for all 26 continuous-batching steps in the fixture. Recompute every start and finish time, slot utilization, TTFT pass, TPOT pass, and goodput. Then permute request order to improve fixed-batch utilization and state what queueing assumption your permutation violates.
Make prefix admission economic. Extend CacheKey with one multimodal and one quantization-layout field. Add an eviction score using entry bytes, predicted reuse, recomputation cost, and read cost. Construct a workload in which LRU evicts the higher-value entry, then test that your policy retains it without violating tenant isolation.
Break speculative speedup without breaking exactness. Sweep draft block length, target/draft total-variation distance, and draft cost. Predict the break-even frontier before running. Add a statistical target-distribution test and show a configuration whose isolated latency improves while saturated goodput falls.
Validate a quantized deployment. Choose a model, engine, accelerator, and two supported recipes. Write the full numerical contract, including scales and accumulators. Evaluate loading, kernel selection, memory, TTFT, TPOT, goodput, perplexity, task quality, structured-output validity, tool selection, long context, and safety gates. Define acceptance thresholds before observing results.
Measure the disaggregation inequality. Replace the lab’s synthetic transfer terms with measurements from an aggregated and a split deployment. Sweep prompt length, output length, concurrency, chunk size, and prefill/decode ratio. Include cancellation and one worker failure. Report where phase splitting changes from a loss to a win and which term moves the boundary.
Build an engine feature lattice. For one production workload, compare at least two engines at pinned revisions. Test the intersection of quantization, parallelism, prefix caching, structured decoding, adapters, speculation, and observability that you actually require. Record fallback kernels, warm-up, unsupported cells, semantic API differences, and goodput curves; defend the selected engine and its residual migration risk.
Design a load-aware reasoning budget. Define task value, error harm, latency SLO, tool waits, visible and hidden token accounting, KV holding cost, verifier, escalation tiers, hard stops, and overload behavior. Estimate marginal success by difficulty slice. Demonstrate one overthinking case and one underthinking case, then specify telemetry that avoids storing private chain of thought.

10.17 Notes and sources
Orca establishes iteration scheduling. PagedAttention develops paged KV and vLLM; SGLang develops RadixAttention. Preble, CachedAttention, and Mooncake study distributed and hierarchical KV reuse.
Speculative Decoding and Speculative Sampling give target-preserving algorithms. XGrammar develops CFG mask execution; Let Me Speak Freely? evaluates format-restriction effects.
GPTQ uses approximate second-order information; AWQ protects activation-salient weights; SmoothQuant migrates outlier difficulty. The OCP MX specification defines the format.
DistServe, Splitwise, and Sarathi-Serve establish phase-splitting and chunked-prefill trade-offs.
Punica and S-LoRA develop multi-adapter serving. LeftoverLocals documents local-memory recovery on affected GPUs.
s1 demonstrates budget forcing; L1 introduces Length Controlled Policy Optimization; Beyond Accuracy studies tool-integrated KV eviction and latency. Chapter 8 owns test-time-scaling quality.

Assumed: neural-network fundamentals, backpropagation, basic Python, and beginner PyTorch.
From earlier chapters: Chapter 5’s data-mixture and contamination treatment established why provenance, deduplication, and clean evaluation data matter. Chapter 7, Supervised and preference post-training owns the derivations of SFT, reward modeling, DPO, and RLHF. Chapter 8, Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards owns reasoning-policy optimization. Chapter 10, Quantization is a validation problem owns inference quantization and multi-LoRA serving mechanics. This chapter turns those ingredients into a customization decision and release workflow.
Not required: a training cluster, access to a proprietary teacher, experience with a fine-tuning service, or a seven-billion-parameter checkpoint. The default build is a deterministic CPU proxy; an optional adapter exposes the same QLoRA configuration to a real open-weight model.
