title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
text:
11.3 The customization decision tree
A support assistant repeatedly answers with the wrong refund policy. The team proposes fine-tuning because the error appears in the model’s output. That diagnosis is one layer too late. The policy changes every quarter, must be cited, and depends on the customer’s jurisdiction. Encoding today’s answer in weights would make updates slow, provenance invisible, and stale behavior difficult to revoke. The failure is missing evidence and state, not missing response behavior.
The first customization skill is deciding not to customize weights. Name the failing contract before choosing a method:

Evidence: the answer requires facts that are private, mutable, attributable, or too rare to trust to parametric recall. Retrieve the facts and require grounded use.
State: the answer depends on an account, workflow, prior action, or current environment. Read state through an authorized interface.
Effect: success means changing the world—issuing a refund, compiling code, or updating a ticket. Use a typed tool with validation and authorization.
Behavior: the necessary information is already present, but the model selects the wrong format, tone, classification, transformation, or refusal policy. Prompting or post-training may fit.
Representation: retrieval fails because semantically related items are poorly embedded or ranked. Tune the retriever, embedding model, or reranker against retrieval judgments.
Economics: a strong model succeeds, but latency or per-query compute prevents deployment. Distill into a smaller student, prune a family member, or route easy cases to it.

The RAG branch is completed in Ch 14. This section is deliberately self-contained because Chapter 14’s Route B reader uses it to decide whether retrieval is the missing mechanism.
Before any training, build the strongest non-training baseline that the production interface permits: a precise instruction, representative few-shot examples, a typed output schema, retrieved evidence when appropriate, and a deterministic verifier. Run that baseline on a frozen evaluation set. Training without this baseline cannot distinguish a learned capability from an expensive way to shorten a prompt.
Once the defect is genuinely model behavior, the kind of supervision selects the method:








Supervision the team can produce
Most direct intervention
What it teaches




“Here is the response to write”
SFT, often through LoRA or QLoRA
conditional imitation: input to target output


“Response A is better than B”
preference tuning such as DPO
relative choice under a prompt distribution


“This outcome scores 0.8”
reinforcement fine-tuning
search against a stable reward or grader


“Here is the teacher distribution”
logit distillation
relative probability across alternatives


“Here is the teacher completion or solution trace”
sequence distillation
imitation of teacher-generated trajectories


“These specialized checkpoints share one base”
model merging
an evaluated combination of parameter updates


“Here is a large unlabeled domain corpus”
continued pretraining
the domain’s token distribution, vocabulary, and regularities



Do not climb this table as a maturity ladder. SFT is not an inferior form of RL when exact targets are available; RFT is not useful when a grader is noisy or exploitable. Pick the supervision that most directly expresses the contract.
Figure Figure 11.1 makes escalation and its stop conditions explicit. Every weight-changing path returns to the same release gate.

flowchart TD
    F(["Observed production failure"]) --> B["Freeze a baseline and reproduce it"]
    B --> Q{"What is missing?"}
    Q -->|"mutable facts or citations"| R["Retrieval / RAG — completed in Ch 14"]
    Q -->|"private or live state"| S["Authorized state read"]
    Q -->|"external effect"| T["Typed tool + policy"]
    Q -->|"retrieval geometry"| E["Embedding or reranker tuning"]
    Q -->|"response behavior"| H{"Available supervision?"}
    H -->|"target response"| SFT["SFT with PEFT or full tuning"]
    H -->|"ranked pair"| DPO["Preference tuning"]
    H -->|"reliable scalar verifier"| RFT["Reinforcement fine-tuning"]
    Q -->|"teacher succeeds, serving is costly"| KD["Distill to a smaller student"]
    Q -->|"compatible specialists exist"| M["Merge task vectors"]
    R --> G["Four-set task and regression gate"]
    E --> G
    SFT --> G
    DPO --> G
    RFT --> G
    KD --> G
    M --> G
    G -->|"pass"| P(["Canary, observe, promote"])
    G -->|"fail"| B





flowchart TD
    F(["Observed production failure"]) --> B["Freeze a baseline and reproduce it"]
    B --> Q{"What is missing?"}
    Q -->|"mutable facts or citations"| R["Retrieval / RAG — completed in Ch 14"]
    Q -->|"private or live state"| S["Authorized state read"]
    Q -->|"external effect"| T["Typed tool + policy"]
    Q -->|"retrieval geometry"| E["Embedding or reranker tuning"]
    Q -->|"response behavior"| H{"Available supervision?"}
    H -->|"target response"| SFT["SFT with PEFT or full tuning"]
    H -->|"ranked pair"| DPO["Preference tuning"]
    H -->|"reliable scalar verifier"| RFT["Reinforcement fine-tuning"]
    Q -->|"teacher succeeds, serving is costly"| KD["Distill to a smaller student"]
    Q -->|"compatible specialists exist"| M["Merge task vectors"]
    R --> G["Four-set task and regression gate"]
    E --> G
    SFT --> G
    DPO --> G
    RFT --> G
    KD --> G
    M --> G
    G -->|"pass"| P(["Canary, observe, promote"])
    G -->|"fail"| B




Figure 11.1: Which missing mechanism should be repaired before a team changes model weights?





Two tempting shortcuts fail this tree. Fine-tuning on documents is not a dependable database: memorization is uneven, updates require training, and answers do not carry source identity. Fine-tuning on a handful of bad outputs is also not a specification: the examples may encode an accidental wording, judge bias, or one traffic slice. Diagnose the invariant, then collect supervision around it.

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
text:
11.4 What you will build






TipThe customization decision lab



You will extend one integrated artifact rooted at code/ch11/customization_lab.py. Its declared synthetic linear fixture has a frozen base classifier, two related tasks, and four data partitions. The lab reconstructs the base through the NF4 codebook, trains a rank-two LoRA update, adds temperature-scaled logit distillation, evaluates every checkpoint on train, development, untouched task-test, and general-regression sets, then sweeps a two-adapter linear merge against TIES.
render_metrics.py renders the numeric result from the same functions. hf_qlora_adapter.py is an optional training seam for a real seven-billion-parameter-class conversational checkpoint and teacher-trace JSONL; it does not replace the release gate. Success in the default fixture means the task-test gain reaches eight percentage points, general regression loses no more than eight points, every mechanism test passes, and the merge result is reported rather than presumed.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
section: 11.5 LoRA and QLoRA isolate a trainable update
text:
11.5 LoRA and QLoRA isolate a trainable update
Full fine-tuning gives every parameter permission to move. That is useful when a broad distribution shift needs high capacity, but it couples one adaptation to the storage, optimizer state, checkpointing, and serving cost of an entire model. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) constrains the update and keeps a reusable base.
For a pretrained linear map W_0\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{out}\times d_{in}}, LoRA freezes W_0 and learns

h=W_0x+\frac{\alpha}{r}BAx,

where A\in\mathbb{R}^{r\times d_{in}}, B\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{out}\times r}, rank r\ll\min(d_{in},d_{out}), and \alpha controls update scale. The trainable parameter count is r(d_{in}+d_{out}) instead of d_{in}d_{out}. A common initialization draws A and sets B=0, so the first forward pass exactly matches the base. After training, \Delta W=(\alpha/r)BA can remain as an adapter or be added to a compatible base for inference.
A common mixed-precision Adam accounting is roughly 16 bytes per trainable parameter—half-precision weight and gradient, a full-precision master weight, and two full-precision moments—before activations and temporary buffers. LoRA reduces that term and the saved artifact. It does not erase activation memory, base-weight memory, optimizer implementation details, or the cost of forwarding through the full model.
Configuration is a capacity allocation problem:

Establish an all-linear baseline when the architecture and PEFT implementation support it; restricting updates to query and value projections is a historical default, not a universal optimum.
Sweep rank against held-out quality. Rank is update capacity, not an accuracy knob with monotonic returns.
Treat \alpha, learning rate, dropout, target modules, sequence length, packing, and epochs as a coupled recipe. The heuristic \alpha=2r is a starting point, not a law.
Inspect trainable parameter names and counts before the first batch. A misspelled module pattern can create a no-op or update an unintended head.
Save the base revision, tokenizer, chat template, PEFT configuration, and adapter together. An adapter is not self-describing enough to deploy safely.

Rank-stabilized LoRA replaces \alpha/r scaling with \alpha/\sqrt{r} so updates do not vanish as quickly at high rank. DoRA separates weight magnitude from direction and applies a low-rank update to the direction. Both are hypotheses to evaluate under the same gate; neither repairs poor supervision.
Full fine-tuning remains an important control. Escalate when a well-tuned PEFT sweep saturates, the required update is broad rather than approximately low rank, embeddings or architectural components must change, or continued pretraining is the actual objective. Compare it with PEFT at matched data and a declared compute budget; a full update can gain capacity while increasing optimizer memory, checkpoint size, forgetting surface, and the cost of keeping variants independently deployable.
QLoRA makes the frozen base cheaper to hold during adapter training. It stores base weights in four-bit NormalFloat (NF4), dequantizes them to a higher-precision compute type for matrix operations, and backpropagates through that fixed reconstruction into LoRA parameters. The optimized object remains the adapter; the four-bit codes are not updated.
NF4 places code points according to a normal-distribution prior and includes an exact zero. Double quantization compresses the blockwise quantization constants, saving about 0.37 bits per parameter in the QLoRA experiments. Paged optimizers move optimizer pages through unified memory to control transient spikes. These mechanisms solve different memory terms and should be measured separately.
Figure Figure 11.2 answers the frequent question “what receives a gradient?” The quantized base participates in the computation but is immutable.

flowchart LR
    X(["Activation x"]) --> Q["Frozen NF4 codes + block scales"]
    Q -->|"dequantize for compute"| W["Base matrix multiply in bf16/fp16"]
    X --> A["Trainable LoRA A"]
    A --> B["Trainable LoRA B"]
    W --> SUM(("+"))
    B -->|"scaled low-rank update"| SUM
    SUM --> H(["Activation h"])
    L["Loss"] -.->|"gradient"| B
    L -.->|"gradient"| A
    L -.->|"no update"| Q





flowchart LR
    X(["Activation x"]) --> Q["Frozen NF4 codes + block scales"]
    Q -->|"dequantize for compute"| W["Base matrix multiply in bf16/fp16"]
    X --> A["Trainable LoRA A"]
    A --> B["Trainable LoRA B"]
    W --> SUM(("+"))
    B -->|"scaled low-rank update"| SUM
    SUM --> H(["Activation h"])
    L["Loss"] -.->|"gradient"| B
    L -.->|"gradient"| A
    L -.->|"no update"| Q




Figure 11.2: During QLoRA, which representation is stored, which precision computes, and which parameters update?





The lab implements the NF4 reconstruction and the low-rank gradient explicitly:
# code/ch11/customization_lab.py — changed regions: nf4_quantize, LoRA
quantized_base, rmse = nf4_quantize(full_base, group_size=16)
adapter = LoRA(quantized_base, rank=2, alpha=4.0, seed=1)

frozen = quantized_base.copy()
train_sft(adapter, sets["train"])
assert np.array_equal(quantized_base, frozen)
assert np.linalg.norm(adapter.delta) > 0

Invariant now verified

The base reconstruction never changes; only the low-rank update receives optimization steps.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
section: 11.6 Distillation moves behavior down the size ladder
text:
11.6 Distillation moves behavior down the size ladder
A frontier teacher classifies difficult tickets accurately, but its latency and price make it unsuitable for every request. Distillation asks a smaller student to reproduce the teacher on a declared operating distribution. It does not copy an abstract capability object. What transfers depends on prompts, teacher access, targets, student capacity, and loss.
Sequence-level distillation samples teacher completions and uses them as SFT targets. It works through a black-box API and can transfer format, style, tool-selection patterns, or solution traces. It discards the teacher’s probability mass over alternatives and inherits sampling errors. Generate multiple candidates when a verifier exists, retain provenance and teacher settings, filter invalid outputs, and preserve human or executable gold data as an anchor.
Logit distillation uses the teacher distribution. Let z_T and z_S be teacher and student logits and let temperature \tau>0 soften both distributions:

p_T^{(\tau)}=\operatorname{softmax}(z_T/\tau),\qquad
p_S^{(\tau)}=\operatorname{softmax}(z_S/\tau).

A standard objective contains

\mathcal{L}_{KD}=\tau^2D_{KL}\!\left(p_T^{(\tau)}\,\|\,p_S^{(\tau)}\right).

The teacher can reveal that two wrong labels are nearly plausible even when its sampled output supplies only one hard target. The \tau^2 factor compensates for gradient shrinkage introduced by temperature. This white-box signal requires compatible vocabularies or an explicit mapping and can be expensive across all tokens.
Direction matters. Forward KL, D_{KL}(p_T\|p_S), penalizes the student for under-covering teacher-supported regions. Reverse KL, D_{KL}(p_S\|p_T), emphasizes modes the student itself assigns mass to; MiniLLM developed a reverse-KL formulation for generative LMs. These are different objectives, not interchangeable spelling.
On-policy distillation addresses exposure mismatch. Static teacher sequences condition training on teacher prefixes, while deployment conditions the student on its own prior tokens. Generalized Knowledge Distillation lets the student generate prefixes and asks the teacher for feedback there. It spends teacher compute on states the student actually visits and can focus learning on self-generated mistakes. It also needs safeguards against low-quality or adversarial student trajectories.
Reasoning traces add a learnability constraint. A correct, very long teacher solution can exceed the student’s capacity or teach verbal mannerisms rather than a reusable procedure. Evidence on the small-model learnability gap found that students at or below three billion parameters did not consistently benefit from longer traces or larger teachers; shorter or mixed-complexity traces could work better. Treat trace length, abstraction, correctness, diversity, and student likelihood as data-selection variables. Compare answer-only, concise derivation, long trace, and mixed curricula on an untouched set. Do not assume access to a provider’s private hidden chain of thought; use outputs the provider and license permit, executable trajectories, or concise authored rationales.
Distillation becomes an economic replacement only after amortization. If data generation and training cost C_{build}, the teacher costs C_T per in-scope query, the student costs C_S<C_T, and N such queries are expected, the direct-cost break-even is

N>\frac{C_{build}}{C_T-C_S}.

Add routing, monitoring, retraining, and teacher fallback before using this as a business case. A student matching the teacher on support-ticket triage has not matched it on open-domain reasoning. Record the envelope and route outside it.
The lab begins KD from the SFT adapter and uses softened teacher logits on a separate distillation set:
# code/ch11/customization_lab.py — changed region: LoRA.kd_step
teacher = softmax(teacher_logits, temperature)
student = softmax(adapter.logits(x), temperature)
loss = np.mean(np.sum(teacher * np.log((teacher + 1e-12) / (student + 1e-12)), axis=1))
grad_logits = (student - teacher) * temperature / len(x)
adapter._apply_gradient(grad_logits.T @ x, learning_rate)









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




customization_lab.py after logit KD
30 lines
Temperature changes the supervised distribution, and KD loss falls without granting updates to the base.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
section: 11.7 Model merging combines task vectors, not capabilities
text:
11.7 Model merging combines task vectors, not capabilities
Two teams fine-tune the same base: one for ticket routing, another for urgency detection. Retraining a joint model requires both datasets and another training run, so the teams average checkpoints and call the result a combined expert. Sometimes that works because the checkpoints remain in a connected low-loss region. Sometimes opposing updates cancel the features each task needs.
For a base parameter vector \theta_0 and task-fine-tuned parameters \theta_i, define the task vector

\tau_i=\theta_i-\theta_0.

Linear task arithmetic constructs \theta'=\theta_0+\sum_i\lambda_i\tau_i. A model soup instead averages compatible fine-tuned checkpoints, often selected by validation performance. Both require aligned parameters: the same architecture, tensor shapes, tokenizer and vocabulary layout, and normally the same base revision. Matching filenames or model family names is insufficient.
LoRA makes the subtraction cheap because \tau_i is already represented by low-rank deltas. Merge the realized deltas B_iA_i, not A matrices separately from B matrices: in general,

(B_1+B_2)(A_1+A_2)\ne B_1A_1+B_2A_2.

If one compact adapter must represent the merged delta, add the deltas and refactor the result with a rank-constrained SVD, accepting approximation error.
TIES addresses two common interference sources:

Trim: zero small-magnitude changes that are more likely redundant or noisy.
Elect: choose an aggregate sign for each surviving coordinate.
Disjoint merge: combine only task updates whose sign agrees with the elected sign.

DARE randomly drops delta parameters and rescales survivors before a merge, using redundancy to reduce interference. SLERP follows a spherical interpolation path between two parameter vectors rather than a straight Euclidean segment. Names do not select a winner. Coefficients, density, layer treatment, task mix, and regression behavior still need a sweep.
The lab’s TIES implementation is intentionally small enough to audit:
# code/ch11/customization_lab.py — changed region: ties_merge
cutoff = np.quantile(np.abs(delta), 1.0 - density)
trimmed = np.where(np.abs(delta) >= cutoff, weight * delta, 0.0)
elected = np.sign(stack.sum(axis=0))
keep = np.sign(stack) == elected
count = keep.sum(axis=0)
merged = np.divide(
    (stack * keep).sum(axis=0), count,
    out=np.zeros_like(stack[0]), where=count > 0,
)
At equal weights in the fixed fixture, TIES beats linear averaging on mean task accuracy. Across the full coefficient sweep, a linear merge reaches the higher best mean. That result is more useful than declaring a universal algorithm: interference handling can improve one operating point while coefficient selection changes the conclusion.
Merging is attractive when training data are unavailable, when ablation is cheap, or when one artifact reduces serving complexity. It is a bad fit for checkpoints with different bases, incompatible tokenizers, safety policies that must remain independently revocable, or tasks whose joint regressions cannot be measured. A merged model inherits licenses and risks; it does not erase lineage.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




customization_lab.py after the merge sweep
47 lines
Only deltas from one quantized base are combined, and linear versus TIES quality is measured at every coefficient.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
section: 11.8 Preference tuning and reinforcement fine-tuning are surgical tools
text:
11.8 Preference tuning and reinforcement fine-tuning are surgical tools
SFT can demonstrate a desired refusal or concise answer, yet it treats every target token as equally authoritative. When several responses are valid but one is preferred, comparative or scalar feedback carries a better-shaped signal.
For preference tuning, collect two responses to the same prompt and label a chosen and rejected output. Pairs should expose the defect: excessive hedging, unsupported claims, a refusal boundary, verbosity, instruction priority, or tone. Near-ties can be informative when annotators agree; easy pairs waste capacity. Preserve the policy that generated each response, randomize presentation order, measure annotator agreement, and keep a slice from real production failures.
DPO optimizes a classification-style loss over the chosen/rejected log-probability margin relative to a reference policy. Its \beta participates in the policy-versus-reference trade-off and interacts with learning rate and pair difficulty. Tune it against both preference win rate and general regressions. Chapter 7 owns the derivation; the practical point here is that a higher training win rate can still indicate memorized phrasing or excessive drift.
Reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) fits tasks with multiple valid trajectories and a reliable outcome score: code that passes tests, mathematics with a checked final answer, structured extraction against a validator, or a simulator score. It is usually a poor first move for subjective tone when the grader is an unstable judge model. Start by evaluating the grader independently:

Does it score known good and bad outputs correctly across slices?
Can formatting, verbosity, or leaked references raise reward without solving the task?
Is reward stable across retries and grader versions?
Are training prompts, grader development cases, and final tests separate?
Does the model have enough initial success to receive a useful signal?

If almost every sample receives zero, provide SFT demonstrations or a curriculum before RFT. If almost every sample already receives full reward, the optimization has little headroom. Hold out adversarial grader cases and inspect high-reward failures for reward hacking. Chapter 8 owns RLVR/GRPO mechanics, and Chapter 23 extends reinforcement tuning to complete agent trajectories; this chapter owns the model-customization choice and gate.
Use the smallest intervention that matches the feedback. A refusal wording issue may need a few-shot prompt or a narrow preference adapter. A verifiable reasoning strategy may justify RFT. Neither should be used to inject a quarterly policy document.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
section: 11.9 Data engineering determines what customization learns
text:
11.9 Data engineering determines what customization learns
Optimization makes the model imitate the empirical training distribution. It cannot infer which examples were mislabeled, which teacher outputs violate a license, which repeated template dominates by accident, or which benchmark answer leaked into a prompt. Most customization leverage therefore sits before the trainer.
Define one canonical record schema with prompt, context, response or feedback, task and source identifiers, provenance, license, timestamp, policy version, generator revision and settings, validation result, safety labels, and split. Hash normalized content and near-deduplicate at the semantic unit users see—a conversation or problem, not only an individual message.
Split by source or entity before augmentation and synthesis. If one customer template is paraphrased into train and test, lexical differences conceal shared information. Quarantine the final test and regression sets from teacher generation, rejection sampling, hyperparameter selection, and judge-prompt development. A repeatedly consulted test set becomes a development set; rotate it.
Synthetic data is a controlled measurement instrument, not free truth:

Self-Instruct bootstraps instructions, inputs, and outputs from a model, then filters invalid or similar generations.
Magpie elicits user-like queries from the pre-query template of an aligned model and generates responses at scale.
Rejection sampling generates several outputs and retains those passing a verifier or quality model.
ReST and ReST-EM alternate generation, feedback-based filtering, and offline fine-tuning.

Each loop can narrow diversity, amplify a grader blind spot, or train on its own mistakes. The model-collapse results in Chapter 5 concern recursive replacement of real-distribution support, not a ban on all synthetic supervision. Keep licensed human or environment-grounded anchors, track synthetic fraction by slice and generation, measure diversity and tail coverage, and stop when held-out improvement saturates.
Reasoning and tool data need extra validation. Execute code and tool calls in a sandbox; recompute final answers; reject traces that reach a correct result through invalid steps; normalize tool schemas without erasing meaningful variation. Do not train on secrets or raw production conversations merely because they improved the teacher prompt. Apply consent, retention, access, and deletion policies before data enters an immutable artifact.
Figure Figure 11.3 places the irreversible boundary at dataset approval and keeps evaluation outside the self-improvement loop.

flowchart LR
    P["Versioned real prompts and labels"] --> S["Split by source / entity"]
    S --> TR["Train partition"]
    S --> DV["Development partition"]
    S --> TT["Sealed task test"]
    S --> RG["General regressions"]
    TR --> G["Teacher or current policy generates candidates"]
    G --> V["Executable checks, humans, dedup, license filter"]
    V -->|"approved records + provenance"| MX["Versioned training mixture"]
    MX --> FT["SFT / preference / RFT / distillation"]
    FT --> G
    FT --> DV
    DV -->|"recipe selection only"| MX
    FT --> Q["Release candidate"]
    TT --> Q
    RG --> Q





flowchart LR
    P["Versioned real prompts and labels"] --> S["Split by source / entity"]
    S --> TR["Train partition"]
    S --> DV["Development partition"]
    S --> TT["Sealed task test"]
    S --> RG["General regressions"]
    TR --> G["Teacher or current policy generates candidates"]
    G --> V["Executable checks, humans, dedup, license filter"]
    V -->|"approved records + provenance"| MX["Versioned training mixture"]
    MX --> FT["SFT / preference / RFT / distillation"]
    FT --> G
    FT --> DV
    DV -->|"recipe selection only"| MX
    FT --> Q["Release candidate"]
    TT --> Q
    RG --> Q




Figure 11.3: Where must provenance, filtering, and evaluation quarantine interrupt a synthetic self-improvement loop?





Mixture weights are part of the objective. Sampling a million synthetic classification records beside ten thousand safety cases does not merely add data; it changes gradient frequency. Report raw counts, sampled counts, token counts, duplication, loss masks, and per-source learning curves. Pack short examples for efficiency only after verifying boundary tokens and assistant-only loss masking.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
section: 11.10 Forgetting and compression define the capability budget
text:
11.10 Forgetting and compression define the capability budget
A frozen base does not guarantee preserved behavior. LoRA leaves W_0 unchanged, but inference uses W_0+\Delta W. For any input x and compatible operator/vector norms,

\left\|\frac{\alpha}{r}BAx\right\|
\le
\frac{\alpha}{r}\|B\|\,\|A\|\,\|x\|.

The adapter can dominate particular activations even when it contains few parameters. Catastrophic forgetting is observed behavior on earlier capabilities, not proof that base tensors moved.
Mitigate drift by matching intervention strength to the defect: lower rank or fewer target modules, a smaller learning rate, fewer epochs, early stopping on regression metrics, KL or weight regularization, and replay of representative general examples. Separate adapters can preserve revocability when tenants or policies differ. Replay must sample meaningful capabilities and safety boundaries; generic benchmark averages can miss a critical slice.
A practical adaptation ladder is:

Continued pretraining (CPT): use the language-model objective on a large unlabeled domain corpus when vocabulary, style, or domain regularities are absent. Mix replay and expect the widest regression surface.
SFT: teach explicit input-to-output behavior when targets can be written or verified.
Preference tuning: resolve subjective trade-offs that exact demonstrations underspecify.
RFT: optimize a stable outcome verifier when trajectories have many valid forms.
Distillation: transfer the resulting in-scope behavior into the deployment-sized student.

These stages are optional and can branch. Do not run CPT to fix JSON formatting or preference tuning to memorize terminology. Gate after every stage so the source of gain or damage remains identifiable.
Compression can create a smaller model family rather than one adapter:

Structured pruning removes complete layers, hidden dimensions, attention heads, or MLP channels. It can reduce dense tensor shapes and produce speedups on ordinary kernels, but requires importance estimation and recovery training.
Unstructured pruning zeros individual weights. Parameter count falls, yet wall-clock speed improves only with a supported sparse representation and kernel.
Low-rank compression factorizes an existing matrix W\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n} as UV with inner dimension k, reducing parameters from mn to k(m+n). Truncated SVD minimizes matrix reconstruction error under a Frobenius norm; that is not the same as minimizing language-model loss.
Prune then distill uses the original model to recover quality in the structurally smaller student. Minitron combines depth, width, attention, and MLP pruning with distillation; SliceGPT removes rows and columns after rotations that preserve the network function before slicing.

Quantization is another compression axis, but Chapter 10’s validation ladder remains authoritative. A pruned or distilled model can still be quantized afterward; each composition needs its own quality and kernel measurement. Compare against a natively pretrained model of similar size. Creating a family member is worthwhile when recovered quality and lifetime serving savings exceed the pruning, distillation, and revalidation burden.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
section: 11.11 The four-set gate decides whether a model ships
text:
11.11 The four-set gate decides whether a model ships
Training loss tells whether an optimizer fits its objective. It does not tell whether the model generalizes, preserves prior behavior, obeys safety policy, or runs within the serving contract. A customization project needs four data roles whose access rules differ:









Set
Used for
Forbidden use
Typical failure it reveals




Train
gradient updates
reporting generalization
memorization and source imbalance


Development
hyperparameters, checkpoints, mixture and prompt selection
final unbiased claim
recipe sensitivity and overfitting to train


Untouched task test
one release decision after the recipe is frozen
iterative tuning
true in-scope generalization


General regression suite
existing capability, safety, calibration, and operational invariants
optimizing only an aggregate score
collateral damage outside the target



“Untouched” is a process property. Store access logs, restrict labels, and promote a test to development after repeated inspection. Create a new sealed set for the next release. Decontaminate by source, template, entity, and semantic overlap, not only exact strings.
Predeclare a release rule. Let M_{task} be task-test quality, R_j be a regression loss for capability j, \delta the required task gain, and \epsilon_j each tolerated regression. A simplified gate is

\operatorname{ship}=
\mathbf{1}\!\left[
\Delta M_{task}\ge\delta
\land
\forall j:\Delta R_j\le\epsilon_j
\land
safety=pass
\land
serving=pass
\right].

Use paired confidence intervals or a test appropriate to the metric when samples are noisy. Report slices and worst-case constraints; an aggregate gain must not buy an unacceptable jurisdiction, language, safety, or accessibility regression. Run deterministic or executable metrics first. When model judges are needed, calibrate position, length, style, and self-preference biases as Chapter 22 describes.
Hold the inference contract fixed: model base, adapter, tokenizer, template, prompt, tools, decoding, quantization, and context budget. Comparing a tuned model with greedy decoding to a base model with a different system prompt is a system comparison, not an isolated adaptation estimate. Both can be useful if labeled accurately.
The gate should include deployment metrics when customization is motivated by economics: peak memory, cold start, time to first token, output rate, structured-output validity, and cost under the same workload. Distillation that loses one quality point but halves latency may win a predeclared utility function; a post hoc story does not.
The lab encodes the minimum task gain and maximum regression drop before inspecting its final candidate:
# code/ch11/customization_lab.py — changed region: release_gate
task_gain = candidate["task_test"] - baseline["task_test"]
regression_drop = baseline["general_regression"] - candidate["general_regression"]
passed = task_gain >= 0.08 and regression_drop <= 0.08









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




customization_lab.py after the four-set gate
22 lines
A task improvement cannot ship by hiding an excessive general-regression loss.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
text:
11.13 Landscape 2026 (dated)

Verified on 2026-07-19. The current open-weight stack exposes most of this chapter as composable libraries. Hugging Face PEFT documents LoRA configuration, rsLoRA, DoRA, quantized training preparation, adapter merge, and newer initialization/routing variants. Transformers bitsandbytes documentation documents NF4, nested quantization, and higher-precision compute for QLoRA. TRL provides SFT and DPO trainers. Mergekit implements families of checkpoint-merging recipes. Their supported intersections move faster than the LoRA and task-vector equations.
Managed customization now spans more than SFT. Official OpenAI documentation describes supervised fine-tuning, DPO, and grader-driven RFT. Google Vertex AI tuning documentation lists supervised and preference tuning for selected Gemini models. Amazon Bedrock documents supervised fine-tuning and managed model distillation, while its RFT documentation exposes reward functions through code or model judges for selected models. Model lists, regions, quotas, data policies, and artifact access differ.
The research frontier is moving toward feedback on the student’s own states. On-Policy Context Distillation trains on student trajectories while comparing with a context-conditioned teacher and reports cross-size transfer of experiential and system-prompt behavior. Treat this as a paper result requiring reproduction, not a default production recipe. Structured pruning plus recovery distillation remains a practical route to model-family creation; current NVIDIA tooling documents the Minitron pruning workflow.
Hosted tuning buys managed training, security integration, and deployment but restricts available bases, algorithms, gradients/logits, merging, export, and sometimes data inspection. Open-weight DIY work grants tensor-level control, offline evaluation, adapter portability, distillation logits, pruning, and custom merges while making the team responsible for accelerator software, licenses, supply chain, privacy, kernels, serving, and incident response. Choose from the required control surface, not ideology.
Verify live: tunable model IDs and retirement dates; supported regions and modalities; SFT, preference, RFT, and distillation availability; grader semantics; training-data retention and use; output rights for distillation; whether weights or adapters can be exported; PEFT/TRL/bitsandbytes compatibility; target-module names; compute dtypes and accelerator support; merge methods and tokenizer handling; serving-engine adapter/quantization intersections; prices, quotas, licenses, security advisories, and deprecations.

11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
text:
11.14 Build
Run the deterministic build from the newbook directory:
python code/ch11/customization_lab.py
python code/ch11/render_metrics.py
python -m unittest discover -s code/ch11 -p "test_*.py" -v
The fixture uses four classes and twelve features. A declared task update acts primarily on the first seven features; the general-regression distribution emphasizes the remaining features. That construction gives the gate a capability it should preserve without pretending to model language. The base is quantized and reconstructed in groups of sixteen with NF4, producing RMSE 0.0671 fixture units. Rank-two adapters train on 320 task examples, KD uses 400 additional inputs, and the untouched task test has 320 examples.










Candidate
Train
Development
Untouched task test
General regression




Frozen NF4 base
0.7625
0.7583
0.7750
0.9625


QLoRA-style SFT adapter
0.9438
0.9125
0.9313
0.9333


SFT plus logit KD
0.9750
0.9625
0.9625
0.9417



The distilled candidate gains 0.1875 task-test accuracy and loses 0.0208 general-regression accuracy against the same NF4 baseline. It passes the predeclared gain and loss limits of 0.08. SFT cross-entropy falls from 0.5694 to 0.2825; KD KL falls from 0.0117 to 0.0044.
At a 0.5/0.5 merge, linear task arithmetic reaches mean task accuracy 0.8469 and TIES reaches 0.8578. Sweeping coefficients reverses the simple ranking: linear reaches 0.8656 at adapter-A weight 0.8, while the best TIES point reaches 0.8578 at weight 0.7. Figure Figure 11.4 shows both the release gate and the merge ablation.






Figure 11.4: What changed after each customization stage, and did an interference-aware merge remove the need to tune coefficients? The left panel evaluates the frozen NF4 base, QLoRA-style SFT adapter, and logit-distilled adapter on all four deterministic fixture sets. The right panel sweeps linear and TIES merge weights; TIES wins at equal weights but not at each method’s best coefficient. All values are synthetic CPU measurements, not language-model estimates.



For a real seven-billion-parameter-class run, create conversational JSONL with one messages list per record. Generate teacher traces only in the training partition, validate and deduplicate them, and keep development, sealed task-test, and general-regression files independent. Install a compatible PyTorch/Transformers stack and invoke the optional seam:
python code/ch11/hf_qlora_adapter.py \
  data/train_teacher_traces.jsonl \
  data/development.jsonl \
  artifacts/support-qlora \
  --model MODEL_ID \
  --epochs 1
The adapter pins the mechanism: NF4 base storage, double quantization, bf16 compute, gradient checkpointing, rank 16, \alpha=32, dropout 0.05, and all-linear targets. It does not claim those values are optimal. Start with roughly one to two thousand high-quality task records and a separately labeled set of teacher traces only when licensing and privacy permit, then sweep rank, learning rate, epochs, modules, and data mixtures on development data. Generate outputs from the frozen base and every candidate under identical decoding, score all four sets, and apply thresholds declared before the run. Pin the model revision and inspect target-module names before training.






WarningHonesty boundary



The default build is a linear classification proxy. Its NF4 codebook, LoRA algebra, gradients, KL loss, task vectors, TIES operations, splits, and gate are real; its features, labels, accuracy, and thresholds estimate no LLM, teacher, domain, or deployment. The optional adapter performs a real QLoRA training path when its third-party dependencies, checkpoint, data, license, and GPU are supplied, but task-specific generation and grading remain the user’s evaluation harness. A successful proxy run is evidence that the mechanisms execute, not that a seven-billion-parameter model will pass.
crumbs: Part III — Inference, Serving, and Customization
11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
text:
11.15 What endures, what changes
What endures. Diagnose evidence, state, effects, representation, behavior, and economics before changing weights. Match supervision to the signal: write for SFT, rank for preference tuning, verify for RFT, distribute probabilities or sequences for distillation. LoRA constrains an update but does not guarantee retained behavior. QLoRA quantizes a frozen base while optimizing higher-precision adapters. Distillation transfers behavior on a sampled envelope, not general intelligence. Task-vector arithmetic requires parameter alignment, and every merge coefficient is an empirical choice. Split before synthesis, preserve provenance, and quarantine tests. Every candidate must pass an untouched task test and general regressions under the intended serving representation.
What changes. Base models, tokenizer templates, provider tuning methods, trainable model lists, adapter variants, quantization kernels, managed graders, merge implementations, pruning tools, accelerator memory, prices, licenses, and benchmark status move. Keep them in pinned recipes and the dated landscape. Removing the landscape leaves the decision tree, low-rank algebra, distillation objectives, task-vector definition, data boundaries, and four-set gate intact.
Chapter 14 completes the evidence branch with embeddings and RAG. Chapters 22 and 26 deepen evaluation validity and lifecycle economics, while Chapter 23 applies reinforcement training to agents rather than only model responses.
crumbs: Part III — Inference, Serving, and Customization
11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
text:
11.16 Exercises

Defend a non-training decision. Choose a real failure and classify it as evidence, state, effect, representation, behavior, or economics. Build the strongest prompt/schema/retrieval/tool baseline you can. State what new evidence would justify SFT, preference tuning, or RFT, and why training before that evidence would be premature.
Budget a LoRA configuration. For one transformer projection, compute trainable parameters for ranks 8, 16, 64, and 256, plus approximate mixed-precision Adam state. Compare \alpha/r and \alpha/\sqrt{r} scaling. Predict which rank will win on development data, run the sweep, and explain any non-monotonic result.
Audit QLoRA numerics. Extend nf4_quantize to return codes and scales. Implement double quantization of scales, calculate effective bits per parameter including metadata, and compare reconstruction error across group sizes. Break the exact-zero invariant, predict its effect on zero-valued groups, and add a failing test before repairing it.
Compare distillation signals. Add hard-label sequence KD, forward-KL logit KD, reverse KL, and an on-policy prefix sampler to the fixture or a small language model. Hold examples and compute constant. Compare task gain, calibration, regression, and sensitivity to temperature. For reasoning data, ablate answer-only, concise, long, and mixed-length traces.
Make merging earn its artifact. Add a third compatible adapter. Compare linear task arithmetic, model soup selection, TIES, and DARE across coefficient and density sweeps. Include all task tests and general regressions. Construct one sign-conflict failure, then determine whether keeping independently routed adapters is safer than any merge.
Operate the four-set gate. Replace the fixture thresholds with confidence intervals and at least three regression slices. Log every access to the sealed test. Simulate ten rounds of recipe selection, show how repeated test inspection biases the release decision, then design a rotation policy and a predeclared utility function that includes latency.
Create and serve a smaller specialist. Use licensed teacher outputs to QLoRA-tune a seven-billion-parameter-class or smaller open-weight model on one narrow task. Compare it with a stronger teacher and a natively small baseline. Quantize the winner under Chapter 10’s ladder, canary it with a fallback, and report training/data cost, break-even volume, task envelope, regression budget, model-card lineage, and residual risk.
crumbs: Part III — Inference, Serving, and Customization
11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]

title: 11  Customization: Distillation, Merging, and Fine-Tuning in Practice [F+S]
text:
11.17 Notes and sources
LoRA introduces frozen-base low-rank updates. QLoRA introduces NF4, double quantization, and paged optimizers. rsLoRA studies rank-stabilized scaling; DoRA decomposes magnitude and direction.
Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network establishes temperature-based KD. MiniLLM develops reverse-KL generative distillation. Generalized Knowledge Distillation trains on student-generated sequences. Small Models Struggle to Learn from Strong Reasoners documents the learnability gap for long traces and large teachers.
Model Soups studies checkpoint averaging. Task Arithmetic defines task vectors; TIES-Merging resolves redundancy and sign interference; DARE drops and rescales task deltas.
InstructGPT gives the demonstration, preference-model, and RLHF pipeline. DPO directly optimizes pairwise preferences. Tülu 3 provides an open SFT, DPO, RLVR, decontamination, and multi-task evaluation recipe.
Self-Instruct bootstraps instruction data; Magpie synthesizes alignment prompts; ReST-EM iterates generation, filtering, and training. The Curse of Recursion studies model collapse under recursive generated-data replacement. Don’t Stop Pretraining establishes domain- and task-adaptive pretraining.
Minitron combines structured pruning with distillation, Sheared LLaMA uses structured pruning and continued training, and SliceGPT deletes rows and columns after computational invariance transformations. HELM motivates multi-scenario, multi-metric evaluation; Chapter 22 owns the full evaluation system.

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
Assumed: neural-network fundamentals, basic Python, and production-backend basics: HTTP requests, JSON, exceptions, tests, and the distinction between authentication and authorization.
Route A: Chapters 7 and 9 already established chat templates, decoding, and the reproducibility boundary. This chapter moves those mechanisms to the caller side of an API.
Not required: a provider SDK, an API key, a GPU, an agent framework, or prior experience with tool calling. The default build is deterministic and offline. An optional seam can call any local OpenAI-compatible server over raw HTTP.







NoteRoute B backfill — required, about eight pages



Route B arrives here from Chapter 1. Before continuing, read Chapter 9, “Decoding turns logits into a choice”, “Truncation shapes the candidate support”, and “Seeded is not batch invariant”, about five pages together. Then read the “Chat templates are a model ABI” subsection inside Chapter 7, “Supervised fine-tuning makes the first policy”, about three pages. The first backfill explains sampling and the limit of determinism; the second explains how messages become the token sequence a model actually receives.

12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
12.3 The wire format is semantics
A support assistant asks for order O-0042. The model proposes lookup_order, the application runs it, and the database returns {"status":"shipped"}. A developer appends that result as a plain user message: “The order shipped.” Sometimes the model answers. Sometimes it calls the tool again. Sometimes it invents a delivery date. The database and model are both functioning; the conversation is malformed.
The missing fact is not English phrasing. A tool result is a typed observation correlated to a particular proposal ID. When the caller discards the type and ID, it changes the meaning of the transcript. The wire format is semantics, not plumbing.
A common chat-completions request is an HTTP POST to /v1/chat/completions. Its body names a model and carries an ordered messages list. It may also carry sampling controls, an output schema, and tool declarations. The response contains one or more choices; each choice has an assistant message and a finish reason. Usage fields report token accounting. A model server can expose this shape without using any vendor SDK.
The chapter uses that OpenAI-compatible shape as a teaching language because local servers implement it and its key ideas transfer: ordered input, typed output parts, correlation identifiers, a terminal reason, and usage. “Compatible” does not mean identical behavior. Provider deltas belong in the dated section; the durable spine depends only on those five ideas.
The reference artifact starts with chat() in code/ch12/chat_api.py. The function constructs JSON, sends raw HTTP, and extracts the fields the caller must inspect:
# code/ch12/chat_api.py — changed region: chat request and response
body = {
    "model": model,
    "messages": messages,
    "temperature": temperature,
    "top_p": top_p,
    "max_tokens": max_tokens,
}
endpoint = f"{base_url.rstrip('/')}/v1/chat/completions"
wire = (transport or _http_transport)(endpoint, body)
choice = wire["choices"][0]
return {
    "message": choice["message"],
    "finish_reason": choice.get("finish_reason"),
    "usage": wire.get("usage", {}),
    "request_id": wire.get("id"),
}
The production seam uses httpx.post; the test seam accepts a function with the same URL-and-body contract. This is dependency inversion at one boundary, not a provider abstraction layer. Chapter 26 owns gateways, routing, fallback, and multi-provider ports.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




chat_api.py after chat()
46 lines
Every call exposes the assistant message, finish reason, usage, and request ID instead of returning text alone.




The SDK is not the API. An SDK can add retries, object models, streaming helpers, and authentication, but the network still carries a versioned contract. Inspecting that contract once makes later framework behavior easier to debug.

12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
12.4 What you will build






TipOne typed model boundary



You will build a strict-schema refund-ticket extractor and a hand-rolled single-tool round trip rooted at chat_api.py. The same functions can use raw HTTP against a local OpenAI-compatible server or a deterministic transport in CI.
The offline experiment in chapter_build.py sends 50 messy tickets through three simulated guarantee levels, applies bounded application repair, and executes 20 lookup_order turns. It measures schema-validity rate, schema-valid but business-invalid records, repair frequency, tokens per success, and tool-loop termination. render_metrics.py generates the numeric SVG from those measurements.
Success means that every accepted extraction passes Draft 2020-12 validation and the order-existence rule; every tool proposal receives exactly one result carrying the same call ID; no streamed proposal becomes actionable before both call-close and response-terminal events; and all 20 tool loops terminate.


This build is one boundary grown by mechanism. It is not an agent loop. The model may propose one tool once; Chapter 16 adds repeated observation and stopping, while Chapter 17 adds approval, authorization, and a production tool harness.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
12.5 Messages compile into tokens
An API message is structured input. Its role says who or what produced the turn; its content may be text or a list of typed blocks. Four roles are useful in the chat-completions teaching shape:

system carries caller-controlled operating instructions when the surface supports that role;
user carries user-originated input;
assistant preserves model output, including tool-call proposals;
tool carries an application-produced result correlated to one assistant proposal.

Do not assume that message["content"] is always a string. An assistant message can have content: null and a tool_calls array. A multimodal message can contain text and media parts. A refusal may arrive in a field distinct from ordinary text. Walking typed parts is more robust than taking “the first string that looks like an answer.”
Roles are not words the network reads by magic. Before inference, the server applies the checkpoint’s chat template: a tokenizer-owned program that renders messages, tools, and control metadata into one token sequence. A conceptual rendering might surround each turn with role markers and append an assistant-start marker. Another checkpoint may use different markers, whitespace, order, or tool syntax. Supplying the wrong template is an input-distribution change even when the visible messages are identical.
Figure Figure 12.1 answers the question hidden by a convenient API: what happens between a JSON body and a typed response?

flowchart LR
    J["JSON messages, tools, and controls"] --> T["Pinned chat template"]
    T --> I["Tokenizer: token IDs"]
    I --> M(["Model logits"])
    M --> S["Sampling and structural masks"]
    S --> D["Detokenizer and tool parser"]
    D --> P["Typed assistant parts"]
    P --> C{"Caller checks finish reason and types"}





flowchart LR
    J["JSON messages, tools, and controls"] --> T["Pinned chat template"]
    T --> I["Tokenizer: token IDs"]
    I --> M(["Model logits"])
    M --> S["Sampling and structural masks"]
    S --> D["Detokenizer and tool parser"]
    D --> P["Typed assistant parts"]
    P --> C{"Caller checks finish reason and types"}




Figure 12.1: How does a structured chat request become the token continuation returned to the caller?





The invariant is that the template and tokenizer are part of the model’s application binary interface. Pin them with the model revision, render a golden conversation in tests, and reconcile local counts with server-reported usage. A message list by itself does not determine the prompt tokens.
The artifact’s count_tokens() deliberately delegates to the real tokenizer rather than approximating characters:
# code/ch12/chat_api.py — changed region: count_tokens
token_ids = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
    messages,
    tools=tools,
    tokenize=True,
    add_generation_prompt=True,
)
return len(token_ids)
Some templates support assistant prefilling: the caller supplies a partial final assistant turn and asks the model to continue it. This can steer a prefix, but it is not a portable structured-output guarantee. A template may distinguish “start a new assistant message” from “continue the final message,” and a hosted surface may reject prefilling. Use a native schema feature for structure and treat prefill support as a dated capability.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




chat_api.py after count_tokens()
10 lines
Token budgeting uses the same tokenizer and chat template that construct the model input.

12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
section: 12.6 Sampling controls belong to the request contract
text:
12.6 Sampling controls belong to the request contract
The caller does not merely send prose. It chooses a decoding policy. Chapter 9 derived that policy; this section records the API consequence.
For logits z_i and temperature T>0, the next-token distribution is

p_i(T)=\frac{\exp(z_i/T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j/T)}.

Smaller T concentrates probability; larger T spreads it. Nucleus sampling then keeps the smallest probability-ranked prefix whose cumulative mass reaches top_p, and renormalizes. Thus top_p=0.7 does not keep 70 percent of the vocabulary. It may retain two tokens, two hundred, or one, depending on the distribution.
Put these values in versioned request configuration rather than hiding them in prompt prose. They affect behavior, replay evidence, and evaluation:








Control
What it controls
What it does not guarantee




temperature
relative sharpness before sampling
factuality or identical future output


top_p
eligible probability mass
a fixed number of candidates


maximum output tokens
generation budget
task completion or valid closing syntax


stop sequence
termination on a matching sequence
a valid object or semantic stopping condition


seed, when exposed
pseudorandom draws under some conditions
identity across model, hardware, kernel, or batch changes


schema constraint
structurally legal continuations
correct field values or authorization



Always read finish_reason. A normal stop means the server observed one of its terminal conditions. A tool-call finish means the model is waiting for observations. A length finish means the configured output budget interrupted generation; accepting a partially emitted object as complete is an application bug. A refusal or content-filter outcome is also not ordinary text.
temperature=0 commonly selects a highest-scoring token, but it is not a system invariant. Model aliases move. Chat templates change. Parallel reductions can perturb nearly tied logits. Dynamic batching can select a different legal kernel. Record the exact model revision when available, template and schema versions, endpoint, controls, request ID, and provider fingerprint when exposed. For hermetic CI, replay a scripted transport and assert types and invariants. For live drift, compare a versioned evaluation distribution rather than one golden sentence.
Token accounting follows the same discipline. Compare the local rendered count with the response’s prompt-token usage. A delta often exposes a template mismatch, server-added tool instructions, or different tokenizer revision. Budgeting by characters hides all three.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
section: 12.7 Structured output is a spectrum of guarantees
text:
12.7 Structured output is a spectrum of guarantees
“The model returns JSON” can describe four different contracts. Ordering them prevents a syntactic convenience from becoming a false business guarantee.









Level
Mechanism
Guarantee
Typical failure




1
prompt-only instruction
none beyond model behavior
prose fences, malformed JSON, wrong shape


2
JSON mode
syntactically parseable JSON
missing fields, extra fields, invalid enums


3
schema-constrained decoding
conformance to the engine’s supported schema subset
unsupported schema, legal but wrong value


4
application validation
checks implemented and versioned by your code
a missing business rule in your validator



At level 1, the model may answer “Here is the result” and then an object. A tolerant parser can extract that object, but tolerance is not enforcement. At level 2, every response may parse while currency: "usd" violates the application enum. JSON syntax knows objects and strings; it does not know the refund contract.
At level 3, a decoding engine converts the schema into a grammar or state machine. Before each token, it masks continuations that cannot lead to a legal output. The model still scores the remaining tokens. If both O-0042 and nonexistent O-9042 fit a string pattern, the grammar permits both. Constrained decoding therefore moves validation earlier without turning a language model into a database.
The artifact sends a strict JSON-Schema request using the common chat-completions shape:
# code/ch12/chat_api.py — changed region: schema_response_format
return {
    "type": "json_schema",
    "json_schema": {
        "name": name,
        "schema": schema,
        "strict": True,
    },
}
Level 4 is different in kind. It runs inside the application release boundary. The caller chooses the JSON Schema draft and validator version, checks values against current state, decides whether a retry is safe, and returns a typed failure when the attempt budget ends. Provider-side enforcement can reduce how often that layer rejects; it cannot replace ownership of it.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




chat_api.py after schema_response_format()
8 lines
The request distinguishes JSON syntax from one named, strict application schema.

12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
section: 12.8 Constrained decoding has coverage and quality costs
text:
12.8 Constrained decoding has coverage and quality costs
A JSON Schema is a language; a decoding engine implements some of that language. Those sets are not automatically equal. Recursion, references, unions, numeric bounds, string formats, regular-expression dialects, and interactions among object keywords require different compiler and runtime support. An application schema can be valid under Draft 2020-12 yet rejected by a provider, accepted but only partially enforced, or compiled differently by another engine.
Call this the schema-coverage gap. Test it at deployment time with the exact schema and engine. A portable design has two explicit targets: the canonical application schema used by the validator, and a provider schema compiled from it. The compiler may flatten references or move a bound to application validation, but it must report lost constraints. Silently deleting keywords creates a guarantee gap no test can name.
Constrained decoding also changes the distribution. If C(x_{<t}) is the set of tokens that remain structurally legal after prefix x_{<t}, a masked distribution is

p_i^{C}=\frac{p_i\mathbf{1}[i\in C(x_{<t})]}
{\sum_j p_j\mathbf{1}[j\in C(x_{<t})]}.

Every symbol has a concrete role: p_i is the model’s unconstrained probability for token i; the indicator is one only for legal tokens; the denominator renormalizes surviving mass. If the intended answer lies outside the grammar because the schema is wrong, masking makes it unreachable. If the model planned to reason in free text before writing an object, a grammar that requires { immediately removes that route.
This is the constraint tax, but its sign is workload-dependent. A 2024 study reported reasoning degradation under restrictive formats. Later critiques argued that prompt and task construction confounded format with reasoning and showed benefits from separating unconstrained reasoning from constrained answers. The engineering conclusion is not that constraints always hurt or always help. Measure semantic task quality, latency, compile time, first-request warmup, schema coverage, and validity together.
Three patterns are useful:

For extraction and classification, constrain the final object directly and score field correctness, not only parse rate.
For reasoning-heavy work, let the model reason in a permitted region and constrain only the answer region, or use a second extraction call.
For tool proposals, keep the argument schema small and validate it again before policy and execution.

The mask can guarantee a structural property only within the engine’s implemented subset. It cannot guarantee that the schema expresses the product’s intent.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
section: 12.9 Application validation is the guarantee you own
text:
12.9 Application validation is the guarantee you own
The application boundary has two jobs. Structural validation checks types, required fields, enum membership, patterns, ranges, and extra properties. Semantic validation checks facts and policy that the schema cannot know: whether an order exists, whether the extracted amount matches the ticket, whether the actor may refund it, and whether the currency belongs to that account.
The reference artifact uses Draft202012Validator, gathers specific complaints, and feeds only those complaints back within a fixed attempt budget. It never retries forever:
# code/ch12/chat_api.py — changed region: bounded extract loop
for attempt in range(1, max_attempts + 1):
    result = invoke(
        transcript,
        response_format=schema_response_format(
            "refund_decision", schema
        ),
    )
    tokens += int(result.get("usage", {}).get("total_tokens", 0))
    text = result["message"].get("content") or ""
    try:
        value = parse_object(text)
        complaint = _complaint(value, validator, semantic_check)
    except ValueError as exc:
        complaint = str(exc)
    if complaint is None:
        return Extraction(value=value, attempts=attempt, total_tokens=tokens)
    transcript.extend([
        {"role": "assistant", "content": text},
        {"role": "user", "content": (
            f"The candidate failed validation: {complaint}. "
            "Return only one object matching the supplied schema."
        )},
    ])
raise StructuredOutputFailed(f"no valid object after {max_attempts} attempts")
The important behavior is observable: each rejection produces one exact complaint, each new attempt adds token cost, and exhaustion becomes StructuredOutputFailed rather than a half-valid dictionary.
Figure Figure 12.2 shows where trust ends. The model may repair a candidate; only code may accept it.

flowchart TD
    R["Receive typed assistant content"] --> P{"JSON object parses?"}
    P -->|"no"| E["Create specific complaint"]
    P -->|"yes"| S{"Canonical schema passes?"}
    S -->|"no"| E
    S -->|"yes"| B{"Business rules pass?"}
    B -->|"no"| E
    B -->|"yes"| A(["Accept typed value"])
    E --> Q{"Attempt budget remains?"}
    Q -->|"yes"| M["Append candidate + complaint"]
    M --> R
    Q -->|"no"| F(["StructuredOutputFailed"])





flowchart TD
    R["Receive typed assistant content"] --> P{"JSON object parses?"}
    P -->|"no"| E["Create specific complaint"]
    P -->|"yes"| S{"Canonical schema passes?"}
    S -->|"no"| E
    S -->|"yes"| B{"Business rules pass?"}
    B -->|"no"| E
    B -->|"yes"| A(["Accept typed value"])
    E --> Q{"Attempt budget remains?"}
    Q -->|"yes"| M["Append candidate + complaint"]
    M --> R
    Q -->|"no"| F(["StructuredOutputFailed"])




Figure 12.2: Where does a bounded repair loop stop trusting model output?





The loop retries a model call, not an external effect. Extraction is safe to recompute because it changes no account state. If a model produced a tool proposal that might already have executed, replay requires an idempotency record; Chapter 26 owns that durable-effect machinery.
Repair is not automatically the best fallback. A syntax error may be locally repairable. An unknown order ID may require clarification or a fresh state read. A refusal should remain a typed refusal, not be bullied through repeated prompts. Treat the complaint class as a routing signal.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




chat_api.py after extract()
53 lines
No extraction crosses the boundary until parsing, canonical schema, and optional semantic checks all pass within a finite budget.

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
section: 12.10 Schemas should make mistakes hard to express
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
section: 12.11 A tool call is a proposal, not an execution
text:
12.11 A tool call is a proposal, not an execution
A tool declaration tells the model which action shape it may propose. It does not grant credentials, call a function, or authorize an effect. One complete tool-calling round trip requires at least two model requests:

The application sends messages plus tool declarations.
The model returns an assistant turn containing a call ID, tool name, and serialized arguments. Its finish reason indicates tool_calls in this teaching shape.
The application parses and validates arguments, applies policy, and either executes or refuses.
The application appends the original assistant turn and a typed tool result carrying the same call ID.
The application sends the second model request; the model can now answer from the observation.

Figure Figure 12.3 places authority on the only arrow that can reach the tool.

sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application
    participant API as Model API
    participant Tool as Application tool
    App->>API: request 1: messages + declaration
    API-->>App: assistant tool_call id=call_0042, arguments as JSON string
    App->>App: parse, validate, authorize
    App->>Tool: lookup_order(order_id="O-0042")
    Tool-->>App: status="shipped"
    App->>API: request 2: assistant turn + tool result id=call_0042
    API-->>App: final assistant text, finish=stop





sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application
    participant API as Model API
    participant Tool as Application tool
    App->>API: request 1: messages + declaration
    API-->>App: assistant tool_call id=call_0042, arguments as JSON string
    App->>App: parse, validate, authorize
    App->>Tool: lookup_order(order_id="O-0042")
    Tool-->>App: status="shipped"
    App->>API: request 2: assistant turn + tool result id=call_0042
    API-->>App: final assistant text, finish=stop




Figure 12.3: Who actually invokes a tool, and how is its result correlated to the proposal?





The invariant is worth stating without qualification: the provider never calls your application tool. The application owns the credentials and the only execution arrow. A managed provider-side tool is a different architecture and must be labeled as such.
In chat completions, function arguments commonly arrive as a JSON string inside tool_calls[*].function.arguments. Parse at the boundary. A truncated string should raise near the response, not travel two layers until a handler fails on the wrong type. Verify that the proposed name is on the allowed list and validate the parsed object against that tool’s schema before invoking a handler.
The artifact’s run_tool_turn() admits exactly one proposal, executes one injected handler, and echoes the identifier:
# code/ch12/chat_api.py — changed region: one tool-call round trip
calls = first["message"].get("tool_calls", [])
if len(calls) != 1:
    raise ValueError("this boundary permits exactly one tool proposal")
call = calls[0]
arguments = json.loads(call["function"]["arguments"])
argument_errors = list(
    Draft202012Validator(
        tool["function"]["parameters"]
    ).iter_errors(arguments)
)
if argument_errors:
    raise ValueError(
        f"tool arguments failed validation: {argument_errors[0].message}"
    )
output = handler(**arguments)
result_message = {
    "role": "tool",
    "tool_call_id": call["id"],
    "content": json.dumps(output, sort_keys=True),
}
assert_all_calls_answered(calls, [result_message])
return invoke(
    [*messages, first["message"], result_message],
    tools=[tool],
    parallel_tool_calls=False,
)
Preserve the complete assistant tool-call turn. Reconstructing only its visible name and arguments can drop provider metadata needed on the next request. Treat tool results as untrusted observations when they can contain external text; Chapter 24 develops indirect prompt injection and protocol-neutral action security.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




chat_api.py after run_tool_turn()
40 lines
Only application code invokes the handler, and the second request answers every admitted proposal ID exactly once.

12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
section: 12.12 Forcing, parallel calls, and streaming change the boundary
text:
12.12 Forcing, parallel calls, and streaming change the boundary
The common tool_choice control changes which proposal shapes the decoder may emit:



Choice
Intended contract




auto
model may answer or propose an allowed tool


required
model must propose at least one allowed tool


named tool
model must propose that particular tool


none
model must not propose a tool



Use forcing to express a route contract, not to rescue a poor tool description. A classification endpoint may require one classifier tool. A conversational answer route may use auto. A stage after an irreversible effect may use none to prevent another proposal. Current spellings and model support belong to capability tests.
Parallel tool calls are parallel proposals. Each carries its own ID. Execute independent reads concurrently only after validating all calls and preserving their IDs. Do not match results by list position: completion order can differ from proposal order. Return one result per ID, including typed errors. Two proposed payments are not safe merely because the model emitted them in one response; concurrency policy and effect idempotency remain code responsibilities.
Streaming splits one assistant turn into deltas. Tool argument strings can arrive across chunks: {"order_ followed by id":"O-0042"}. A prefix may be invalid JSON because it is incomplete; a later prefix may parse by coincidence while the server has not committed the turn. This creates two boundaries:

parseable means a closed call block contains decodable arguments;
actionable means the whole response reached a valid terminal event and application policy admitted the proposal.

Figure Figure 12.4 prevents those states from collapsing.

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Collecting
    Collecting --> Collecting: argument delta
    Collecting --> CallClosed: call-close event
    CallClosed --> ParseFailed: JSON decode or schema failure
    CallClosed --> Parsed: parse + schema pass
    Parsed --> Eligible: response-terminal event
    Collecting --> Incomplete: transport ends
    CallClosed --> Incomplete: response never terminal
    Eligible --> [*]
    ParseFailed --> [*]
    Incomplete --> [*]





stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Collecting
    Collecting --> Collecting: argument delta
    Collecting --> CallClosed: call-close event
    CallClosed --> ParseFailed: JSON decode or schema failure
    CallClosed --> Parsed: parse + schema pass
    Parsed --> Eligible: response-terminal event
    Collecting --> Incomplete: transport ends
    CallClosed --> Incomplete: response never terminal
    Eligible --> [*]
    ParseFailed --> [*]
    Incomplete --> [*]




Figure 12.4: When may a streamed tool proposal become eligible for application policy?





The text equivalent is short: collect bytes, observe call closure, parse and validate, then wait for response termination before exposing the proposal to policy. Any missing close or terminal event fails closed.
assemble_tool_stream() implements that rule. It accumulates by call index, remembers the provider ID, and returns parsed proposals only when every call is closed and the response is terminal. The focused test deletes the terminal event and expects IncompleteToolStream.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




chat_api.py after stream assembly
25 lines
Parseability alone cannot make an interrupted streamed proposal actionable.
