12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
section: 12.13 Errors are observations with different owners
text:
12.13 Errors are observations with different owners
“Retry on error” is not a policy until the error has a class and an owner. A transport timeout, rejected HTTP request, truncated generation, refusal, invalid argument string, and tool failure describe different states.








Observation
What knows enough to handle it
Safe next move at this boundary




connection timeout before a response
application transport
retry the model call under an outer budget


HTTP 429 or retryable 5xx
application transport
bounded retry or route decision; Chapter 26 owns full policy


HTTP 400 schema rejection
application deployment/configuration
stop; compile or fix the provider schema


finish_reason: length
application plus model
retry generation without claiming an effect occurred


refusal/content filter
product policy
preserve a typed refusal or escalate through an approved path


arguments fail JSON/schema validation
application model boundary
bounded repair, typed tool error, or reject


tool returns a domain error
tool handler
return a correlated, structured error observation when useful


tool effect status is unknown
durable effect system
do not ask the model to guess; reconcile by idempotency record



Suppose a streamed proposal stops after {"order_id":"O-00. The JSON decoder raises. That is useful proximity: the failure occurs before a handler sees anything. If the model emits a complete object but the response lacks a terminal event, the stream assembler still fails closed. If the tool ran and the network failed before its result was recorded, this chapter cannot safely retry the effect; that is precisely why production runtimes need durable execution.
Some errors belong in the next model observation. “Order not found” may help the model ask for a corrected ID. “Actor is not authorized” should remain a policy denial and may be summarized without exposing sensitive details. A provider authentication failure, timeout, or internal stack trace does not help the model reason; code owns it.
The artifact’s small classify_failure() makes only this chapter’s distinction. It does not implement backoff, deadlines, hedging, circuit breakers, or fallback. Those mechanisms interact with cost, overload, model behavior, and externally visible effects, so Chapter 26 treats them together.
The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) and related tool-use scores are evaluated as part of the broader statistical evaluation system in Chapter 22; one aggregate score is not a substitute for testing the exact schemas, correlations, refusals, and failure paths used by an application.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
12.14 Landscape 2026 (dated)

Verified on 2026-07-19. Provider surfaces now converge on typed tool proposals and structured outputs while differing in transcript rules and state ownership. The table is an interoperability checklist, not a normalized contract.









Surface
Instruction and conversation shape
Tool proposal and result
State detail that bites




OpenAI Chat Completions
ordered messages; system/developer behavior depends on model and reference
tool_calls; function arguments are serialized JSON; result commonly uses a tool message with tool_call_id
caller resends transcript; inspect finish_reason and usage


OpenAI Responses
typed input/output items rather than only chat messages
function-call and function-call-output items correlated by call ID
can be stateless or chained by reference; stored-state choices affect replay and data handling


Anthropic Messages
system instruction is outside the messages array; content is typed blocks
assistant tool_use contains object input; results are tool_result blocks inside the next user message and refer to tool_use_id
results for parallel calls belong together in that next user message, before extra text; stop_reason names differ


Gemini Interactions / Generate Content
typed steps or content parts, depending on surface
function-call arguments are objects and results correlate by call ID on current surfaces
stateful interactions can carry state by reference; stateless reasoning/tool turns must preserve returned thought signatures exactly


vLLM OpenAI-compatible server
Chat Completions and Responses are exposed for supported model classes
named, required, auto, and none tool modes have model/parser/backend conditions
chat template, tool parser, reasoning parser, structured-output backend, and supported fields are deployment configuration



OpenAI’s current Structured Outputs guide distinguishes schema-conforming output from ordinary JSON and documents supported-schema constraints; its function-calling guide, Chat API reference, and Responses migration guide define the live field shapes. Anthropic’s tool-use implementation guide documents content-block ordering and tool-result correlation. Google’s function-calling guide and thought-signature guide document current state and signature rules. vLLM’s OpenAI-compatible server, tool-calling, and structured-output pages document the local serving path.
No provider SDK appears in the reference artifact. That choice keeps the wire visible; it is not a recommendation to avoid maintained clients in production. A production adapter should preserve provider-native metadata, refusals, reasoning signatures, and usage even when it maps them into an application contract. vLLM has exposed continue_final_message for template-level assistant continuation, while hosted prefill support has changed across model generations; neither should be treated as the structured-output contract.
Verify live: endpoint and field names; current model support; role and instruction placement; content-part types; function-argument string versus object encoding; strict-schema subset and recursion/reference limits; additionalProperties and required-field rules; finish/stop reasons; refusal representation; parallel-result ordering; streaming terminal events; tool/parser launch flags; prefill support; state storage defaults, retention, regions, quotas, pricing, and deprecations.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
12.15 Build
Install the two chapter-local dependencies and run the deterministic build from the newbook directory:
python -m pip install -r code/ch12/requirements.txt
python code/ch12/chapter_build.py
python code/ch12/render_metrics.py --output assets/figures/ch12-structured-validity.svg
python -m pytest tests/test_ch12_api_surface.py -q
The fixture contains 50 synthetic support tickets. Each ticket includes a traceable ticket ID, order ID, amount, currency, and messy prose. Three deterministic candidate generators stand in for prompt-only, JSON-mode, and schema-constrained behavior. They are not model measurements; they create known failures so the application boundary can be inspected.








Guarantee stage
Schema-valid outputs
What remains wrong




prompt only
38 / 50 (76%)
lower-case enums and wrapper text cause contract failures


JSON-mode proxy
46 / 50 (92%)
four objects contain an extra property


schema-constrained proxy
50 / 50 (100%)
five legal-looking order IDs do not exist


application-validated extraction
50 / 50 (100%)
all values also pass the order-existence rule



The important comparison is not 92 versus 100 percent. It is the five records that are structurally perfect and operationally false. Figure Figure 12.5 hatches that remainder so schema conformance cannot visually masquerade as correctness.






Figure 12.5: How do stronger output guarantees change structural validity, and what error survives schema enforcement? Bars show schema-valid records from a deterministic 50-ticket fixture. The hatched portion of the schema-constrained bar marks five schema-valid but nonexistent order IDs. These are synthetic CPU results, not measurements of a language model or provider.



The application repair loop ran on the JSON-mode candidates. Four of 50 records required a second call, so the repair-invocation rate was 8 percent. The deterministic accounting was 46.32 tokens per successful extraction. That number has fixture units: it proves the code adds the cost of each attempt, not what a live model will cost.
The tool half of the build issued 20 status questions. Every first response proposed exactly one lookup_order call; every application result echoed the proposal ID exactly once; and all 20 second responses terminated with an ordinary stop. The tests also delete a result ID, duplicate one, exhaust the extraction budget, and remove the terminal stream event. Each deliberate break fails at the boundary that owns it.
To use a local server, start a tool-capable model under an OpenAI-compatible implementation using its current chat template and tool-parser instructions. Then bind the raw function:
from functools import partial
from chat_api import chat, extract, REFUND_SCHEMA
from chapter_build import make_tickets, semantic_check

invoke = partial(
    chat,
    "http://localhost:8000",
    "YOUR_LOCAL_MODEL_ID",
)
ticket = make_tickets()[0]
result = extract(
    invoke,
    [{"role": "user", "content": ticket["text"]}],
    REFUND_SCHEMA,
    semantic_check=semantic_check,
)
print(result.value)
Replace the fixture ticket and semantic_check with your input and application-state lookup. A live run must pin the checkpoint, tokenizer, template, server revision, parser/backend flags, schema digest, controls, and seed when supported. Report field accuracy, schema validity, semantic validity, latency distribution, repair rate, and tokens per accepted record. Do not compare the fixture percentages to a live model.






WarningHonesty boundary



The default transport is a scripted fault fixture. Its HTTP body, message ordering, schema validation, bounded repair, tool-call parsing, call-ID correlation, stream terminal rule, metrics, and tests are real. Its candidate quality, token counts, and success rates estimate no checkpoint or provider. A live local server uses the same chat() seam, but model quality and parser compatibility must be evaluated on your own corpus.

12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
12.16 What endures, what changes
What endures. Messages are structured inputs that a pinned template compiles into tokens. Sampling controls are versioned caller policy, not correctness guarantees. Output guarantees form a ladder: prompt behavior, JSON syntax, engine-supported schema conformance, and application validation. Only the last layer can combine canonical schema with current business state. Tool calls are proposals; application code parses, validates, authorizes, executes, and returns one result per call ID. Streaming separates parseability from eligibility. Finish reasons and typed failures are part of the contract.
What changes. Endpoint names, role spellings, item types, strict-schema subsets, tool-choice modes, function-argument encodings, stop reasons, prefill support, thought-signature rules, state-by-reference features, vLLM parsers, structured-generation engines, SDK conveniences, quotas, prices, and model capabilities will move. Keep those details in adapter tests and the dated landscape. Removing the dated section leaves a complete boundary design.
Chapter 13 builds context on top of messages. Chapters 16 and 17 turn the one-tool round trip into a bounded agent loop and an authorized harness without changing the propose-versus-execute invariant learned here.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
12.17 Exercises

Reconcile token counts. Load a tokenizer whose checkpoint publishes a chat template. Count one transcript with count_tokens(), call the matching local server, and compare with prompt-token usage. Add a tool declaration and one assistant tool-call turn. Explain every delta by rendered tokens rather than by characters.
Expose nucleus support. Request or compute next-token log probabilities for a small prompt. Find a distribution where top_p=0.7 retains at least two tokens. Show the sorted probabilities, crossing token, retained cumulative mass, and renormalized distribution. Explain why the result is not “70 percent of the vocabulary.”
Break correlation on purpose. Extend the fixture to propose two independent reads. First omit one result; then return one call ID twice; then return correct results in reverse completion order. Make the first two cases fail and the third succeed. State why position is not identity.
Audit a schema-coverage gap. Add recursion, references, numeric bounds, string formats, and a nested union to the canonical application schema one at a time. Compile each against your local engine and one hosted surface if available. Produce a matrix of accepted, enforced, ignored, and rejected keywords. Keep every lost constraint in application validation.
Interrupt the stream. Capture or synthesize a streamed tool call. Delete an argument delta, the call-close event, and the response-terminal event in three separate tests. Attempt to parse each intermediate buffer, then prove that no variant becomes eligible for execution. Add one complete stream with two call indices and correlate by ID.
Measure the constraint tax. On a reasoning task with a machine-checkable answer, compare direct constrained output with free reasoning followed by constrained extraction. Hold model, examples, and total token budget as constant as possible. Report answer accuracy, schema validity, latency, generated tokens, and failures by class; do not select a winner from parse rate alone.
Defend the application validator. A reviewer proposes deleting it because the provider now enforces the schema. Use the build’s five nonexistent order IDs to specify the counterexample. Then write the evidence that could reduce—but not erase—the validator: provider conformance tests, semantic-error rate, validator latency, repair cost, drift alerts, and a rollback condition.

title: 12  The API Surface: Messages, Structured Outputs, and Tool Calling [F]
text:
12.18 Notes and sources
Hugging Face chat-template documentation explains how structured messages are rendered with apply_chat_template and why generation prompts are template-specific. JSON Schema Draft 2020-12 defines the canonical schema language; the Python jsonschema documentation documents the versioned validator used by the build.
Let Me Speak Freely? studies task-quality changes under format restrictions; dottxt’s “Say What You Mean” response challenges its prompt and evaluation setup and motivates separating reasoning from the constrained answer. XGrammar develops grammar execution for efficient structured generation, while XGrammar 2 targets more dynamic agentic structures. JSONSchemaBench separates schema coverage, efficiency, and output quality across real-world schemas. Together they support measuring constraints as an inference mechanism rather than assuming a universal quality effect.
The dated provider documentation is linked inside Landscape 2026. Chapter 22 owns benchmark construction and judge validity; Chapter 24 owns hostile tool-result content; Chapter 26 owns retry, fallback, and durable-effect policy.

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
Assumed: neural-network fundamentals, basic Python, and the API vocabulary from Chapter 12: messages, tool descriptions, output schemas, and token usage.
Route A: Chapters 3, 7, 9, 10, and 12 established long-context behavior, behavior specifications, decoding, serving, and the caller-model boundary. This chapter decides what the caller should put on that boundary.
Not required: a provider account, an agent framework, a vector database, or a production tokenizer. The lab is deterministic and offline; its whitespace-token proxy is stated wherever a number depends on it.







NoteRoute B backfill — required, about seven pages



Route B arrives here from Chapter 12. Before continuing, read Chapter 10, “Prefix caching and cache economics”, about four pages, and Chapter 3, “Advertised context is not effective context”, about three pages. Chapter 10 supplies exact-prefix reuse and its write/read economics. Chapter 3 supplies the distinction between admitted tokens and tokens a model uses reliably. This chapter applies both facts at the caller side; it does not rederive their serving or attention mechanisms.

13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
section: 13.3 Prompting becomes engineering when the change is measured
text:
13.3 Prompting becomes engineering when the change is measured
On Friday afternoon, a ticket extractor begins routing delivery problems to refunds. Its prompt changed that morning: one adjective became another in a paragraph that had been edited dozens of times. The team can show the current prompt and the current failures. It cannot show which edit caused the regression, what cases improved, which model revision ran, or whether the context around the prompt also changed. Reverting the adjective appears to help five hand-picked examples, so the team ships the revert. On Monday, another slice fails.
That is not a wording problem. It is an experimental-design problem.
A prompt is executable configuration applied to a particular model and input distribution. A useful prompt record is therefore not a string. It is a tuple such as

P=(m,v,i,d,s,c,g),

where m is the exact model revision, v the prompt version, i the instructions, d the demonstrations, s the output schema, c the decoding controls, and g the context-assembly policy. Its quality is meaningful only relative to an evaluation distribution D and metric M:

Q(P)=\mathbb{E}_{x\sim D}[M(P,x)].

Changing “precise” to “careful” changes P. Adding a tool, moving a timestamp, changing example order, or compacting history also changes P. If two variants are not run on the same versioned cases with the same scorer and model, the comparison is anecdote.
This framing changes the daily workflow:

Freeze a representative case set, including expensive edge cases.
Define a scorer before editing the prompt.
Change one named mechanism or search a declared candidate space.
Record per-case outputs, not only the aggregate.
Promote a variant only after held-out and safety checks.

Exact match works for a finite label. Schema validity works for shape. Unit tests can execute generated code. A rubric grader can assess an explanation, but then the grader model, rubric, calibration cases, and disagreement rate become part of the measurement system. No scorer is “just evaluation.” It encodes what the application values.
A useful failure table is more actionable than one score. Keep input ID, expected behavior, actual behavior, prompt version, model revision, context hash, and failure category. When a total rises from 82 to 86 percent, the table reveals whether four difficult cases improved or eight cases swapped correctness. Chapter 23 builds the full evaluation program. Here, the smaller lesson is enough: prompting is measurement before it is wordsmithing.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.4 What you will build






TipOne context pipeline, grown by mechanism



You will build context_pipeline.py, a deterministic facade over three connected experiments. prompt_optimizer.py owns finite prompt search, context_assembly.py owns typed selection and rendering, and context_history.py owns compaction and prefix measurement.
First, a miniature prompt optimizer evaluates four ticket-classification instructions against an exact-match golden set and keeps every losing trial. Second, a context assembler orders trust-labelled segments and a compactor reduces a synthetic 40-message debugging history while asserting that every decision and open question survives literally. Third, a 50-turn simulation crosses compaction on/off with a stable/broken prefix and records utilization, reusable-prefix fraction, and compaction events.
render_metrics.py turns that ledger into one numeric figure. The offline proxy classifier and whitespace-token counter teach control flow; their scores are not evidence about a language model. Success means a measured prompt improvement, zero lost durable-state assertions, deterministic rendering, a bounded compacted context, and four complete 50-turn ledgers.


The artifact stays one public pipeline because the concept is one build, while focused modules keep each mechanism readable. There is no prompt_v1.py, prompt_v2.py, and prompt_v3.py progression to reconcile. Each section adds one responsibility to the same facade; tests state the invariants.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
section: 13.5 Context is a constructed input, not a transcript
text:
13.5 Context is a constructed input, not a transcript
Prompt engineering usually asks, “What instruction should I write?” Context engineering asks the larger question: what should the model see on this call? For an agentic application, a conceptual context function is

C_t=f(S,T,M,R,H_t,Q_t;B),

where S is the system instruction, T the available tool descriptions, M durable remembered state, R retrieved evidence, H_t selected interaction history, Q_t the current query, and B the input budget. The semicolon matters: the budget constrains the function; it is not another source to concatenate.
The context window resembles RAM only in one limited sense: it is finite working state for the current computation. Unlike ordinary RAM, its addresses are not equally reliable, and an irrelevant resident byte is usually harmless. An irrelevant token can change the model’s answer. Tokens compete for attention, can contradict one another, can imitate instructions, and have position-dependent usefulness. The objective is not to fill the window. It is to choose the smallest high-signal input that makes the target behavior likely.
Chapter 3 separated accepted context from effective context. A million admitted tokens do not imply uniform use of a million tokens. “Lost in the Middle” found position sensitivity on its tested multi-document QA and key-value tasks; Chroma’s later context-rot experiments found that performance on tested models and tasks often degraded as input grew, even under controlled constructions. Neither result gives a universal safe percentage. Together they invalidate “send everything because it fits” as a design rule.
Figure Figure 13.1 shows the application boundary. The model does not fetch this state by intuition; an assembler chooses, labels, orders, and budgets it before the API call.

flowchart LR
    S["System contract"] --> A["Deterministic context assembler"]
    T["Tool descriptions"] --> A
    M["Durable state references"] --> A
    R["Retrieved evidence"] --> A
    H["Selected history"] --> A
    Q["Current query"] --> A
    B["Token budget"] -. "constrains" .-> A
    A --> O["Ordered, trust-labelled model input"]
    O --> L["Context ledger"]





flowchart LR
    S["System contract"] --> A["Deterministic context assembler"]
    T["Tool descriptions"] --> A
    M["Durable state references"] --> A
    R["Retrieved evidence"] --> A
    H["Selected history"] --> A
    Q["Current query"] --> A
    B["Token budget"] -. "constrains" .-> A
    A --> O["Ordered, trust-labelled model input"]
    O --> L["Context ledger"]




Figure 13.1: What turns distributed application state into the one ordered input a model receives?





The diagram also marks an ownership boundary. Chapter 18 owns how memory is represented and updated. Chapters 14 and 15 own retrieval quality. Chapter 17 owns tool discovery and execution. This chapter consumes their outputs as typed segments and decides what enters the next call.
Think in segments, not one growing string. Each segment should carry at least a source type, trust label, content, stability flag, and priority. Production systems often also need tenant, creation time, expiry, sensitivity, provenance, and a pointer to the full source. These fields permit explicit policy: stable developer instructions precede volatile user data; stale observations can expire; a summary can be distinguished from a verbatim tool result.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
section: 13.6 Few-shot examples are data, not decoration
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.7 Structure controls parsing, not trust
Long prose makes boundaries implicit. Structural markup makes them visible to people, template code, and often the model. XML-like tags, Markdown headings, JSON objects, and explicit delimiters can distinguish instructions, examples, retrieved documents, and the current query:
<retrieved_document trust="external" source="invoice-41">
...document text...
</retrieved_document>
<task>
Answer from the documents and identify unresolved conflicts.
</task>
Use names that express semantics. <data> is weaker than <untrusted_search_result>. Close every delimiter, escape nested content, and render through one tested function. A delimiter mismatch is a serialization bug. Chapter 12’s structured-output guarantees apply to the response; this section structures the input.
Markup is not an authorization boundary. Text inside <untrusted_search_result> can still contain an instruction-like sentence, and a model can still follow it. Trust labels give the prompt and later audit code information with which to reason; they do not make hostile text inert. Chapter 24 owns prompt-injection defenses, isolation, and least privilege. Here the invariant is narrower: preserve provenance and never imply that fencing equals security.
The system prompt should operate at the right altitude. It should state purpose, non-negotiable behavior, decision policy, tool-use expectations, and output contract without encoding a giant flowchart in prose. Brittle micro-rules are difficult to reconcile; slogans are too vague to test. Chapter 7 owns behavior-specification design. This chapter renders the selected version and keeps it stable.
Prompting advice around reasoning is model-scoped. “Think step by step” historically helped some tasks and models, but it is not a durable default for every reasoning-tuned model. Some APIs expose a reasoning effort, thinking level, or budget; some models reason internally and return a summary rather than hidden tokens; some combinations constrain temperature or tool use. Treat reasoning configuration as a versioned request control. Evaluate task success, latency, input/output usage, and failure modes. Do not require a hidden chain of thought as an application contract. Ask for checkable work products—assumptions, calculations, citations, tests, or a concise rationale—when the task needs them.






NoteLandscape 2026 — reasoning controls are provider and model surfaces



As of 2026-07-19, current OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini APIs expose different controls and accounting for reasoning or thinking. Names, supported values, preservation across turns, and interactions with other parameters vary by model. The durable design is to keep a model-capability profile next to prompt configuration and gate every migration with the target evaluation suite.
Verify live: inspect the official OpenAI reasoning guide, Claude extended-thinking guide, and Gemini thinking guide for the selected model immediately before deployment. Verified: 2026-07-19.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
section: 13.8 Prompt optimization is search over a metric
text:
13.8 Prompt optimization is search over a metric
Once prompts have versions and scores, prompt optimization becomes ordinary black-box search. Let \mathcal{P} be a candidate set and \hat Q_D(P) the measured score on development cases. The simplest optimizer is

P^*=\arg\max_{P\in\mathcal{P}} \hat Q_D(P).

The candidate generator might enumerate hand-written instructions, select demonstrations, propose edits from failure traces, or combine all three. The evaluator can be exact match, executable tests, a calibrated judge, or a weighted vector of quality, latency, and cost. The loop is useful because proposals and evidence are separated.
The lab searches four instructions. Its proxy model makes two ambiguous technical tickets look like status until the instruction defines labels and says that explicit refund intent outranks malfunction, which outranks delivery status. Three candidates score 10/12; the winner scores 12/12. The result is not that these English words are universally superior. It is that a declared rule fixes two named cases under a declared classifier.
# code/ch13/prompt_optimizer.py — changed region: optimize_prompt
trials = [{"prompt": prompt, **evaluate(prompt)} for prompt in candidates]
winner = max(
    enumerate(trials),
    key=lambda pair: (pair[1]["score"], -pair[0]),
)[1]
The index term makes ties deterministic and keeps the earlier candidate. The output retains losers and their failure rows. Without the losers, a future engineer cannot tell whether the winner dominated or won on one case.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




prompt_optimizer.py complete
80 lines
Every candidate is scored by the same exact-match grader; ties are deterministic; failures and losing prompts remain inspectable.




Optimization can overfit prompts just as training can overfit weights. Searching more candidates against the same twelve cases leaks information about those cases into the chosen prompt. Keep a final set untouched, slice results by risk category, and retest after model changes. For judge-based metrics, calibrate the judge against human labels and inspect preference for length, style, self-reference, and shared model-family artifacts. For multi-objective systems, define the promotion rule before search—for example, maximize task success subject to a p95 latency ceiling and zero authorization regressions.
An optimizer without a trustworthy metric accelerates the wrong behavior. It may learn verbosity that flatters a judge, demonstrations copied from the evaluation set, or loopholes in a unit test. Automatic prompt optimization does not remove specification work; it makes specification quality more consequential.






NoteLandscape 2026 — optimizers search instructions, demonstrations, and traces



MIPRO introduced joint optimization of free-form instructions and demonstrations for multi-stage language-model programs. GEPA later used natural-language reflection over trajectories and a Pareto frontier of candidates. The GEPA paper, revised in 2026 and accepted to ICLR 2026, reports gains over GRPO and MIPROv2 on its evaluated tasks with fewer rollouts; those are paper-specific results, not a universal ranking. Framework APIs and default optimizers change faster than the metric-first loop described above.
Verify live: inspect the primary MIPRO paper, current DSPy MIPROv2 documentation, and GEPA paper before selecting an optimizer. Reproduce on the application’s own held-out tasks. Verified: 2026-07-19.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.9 Build the context window deterministically
Context assembly should behave like a compiler pass, not scattered string concatenation. The inputs are typed segments; the output is one reproducible sequence plus an audit record. If the same state, policy, query, and budget enter twice, the same bytes should leave twice.
The artifact begins with a small segment type:
# code/ch13/context_assembly.py — changed region: Segment
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Segment:
    kind: str
    trust: str
    content: str
    stable: bool = False
    priority: int = 0
    tags: tuple[str, ...] = ()
kind determines structural position. trust records who controls the content. stable identifies material that can form a reusable prefix. priority and tags support selection. This is an instructional minimum; a regulated system may require source URI, tenant, access decision, data class, effective time, expiry, and content digest.
A sensible default order is stable to volatile:

system contract and invariant policy;
stable tool or output schemas;
stable demonstrations;
selected evidence and state;
recent history;
the current query.

Ordering has three effects. It expresses precedence to the model, makes inspection predictable, and maximizes the chance that repeated leading bytes can be reused. There are exceptions: a provider’s wire format may place tools outside the message list, and a model-specific prompting guide may recommend another arrangement. Encode such variation in the renderer for that model profile, not in every caller.
The renderer sorts by a fixed table, wraps each block with kind and trust, and applies a budget. A required stable segment or query raises an error instead of disappearing. Lower-priority optional segments may be omitted, but selection should occur before rendering so the reason is visible:
# code/ch13/context_assembly.py — changed region: render_context
ordered = sorted(
    enumerate(segments),
    key=lambda pair: (ORDER[pair[1].kind], pair[0]),
)
for _, segment in ordered:
    block = f'<{segment.kind} trust="{segment.trust}">\n{segment.content}\n</{segment.kind}>'
    if used + token_count(block) > budget and not segment.stable and segment.kind != "query":
        continue
    if used + token_count(block) > budget:
        raise ValueError(f"required {segment.kind} segment exceeds the budget")
Silent truncation is particularly damaging. Removing the end may delete the user query. Removing the beginning may delete policy. Cutting a serialized tool schema midway leaves invalid structure. Budget at segment boundaries, reserve output capacity separately, and fail closed when required material cannot fit.
Production budgeting uses the exact tokenizer and chat template, as Chapter 12 demonstrated. The lab’s token_count() uses whitespace solely so the experiment is portable and its arithmetic inspectable. Never transfer its counts to a hosted endpoint. Reconcile local rendered tokens against provider usage and alert on persistent drift; a hidden template or tool-description change may be consuming the difference.
System instructions are not access control. A trust="developer" label expresses provenance to the model and logs. Authorization still belongs to executable code around tools and data. Conversely, putting user text in a system-position string does not convert it into trusted developer intent. Preserve origin through transformations.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




context_assembly.py complete
82 lines
Rendering is deterministic, stable-to-volatile, trust-labelled, and unable to silently drop required system or query material.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.10 Write, select, compress, and isolate
Four operations cover most caller-side context work. They are easier to reason about when named separately:

Write moves a durable fact or artifact outside the active window and leaves an addressable reference.
Select chooses the subset relevant to the current decision.
Compress replaces detail with a smaller representation that preserves declared invariants.
Isolate performs a noisy subtask in a separate context and returns only its result, evidence, and reference.

These moves can compose. An agent writes a test log to an artifact, selects the failed tests, isolates root-cause analysis in a worker, then compresses the result into three evidence-backed findings. Figure Figure 13.2 distinguishes what happens to information in each move.

flowchart TB
    X["Candidate information"] --> D{"Needed on this call?"}
    D -- "No, but durable" --> W["Write externally and keep a reference"]
    D -- "Yes, only a subset" --> S["Select relevant segments"]
    D -- "Yes, but too large" --> C["Compress under survival invariants"]
    D -- "Requires noisy independent work" --> I["Isolate the work and return a digest"]
    W --> A["Active context"]
    S --> A
    C --> A
    I --> A





flowchart TB
    X["Candidate information"] --> D{"Needed on this call?"}
    D -- "No, but durable" --> W["Write externally and keep a reference"]
    D -- "Yes, only a subset" --> S["Select relevant segments"]
    D -- "Yes, but too large" --> C["Compress under survival invariants"]
    D -- "Requires noisy independent work" --> I["Isolate the work and return a digest"]
    W --> A["Active context"]
    S --> A
    C --> A
    I --> A




Figure 13.2: Which operation should reduce pressure on the active context without erasing recoverability?





Write is not “remember everything.” A durable store needs its own schema, lifecycle, retrieval, and deletion policy, all owned by Chapter 18. At this boundary, write only produces a compact reference such as REF incident-91: replay bundle and trace index. A reference without enough scent to know when it matters is dead state; include a descriptive name, type, and short summary.
Select is a precision decision. Filter by task, provenance, freshness, permissions, and expected information gain—not just lexical overlap. Chapter 14 will build retrieval candidates and Chapter 15 will rank and verify them. The Chapter 13 assembler accepts their selected segments and ensures their labels and ordering survive.
Compress is lossy unless the representation is reversible. State what may be lost and what must survive. A tool transcript can often lose repeated progress output but not the exit status, error text, affected file, or command identity. A conversation summary can merge paraphrases but must not turn “do not deploy” into “deploy later.” The next compaction section builds this as an assertion.
Isolate is useful when a subtask emits many exploratory tokens or works with a different evidence set. Isolation can reduce distraction and protect the primary prefix. It also loses tacit context, adds coordination latency, and can hide errors behind a neat summary. Require a return contract: conclusion, evidence, uncertainty, artifacts, and unresolved questions. Chapter 20 handles subagent topology and economics; this section only defines the context boundary.
The operations respond to four recurring failure modes:









Failure
What happens
First diagnostic
Typical response




poisoning
an early mistaken fact or plan persists and shapes later turns
trace the first appearance and every derived decision
replace or quarantine it; preserve the correction and rationale


distraction
irrelevant detail consumes attention and changes behavior
remove one context class at a time
select fewer high-signal segments


confusion
similar tools, examples, or facts create an ambiguous choice
inspect competing names, descriptions, and demonstrations
simplify, rename, or isolate alternatives


clash
instructions or facts conflict without an explicit precedence rule
render the full ordered context with provenance
resolve in application policy; do not ask the model to guess authority



These labels organize debugging; they are not mutually exclusive diagnoses. A stale summary can poison the trace, distract from new evidence, and clash with a current instruction. Also separate them from context rot: Chapter 3 used context rot for measured performance degradation as input grows. The four labels describe application-level content failures that can occur even in a short request.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




context_assembly.py with four context moves
Focused module remains 82 lines
Every reduction returns a typed segment or reference; isolated output is labelled as a tool observation rather than silently promoted to policy.

13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
section: 13.11 Prefix stability is an application invariant
text:
13.11 Prefix stability is an application invariant
Chapter 10 explained the serving mechanism: a prefix cache can reuse previously computed KV state only for an exact compatible token prefix. Caller-side layout determines whether such a prefix exists. Semantic similarity is irrelevant. A reordered JSON schema, one whitespace-sensitive token, a changed tool description, or a timestamp near the front can end reuse at the first difference.
Put slow-changing content first and fast-changing content last. A useful layout is

\underbrace{S\Vert T\Vert E}_{\text{stable prefix}}
\Vert
\underbrace{R_t\Vert H_t\Vert Q_t}_{\text{volatile suffix}},

where S is system policy, T stable tool/schema material, E fixed examples, and the remaining segments vary by turn. Do not insert current time, request ID, user name, random nonce, or per-request feature flags before the stable prefix unless their position is semantically required. If a policy fragment changes at a different cadence, make that boundary explicit and measure the provider’s cache behavior.
Figure Figure 13.3 compares two turns. The content is nearly identical in meaning; its leading bytes are not.

sequenceDiagram
    participant A as "Turn t assembler"
    participant C as "Prefix cache"
    participant B as "Turn t+1 assembler"
    A->>C: "[stable policy][tools][history t]"
    Note over C: "Reusable entry ends after the stable leading blocks"
    B->>C: "[stable policy][tools][history t+1]"
    C-->>B: "Leading prefix hit"
    B->>C: "[new timestamp][stable policy][tools][history]"
    C-->>B: "Miss near the first differing byte"





sequenceDiagram
    participant A as "Turn t assembler"
    participant C as "Prefix cache"
    participant B as "Turn t+1 assembler"
    A->>C: "[stable policy][tools][history t]"
    Note over C: "Reusable entry ends after the stable leading blocks"
    B->>C: "[stable policy][tools][history t+1]"
    C-->>B: "Leading prefix hit"
    B->>C: "[new timestamp][stable policy][tools][history]"
    C-->>B: "Miss near the first differing byte"




Figure 13.3: Why can one volatile timestamp erase reuse for an otherwise stable request?





Measure cache economics in one unit. Let S be stable-prefix tokens, V new suffix tokens, u uncached input cost per token, and r cached-read cost per token. Ignoring the first write for a moment, a later hit costs

C_{hit}=rS+uV,

while breaking the prefix costs

C_{miss}=u(S+V).

The artifact uses illustrative units S=7{,}000, V=300, u=1, and r=0.1. Then C_{hit}=1{,}000, C_{miss}=7{,}300, and the broken-prefix call costs 7.3 times as much input work in that fixture. These are not provider prices. The example isolates a multiplier; Chapter 10’s full equation includes cache-write cost, reuse count, routing, storage, and eviction.
Log server-reported cached reads and writes where available. A local common-prefix count is a diagnostic, not proof of a server hit: model revision, tokenizer, template, adapter, tenant isolation, cache lifetime, and routing also matter. A high token hit rate can still fail to improve latency if the remaining suffix dominates preprocessing or if queueing dominates time to first token.






NoteLandscape 2026 — prompt caches share a principle, not one API



As of 2026-07-19, OpenAI documents exact-prefix matching, automatic caching above model-dependent thresholds, usage fields for cache reads and—for newer families—writes, plus explicit cache controls on supported models. Anthropic documents prompt order as tools → system → messages, automatic and explicit cache breakpoints, and invalidation when earlier cached blocks change. Google’s current Interactions API documents implicit caching for Gemini 2.5 and newer models, recommends common content first, and exposes cached-token usage. Thresholds, prices, lifetimes, and control fields differ and can change.
Verify live: inspect the official OpenAI prompt-caching guide, Claude prompt-caching guide, and Gemini context-caching guide, then confirm behavior with usage telemetry on the exact endpoint. Verified: 2026-07-19.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.12 Compact by classifying before summarizing
A long-running interaction eventually approaches its effective or admitted budget. Dropping the oldest message is simple and unsafe. Summarizing everything into one paragraph is compact and unauditable. Good compaction first classifies state by survival requirement, then transforms only what may be transformed.
A debugging trace often contains:

specifications and constraints that must remain verbatim;
decisions and their rationale;
open questions and current blockers;
unresolved errors and recent tool observations;
completed exploration that can be summarized;
bulky artifacts that can be replaced by references.

The order matters. Extract durable items before asking any summarizer to rewrite the middle. Give the compactor a schema with separate fields rather than “summarize the conversation.” Verify that identifiers, negations, numeric limits, ownership, and uncertainty survive. Keep the original transcript addressable under the product’s retention policy; compaction should not erase audit evidence merely because it leaves the active prompt.
Figure Figure 13.4 makes the survival check an explicit gate.

flowchart TD
    U["Utilization crosses a measured trigger at a clean turn boundary"] --> K["Classify specifications, decisions, open questions, errors, and references"]
    K --> P["Pin verbatim durable state"]
    K --> M["Summarize expendable middle history"]
    K --> R["Keep recent observations"]
    P --> V{"Survival assertions pass?"}
    M --> V
    R --> V
    V -- "No" --> F["Reject compaction and surface a typed failure"]
    V -- "Yes" --> N["Start the next context with a labelled compacted state"]





flowchart TD
    U["Utilization crosses a measured trigger at a clean turn boundary"] --> K["Classify specifications, decisions, open questions, errors, and references"]
    K --> P["Pin verbatim durable state"]
    K --> M["Summarize expendable middle history"]
    K --> R["Keep recent observations"]
    P --> V{"Survival assertions pass?"}
    M --> V
    R --> V
    V -- "No" --> F["Reject compaction and surface a typed failure"]
    V -- "Yes" --> N["Start the next context with a labelled compacted state"]




Figure 13.4: How should a context manager compact history without treating every token as equally disposable?





The lab triggers at 65 percent of its synthetic budget. That is an illustrative control point, not a recommendation. A real trigger should leave enough headroom for tool results, the next user turn, and output; it should be validated against effective-context quality and latency. Compact at clean boundaries when possible. Compaction during a partial tool call can separate a proposal from its result or discard an error still needed for recovery.
The synthetic trace has 40 messages containing three literal DECISION lines, two literal OPEN lines, and one ERROR. The compactor extracts those classes, adds one summary of routine messages, and retains four recent messages:
# code/ch13/context_history.py — changed region: compact_history
durable = [
    message for message in messages
    if message.startswith(("DECISION ", "OPEN ", "ERROR "))
]
recent = list(messages[-keep_recent:])
compacted = [*durable, summary, *[m for m in recent if m not in durable]]
assert_survival(messages, compacted)
assert_survival() searches for every pre-compaction decision and open question as an exact substring. Literal survival is stronger than a model grader for these fields and cheaper to debug. Other applications need typed comparisons: a set of requirements, checksums for pinned excerpts, schema validation, or executable state-machine equivalence.
Compaction can itself poison future context. A summary that says “timeout fixed” when one occurrence disappeared converts an observation into a conclusion. Record the compactor version, input and output digests, trigger reason, preserved-item counts, and any validation failures. Treat the compacted block as an application-produced summary, not as developer policy or verbatim user speech.






NoteLandscape 2026 — context editing is becoming an API feature



As of 2026-07-19, provider surfaces include server-side or SDK-assisted context-management features. Anthropic’s current beta context-editing documentation describes clearing older tool results and thinking blocks and an SDK compaction path. Such features can reduce client work, but their model coverage, defaults, beta headers, preservation rules, and usage accounting are volatile. An API feature does not remove the application’s obligation to define what must survive.
Verify live: inspect the official Claude context-editing guide and the selected provider’s conversation-state or compaction documentation. Run a survival suite containing decisions, negations, open errors, call IDs, and recent tool results before enabling it. Verified: 2026-07-19.











Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




context_history.py complete
96 lines
A 40-message trace is classified before reduction, and every literal decision and open question must survive or compaction fails.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
section: 13.13 Load context just in time and keep a ledger
text:
13.13 Load context just in time and keep a ledger
Preloading every manual, tool description, example, and project file pays input cost whether or not the call needs it. Just-in-time context keeps compact references in the active window and resolves a subset when the task provides enough scent.
If N documents average L tokens, preloading costs roughly NL input tokens per call. If selection loads k\ll N documents plus an index of I tokens, the active cost is

C_{JIT}=I+kL.

The token saving is NL-(I+kL), but selection is not free. It adds retrieval latency, can miss the needed item, and may require another model-tool round trip. JIT wins when references are searchable, the selector has high recall on important items, and avoided context cost exceeds selection cost and failure risk. A list of opaque IDs has no scent; a compact catalog with names, purposes, and constraints lets the model or application choose.
Use preloading for a small stable contract needed on nearly every call. Use JIT for a large catalog with sparse per-call use. Hybrid designs preload names and one-line descriptions, then fetch full definitions on demand. Chapter 17 applies this to tools and skills; Chapters 14 and 15 apply it to evidence. Do not implement a second retrieval pipeline inside the prompt template.
None of these decisions is operable without a context ledger. For every model call, record enough metadata to reconstruct why a segment appeared:







Field
Question it answers




model, template, prompt, and assembler versions
which executable configuration ran?


segment kind, source, trust, digest, and token count
what entered, from where, and under whose authority?


selection reason and score
why was this item chosen?


position and truncation outcome
where did it land, and what was omitted?


input budget and utilization
how close was the call to its operating envelope?


cached-read and cache-write tokens
did the expected prefix actually reuse?


compaction event and preserved-state counts
what changed between turns?


output usage, latency, cost, and evaluation outcome
what did the context buy?



Avoid logging sensitive raw content by default. Digests, typed metadata, protected replay bundles, and sampled redacted payloads can provide observability without turning the trace system into an uncontrolled copy of user data. Chapter 28 owns the complete observability design.
The artifact’s 50-turn experiment emits one row per scenario and turn. The stable system portion contains 7,000 proxy tokens; each turn adds 108. Compaction fires at the 65-percent trigger and retains a compact summary plus three recent turns. The broken-prefix variants put a changing timestamp before the stable policy. Figure Figure 13.5 plots the measured ledger rather than a hand-drawn expectation.






Figure 13.5: Across a deterministic 50-turn simulation, compaction keeps context utilization below the 65-percent trigger envelope while an unchanged leading prefix preserves almost all reusable bytes. A timestamp at the front reduces reusable leading bytes to nearly zero whether compaction is enabled or not.



Without compaction, utilization reaches 1.033 of the synthetic budget at turn 50. With compaction, its maximum is 0.6465 and compaction occurs 11 times. Across turns 2–50, the mean reusable leading-byte fraction is 0.992 without compaction and 0.983 with compaction for stable-prefix runs; it is below 0.00015 for both timestamp-first runs. A compaction event changes middle history and can reduce reuse after the stable base, but it does not destroy the base. Conversely, prefix stability does not prevent the history from overflowing. The two controls solve different constraints.
Version prompts and context policies together. A prompt registry entry that points only to an instruction string cannot reproduce selection, ordering, compaction, or tool visibility. Make model scope explicit: ticket-router/prompt-17 on one revision is not assumed equivalent on another. Chapter 26 will add registry promotion and canaries. At this point, a content hash, configuration record, ledger, and evaluation gate are enough.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.14 Build
Run the integrated experiment from the book root:
python code/ch13/context_pipeline.py \
  --output code/ch13/generated/context-metrics.json
python code/ch13/render_metrics.py \
  --output code/ch13/generated/context-ops.svg
pytest -q tests/test_ch13_context.py
The first command grows the artifact through the complete path: prompt search, 40-message compaction, cache-cost calculation, and four 50-turn simulations. The second command imports that same build and renders the figure. The third checks mechanism-level invariants without an API key.
The committed deterministic run reports:








Measurement
Result
Interpretation boundary




baseline exact-match score
10/12 (83.3%)
proxy classifier on the chapter fixture


optimized score
12/12 (100%)
candidate-selection result, not a generalization estimate


losing candidate scores
10/12, 10/12, 10/12
retained with their two failure rows


compaction size
774 → 155 proxy tokens
whitespace count on a synthetic trace


durable state lost
0 decisions; 0 open questions
literal assertions, not semantic equivalence


illustrative broken-prefix multiplier
7.3×
configured unit costs, not provider pricing


simulation rows
200
50 turns for each of four cells



Read the output in that order. The prompt score demonstrates why a metric must precede search. The compaction result demonstrates that “shorter” is incomplete unless survival is tested. The grid demonstrates that context pressure and prefix reuse are independent axes.
Try one controlled mutation at a time:

Remove PRECEDENCE from the winning prompt and inspect the two failures rather than only the score.
Delete one OPEN item from the compacted output and observe the typed assertion failure.
Move the timestamp behind the stable policy and compare reusable leading bytes.
Change the compaction trigger and plot peak utilization and event count together.

Do not tune the fixture until it looks impressive. Its value comes from causal isolation. A production successor should replace the proxy classifier with pinned model calls, the whitespace counter with the real template-aware tokenizer, the byte-prefix diagnostic with provider telemetry, and the synthetic cases with versioned development and held-out sets. The control flow and invariants remain.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.15 What endures, what changes
What endures. A prompt is executable, model-scoped configuration. Improvements require a fixed distribution and metric. Demonstrations are data and can help or hurt. Context is deliberately assembled from typed, provenance-preserving segments under a budget; it is not an unlimited transcript. Structure improves parsing but does not create trust. Write, select, compress, and isolate are distinct operations with distinct loss modes. Exact prefix reuse requires stable leading content. Compaction must classify durable state before summarizing and verify survival afterward. JIT loading trades preload cost for selection latency and miss risk. A context ledger connects every choice to quality, latency, and cost.
What changes. Model-specific prompting advice, reasoning controls, context limits, cache thresholds and prices, provider compaction features, optimizer algorithms, and framework APIs will move. Keep those facts in dated capability profiles and reverify them. Do not bake a 2026 parameter name or price into the conceptual architecture.
The next chapter takes selected external evidence as its subject. It builds retrieval and ranking rather than asking the context assembler to improvise them. The handoff is clean: Chapter 14 produces evidence candidates with provenance and scores; Chapter 13’s assembler decides how those selected candidates occupy a model call.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.16 Exercises

Break a few-shot set. Add three redundant refund demonstrations and one mislabeled ambiguous example to a real or simulated classifier. Measure zero-shot, original few-shot, and corrupted few-shot performance by label. Explain whether the failure came from label balance, boundary definition, order, or format.
Specify a context contract. For a code-review agent, define segment kinds, trust labels, stable fields, priority, expiry, and required provenance. Write three invariants that the assembler can test without calling a model, including behavior when required content exceeds the budget.
Compute cache economics. With a 12,000-token stable prefix, 600-token suffix, cached-read rate 0.2 units, uncached rate 1 unit, and first-write rate 1.25 units, calculate total cost over one through ten calls. Find the first reuse count at which caching wins, then list two reasons server telemetry may differ from this arithmetic.
Attack compaction semantically. Create a 30-turn trace containing negation, a superseded decision, two similar IDs, an unresolved error, and a tool result whose units matter. Design typed survival checks that are stronger than substring matching. Show one summary that passes a generic quality judge but fails your checks.
Choose a JIT boundary. A catalog has 400 tools averaging 180 description tokens. A compact index costs 4,000 tokens, and a call uses five tools on average. Compute preload and JIT input sizes. Then write a decision memo incorporating lookup latency, selection recall, cache reuse, and the cost of omitting the correct tool.
Design an optimization study. Propose a development set, untouched test set, metric vector, candidate generator, stopping rule, and promotion gate for a customer-support prompt. Include how you will detect judge verbosity bias and model-version overfitting. State what evidence would cause you to reject the highest aggregate score.
Operate the ledger. Extend the 50-turn experiment with a third prefix policy that changes one tool description every tenth turn. Plot utilization, reusable bytes, compaction events, and cost units. Write an alert that detects an unexpected prefix-regression without firing on planned prompt releases.
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]

title: 13  Prompting and Context Engineering [F+S]
text:
13.17 Notes and sources

Anthropic’s Effective context engineering for AI agents motivates the smallest-high-signal-context principle and discusses system prompts, tools, long-horizon tasks, compaction, and just-in-time retrieval. It is an engineering essay, so this chapter treats its prescriptions as practices to test rather than universal laws.
LangChain’s Context Engineering for Agents organizes contemporary techniques into write, select, compress, and isolate. This chapter uses that taxonomy while keeping framework implementation out of the durable spine.
Liu et al., “Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts”, and Chroma’s Context Rot support the warning that admitted length is not uniform effective context. Chapter 3 owns the detailed treatment.
Zheng et al., “When ‘A Helpful Assistant’ Is Not Really Helpful”, report the persona experiment summarized in Section Section 13.6.
Opsahl-Ong et al., “Optimizing Instructions and Demonstrations for Multi-Stage Language Model Programs”, introduce MIPRO; Agrawal et al., “GEPA: Reflective Prompt Evolution Can Outperform Reinforcement Learning”, describe reflective prompt evolution. Reported benchmark gains are attached to their evaluated tasks.
Current cache behavior is sourced from the official OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google documentation linked in the dated box. Recheck rather than copying thresholds or prices into architecture documents.

title: 14  Embeddings and RAG Systems [F+S]
text: 14.1 What you need going in
crumbs: Part IV — Programming with Models and Retrieval
14  Embeddings and RAG Systems [F+S]

title: 14  Embeddings and RAG Systems [F+S]
text:
Assumed: neural-network fundamentals, basic Python, and the caller-side ideas from Chapters 12 and 13: model requests, structured inputs, token budgets, and trust-labelled context.
Route A: Chapters 3, 9, 10, 11, 12, and 13 established effective context, hallucination and abstention, serving constraints, customization choices, API contracts, and context assembly. This chapter turns external knowledge into one measured input to that assembler.
Not required: prior information-retrieval coursework, a hosted embedding API, a vector database, or a GPU. The lab is deterministic and offline. Its hashed vectors and joint scorer expose retrieval mechanisms; they are not measurements of a trained model.







NoteRoute B backfill — required, about seven pages



Before continuing, read Chapter 9, “Hallucination is a behavior, not a hidden fact”, “Confidence needs an observable definition”, and “Abstention is a decision rule”, about five pages together. Then read Chapter 11, “Choose the smallest intervention that can change the failure”, about two pages. Chapter 9 supplies the support, calibration, and risk–coverage vocabulary used for grounded answers. Chapter 11 supplies the decision branch that asks whether retrieval is the right intervention before a team builds it.

title: 14  Embeddings and RAG Systems [F+S]
section: 14.3 RAG makes evidence an explicit stage
text:
14.3 RAG makes evidence an explicit stage
Two weeks after a support assistant launches, it confidently tells a customer that the United States refund window is 30 days. The current policy says 45. The corpus contains both documents: version 2 is retired, version 3 is active. Another user asks about error E1492; the assistant retrieves a page about asset A1492. A third asks, “What about annual plans?” after discussing cancellation, and the retriever searches those four words without the missing subject.
The team has one end-to-end quality score. It cannot answer the operational question: did the knowledge system fail to contain, find, rank, deliver, or use the evidence? Prompt edits move the score but do not identify the broken stage.
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) makes external evidence an explicit input to generation. A model checkpoint supplies parametric memory: patterns encoded in weights during training and post-training. A retrieval system supplies non-parametric memory: addressable records that can be added, versioned, filtered, cited, and retired without changing model weights. For query q, a retriever selects evidence z from corpus C, and the generator answers from both:

z_{1:k}=R(q,C), \qquad y \sim G(y\mid q,z_{1:k}).

That compact equation hides a production system. Documents must be parsed and versioned. A query may need history, filters, or rewriting. Sparse and dense indexes produce candidates. A reranker spends more computation on a shortlist. A context assembler enforces a budget and preserves provenance. The answerer cites supporting spans or abstains. Evaluation and telemetry observe every boundary.
RAG is the right branch of Chapter 11’s intervention tree when facts change faster than weights, answers require source attribution, knowledge is private or tenant-scoped, or the corpus is too large to place in every request. It is not a default wrapper for every model call. A closed-book transformation, a task already solved by supplied input, or a latency-critical classifier may gain cost and failure modes without gaining information. Fine-tuning can change behavior or teach a stable task; it is a poor database update protocol. Retrieval can expose current facts; it does not teach the model a new algorithm.
Figure Figure 14.1 is both architecture and debugging map. Each arrow is an observable contract. An answer can be fluent while any upstream contract is broken.

flowchart LR
    subgraph Offline["Offline knowledge path"]
      D["Versioned sources"] --> P["Parse + normalize"]
      P --> K["Chunk + metadata"]
      K --> I["Sparse + vector indexes"]
    end
    subgraph Online["Online answer path"]
      Q["Query + selected history"] --> U["Understand + filter"]
      U --> C["Candidate retrieval"]
      I --> C
      C --> R["Fuse + rerank"]
      R --> A["Budgeted evidence context"]
      A --> G["Answer, cite, or abstain"]
    end
    M["Stage-local metrics"] -. "observe" .-> P
    M -. "observe" .-> C
    M -. "observe" .-> R
    M -. "observe" .-> A
    M -. "observe" .-> G





flowchart LR
    subgraph Offline["Offline knowledge path"]
      D["Versioned sources"] --> P["Parse + normalize"]
      P --> K["Chunk + metadata"]
      K --> I["Sparse + vector indexes"]
    end
    subgraph Online["Online answer path"]
      Q["Query + selected history"] --> U["Understand + filter"]
      U --> C["Candidate retrieval"]
      I --> C
      C --> R["Fuse + rerank"]
      R --> A["Budgeted evidence context"]
      A --> G["Answer, cite, or abstain"]
    end
    M["Stage-local metrics"] -. "observe" .-> P

Figure 14.1: At which boundary did an evidence-grounded answer first become impossible?





Retrieval makes evidence available, not true. A citation gives a support checker an address; it does not prove support. An abstention becomes useful only with a decision threshold and risk–coverage evaluation. RAG engineering makes those distinctions executable.

title: 14  Embeddings and RAG Systems [F+S]
text:
14.4 What you will build






TipOne hybrid RAG system, grown by mechanism



You will build rag_pipeline.py against a versioned 26-document support corpus and 25-query golden set. Its facade composes rag_types.py, retrieval_metrics.py, and hybrid_retrieval.py, so each mechanism remains inspectable without creating competing pipeline versions. The same pipeline acquires ranked metrics, exact BM25, an exact dense baseline, reciprocal-rank fusion, shortlist reranking, bounded query rewriting, deterministic chunking, provenance-preserving context assembly, citations, abstention, and a stage-local evaluation harness.
chapter_build.py executes one end-to-end experiment and sweeps four chunk sizes. render_metrics.py turns its ledger into the measured figure used later in the chapter. The dense encoder is signed feature hashing with declared concept aliases; the reranker is a transparent token-and-concept pair scorer. They let every test run offline and deterministically. Replace them before measuring a real embedder or reranker.
Success means that the active policy—not its retired predecessor—is indexed; rare identifiers and paraphrases both remain retrievable; candidate recall is measured before reranking; context stays within budget at source boundaries; citations point to supporting text; unknown questions abstain; and repeated runs produce identical reports.

14  Embeddings and RAG Systems [F+S]

title: 14  Embeddings and RAG Systems [F+S]
text:
14.5 Measure ranked retrieval before generation
Retrieval returns an ordered list, so accuracy alone discards useful information. Begin with query-level relevance judgments. Let L_q=(d_1,\ldots,d_k) be the first k returned source identities for query q, and let G_q be the set of sources judged relevant. Source identities are deliberate: if three adjacent chunks come from one policy, counting them as three independent successes inflates the result.
Recall at k asks how much required evidence entered the first k results:

\operatorname{Recall@k}(q)=\frac{|\{d_1,\ldots,d_k\}\cap G_q|}{|G_q|}.

For a query with one relevant source, this is a hit indicator. For a comparison question requiring two policies, one returned policy gives recall (0.5), not success. Candidate recall is the ceiling for every later stage: a reranker cannot promote a document the retriever never supplied.
Reciprocal rank emphasizes the first useful result. If r_q is the rank of the first relevant source, \operatorname{RR}(q)=1/r_q, or zero when none is returned. Mean reciprocal rank (MRR) averages this over queries. It fits tasks where one answer-bearing source is enough.
Average precision at k rewards placing all relevant items early. At each relevant rank i, compute precision through i, sum those precisions, and divide by the smaller of |G_q| and k. Mean average precision (MAP) averages over queries. It is more informative than MRR when several pieces of evidence are required.
Normalized discounted cumulative gain supports graded judgments. With gain g_i at rank i,

\operatorname{DCG@k}=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{2^{g_i}-1}{\log_2(i+1)},
\qquad
\operatorname{nDCG@k}=\frac{\operatorname{DCG@k}}{\operatorname{IDCG@k}}.

IDCG is the score of the ideal ordering. A policy paragraph that directly answers a question can receive higher gain than a navigation page that mentions the topic. State the judgment unit, gain scale, and cutoff whenever reporting nDCG.
The artifact implements all four without a metrics package:
# code/ch14/retrieval_metrics.py — changed region: ranked metrics
def recall_at_k(ranked, relevant, k):
    if not relevant:
        return 1.0
    return len(set(ranked[:k]) & relevant) / len(relevant)

def reciprocal_rank(ranked, relevant):
    return next(
        (1.0 / rank for rank, item in enumerate(ranked, 1)
         if item in relevant),
        0.0,
    )
The omitted functions in the excerpt implement AP and nDCG in the same inspectable style. The tests use a ranking whose first relevant item is at rank one but whose second relevant item is at rank three. MRR is perfect while recall at two is only one half. That disagreement is the lesson: metrics answer different questions.
Build a golden set from real query logs, support tickets, expert-written edge cases, and deliberate negatives. Include identifiers, paraphrases, multi-hop questions, temporal qualifiers, conflicting versions, missing answers, and tenant filters. Split development from final evaluation. Relevance judgments can be incomplete when a large corpus has many valid sources, so periodically pool results from multiple retrievers and adjudicate unseen candidates. Do not let a generator’s answer score substitute for retrieval judgments; it can answer from parametric memory and conceal a retrieval miss.









Artifact state
New mechanism
Invariant now verified




retrieval_metrics.py complete
Recall@k, reciprocal rank, AP@k, nDCG@k
Metrics are deterministic, duplicate source hits do not become extra evidence, and candidate recall can be reported independently of answer quality.

title: 14  Embeddings and RAG Systems [F+S]
text:
14.6 BM25 preserves rare lexical evidence
Lexical retrieval is often described as a baseline, but a rare literal token is sometimes the strongest evidence available. Error codes, invoice identifiers, function names, product SKUs, legal clauses, and quoted phrases should not depend on semantic similarity. BM25 ranks documents from term frequency, inverse document frequency, and length normalization. One common positive-IDF form is

\operatorname{BM25}(q,D)=
\sum_{t\in q}
\log\!\left(1+\frac{N-n_t+0.5}{n_t+0.5}\right)
\frac{f(t,D)(k_1+1)}
{f(t,D)+k_1\left(1-b+b\frac{|D|}{\operatorname{avgdl}}\right)}.

N is the number of indexed chunks, n_t the number containing term t, and f(t,D) its frequency in chunk D. The saturation controlled by k_1 prevents twenty repetitions from counting as twenty independent signals. Parameter b controls length normalization. Typical defaults are starting points, not corpus-independent truths.
The tokenizer is part of the retrieval model. Lowercasing may be safe for E1492; splitting it into E and 1492 may not be. Stemming helps authorize match authorization but can damage product names. Stop-word removal can erase meaningful legal phrases. Index title, body, section path, and selected metadata as separate fields when the search engine supports field weighting. Keep an analyzer test suite alongside retrieval tests.
The chapter’s exact index keeps its mechanics visible:
# code/ch14/hybrid_retrieval.py — changed region: BM25.search
for chunk, freq, length in zip(
    self.chunks, self.freqs, map(len, self.rows)
):
    score = 0.0
    for term in terms(query):
        tf = freq.get(term, 0)
        norm = self.k1 * (
            1 - self.b + self.b * length / self.avg_len
        )
        score += self.idf(term) * (
            tf * (self.k1 + 1) / (tf + norm) if tf + norm else 0
        )
On the fixture, E1492 has higher inverse-document frequency than a common word and retrieves error-e1492-v5 ahead of the unrelated asset-a1492-v1. That behavior is not guaranteed by substring search: E1492 and A1492 share digits but are distinct tokens.
BM25’s limitation is equally useful to understand. “Stop my yearly membership from renewing” may share few surface forms with “cancel an annual subscription before its renewal date.” Synonyms, translations, and implicit intent weaken lexical overlap. Query expansion can bridge some gaps, but maintaining an exhaustive synonym list becomes another knowledge system. Dense retrieval supplies a complementary signal; hybrid retrieval keeps both.

New mechanism
Invariant now verified

Rare-term weighting and length normalization
E1492 retrieves the correct error page, and its IDF exceeds that of a corpus-wide common token.
