title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
section: 18.7 Record lifecycle: provenance, contradiction, temporal truth, and forgetting
text:
18.7 Record lifecycle: provenance, contradiction, temporal truth, and forgetting
A memory record should be immutable in meaning even when its status changes. If Mina moves, do not overwrite “Boston” in place and erase the evidence that it was once true. Close its validity interval and add a new record. Current truth becomes a query over time, scope, status, and evidence—not the last string written to a key-value store.

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Candidate: user / tool / model proposal
    Candidate --> Rejected: missing evidence or policy violation
    Candidate --> Review: sensitive, inferred, or procedural
    Review --> Rejected: denied
    Review --> Active: approved
    Candidate --> Active: trusted path validates
    Active --> Superseded: newer contradictory fact
    Active --> Expired: TTL or purpose ends
    Active --> Consolidated: verified episodes support abstraction
    Consolidated --> Active: new semantic or procedural record
    Active --> Deleted: subject or policy deletion
    Superseded --> Deleted: propagation request
    Expired --> Deleted: retention cleanup





stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Candidate: user / tool / model proposal
    Candidate --> Rejected: missing evidence or policy violation
    Candidate --> Review: sensitive, inferred, or procedural
    Review --> Rejected: denied
    Review --> Active: approved
    Candidate --> Active: trusted path validates
    Active --> Superseded: newer contradictory fact
    Active --> Expired: TTL or purpose ends
    Active --> Consolidated: verified episodes support abstraction
    Consolidated --> Active: new semantic or procedural record
    Active --> Deleted: subject or policy deletion
    Superseded --> Deleted: propagation request
    Expired --> Deleted: retention cleanup




Figure 18.3: How does one proposed memory become current truth, history, or deletion evidence?





At minimum, preserve:

identity: stable record and subject identifiers;
ownership: tenant, user, agent, task, project, and purpose as needed;
content type: fact, event, preference, procedure, summary, or pointer;
provenance: source type and evidence identifiers;
time: event time, ingestion time, and validity interval;
confidence: calibrated for the source and extraction path;
status: candidate, active, superseded, expired, quarantined, or deleted;
derivation: parent records and the transformation that produced a summary.

Provenance is operational, not decorative. When a user corrects a fact, support staff should be able to explain where the old value came from. When a poisoned episode is discovered, derivation links identify summaries and skills that depend on it. When an evaluator finds a regression, the trace can distinguish bad retrieval from bad source data.
Contradiction handling begins by asking what kind of contradiction occurred. “I live in New York” may supersede an older city. “Ship this order to Boston” is an event-specific destination and should not alter the home-city fact. “I sometimes work in Boston” can coexist with both. The model may propose the relation, but application schemas and source-of-truth tools should decide which key and temporal effect apply.
The artifact demonstrates the simplest temporal update:
# memory.py — preserve old truth and open a new validity interval
if candidate.kind is Kind.SEMANTIC:
    for index, old in enumerate(self.records):
        same_fact = old.key == candidate.key and old.scope == candidate.scope
        if same_fact and old.status is Status.ACTIVE:
            self.records[index] = replace(
                old,
                status=Status.SUPERSEDED,
                valid_to=candidate.event_time,
            )
This policy assumes one value per semantic key and scope. Real domains may allow sets, uncertainty, source disagreement, or bitemporal corrections. Valid time says when the fact applied in the world; transaction time says when the system learned or corrected it. Both matter when a late-arriving event changes what the system should have known at a past decision.
Consolidation turns several records into a more useful abstraction. A cluster of verified episodes might support “the user usually schedules meetings after 10:00,” or repeated successful repairs might suggest a troubleshooting procedure. The new record must point back to its evidence and state the policy that formed it. A summary without parents is an information-laundering step: uncertainty and deletion obligations disappear while the claim survives.
Forgetting is not merely running out of storage. It can improve relevance and reduce privacy risk. Common policies include fixed retention, time decay, access-frequency decay, capacity-based eviction, purpose completion, and explicit user removal. Importance scores inspired by systems such as Generative Agents can help rank reflections, but model-assigned “importance” should not overrule legal retention, user choice, or authoritative status.
Avoid deleting history just because it is not retrieved by default. Superseded means retained as historical evidence; expired means outside normal use; deleted means a removal process owns every copy. Those states have different audit, privacy, and recovery implications.

title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]

title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
section: 18.9 Experiential learning, skills, and sleep-time compute
text:
18.9 Experiential learning, skills, and sleep-time compute
Memory becomes experiential learning when prior outcomes change how the agent approaches future tasks. The change need not touch model weights. An agent can retrieve a verified reflection, reuse a plan skeleton, load a tested skill, or avoid a failure pattern. External memory is attractive because the change is inspectable, reversible, scoped, and cheap to evaluate.
Reflexion showed the core idea: turn task feedback into linguistic reflections that inform later attempts. Voyager coupled an automatic curriculum with a growing library of executable skills. Their lasting lesson is not “let the model rewrite itself.” It is that outcome-grounded artifacts can carry learning across episodes.
Keep three layers distinct:









Layer
Artifact
Promotion evidence
Rollback




Episodic reflection
A bounded note tied to one trace
Verified outcome and attribution
Remove or expire record


Procedural skill
Instructions, script, tests, declared tools
Repeated success on held-out cases
Revert immutable version


Parametric update
Adapter, edited weights, or new checkpoint
Training and safety evaluation
Restore prior model artifact



An episode should not become a skill after one apparent success. The environment may have changed, the outcome may be luck, or the trace may contain a hidden policy violation. A safer promotion path is:

capture the trace, environment version, outcome, and verifier evidence;
propose a compact reflection that attributes the failure or success;
replay it on similar and confusingly different tasks;
turn repeated, stable behavior into a candidate procedure;
test activation precision, authority requirements, and side effects;
review and publish an immutable skill version;
monitor regressions and retain a rollback link.

This is the memory analogue of a software release. A skill packages how to act, so it deserves stricter promotion than a fact. It should declare applicable tasks, negative boundaries, required tools, permissions, inputs, expected outputs, and verification steps. Chapter 17 owns progressive disclosure and tool enforcement; this chapter owns how verified experience proposes a new version.
Sleep-time compute moves this expensive work out of the user-facing path. During idle or scheduled periods, a system can cluster episodes, refresh summaries, resolve contradictions, mine failure patterns, synthesize candidate evals, and rebuild projections. The metaphor is useful if the contract remains concrete: jobs have snapshots, budgets, lineage, gates, and observable outputs. “The agent reflected overnight” is not an operational specification.
The parametric frontier includes continual learning, knowledge editing, test-time training, per-user adapters, and architectures with learned long-term state. These approaches may compress broad recurring knowledge more efficiently than external records. They also make provenance, isolation, correction, and deletion harder. In 2026, the conservative production default remains frozen or slowly released weights plus governed external memory for user- and environment-specific facts. Move a behavior into weights only when its frequency and generality justify the loss of easy inspection.
Multi-agent systems need the same discipline. A shared blackboard can coordinate verified facts and task claims; private working memory can protect role-specific context. Do not give every agent write access to a global semantic store. Use single-writer ownership or a memory service with typed proposals, and record which agent, model, and evidence produced each candidate. Chapter 20 develops communication and shared-state topologies.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]

title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
text:
18.10 Deletion propagation and machine unlearning
Deletion is a graph traversal through every representation derived from a subject’s data. Removing the canonical row is the beginning, not the end. Search indexes, graph edges, caches, summaries, checkpoints, logs, evaluation corpora, adapters, distilled students, and backups may contain exact or transformed copies.

flowchart LR
    Request["authenticated deletion request"] --> Resolve["resolve subject + record IDs"]
    Resolve --> Manifest["deletion manifest"]
    Manifest --> Primary[("primary records")]
    Manifest --> Search[("search / vector / graph projections")]
    Manifest --> Cache[("caches and materialized views")]
    Manifest --> Runs[("checkpoints, traces, logs")]
    Manifest --> Derived[("summaries, skills, eval sets")]
    Manifest --> Models[("adapters, distilled or trained models")]
    Primary --> Verify["absence / tombstone checks"]
    Search --> Verify
    Cache --> Verify
    Runs --> Policy["erase, redact, encrypt-shred, or expire"]
    Derived --> Rebuild["invalidate and rebuild from surviving parents"]
    Models --> Unlearn["retrain, SISA shard removal, or approximate unlearning"]
    Policy --> Evidence["completion evidence + exceptions"]
    Rebuild --> Evidence
    Unlearn --> Evidence
    Verify --> Evidence





flowchart LR
    Request["authenticated deletion request"] --> Resolve["resolve subject + record IDs"]
    Resolve --> Manifest["deletion manifest"]
    Manifest --> Primary[("primary records")]
    Manifest --> Search[("search / vector / graph projections")]
    Manifest --> Cache[("caches and materialized views")]
    Manifest --> Runs[("checkpoints, traces, logs")]
    Manifest --> Derived[("summaries, skills, eval sets")]
    Manifest --> Models[("adapters, distilled or trained models")]
    Primary --> Verify["absence / tombstone checks"]
    Search --> Verify
    Cache --> Verify
    Runs --> Policy["erase, redact, encrypt-shred, or expire"]
    Derived --> Rebuild["invalidate and rebuild from surviving parents"]
    Models --> Unlearn["retrain, SISA shard removal, or approximate unlearning"]
    Policy --> Evidence["completion evidence + exceptions"]
    Rebuild --> Evidence
    Unlearn --> Evidence
    Verify --> Evidence




Figure 18.4: How does a subject deletion reach direct copies, derived artifacts, and learned parameters?





Start with a data inventory. Every write path declares downstream targets and a way to address them by subject or source record. Derivation links connect a summary to its parents. Model and dataset registries record which snapshots contributed to an adapter or distillation run. Without lineage, the system cannot know what deletion means.
For non-parametric stores, exact deletion can often be defined precisely: the canonical content and authorized projections are gone or cryptographically inaccessible; caches have been invalidated; queries cannot retrieve the record; and retained tombstones contain no prohibited payload. Append-only audit systems may store a redacted event or keyed digest rather than the original content. Backups need an explicit policy: immediate selective deletion, encryption-key destruction, or expiry without restoration into active service.
For learned parameters, “remove this row” is not an ordinary database operation. Exact unlearning means the resulting model is equivalent, under a stated criterion, to training without the removed examples. Full retraining is the reference but may be expensive. Sharded training schemes such as SISA can reduce retraining scope when designed in advance. Approximate unlearning modifies the model to reduce the influence or recoverability of selected data and must report its empirical criterion; it is not proof that every trace vanished.
This distinction prevents a common overclaim. Deleting an external memory can be exact for the stores under control. Editing a weight or applying an “unlearning” update is a measured mitigation whose guarantee depends on the algorithm, attacker, and evaluation. Membership inference, extraction probes, utility retention, and comparison with a retrained reference test different properties.
Derived procedural artifacts need judgment. If a skill was learned from many episodes and one user requests deletion, remove the source link and evaluate whether the procedure contains user-specific content or materially depends on that episode. A verbatim detail demands removal. A generic algorithm may not, but the decision belongs in the documented policy and lineage system. Chapter 30 covers formal data and model documentation; Appendix C tracks jurisdiction-specific obligations that change over time.
The artifact’s local deletion is deliberately narrow:
# memory.py — delete canonical records and their local projections
def delete_user(self, tenant_id: str, user_id: str) -> DeletionManifest:
    deleted: list[str] = []
    for index, record in enumerate(self.records):
        owned = record.scope.tenant_id == tenant_id and record.scope.user_id == user_id
        if owned and record.status is not Status.DELETED:
            deleted.append(record.record_id)
            self.records[index] = replace(record, status=Status.DELETED)
    for ids in self.search_index.values():
        ids.difference_update(deleted)
    self.cache = {key: value for key, value in self.cache.items() if value not in deleted}
    targets = {"primary_store": "deleted", "search_index": "deleted", "cache": "deleted"}
    return DeletionManifest(user_id, tuple(deleted), targets)
The method returns evidence rather than a boolean. A production manifest would track each target, request and completion time, record count, policy basis, retry state, verifier result, and any exception with expiry. Failed targets remain open work. Chapter 27 owns operational queues and deadlines; Chapter 26 owns durable execution so a crash cannot silently lose the propagation workflow.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]

title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
text:
18.11 Evaluate memory and privacy
An average question-answer score hides the failures that matter. A system can recall static facts while failing updates, temporal questions, abstention, cross-user isolation, or deletion. LongMemEval emphasizes abilities including information extraction, multi-session reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge updates, and abstention. Turn each ability into an explicit slice with its own release floor.
The chapter fixture keeps five binary probes visible:






Figure 18.5: A deterministic memory probe reports lifecycle abilities separately.



The all-pass plot is not evidence that the toy store is production-ready. It demonstrates the shape of an evaluation. A useful suite expands each ability across tenants, paraphrases, time gaps, conflicting sources, languages, sensitivity classes, and attack variants.
Measure at least four layers:








Layer
Representative measures
Diagnostic question




Formation
candidate precision/recall, provenance completeness, write latency
Did the right event become the right record?


Retrieval
authorized recall@k, temporal accuracy, conflict detection, abstention
Did the right record become available—and only that record?


Use
grounded task success, unsupported personalization, policy adherence
Did memory improve the decision without becoming authority?


Lifecycle
staleness, correction latency, deletion coverage/time, rebuild integrity
Can the system repair and remove what it learned?



Add adversarial cases. Put an instruction in a retrieved document and assert that it cannot write memory or call a tool. Create two tenants with identical preferences and assert no cross-scope result. Delete a source episode and verify that its summary is invalidated. Poison one procedural candidate, ensure it fails promotion, and check that later tasks do not retrieve it.
Privacy evaluation asks more than whether deletion succeeds. Check data minimization: could the task work without storing this field? Check appropriateness: would a reasonable user expect the memory to affect this task? Check legibility: can the interface explain the remembered item and source? Check control: do correction, temporary mode, scope disablement, and deletion change the next run immediately? Check isolation: are indexes, caches, logs, and evaluation exports subject-scoped?
Online metrics require careful interpretation. Higher memory-hit rate may mean better recall or more unnecessary personalization. Lower abstention may mean coverage or fabricated certainty. More stored items may mean usefulness or failed minimization. Pair operational counts with task outcomes, complaints, corrections, removals, and inappropriate-memory review.
Evaluate cost and latency as a budget. Formation, embedding, graph updates, reranking, and consolidation all consume resources. Compare against a no-memory baseline and a context-only baseline on the same tasks. The right question is not “does memory improve quality?” but “which abilities improve, for which users, at what privacy exposure, latency, and cost?” Chapter 22 supplies confidence intervals, judge calibration, and release gating for these comparisons.
Finally, audit the evaluator. If the test answer is generated from the same summary that retrieval uses, it may reward shared corruption. Keep authoritative event fixtures separate. For temporal cases, generate expected answers from event intervals. For deletion, query every declared projection directly rather than asking the model whether it remembers. Model-based judges can assess response quality; they cannot certify absence from storage.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]

title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
text:
18.12 Build
Run the deterministic artifact from newbook:
python code/ch18/fixture.py --plot assets/figures/ch18-memory-abilities.svg
python -m pytest tests/test_ch18_memory.py -q
The fixture should report:
{
  "scores": {
    "update": 1,
    "temporal": 1,
    "abstention": 1,
    "poisoning": 1,
    "deletion": 1
  },
  "poison_reason": "retrieved text cannot write durable memory directly",
  "deletion_manifest": {
    "subject": "user-3",
    "records": 2,
    "targets": {
      "primary_store": "deleted",
      "search_index": "deleted",
      "cache": "deleted"
    }
  }
}
Work through one artifact in five passes.
1. Trace formation. Open schema.py and memory.py. Follow one Candidate through validation, stable identity, record creation, and indexing. Remove evidence_id from the fixture and confirm the write is rejected. Then restore it.
2. Observe temporal truth. The fixture writes Boston at time 10 and New York at time 20. Query current state, then query as_of=15. Inspect the two records: the first remains present with SUPERSEDED status and valid_to=20; the second is active. Add a third move and predict every interval before running the tests.
3. Make abstention fail safely. Lower the retrieval threshold until “favorite color” returns an unrelated city. Restore the threshold. Add an explicit result type that distinguishes NO_MATCH, CONFLICT, and FORBIDDEN_SCOPE without exposing forbidden candidates.
4. Attempt durable poisoning. Change the candidate source from RETRIEVED_DOCUMENT to MODEL_INFERENCE. It should still fail because the content looks instruction-like. Replace it with an innocuous model inference and decide whether the policy should reject, queue, or retain it temporarily. Encode that decision as a test rather than a comment.
5. Expand deletion. Populate the cache by retrieving before deletion. Verify that both record identifiers disappear from every search posting and cache value. Add a derived-summary table with parent identifiers, update the manifest, and make deletion fail until the summary is invalidated.

18.12.1 Acceptance checks

Current and historical queries return different, correct values.
A weakly related query abstains instead of returning the nearest record.
Authenticated scope is checked before relevance; add a second tenant test.
Retrieved text cannot directly create durable memory.
Supersession preserves history and provenance.
Deletion reports both records and clears all three local targets.
Every added projection has both a rebuild path and a deletion check.



18.12.2 Honesty note
The artifact uses lexical overlap rather than production retrieval, integer timestamps rather than a temporal database, and in-memory projections rather than distributed services. Its deletion guarantee covers only the three named local targets. It does not claim weight unlearning, backup erasure, legal compliance, or crash-durable propagation. Those omissions are visible so each can become an explicit system boundary.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]

title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
text:
18.13 What endures, what changes
What endures. Memory is a governed lifecycle, not a similarity index. Context, checkpoint state, and cross-session memory have different contracts. Durable writes require typed candidates, authenticated scope, provenance, temporal semantics, and a policy gate. Current truth should preserve historical evidence. Retrieval filters authority before ranking and may abstain. Experience becomes procedure only through verified promotion. Every projection and derived artifact needs lineage, rebuilding, and deletion semantics. Parameter unlearning deserves a narrower claim than store deletion.
What changes. Memory benchmarks, context limits, graph and vector products, consolidation models, continual-learning methods, and consumer interfaces will improve. Some learned-memory architectures may move more state into model internals. The stable questions remain: what was learned, from which evidence, for whom, during which interval, under whose authority, how did it affect a decision, and can every resulting copy be corrected or removed?
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]

title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
text:
18.14 Exercises

Scope before relevance. Add a second user with an identical “home city” sentence. Write a test that fails if global ranking runs before the scope predicate, including a cache-collision variant.
Bitemporal correction. Extend Record with transaction time. Ingest a late correction stating that a move occurred at time 18 but was reported at time 25. Answer both “what was true at time 20?” and “what did the system believe at time 20?”
Conflict without leakage. Add two equally credible sources that disagree. Design a typed conflict result that supports user clarification while hiding records outside the caller’s scope.
Consolidation gate. Use three verified episodes and one failed episode to propose a procedural memory. Define held-out promotion cases, negative activation cases, and a rollback rule.
Deletion graph. Add summaries, a vector projection, one checkpoint, and a mock adapter registry. Produce a manifest with exact, scheduled, approximate, and exempt states; defend each classification.
Memory product review. Design user controls for viewing, correcting, pausing, exporting, and deleting memories. For each control, state which backend invariant proves that the UI action took effect.
Cost-matched evaluation. Compare no-memory, transcript-only, and governed-memory systems on update, temporal, and abstention slices under an equal token-and-latency budget. Pre-register the release floors and failure analysis.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]

title: 18  Agent Memory and Experiential Learning [F+S]
text:
18.15 Notes and sources
The taxonomy and agent-memory framing draw on Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents. Generative Agents is the source for the influential memory-stream and reflection design, while MemGPT develops explicit management of a virtual context hierarchy. LongMemEval and LongMemEval-V2 motivate ability-level evaluation across conversations and environment experience.
For learning from experience, see Reflexion and Voyager. The deletion discussion uses the exact-versus-approximate distinction carefully; Machine Unlearning with SISA is one foundational systems treatment. Chapter 24 expands poisoning and privacy threats, Chapter 26 implements crash-durable propagation, Chapter 27 owns operational deletion queues, and Chapter 30 owns the evidence artifacts that document these guarantees.

19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔UI [S]

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
text:
19.2 Contents

Protocols stabilize boundaries; frameworks own control flow
MCP architecture, primitives, and lifecycle
Design an interoperable MCP server
Authorize MCP without creating a confused deputy

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
text:
19.3 What you will build






TipOne protocol seam, two orchestration hosts



The artifact under code/ch19/ exposes a refund-policy resource and two typed tools through a JSON-RPC-shaped protocol seam. A model-driven loop host and an explicit graph host discover and call the same surface. The graph pauses at a review interrupt, checkpoints state, resumes, and reaches the same domain outcome as the loop. A wrong-audience token is rejected and a replayed write returns the same receipt.
mcp_server.py is a thin adapter for the official Python MCP SDK. The deterministic fixture tests the domain and protocol contract in process, so it remains runnable without a network, model key, or live host.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
section: 19.4 Protocols stabilize boundaries; frameworks own control flow
text:
19.4 Protocols stabilize boundaries; frameworks own control flow
An agent platform connects several independently changing systems: models, tools, data sources, remote agents, user interfaces, identity providers, and durable runtimes. If one framework’s object model becomes the contract between all of them, every upgrade becomes a migration of the entire platform.
A protocol defines what crosses a boundary. A framework helps implement behavior on one side of that boundary.

MCP standardizes exchanges between an AI host and context or capability servers.
A2A standardizes exchanges with a remote, potentially opaque agent.
An agent–UI event protocol standardizes what a frontend observes, renders, and sends back.
A graph or agent SDK implements control flow, state reduction, model calls, and interrupts inside an application.

These concerns can coexist. They are not alternatives in one product comparison.

flowchart LR
    UI["web / mobile UI"] <-->|"run, message, state, tool events"| Host["agent host"]
    Host <-->|"MCP: tools, resources, prompts"| Server["capability servers"]
    Host <-->|"A2A: messages, tasks, artifacts"| Remote["remote agent systems"]
    subgraph Application["Replaceable application internals"]
      Host --> Runtime["loop, graph, or workflow runtime"]
      Runtime --> Model["model adapter"]
      Runtime --> State["checkpointer / event store"]
    end
    Server --> APIs["domain APIs and data"]
    Remote --> Other["other models, tools, and workflows"]





flowchart LR
    UI["web / mobile UI"] <-->|"run, message, state, tool events"| Host["agent host"]
    Host <-->|"MCP: tools, resources, prompts"| Server["capability servers"]
    Host <-->|"A2A: messages, tasks, artifacts"| Remote["remote agent systems"]
    subgraph Application["Replaceable application internals"]
      Host --> Runtime["loop, graph, or workflow runtime"]
      Runtime --> Model["model adapter"]
      Runtime --> State["checkpointer / event store"]
    end
    Server --> APIs["domain APIs and data"]
    Remote --> Other["other models, tools, and workflows"]




Figure 19.1: Which contract should remain stable when an orchestration framework changes?





The design test is substitution. Can a second host use the same capability server? Can the application swap a model-driven loop for a graph without rewriting domain authorization? Can the frontend reconnect and rebuild state without importing a Python runtime object? Can a remote agent be replaced without revealing its internal prompts or memory?
Protocols do not remove application responsibility. MCP can describe a tool; it does not decide whether the current user may call it. A2A can represent a task; it does not prove the remote agent is competent or trustworthy. An event stream can display a proposed action; it does not make the proposal safe. The host still owns policy, context assembly, user consent, observability, and the final mapping from protocol objects into model-visible content.
Frameworks remain valuable. They supply reducers, retries, tracing, streaming, checkpoint adapters, graph scheduling, and model integrations. The mistake is allowing convenient framework classes to become durable business interfaces. Define small application-owned ports—Model, ToolRegistry, CheckpointStore, EventSink, PolicyDecision—and adapt the framework around them.
A protocol also has a version lifecycle. Messages that happen to serialize as JSON are not automatically interoperable. The parties must agree on a revision, capabilities, optional extensions, error semantics, and transport behavior. Persist the negotiated revision in traces. Reject an unknown major or incompatible date instead of quietly interpreting fields under the wrong contract.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
text:
19.5 MCP architecture, primitives, and lifecycle
The Model Context Protocol architecture names three roles:

The host is the AI application. It owns the user relationship, model, permissions, context, and consent surface.
An MCP client is the host-side protocol component for one server connection.
An MCP server exposes bounded capabilities or context. It may run as a local process over standard input/output or remotely over HTTP.

A host can manage many clients; each client connects the host to one server. This prevents a server from receiving the full conversation or learning about other servers unless the host deliberately sends that information.

flowchart TB
    User["user"] --> Host["MCP host\nmodel + consent + context policy"]
    subgraph HostProcess["host boundary"]
      Host --> C1["client A"]
      Host --> C2["client B"]
      Registry["namespaced tool/resource registry"] --> Host
    end
    C1 <-->|"stdio"| Local["local filesystem server"]
    C2 <-->|"streamable HTTP"| Remote["remote support server"]
    Local --> Files[("granted workspace")]
    Remote --> Gateway["domain authorization + API"]
    Gateway --> Data[("tenant data")]
    Local -. no ambient access .-> Data
    Remote -. receives selected requests, not whole thread .-> Host





flowchart TB
    User["user"] --> Host["MCP host\nmodel + consent + context policy"]
    subgraph HostProcess["host boundary"]
      Host --> C1["client A"]
      Host --> C2["client B"]
      Registry["namespaced tool/resource registry"] --> Host
    end
    C1 <-->|"stdio"| Local["local filesystem server"]
    C2 <-->|"streamable HTTP"| Remote["remote support server"]
    Local --> Files[("granted workspace")]
    Remote --> Gateway["domain authorization + API"]
    Gateway --> Data[("tenant data")]
    Local -. no ambient access .-> Data
    Remote -. receives selected requests, not whole thread .-> Host




Figure 19.2: Where do MCP trust, context, and capability boundaries sit?





MCP’s server-side primitives express three different relationships:









Primitive
Purpose
Typical control
Representative method




Tool
Execute a typed operation
Model proposes; host gates
tools/list, tools/call


Resource
Read addressable context
Application selects
resources/list, resources/read


Prompt
Retrieve a reusable interaction template
User or host selects
prompts/list, prompts/get



Tools are not a license for arbitrary code. Their names, descriptions, JSON Schemas, annotations, results, errors, and side-effect semantics are part of the interface taught in Chapter 17. Resources use URIs and media types so the host can decide whether and how to place content into context. Prompts distribute templates; the host must still merge them under its own instruction hierarchy.
The protocol also supports client-side capabilities. A server may request model sampling, user elicitation, or logging when the client declared support. This reverses the usual call direction, so the host must treat each request as a new policy decision. A server asking the host to sample is not allowed to smuggle arbitrary system instructions or spend unbounded tokens.
In the stable 2025-11-25 revision used by this chapter’s fixture, a connection begins with initialization: version negotiation, capability exchange, implementation metadata, and readiness. Discovery follows, then resource reads or tool calls. Requests have correlation identifiers; notifications do not expect a response. Streamable HTTP and standard I/O carry the same data-layer meanings but have different identity, logging, and process-isolation concerns.
The lifecycle can be read as five gates: negotiate a revision and capabilities; discover the permitted surface; select only relevant definitions for model context; treat the model call as a proposal subject to host policy; then route the approved call and return a bounded observation. The server reauthorizes the operation even though the host already gated it. A denial or approval interrupt ends the path before execution.
Keep transport failures, protocol errors, tool-domain errors, and model-visible observations distinct. A disconnected server may be retryable. -32602-class invalid parameters indicate a contract mismatch. “Order not eligible” is a valid domain result. Dumping all three into a string called error forces the model to invent retry policy.






NoteLandscape 2026 — MCP is crossing a revision boundary



Verified 2026-07-19: the current stable protocol line is 2025-11-25. The official MCP project has published a 2026-07-28 release candidate centered on a stateless core and independently negotiated extensions; Tasks and MCP Apps are part of that evolution. The official Python SDK repository still labels v1.x as stable while v2 is prerelease. This book therefore pins mcp>=1.27,<2 and keeps the SDK adapter thin.
Server metadata, registries, task wrappers, UI extensions, deprecated primitives, and lifecycle details are moving. Verify live: check the official specification, SDK migration guide, negotiated revision, and host support matrix before deploying or upgrading.


The enduring implementation rule is revision-aware behavior. Do not infer “latest,” and do not make a session handshake, a stateless request, or an extension available merely because one side knows its type. Negotiate or configure the contract explicitly and test both sides at the pinned boundary.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
text:
19.6 Design an interoperable MCP server
An MCP server should be a protocol adapter around domain services, not the new home of business logic. This keeps domain authorization, transactions, tests, and idempotency usable from a web API, background job, or future protocol.
Design from the resource and action model:

Use a resource when the caller needs addressable, cacheable context and the host should decide whether to read it.
Use a tool when the caller requests computation or an effect.
Use a prompt when distributing an optional workflow template creates value across hosts.
Use an asynchronous task facility only when the operation truly outlives the request; it is not a substitute for ordinary application state.

Give each tool one domain decision. Specify required and optional fields using supported JSON Schema features, add titles and descriptions that distinguish nearby tools, and make results machine-readable. Return stable resource versions, receipts, or artifact URIs. Put large payloads behind resources instead of flooding the next model turn.
Names need server context. Two connected servers may both expose search. The host should retain server identity and namespace internally, even if it presents a friendlier label to the model. Tool changes need compatibility rules: additive optional fields are easier than repurposing an existing field; changed effect semantics deserve a new version or name.
The chapter’s live adapter is intentionally thin:
# mcp_server.py — the official SDK decorates application-owned handlers
service = SupportServer()
mcp = FastMCP("support-contracts", json_response=True)

@mcp.resource("policy://refund/current")
def current_refund_policy() -> str:
    """Read the current refund policy resource."""
    return service.read_resource(
        "policy://refund/current", principal
    )["contents"][0]["text"]

@mcp.tool()
def policy_lookup(topic: str) -> dict:
    """Read the current refund policy for the authenticated tenant."""
    return service.call_tool(
        "policy_lookup", {"topic": topic}, principal
    )["structuredContent"]
The local principal exists only to keep the offline example compact. A remote deployment obtains identity from a validated access token and request context. A standard-I/O server normally inherits a deliberately restricted environment and filesystem grants from its launching host. Neither transport should accept tenant_id or user_role from model-generated tool arguments as proof of identity.
Test the server at three layers:

Domain tests call handlers directly and cover transactions, invariants, idempotency, and tenancy.
Protocol conformance tests initialize, discover, read, call, cancel where supported, and assert typed errors against a pinned SDK/spec revision.
Host compatibility tests connect at least two real hosts or reference clients and compare discovery, content rendering, approvals, streaming, and failure behavior.

The MCP Inspector helps manual development; it does not replace automated contracts. Record golden protocol transcripts with volatile identifiers normalized. Fuzz JSON Schema boundaries, oversized content, pagination cursors, duplicate request identifiers, cancellations, disconnects, and notifications arriving during a call.
Discovery is a supply-chain boundary. The official MCP Registry stores standardized public server metadata and namespace ownership; it does not certify that every published server is safe for a particular organization. Enterprise gateways should maintain an allowlist, pin package or endpoint identity, scan artifacts, attach owners and risk tiers, and expose different catalogs by tenant and role. Private registries should not leak internal capability descriptions through unauthenticated search.
Server deployment follows ordinary service engineering. Remote servers need TLS, authentication, authorization, rate limits, tenancy isolation, timeouts, backpressure, bounded results, audit logs, health checks, and version rollouts. Local servers need package integrity, a minimal environment, process isolation, filesystem and network grants, output discipline, and cleanup. For standard I/O, application logs go to standard error; writing logs to standard output corrupts the protocol stream.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
section: 19.7 Authorize MCP without creating a confused deputy
text:
19.7 Authorize MCP without creating a confused deputy
MCP authorization protects the boundary between a host acting for a user and a resource server exposing tools or data. It does not turn model intent into user authority. The host authenticates the human or workload, obtains a token for the intended MCP resource, and asks the server to perform a specific operation. The server validates the token and independently authorizes that operation.
The current MCP authorization specification builds on OAuth, protected-resource metadata, authorization-server discovery, PKCE for public clients, resource indicators, and incremental scopes. For production, use maintained identity libraries rather than implementing signature verification or OAuth flows inside an agent framework.

sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User
    participant H as MCP host/client
    participant AS as Authorization server
    participant S as MCP resource server
    participant D as Downstream API
    H->>S: request without sufficient token
    S-->>H: protected-resource metadata + scope challenge
    H->>U: identify client, resource, and requested scope
    U-->>H: consent
    H->>AS: authorization + PKCE + resource=S
    AS-->>H: short-lived token, audience=S
    H->>S: tools/call + token
    S->>S: validate issuer, signature, audience, expiry, scope, tenant
    S->>D: server's own downstream credential + delegated context
    D-->>S: result
    S-->>H: bounded result + audit correlation
    Note over H,D: Never pass the user's S-audience token through to D





sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User
    participant H as MCP host/client
    participant AS as Authorization server
    participant S as MCP resource server
    participant D as Downstream API
    H->>S: request without sufficient token
    S-->>H: protected-resource metadata + scope challenge
    H->>U: identify client, resource, and requested scope
    U-->>H: consent
    H->>AS: authorization + PKCE + resource=S
    AS-->>H: short-lived token, audience=S
    H->>S: tools/call + token
    S->>S: validate issuer, signature, audience, expiry, scope, tenant
    S->>D: server's own downstream credential + delegated context
    D-->>S: result
    S-->>H: bounded result + audit correlation
    Note over H,D: Never pass the user's S-audience token through to D




Figure 19.3: How do resource-bound tokens prevent an MCP proxy from becoming a confused deputy?





Validate at least issuer, signature, audience/resource, expiry, not-before time, required scope, subject, client, and tenant binding. The chapter fixture makes the audience check visible: a token issued for inventory://api cannot list tools at support://mcp, even if it carries similarly named scopes.
Token passthrough is forbidden because the server would accept or forward a token not issued for it. This erases audience boundaries and audit attribution. If an MCP server calls a downstream API, it acts as an OAuth client for that API or uses a formal token-exchange/delegation mechanism. It does not relay an opaque user token and hope the downstream service interprets it safely.
A confused deputy appears when a proxy with legitimate downstream power is tricked into using it for an unapproved client. Bind consent to the requesting client, exact redirect URI, user, downstream scopes, and MCP resource. Recheck these bindings on callback. Separate the proxy’s downstream credentials from host tokens and preserve end-user attribution in an application-controlled form.
Protocol-specific supply-chain attacks need controls beyond OAuth:

Tool poisoning: a description or resource contains instructions to reveal secrets or call another tool. Treat all server content as untrusted data and keep policy outside it.
Rug pull: a server changes a tool after review. Pin identity and schema/version, monitor discovery changes, and require reapproval for authority-expanding diffs.
Tool shadowing: two servers use confusing names or one description claims authority over another. Preserve namespaces and do not let servers define cross-server policy.
Local-server escalation: a package runs with the host’s ambient privileges. Sandbox the process and grant filesystem, network, and credentials explicitly.
SSRF through discovery or authorization metadata: validate URLs, redirects, DNS/IP destinations, and HTTPS policy in server-side clients.

An MCP gateway can centralize discovery, token validation, schema policy, rate limits, data-loss controls, approvals, and audit. It must not become an invisible superuser. Preserve caller identity, target server, original tool, exact arguments, and decision evidence end to end. Apply permission filtering before tool descriptions enter the model’s context.
Chapter 24 embeds these controls in the broader agent threat model. The canonical MCP lesson here is narrower: protocol interoperability never implies transitive trust, and authorization is checked at the resource server for every operation.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
section: 19.8 A2A, task lifecycles, and protocol convergence
text:
19.8 A2A, task lifecycles, and protocol convergence
MCP connects a host to capabilities. A2A connects one application to a remote agentic system whose internal model, tools, memory, and workflow may remain opaque. The remote party may ask for clarification, work asynchronously, stream artifacts, or fail after partial progress. Treating it as one tool call loses that lifecycle.
The A2A specification centers several objects:







Object
Role




Agent Card
Discover identity, interfaces, capabilities, skills, and security schemes


Message
One user- or agent-authored turn containing typed parts


Task
A stateful unit of work with a server-issued identity and lifecycle


Artifact
A typed output assembled from parts


Context
A grouping identifier across related messages or tasks


Extension
Negotiated behavior outside the core



An Agent Card is capability metadata, not a credential or trust certificate. Fetch it from an authenticated or well-known location, verify signatures when provided, cache it with ordinary HTTP semantics, and protect extended cards that reveal sensitive internal skills. Never embed static secrets in the card. Registry search or semantic matching can propose a remote agent; policy decides whether it is an allowed delegate.
A task may progress through submitted, working, input-required, completed, failed, canceled, or rejected states, depending on the negotiated revision. Clients can poll, receive a stream, or use authenticated push notifications. Preserve task and context identifiers so reconnecting does not create duplicate work. A terminal task should not silently accept another message as if it were the same execution.
Delegation needs a task brief: objective, relevant evidence, constraints, allowed data, required output schema, deadline, budget, and success predicate. Send the minimum context. Do not forward the parent agent’s hidden instructions, unrelated conversation, ambient credentials, or full memory. When the remote agent returns an artifact, validate its media type, schema, provenance, and content before it enters context or triggers tools.
MCP and A2A can compose. A remote agent exposed through A2A may use MCP servers internally. A host may wrap a simple A2A task as a model-visible tool. The semantic boundary still matters: an MCP tool result is normally a bounded operation result; an A2A task has independent state, messages, artifacts, and cancellation.
Long-running MCP task extensions and A2A tasks may look similar because both represent asynchronous work. Choose by ownership. Use the capability protocol’s task wrapper when one server operation needs deferred completion. Use A2A when delegating to an independently operated agent whose task conversation and artifacts are first-class.
Agentic payment protocols add another boundary: intent, mandate, quote, authorization, fulfillment, and receipt must be distinct objects. A model may propose a purchase or negotiate a quote; a trusted application binds payer identity, limits, merchant, asset, amount, expiry, and approval. Protocol names in this area are changing quickly. Preserve those economic invariants instead of granting a remote agent a reusable payment secret.
Across protocol boundaries, use correlation and provenance that survive translation: parent run, delegation task, tool call, authorization decision, external effect, and returned artifact. Without that chain, a final answer can cite an artifact but operations cannot determine which remote action produced it.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
section: 19.9 Agent↔︎UI events and safe generative interfaces
text:
19.9 Agent↔︎UI events and safe generative interfaces
A chat frontend needs more than token streaming once an agent can plan, call tools, pause, delegate, edit shared state, and produce interactive artifacts. The UI must know whether text is provisional, which tool call it belongs to, what the run awaits, and whether a reconnect requires a full snapshot.
An event protocol should represent:

run start, finish, cancellation, and error;
message start, content deltas, and message end;
tool-call identity, streamed arguments, result, and status;
full state snapshots and ordered deltas;
activities, subagent progress, artifacts, and citations;
interrupts and user responses;
correlation, sequence, schema version, and parent identifiers.

The AG-UI event model is a useful current example: start–content–end event families for streaming content, and snapshot–delta patterns for synchronized state. The stable design lesson is transport-independent. Events are an appendable observation contract; frontend components derive views from them.

flowchart LR
    Agent["agent runtime"] --> Events["typed, ordered event stream"]
    Events --> Validate["schema + run/sequence validation"]
    Validate --> Project["frontend state projector"]
    Project --> Catalog{"trusted component catalog"}
    Catalog -->|known component + validated props| Render["sandboxed rendering"]
    Catalog -->|unknown HTML/script/action| Reject["reject + safe fallback"]
    User["user gesture"] --> Intent["typed UI intent"]
    Intent --> HostGate["host auth, policy, approval"]
    HostGate -->|proposal accepted| Runtime["agent/tool runtime"]
    HostGate -->|denied| Project
    Render -. cannot call domain API directly .-> HostGate





flowchart LR
    Agent["agent runtime"] --> Events["typed, ordered event stream"]
    Events --> Validate["schema + run/sequence validation"]
    Validate --> Project["frontend state projector"]
    Project --> Catalog{"trusted component catalog"}
    Catalog -->|known component + validated props| Render["sandboxed rendering"]
    Catalog -->|unknown HTML/script/action| Reject["reject + safe fallback"]
    User["user gesture"] --> Intent["typed UI intent"]
    Intent --> HostGate["host auth, policy, approval"]
    HostGate -->|proposal accepted| Runtime["agent/tool runtime"]
    HostGate -->|denied| Project
    Render -. cannot call domain API directly .-> HostGate




Figure 19.4: How can a frontend stay expressive without executing model-generated authority?





State synchronization needs recovery. Send a complete snapshot at connection or resynchronization, then ordered deltas. Give each event a stable run identifier and monotonic sequence within its stream. The frontend detects gaps or incompatible schema versions and requests a new snapshot rather than applying patches to unknown state. Make handlers idempotent so reconnect replay does not duplicate messages or approvals.
Frontend tools reverse the direction: a remote agent may ask the UI to focus a panel, collect a form, or render a chart. A model request is still a proposal. The host maps a declared tool name to a trusted component or intent handler and validates every property. Unknown components, inline script, arbitrary URLs, event-handler source, and unrestricted CSS are rejected.
Generative UI should generate data and layout within a component grammar, not executable application code by default. A trusted catalog might allow PolicyCard, DataTable, and ApprovalForm with bounded props. The approval form displays an application-owned preview of an exact action; clicking it sends a typed decision back through the host’s authorization path. The rendered component never holds a payment token or invokes the effect directly.
MCP Apps can deliver server-provided interactive interfaces inside supporting hosts. The same trust questions apply with a sharper sandbox boundary: predeclare the UI resource, sandbox the iframe, constrain messaging, validate tool calls at the host, apply content-security policy, and do not treat server origin as automatic permission. Host support and extension versions belong in the dated landscape, not in the application’s core state model.
Separate display state from authoritative state. The UI can optimistically show that an approval was submitted; the source of truth is the signed decision event and later effect receipt. On reconnect, rebuild from server state. Never let a client-side “completed” badge become evidence that an external action committed.
Test event streams with fragmented deltas, reordered or duplicated events, disconnects, stale snapshots, two concurrent tool calls, nested subagents, canceled runs, malformed component props, and a user response arriving after an interrupt expired. Accessibility is part of the component contract: focus, labels, keyboard actions, and readable status changes cannot depend on model-generated prose.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
section: 19.10 Graph state, checkpointers, interrupts, and time travel
text:
19.10 Graph state, checkpointers, interrupts, and time travel
Framework checkpointers belong with orchestration because they persist the runtime’s explicit state between graph steps. They are not the cross-session memory of Chapter 18 and not the durable external-effect ledger of Chapter 26.
A graph runtime represents nodes, edges, state, and reducers. A node reads one state snapshot and emits an update. Conditional edges select the next node. Reducers define how parallel updates combine. A thread identifier selects one checkpoint history. This makes control flow inspectable and places pauses at named states rather than inside an opaque while loop.

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Discover
    Discover --> Lookup: save checkpoint 1
    Lookup --> Review: save checkpoint 2
    Review --> Paused: interrupt exact proposal
    Paused --> Tag: resume(approved)
    Paused --> Rejected: resume(rejected)
    Tag --> Finish: tool receipt + checkpoint 3
    Review --> ForkedReview: fork checkpoint 2 + edited state
    ForkedReview --> Tag: new branch approval
    note right of Tag
      A checkpoint can replay this node.
      The write still needs idempotency
      and reconciliation.
    end note





stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Discover
    Discover --> Lookup: save checkpoint 1
    Lookup --> Review: save checkpoint 2
    Review --> Paused: interrupt exact proposal
    Paused --> Tag: resume(approved)
    Paused --> Rejected: resume(rejected)
    Tag --> Finish: tool receipt + checkpoint 3
    Review --> ForkedReview: fork checkpoint 2 + edited state
    ForkedReview --> Tag: new branch approval
    note right of Tag
      A checkpoint can replay this node.
      The write still needs idempotency
      and reconciliation.
    end note




Figure 19.5: What does a graph checkpoint preserve, and what happens when execution resumes or forks?





An interrupt serializes a reviewable payload and pauses the graph. Resume uses the same thread and a typed human response. In many graph systems, the node containing the interrupt restarts from its beginning on resume. Side effects before the interrupt must therefore be absent or idempotent. The review must remain bound to exact arguments and target version as developed in Chapter 17.
A checkpointer saves state snapshots and next-node metadata. A separate store may hold data shared across threads. Do not call every stored message “memory”; state that serves one thread has different retention, privacy, and retrieval semantics than a user preference shared across future runs.
Time travel means selecting an earlier checkpoint to inspect, replay, or fork. Replay may re-execute later model calls, tools, and interrupts; it is not a video recording. Forking creates a new branch with an edited state while preserving the old history. This is useful for debugging and counterfactual evaluation, but dangerous if a replay repeats an external effect. Effect calls need stable idempotency keys and a durable result lookup.
The chapter’s graph host saves immutable snapshots before and at review:
# runtimes.py — explicit pause and immutable checkpoint history
def start(self, case_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
    state = {"node": "discover", "case_id": case_id, "status": "running"}
    tools = self.client.connect()
    state.update(node="lookup", tools=tools)
    self.checkpoints.save(state)
    policy = self.client.call("policy_lookup", {"topic": "refund"})
    state.update(node="review", policy=policy, status="awaiting_approval")
    self.checkpoints.save(state)
    return deepcopy(state)
The build uses an in-memory log to expose semantics, not to claim crash recovery. A production checkpointer needs concurrency control, encryption, retention, schema migration, tenant isolation, and atomicity between checkpoint metadata and pending writes. Chapter 26 introduces a workflow/event history and effect ledger for hour- or day-long runs.
Graphs are helpful when branches, joins, interrupts, or replay need names. A simple loop is often easier when the model repeatedly selects among tools and state is small. A deterministic workflow is better when the sequence is known. The artifact implements both graph and loop against the same protocol to show that orchestration choice need not change the server contract.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
section: 19.11 Select a framework without surrendering the architecture
text:
19.11 Select a framework without surrendering the architecture
Most agent frameworks emphasize one of three paradigms:









Paradigm
Control owner
Strength
Failure pressure




Deterministic workflow
Application DAG or code
Predictability, throughput, ordinary testing
Rigid for underspecified tasks


Explicit state graph
Application nodes plus conditional/model edges
Branches, interrupts, replay, state inspection
State and reducer complexity


Model-driven loop
Model proposes the next step inside harness gates
Flexible exploration with small core
Termination, observability, and cost variance



Real systems mix them. A deterministic intake workflow may launch a model-driven research loop, then enter a graph approval node before a deterministic effect. Select at the level of a bounded subproblem rather than declaring one paradigm for the company.
Evaluate a framework on mechanisms, not demo brevity:

Can the application own prompts, messages, tool schemas, and the event stream?
Are model, provider, storage, and protocol adapters replaceable?
What is checkpointed, at what boundary, and under which consistency guarantee?
Can interrupts bind exact action state and resume after a deployment?
How are parallel updates reduced and conflicts surfaced?
Can traces export in a vendor-neutral form with stable correlation identifiers?
Can tests run with deterministic models, tools, time, and failure injection?
What happens to running threads during state-schema and framework upgrades?
Which hosted features disappear if the project self-hosts or migrates?

Framework tax appears as hidden messages, automatic retries, implicit context, proprietary trace schemas, serialized runtime objects, and provider-specific tool wrappers. Any one may be convenient. The risk is relying on behavior the team cannot inspect, pin, or reproduce.
Create an exit seam early. Domain handlers are ordinary functions or services. Protocol servers wrap those handlers. Application state is a versioned schema rather than a framework object graph. Checkpoints use an application-owned repository interface. UI events are mapped from runtime events. Model calls go through one adapter that preserves raw provider response metadata for debugging.
Run a migration spike before adoption: implement one representative branch, one parallel join, one tool failure, one approval interrupt, one resume, one protocol call, and one trace export. Measure code, hidden events, latency, state size, and recovery behavior. Then reimplement the same slice with the simplest custom loop or a second framework. The comparison reveals what the framework actually owns.
Protocols over frameworks does not mean avoiding frameworks. It means allowing a framework to be excellent at orchestration without granting it permanent ownership of identity, business effects, memory, UI, or interoperability.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔UI [S]

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
text:
19.12 Build
Run the dependency-free fixture from newbook:
python code/ch19/fixture.py
python -m pytest tests/test_ch19_protocols.py -q
The fixture should end with:
{
  "protocol": {
    "version": "2025-11-25",
    "wrong_audience": "invalid token audience"
  },
  "loop": {
    "status": "completed",
    "answer": "C-41 routed to manual_review under policy v3",
    "methods": ["initialize", "tools/list", "tools/call", "tools/call"]
  },
  "graph": {
    "paused": "awaiting_approval",
    "status": "completed",
    "answer": "C-41 routed to manual_review under policy v3",
    "methods": ["initialize", "tools/list", "tools/call", "tools/call"],
    "checkpoints": 3
  },
  "equivalent_outcome": true
}
Work through one integrated system in six passes.
1. Inspect the wire contract. In protocol_lab.py, follow initialize, tools/list, and tools/call through dispatch. Change the requested version to latest and observe the typed rejection. Restore the pinned revision.
2. Connect two hosts. LoopHost and GraphHost each create one Client for a fresh server. Compare their method transcripts. The orchestration differs; discovery and effects do not. Add a third host that only reads the policy resource without loading the write tool.
3. Filter discovery by authority. Remove case:write from the principal. tools/list should expose only policy_lookup; the model never sees the unauthorized tool. Attempt a direct tools/call anyway and verify server-side authorization still rejects it.
4. Pause and fork graph state. Run GraphHost.start, inspect its two checkpoints, and resume with False. Then fork the review snapshot with another case identifier and approve the branch. Confirm the original snapshot did not mutate.
5. Replay an effect. Call case_tag twice with the same idempotency key. Both responses match and one receipt exists. Change the key and observe that a new operation is recorded. Chapter 26 will replace this local property with a durable effect contract.
6. Use the official adapter. After installing the pinned requirements, run python code/ch19/mcp_server.py through an MCP Inspector or a compatible host using standard I/O. Connect two different hosts if available and record differences in displayed names, resource selection, approvals, structured content, and errors. Keep these compatibility notes dated.

19.12.1 Acceptance checks

An explicit protocol revision is negotiated; latest is rejected.
Read-only discovery does not reveal the write tool.
A token for another audience cannot list or call tools.
Loop and graph hosts reach the same domain outcome through the same method sequence.
The graph exposes a typed review pause and immutable checkpoints.
Replaying the same idempotency key produces one receipt.
The SDK adapter contains no independent business or authorization rules.



19.12.2 Honesty note
The deterministic client implements only the JSON-RPC-shaped subset used in this lesson; conformance comes from the thin official SDK adapter and must be tested against the pinned MCP revision. The in-memory checkpointer is not durable. The fixture principal is not an OAuth implementation. The scripted planner is not a capability evaluation. These seams isolate protocol, authorization, and orchestration properties without claiming a production deployment.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔UI [S]

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
text:
19.13 What endures, what changes
What endures. Protocols define cross-system meaning; frameworks implement control flow. A host owns user context, consent, policy, and model behavior. MCP tools, resources, and prompts have different control relationships. Identity comes from the transport and is reauthorized at the server. Tokens are audience-bound and never blindly passed through. Remote agent tasks have an independent lifecycle. Frontends consume typed, recoverable events and render only trusted components. Graph checkpoints, cross-session memory, and durable effects remain separate contracts. Application-owned adapters keep framework choice reversible.
What changes. MCP lifecycle details, extensions, Tasks, Apps, server discovery, registries, SDK major versions, A2A bindings, payment protocols, UI event types, and framework APIs will evolve. Appendix C tracks pins. The stable review questions are: which boundary is crossed, which revision was negotiated, whose identity and authority apply, what state owns the lifecycle, what can be replayed, and how can either side be replaced?
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔UI [S]

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
text:
19.14 Exercises

Real client compatibility. Connect mcp_server.py to two compatible hosts. Capture normalized transcripts for initialization, discovery, resource read, tool call, and invalid arguments. Explain every host-level difference.
Step-up authorization. Give the initial principal only policy:read. Design a scope challenge and consent flow for case:write, including resource indicator, PKCE, expiry, denial, and token refresh.
Rug-pull defense. Change case_tag so the same name accepts a new notify_customer field. Build a gateway rule that detects the authority-expanding schema diff and quarantines the update.
A2A translation. Wrap the case-routing operation as an asynchronous remote-agent task with a card, message, status changes, and artifact. State which information must not be copied from the parent run.
UI recovery. Define events for the graph pause, exact approval preview, user decision, tool receipt, and completion. Inject a missing state delta and specify how the client requests a fresh snapshot.
Replay boundary. Replace the in-memory receipt map with a database unique constraint. Crash after the effect but before the checkpoint, resume, and prove the idempotency key prevents a duplicate tag.
Framework exit spike. Port GraphHost to one graph framework, then export checkpoints into the application-owned state schema. List every field or behavior that prevents a clean round trip.

title: 19  Protocols and Frameworks: MCP, A2A, and Agent↔︎UI [S]
text:
19.15 Notes and sources
The MCP material follows the official architecture overview, 2025-11-25 authorization specification, security best practices, server tutorial, Python SDK, and MCP Apps overview. Registry metadata and its non-certification boundary are documented by the official MCP Registry.
The remote-agent lifecycle comes from the official A2A specification and agent discovery guidance. The UI discussion uses the official AG-UI event model. The framework mechanics are grounded in the current LangGraph documentation for persistence, interrupts, and time travel; the chapter treats these as one worked paradigm, not a permanent dependency.

Assumed: concurrency, queues, schemas, optimistic concurrency, budgets, and basic evaluation design.
From earlier chapters: Chapter 16 supplies the single-agent baseline and workflow patterns. Chapter 17 supplies tool and workspace boundaries. Chapter 18 owns memory semantics. Chapter 19 owns A2A and framework mechanics.
Not required: distributed-agent frameworks, game theory, multi-agent RL, or a hosted model. This chapter starts with the decision to keep one agent.
