title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.3 What you will build






TipA team that must earn its coordination cost



The artifact under code/ch20/ splits a read-only policy task into three independent shards. Workers write typed JSON reports with provenance; one orchestrator validates and merges them. A single agent receives the team’s measured total token budget. Both produce the same answer, while the parallel team halves modeled critical-path latency. Then one worker omits provenance. The merge gate contains the result and attributes the failure to that worker and handoff.
There is one runtime and one experiment—not a gallery of supervisor, swarm, debate, and hierarchy implementations.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.4 When a single agent wins
Adding agents creates a distributed system whose messages are lossy, expensive, and partly generated. Before asking which topology to use, ask whether the task should be split at all.
A multi-agent design needs three positive conditions:

The work is genuinely decomposable. Independent or weakly coupled read-heavy branches can run concurrently. A single chain of dependent reasoning cannot be accelerated by pretending its steps are peers.
The split creates a useful boundary. Each worker gets a smaller context, different tools or permissions, a distinct model capability, or an independently operated service. Renaming identical prompts “researcher,” “critic,” and “writer” does not create specialization.
A measured single-agent failure remains. The best simple baseline has already tried sharper tools, just-in-time context, compaction, a deterministic workflow, a specialist call, or ordinary parallel functions. The proposed team targets a named failure or latency constraint.

If any condition is absent, retain one agent or a deterministic workflow. The test is not whether multiple agents can finish the task. It is whether they outperform the strongest simpler system under the resource constraint the product actually has.

flowchart TD
    Start["strong single-agent / workflow baseline"] --> Failure{"measured failure or deadline remains?"}
    Failure -->|no| One["keep one agent"]
    Failure -->|yes| Parallel{"independent or weakly coupled subproblems?"}
    Parallel -->|no| Improve["improve tools, context, planning, or verifier"]
    Parallel -->|yes| Boundary{"useful context, permission, model, or ownership boundary?"}
    Boundary -->|no| Functions["parallel functions, not agent personas"]
    Boundary -->|yes| Writes{"concurrent unresolved writes to same state?"}
    Writes -->|yes| Veto["veto split or appoint one writer"]
    Writes -->|no| Trial["cost-matched team experiment"]
    Trial --> Gate{"quality / latency gain exceeds coordination risk?"}
    Gate -->|yes| Team["ship bounded topology"]
    Gate -->|no| One





flowchart TD
    Start["strong single-agent / workflow baseline"] --> Failure{"measured failure or deadline remains?"}
    Failure -->|no| One["keep one agent"]
    Failure -->|yes| Parallel{"independent or weakly coupled subproblems?"}
    Parallel -->|no| Improve["improve tools, context, planning, or verifier"]
    Parallel -->|yes| Boundary{"useful context, permission, model, or ownership boundary?"}
    Boundary -->|no| Functions["parallel functions, not agent personas"]
    Boundary -->|yes| Writes{"concurrent unresolved writes to same state?"}
    Writes -->|yes| Veto["veto split or appoint one writer"]
    Writes -->|no| Trial["cost-matched team experiment"]
    Trial --> Gate{"quality / latency gain exceeds coordination risk?"}
    Gate -->|yes| Team["ship bounded topology"]
    Gate -->|no| One




Figure 20.1: When does a task earn the coordination cost of multiple agents?





This decision can be formalized as an incremental utility test:

\Delta U = w_q\Delta Q - w_c\Delta C - w_l\Delta L - w_r\Delta R,

where Q is task quality, C total compute or spend, L deadline-relevant latency, and R operational risk. Weights reflect the product. Parallel research may accept higher total tokens to cut wall time and raise source coverage. A low-latency transaction flow may not.
Compare systems, not call counts. Holding “one turn per agent” constant gives the team more model compute. Hold total input/output or reasoning tokens, modeled spend, wall-clock budget, tool calls, and environment access as appropriate. AI Agents That Matter argues for cost-aware, reproducible agent evaluation; the requirement is even more important when one architecture silently multiplies calls.
Task coupling is the practical veto. If worker B needs the exact rejected alternatives, uncertainty, and intermediate observations produced by worker A, a short handoff damages the computation. Passing the full trace restores information but erases much of the context and cost benefit. The system has discovered that the work was one chain.
The best reason for multiple agents is often not “collective intelligence.” It is ordinary parallelism plus isolation: search independent sources concurrently, run reviews with disjoint permissions, or delegate to an external owner. State that reason in the design document. If “agent count” is itself the success metric, the architecture has no falsifiable objective.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.5 Context is the architecture
Once a task is split, the decisive design question is what crosses each boundary. Every handoff compresses one agent’s state into another agent’s context. Missing facts, duplicated history, conflicting versions, and adversarial instructions all enter here.
Common strategies trade fidelity for cost:









Handoff
Preserves
Loses or risks
Good fit




Full trace
Decisions, uncertainty, rejected paths
Context cost, pollution, hidden instructions
Tightly coupled continuation


Rolling summary
Compact progress
Quietly drops constraints and provenance
Low-risk conversation continuity


Last k turns
Recent local detail
Early requirements and rationale
Short recency-dominated loops


Typed artifact
Declared fields, validation, diffability
Undeclared nuance
Bounded delegation and merge


Shared store/blackboard
Common named state
Races, stale reads, write conflicts
Coordinated tasks with ownership


Retrieved handoff
Just-in-time relevance
Retrieval misses and permission errors
Large evidence collections



A summary can be fluent and incomplete. Suppose a user says “on-premises only” early in a long trace. The researcher later compares five services and a summarizer emits “prefers a secure enterprise option.” The writer may recommend a hosted product while believing it respected the handoff. A typed requirement field—deployment: on_prem_only—fails validation if absent and survives paraphrase.
A useful delegation envelope includes:

parent run, task, and worker identity;
objective and excluded work;
input snapshot or evidence handles;
output schema and acceptance predicate;
allowed tools, data, destinations, and effects;
token, call, time, and hop budgets;
deadline and cancellation behavior;
provenance and confidence requirements;
where to write the artifact and who may merge it.

The worker cannot mint new authority. If the parent delegates read-only access to three files, a worker’s plan to “also update the database” is a rejected proposal. Child budgets are carved from a parent-owned total so spawning more workers cannot evade cost or action limits.
Use isolated context when specialization, privacy, or injection containment matters. Give a payroll reviewer only payroll policy and a redacted case; give a code reviewer read-only repository access. Use shared context only for state every participant genuinely needs, and prefer append-only evidence or proposals over mutable prose.
Shared files work well as handoff artifacts because they are addressable, bounded, and inspectable. A worker writes worker-2.json; the orchestrator reads it after completion and verifies its schema and evidence. The file is not automatically trustworthy because it resides in the same workspace. It is another agent’s output.
Keep conversational coordination small. Chat is suitable for clarification and status. Durable facts, task claims, outputs, and decisions belong in structured artifacts or an event store. Otherwise a reconnect or compaction turns “the agent said it finished” into the only completion record.
Memory follows the same ownership. Private working memory belongs to a worker thread. A shared semantic record goes through the Chapter 18 memory policy. Do not concatenate every worker’s transcript into a permanent team memory; doing so amplifies poisoning, retention, and deletion problems.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.6 Orchestrator–worker and parallel fan-out
The most useful production topology is often one orchestrator with bounded workers. The orchestrator decomposes the objective, issues task briefs, watches global budgets, validates reports, and synthesizes the answer. Workers perform independent searches, analyses, or reviews. They return artifacts; they do not decide the parent task is complete.

flowchart TB
    User["objective + constraints"] --> O["orchestrator\nplan, budget, terminate"]
    O --> B1["typed brief A"]
    O --> B2["typed brief B"]
    O --> B3["typed brief C"]
    subgraph Isolated["isolated workers — read/propose only"]
      B1 --> W1["worker 1"]
      B2 --> W2["worker 2"]
      B3 --> W3["worker 3"]
    end
    W1 --> A1["report A + evidence"]
    W2 --> A2["report B + evidence"]
    W3 --> A3["report C + evidence"]
    A1 --> Gate["schema, scope, provenance, version gate"]
    A2 --> Gate
    A3 --> Gate
    Gate --> Merge["single-writer merge"]
    Merge --> Verify["outcome verifier"]
    Verify --> Answer["answer / commit / escalation"]
    W1 -. no direct global write .-> Merge
    W2 -. no direct global write .-> Merge
    W3 -. no direct global write .-> Merge





flowchart TB
    User["objective + constraints"] --> O["orchestrator\nplan, budget, terminate"]
    O --> B1["typed brief A"]
    O --> B2["typed brief B"]
    O --> B3["typed brief C"]
    subgraph Isolated["isolated workers — read/propose only"]
      B1 --> W1["worker 1"]
      B2 --> W2["worker 2"]
      B3 --> W3["worker 3"]
    end
    W1 --> A1["report A + evidence"]
    W2 --> A2["report B + evidence"]
    W3 --> A3["report C + evidence"]
    A1 --> Gate["schema, scope, provenance, version gate"]
    A2 --> Gate
    A3 --> Gate
    Gate --> Merge["single-writer merge"]
    Merge --> Verify["outcome verifier"]
    Verify --> Answer["answer / commit / escalation"]
    W1 -. no direct global write .-> Merge
    W2 -. no direct global write .-> Merge
    W3 -. no direct global write .-> Merge




Figure 20.2: How does orchestrator–worker parallelism preserve isolation and single-writer ownership?





Parallel fan-out changes critical-path latency from a sum toward a maximum:

L_{fanout} \approx L_{plan} + \max_i L_{worker_i} + L_{merge}.

The total compute remains approximately the sum of worker compute plus planning and merging. Parallelism buys time, not free reasoning. Tail latency depends on the slowest required worker. Retries, rate limits, shared bottlenecks, and stragglers can erase the gain.
Shard boundaries should minimize cross-worker dependence. “Search sources A, B, and C independently” is strong. “Worker 1 invents the plan, worker 2 interprets its summary, worker 3 fixes both” is a serial chain with lossy links. If results must be combined, specify compatible schemas and stable entity identifiers before dispatch.
Fan-out needs bounds:

maximum workers per parent and per tenant;
maximum nesting depth and handoff count;
token, tool-call, wall-time, and monetary sub-budgets;
cancellation propagation and orphan cleanup;
deadline behavior for partial results;
concurrency caps for downstream systems;
deterministic tie-breaking and a single completion owner.

The orchestrator should decide whether partial success is usable. A research report might publish three of four independent sections with a visible gap. A compliance decision may require every shard and fail closed. Encode quorum and required-shard rules rather than asking the model whether the answer “seems complete.”
Workers can use different models or tools when specialization is measured. A cheap extraction model may process tables while a stronger model synthesizes. Heterogeneity may improve diversity, but it complicates calibration, security review, capacity, and attribution. Start homogeneous unless the eval demonstrates a slice-specific reason.
The artifact uses deterministic workers to isolate orchestration mechanics. Each consumes 220 token units and five latency units. Three run concurrently; planning costs two latency units and merge costs three. The team and single-agent baseline each consume 1,140 token units, but the modeled critical path is ten versus twenty. This is an example of a justified benefit—latency on independent read work—not a claim that three agents reason better.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.7 Handoffs, topologies, and classical coordination
Topology determines who can send work, share state, and end the run.










Topology
Routing owner
Context path
Strength
Characteristic failure




Star/supervisor
Central parent
Worker ↔︎ parent
Budgets and attribution
Parent bottleneck


Direct handoff
Current agent
Agent → agent
Natural specialization chain
Telephone-game drift


Tree/hierarchy
Parent at each level
Up/down branches
Scales decomposition
Hidden cost and depth explosion


Blackboard
Shared scheduler/state
Artifact store
Flexible contributors
Races and stale state


Peer network
Any peer
Arbitrary graph
Adaptive coordination
Cycles, authority spread, hard termination



Use the smallest topology that expresses the dependency graph. A star is easier to observe and contain than a peer mesh. Hierarchy helps only if branches remain independent and sub-orchestrators receive real budgets. Direct handoff is useful for an ownership transfer, such as intake to a specialized regulated workflow, but the handoff must carry identity, constraints, and acceptance state.
Classical multi-agent and distributed-systems ideas are more useful than new persona names.
Contract Net coordination announces a task, receives bids, awards work, and evaluates results. A modern router can use deterministic capability metadata, cost, deadline, and load instead of free-form agents persuading one another. The parent still verifies the winner’s output.
A blackboard lets specialized processes read and contribute to shared problem state. Make entries typed and append-only where possible. Use claims or leases for work ownership, versions for reads, and idempotent completion records. A shared chat log is not a concurrency protocol.
The single-writer principle is central. Parallel agents may inspect, test, and propose, while one owner commits a file, account, or deployment under a version check. If two code agents edit the same file independently, the issue is not how to make them debate the merge. Change the shape: parallel reviewers return findings; one writer applies them.
Conflicts become observations. A rejected compare-and-swap tells the orchestrator that state changed; it reloads, replans, or escalates. Last-write-wins hides which evidence and approval were overwritten. Chapter 26 provides the durable effect ledger and reconciliation for external writes.
Role specialization should follow authority and context, not theater. “Database migration reviewer with schema-read and test tools” is a real role. “Optimist agent” and “pessimist agent” are sampling prompts unless repeated evaluation shows complementary error. Mirroring an organization chart usually imports human communication overhead without the tacit knowledge that makes the human organization function.
For remote ownership, use the A2A task boundary from Chapter 19. Do not teach the remote agent your internal framework state. Send a task brief and receive typed artifacts. The parent remains responsible for using them safely.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.8 Debate, ensembles, and emergent behavior
Multiple model calls can generate diverse candidates, critique an answer, vote, or debate. These are test-time inference patterns before they are production organizations.
Independent sampling plus aggregation works when errors are not perfectly correlated and an answer can be verified or voted. Self-consistency is the simplest case. It needs no agents with identities or persistent chat. Spend the same compute first on best-of-N, verifier-guided search, or a stronger model as developed in Chapter 8.
Debate exposes candidate reasoning to peers over several rounds. It can surface missed evidence, but it also creates anchoring, conformity, verbosity, and persuasive-error failure modes. A majority can converge on the same injected premise. More turns are not monotonically better, and a judge may reward rhetorical agreement over correctness.
Use debate as an evaluated subroutine when the task has independent perspectives and a final verifier. Bound rounds, preserve original evidence, prevent debaters from rewriting one another’s sources, and compare with equally priced independent samples. Do not deploy a permanent council because one benchmark improved without a compute-matched baseline.
Ensembles gain most when channels differ in a meaningful way: model family, data, retrieval source, tool, or inductive bias. Five copies of one model with near-identical context have correlated blind spots. Diversity without aggregation quality can lower performance because the final synthesizer must discriminate more plausible errors.
Emergence and game-theoretic language can help analyze behavior, not replace a contract. Local incentives, information asymmetry, repeated interaction, and shared resources may produce specialization, collusion, free riding, or escalation. An agent that appears cooperative in a simulation has not acquired a deployable service-level guarantee.
The transferable lessons from social simulations and role-playing systems are modest but useful:

environment and action constraints shape behavior more than character prose;
memory changes repeated interaction;
communication topology changes which errors propagate;
incentives and termination conditions need explicit ownership;
observed group behavior may disappear under a new model or prompt.

Multi-agent RL and co-training can teach policies that anticipate peers instead of prompting independent models to cooperate at inference. That is a different mechanism, introduced in Chapter 23. This chapter evaluates inference-time systems as deployed software.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
section: 20.9 MAST: classify and attribute team failures
text:
20.9 MAST: classify and attribute team failures
“The team failed” is too coarse to guide a fix. MAST, the Multi-Agent System Failure Taxonomy, organizes 14 observed failure modes into three categories. Its initial study used expert annotation and reported strong inter-annotator agreement. The exact prevalence depends on the systems and dataset; the vocabulary is useful because it directs attention to specification, coordination, and verification rather than blaming every outcome on model capability.









Category
Code
Failure mode
Engineering signal




Specification/system design
1.1
Disobey task specification
Output violates an explicit constraint



1.2
Disobey role specification
Worker uses authority or work outside its brief



1.3
Step repetition
Same work or message cycle repeats



1.4
Loss of conversation history
Required earlier state disappears



1.5
Unaware of termination conditions
Team continues after success or cannot decide to stop


Inter-agent misalignment
2.1
Conversation reset
Dialogue unexpectedly restarts and loses progress



2.2
Fail to ask for clarification
Agent guesses across an ambiguous handoff



2.3
Task derailment
Interaction drifts from the objective



2.4
Information withholding
Agent fails to pass material evidence



2.5
Ignored other agent’s input
Received contribution is not incorporated



2.6
Reasoning–action mismatch
Declared plan and executed action diverge


Task verification
3.1
Premature termination
System ends before required objectives are met



3.2
No or incomplete verification
Required checks are missing



3.3
Incorrect verification
The verifier approves bad work or rejects correct work



One trace can contain several modes. “Step repetition” describes behavior; the root cause may be a missing parent hop budget or completion event. Do not treat taxonomy labels as causal proof. Use them to cluster failures, inspect first divergence, and choose an owned intervention.
Attribution adds who and when. Record agent identity, model and prompt version, task brief, state snapshot, incoming artifact identifiers, proposed action, gate decision, observation, and outgoing artifact. Find the earliest decisive step after which the run cannot recover under normal policy. Later agents may amplify an error without originating it.
The artifact’s poisoned worker omits an evidence identifier. Its finding happens to be correct, but the handoff contract requires provenance. The validator returns:
{
  "category": "inter-agent misalignment",
  "agent": "worker-2",
  "step": "handoff",
  "reason": "missing provenance"
}
This coarse category corresponds most closely to information withholding. The run status is contained, not completed: a gate prevented an unsupported answer, but the user’s task remains unsatisfied. Containment success and task success must be separate metrics.
Automated taxonomy judges require calibration. A model may infer motives or hidden state not present in the trace. Maintain human-reviewed examples, permit multiple labels, report per-mode precision/recall, and keep the raw evidence span. Chapter 22 supplies judge and statistical discipline.

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
section: 20.10 Contain error cascades and cross-agent attacks
text:
20.10 Contain error cascades and cross-agent attacks
Every worker output is untrusted input, even when all workers run in one application. A mistaken premise, poisoned document, or malicious instruction can cross several model calls and acquire credibility through repetition.

sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant D as Retrieved document
    participant W as Worker 2
    participant A as Report artifact
    participant G as Merge gate
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant E as Effect tool
    D->>W: evidence plus injected instruction
    W->>A: confident finding, provenance omitted
    A->>G: typed report
    G->>G: validate schema, scope, evidence, version
    alt fail closed
        G-->>O: attributed rejection: missing provenance
        O-->>O: retry, omit shard, or escalate
    else unchecked propagation
        G-->>O: accepted claim
        O->>E: action based on laundered premise
    end





sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant D as Retrieved document
    participant W as Worker 2
    participant A as Report artifact
    participant G as Merge gate
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant E as Effect tool
    D->>W: evidence plus injected instruction
    W->>A: confident finding, provenance omitted
    A->>G: typed report
    G->>G: validate schema, scope, evidence, version
    alt fail closed
        G-->>O: attributed rejection: missing provenance
        O-->>O: retry, omit shard, or escalate
    else unchecked propagation
        G-->>O: accepted claim
        O->>E: action based on laundered premise
    end




Figure 20.3: Where can one poisoned worker report be stopped before it becomes a team conclusion or effect?





The merge boundary should validate:

schema and bounded size;
worker and task identity;
allowed shard, subject, and target;
evidence identifiers and source policy;
artifact and source versions;
unsupported instructions or executable content;
confidence and conflict state;
signature or integrity metadata for remote peers.

Do not paste worker reports into the orchestrator’s system prompt. Delimit them as data. Preserve quotations and source handles so the parent can inspect evidence rather than trust a summary. A worker may recommend an action but cannot sign an approval or elevate the parent’s credentials.
Topology affects blast radius. A star routes every cross-worker claim through a validation point. A peer mesh lets an infection propagate laterally. Hierarchy can contain branches if sub-orchestrators have disjoint permissions; it can also launder content through several summaries. Give one agent final effect authority and keep other agents read-only or proposal-only when possible.
The “chained low-risk actions” problem becomes worse with teams. Worker A reads a secret, worker B opens an external channel, and worker C combines both even if no individual tool looks high-risk. Policy evaluates information flow and aggregate run authority, not only each call. Chapter 24 develops taint, injection, and least-privilege defenses.
Cross-agent denial of service also matters. A malicious or confused worker can spawn children, bounce handoffs, emit huge artifacts, hold a required shard, or force repeated clarification. Parent-owned fan-out, depth, size, deadline, and total-token limits prevent local decisions from expanding the run indefinitely.
Verification should be independent of the producer’s claim. Re-run tests, query the source of truth, compare versions, or use a separately controlled verifier. Asking the same worker “are you sure?” often preserves the premise that caused the error.

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.11 Economics and cost-matched evaluation
Multi-agent cost is not simply agent count times one call. Each loop re-reads instructions, tools, conversation, and artifacts; parents also read worker outputs and may retry. Context topology determines repeated input.
For workers i=1\ldots n and a parent, a useful accounting identity is

C_{team}=C_{plan}+\sum_i(C_{worker,i}+C_{handoff,i})+C_{merge}+C_{verify}+C_{retry}.

Break each term into input, output, cached input, tool, and infrastructure units. Attribute them to the parent run and task slice. A fixed “cost per agent” obscures long traces and large tool catalogs.






Figure 20.4: At equal token budget, this decomposable fixture buys latency rather than extra quality.



The build’s result is intentionally restrained. The team does not score higher. It returns the same three evidence-backed findings with the same measured token units. Its twofold modeled speedup comes from three independent five-unit workers running concurrently. A sequential task would not get that benefit.
Compare at least these baselines:

deterministic workflow;
one agent with all relevant context;
one agent with retrieval, tool search, and compaction;
deterministic parallel workers with a non-model merge;
one orchestrator with model workers;
richer handoffs or peer coordination only if earlier variants fail.

For each, report task success, slice floors, total tokens/spend, wall-clock distribution, tool calls, retries, handoffs, reviewer work, and forbidden effects. Use the same task snapshots, models, tool state, concurrency limits, and success predicates. Run enough repetitions for stochastic variance.
Evaluate milestones as well as outcomes. Did decomposition cover the requirements? Did every required worker finish? Did reports pass schema and provenance? Did the merge preserve constraints? Did the verifier check the result? These signals localize gains and regressions.






NoteLandscape 2026 — measured scaling replaced “more agents” as the default story



Verified 2026-07-19: Anthropic’s engineering report says its agents used roughly four times the tokens of chat interactions and its production multi-agent research system roughly fifteen times, while describing gains on breadth-first research. These are vendor-reported workload figures, not constants. Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems reports controlled comparisons across many configurations and finds strong dependence on task parallelism and topology. A 2026 equal-thinking-budget study, Single-Agent LLMs Outperform Multi-Agent Systems on Multi-Hop Reasoning, reports single-agent advantages on coupled multi-hop tasks under its tested conditions.
SDK names, hosted team features, model families, and quantitative rankings are volatile. Verify live: recheck paper revisions, exact budgets, task definitions, model versions, and production reports before quoting a multiplier or recommending a framework.


The decision artifact should state why the team exists, which simpler variants were tested, the budget, the residual gain, failure distribution, rollback trigger, and owner. A well-supported rejection of multi-agent architecture is a successful engineering result.
Chapter 26 turns this accounting into platform cost and capacity controls. Here the invariant is simple: complexity must earn measurable utility under matched resources.

20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.12 Build
Run the deterministic experiment from newbook:
python code/ch20/fixture.py --plot assets/figures/ch20-cost-latency.svg
python -m pytest tests/test_ch20_team_runtime.py -q
The central result should be:
{
  "cost_matched": {
    "single": {"score": 1.0, "tokens": 1140, "latency_units": 20},
    "team": {"score": 1.0, "tokens": 1140, "latency_units": 10},
    "team_speedup": 2.0,
    "same_answer": true
  },
  "fault_injection": {
    "status": "contained",
    "failure": {
      "category": "inter-agent misalignment",
      "agent": "worker-2",
      "step": "handoff",
      "reason": "missing provenance"
    }
  }
}
Work through one runtime in six passes.
1. Defend the split. Read CORPUS: three independent regions, one finding each. Call should_split with each condition false in turn. Write down why a coupled chain or an unmeasured single-agent baseline should veto this architecture.
2. Inspect task briefs. In Orchestrator.run, verify each worker receives one shard, one read tool, an output schema, and budgets. Add an unauthorized write tool and make the worker reject the brief.
3. Follow file handoffs. Each worker writes one immutable JSON report. Inspect the temporary artifacts by replacing TemporaryDirectory with a test-owned directory. Add a fourth shard and confirm the orchestrator, not a worker, still owns the final answer.
4. Reproduce the cost match. The team spends 180 planning units, three times 220 worker units, and 300 merge units. Pass that measured total to SingleAgent. Change one budget and make the comparison report itself as unmatched rather than silently plotting it.
5. Break provenance. Set poison_worker="worker-2". The finding remains textually correct, but the evidence tuple is empty. Follow the first decisive failure from report creation to gate rejection. Change the gate to accept it and observe how unsupported content becomes a final answer.
6. Change task shape. Replace the three shards with a dependent chain where each input requires the prior result. Model the team’s communication latency and repeated context. The single agent should now win or the splitter should veto the run.

20.12.1 Acceptance checks

All three split conditions are necessary.
Worker authority is narrower than parent authority.
Reports are typed, immutable, scoped, and provenance-bearing.
Exactly one component merges final state.
Single and team runs use equal measured token units.
Parallel latency uses the maximum worker path, not the sum.
Missing provenance is contained and attributed; it is not called task success.



20.12.2 Honesty note
The workers are deterministic functions, token and latency units are modeled, and the fixture has perfectly independent shards. It isolates decomposition, accounting, handoff, and attribution properties; it does not estimate the quality of a particular model or hosted agent product. Re-run the same contract with a local model and repeated task suite before making a deployment decision.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.13 What endures, what changes
What endures. Multiple agents are a decomposition choice, not a maturity level. A team needs parallelizable work, a useful boundary, and a measured failure of a simpler baseline. Context architecture determines whether information survives a handoff. Typed artifacts beat conversational summaries for bounded delegation. Parent-owned budgets, isolated authority, validation, and a single writer contain distributed failure. Evaluate under matched compute and report containment separately from task success. Attribute the first decisive error by agent and step.
What changes. Model coordination ability, long-context quality, SDK team features, learned topologies, co-trained policies, and benchmark results will move. Named agent roles will come and go. The stable questions are: why split, what travels, who may write, what is the total budget, where can an error propagate, how is success verified, and does the team beat one well-equipped agent?
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.14 Exercises

Compression trap. Take a real long trace containing five hard constraints and three rejected alternatives. Compare full trace, rolling summary, last-k, and typed-artifact handoffs. Measure retained constraints and token units.
Tail latency. Give three workers different latency distributions and retry policies. Simulate the fan-out p50 and p95, then choose a deadline and partial-result policy.
Single writer. Convert two parallel code writers into parallel reviewers plus one writer. Add a stale-version conflict and compare regression rate.
MAST injection suite. Inject one failure from each MAST category. Attribute the earliest decisive agent and step, and measure the classifier against human labels.
Topology defense. Compare star, direct handoff, hierarchy, and blackboard for an incident-response task. State context, permission, termination, and write ownership for each; select one.
Cost curve. Repeat the build at three total budgets and two task shapes. Plot quality, latency, and cost; identify the range, if any, where a team has positive \Delta U.
Design rejection. A product manager proposes five persona agents for a tightly coupled refactor. Write MULTI_AGENT_DECISION.md with the baseline, veto condition, evidence, and a simpler alternative.

title: 20  Multi-Agent Systems: Architectures, Failure Modes, and Economics [F+S]
text:
20.15 Notes and sources
The decision and cost discipline builds on AI Agents That Matter, the controlled experiments in Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, and the equal-budget analysis in Single-Agent LLMs Outperform Multi-Agent Systems on Multi-Hop Reasoning. The production orchestrator–worker example and reported token magnitudes come from Anthropic’s dated engineering account, How we built our multi-agent research system.
The failure vocabulary follows Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?, with failure-attribution motivation from Which Agent Causes Task Failures and When?. Classical coordination predates LLM agents: Reid Smith’s Contract Net Protocol and the Hearsay-II blackboard architecture remain useful foundations. Multiagent Debate motivates the debate discussion; Chapter 8 remains the canonical home for verifier-guided inference. Chapter 24 develops cross-agent injection and Chapter 23 develops agent-policy training.

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
text:
21.3 What you will build






TipOne coding agent, six repositories, one honest verifier



The artifact under code/ch21/ materializes six tiny repository fixtures, observes a structural repository map, accepts a constrained edit proposal, emits a unified-diff receipt, runs the repository’s tests, and feeds a failed test back into a bounded repair loop. One task requires repair; another proposes a path outside the workspace and is rejected before execution. The proposal source is scripted so the default run is local and deterministic. Success means six resolved tasks, seven test runs, eight proposals, one rejected edit, no edited tests, and no write outside an isolated temporary workspace.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
text:
21.4 One loop, three application contracts
A coding agent edits the right function, reports that the bug is fixed, and leaves one regression failing. A research agent produces a polished report whose citations lead to relevant pages but do not support the adjacent claims. A browser agent says an order was cancelled after clicking a stale control, while the order remains active. These look like different product failures. Structurally, they are the same failure: the system accepted the model’s account of success instead of checking an authoritative postcondition.
“Agent application” should therefore mean more than a model plus domain-flavored tools. It is a contract over six application-specific elements:

Observation: which representation of the current environment enters the loop, and how fresh it is.
Proposal vocabulary: which next actions the model may express.
Execution boundary: which component validates and performs a proposal.
Verifier: which observation can establish the requested postcondition.
Commit point: when provisional work becomes externally meaningful.
Recovery path: how the system repairs, reverses, compensates, or escalates after failure.

The generic loop from Chapter 16 remains intact. What changes is the body attached to each seam.









Contract element
Coding
Deep research
Computer use




Primary observation
Repository snapshot, issue, build metadata, test output
Research brief, source records, claim ledger, contradictions
Live DOM or accessibility tree, application state, optional screenshot


Proposal vocabulary
Inspect, patch, run a bounded command, test
Search, browse, extract, calculate, challenge, draft
Navigate, select semantic element, type, invoke command, request takeover


Strongest ordinary verifier
Deterministic tests and static checks against final files
Claim–evidence support, coverage, provenance, contradiction review
Application state, receipt, or backend record after the action


Commit point
Accepted patch, branch, or merge
Reviewed report or downstream decision
Externally visible state change


Recovery
Revert patch and rerun
Correct or retract claims; reopen search
Undo when supported; otherwise compensate or escalate


Characteristic risk
Test gaming and repository damage
Plausible unsupported synthesis
Acting on stale or misperceived state




flowchart LR
    O["Observe"] --> P["Propose"] --> X["Validate and execute"] --> V["Verify"]
    V -->|"not done"| O
    V -->|"postcondition holds"| C["Commit"]
    K["Coding: repo + tests"] -. "supplies contract" .-> O
    R["Research: ledger + claim support"] -. "supplies contract" .-> O
    U["Computer use: live UI + state receipt"] -. "supplies contract" .-> O





flowchart LR
    O["Observe"] --> P["Propose"] --> X["Validate and execute"] --> V["Verify"]
    V -->|"not done"| O
    V -->|"postcondition holds"| C["Commit"]
    K["Coding: repo + tests"] -. "supplies contract" .-> O
    R["Research: ledger + claim support"] -. "supplies contract" .-> O
    U["Computer use: live UI + state receipt"] -. "supplies contract" .-> O




Figure 21.1: The invariant loop stays fixed; each application supplies a different observation, action language, verifier, and commit boundary.





The figure’s design claim is narrow: reuse the loop kernel, not the entire application architecture. A universal act() tool or universal “critic” hides the distinctions that determine whether an application can prove completion.
This comparison also explains why capability transfers unevenly. A model can be excellent at proposing code and weak at manipulating a desktop because its observation representation and grounding problem changed. It can browse broadly and still produce unreliable research because discovering a source and proving a claim are separate operations. Model choice matters, but the application contract determines how much unverified model judgment reaches the user.

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
section: 21.5 The action–verification boundary is the architecture
text:
21.5 The action–verification boundary is the architecture
Every application should separate provisional state from committed state. A proposed patch in an isolated checkout is provisional. Extracted notes in an evidence ledger are provisional. Text entered into a form before submission is provisional. The model may change provisional state repeatedly because mistakes remain cheap. Promotion to committed state requires an application-owned rule.
Let (s_0) be the observed starting state, (a) a proposed action, and (s_1) the state observed after execution. The model predicts that (a) advances goal (g). The application decides completion with a verifier:
[ V(s_0, a, s_1, g) {,,}. ]
The third value matters. A timeout does not prove a code change wrong. An inaccessible article does not prove a research claim false. A missing confirmation banner does not prove that a backend write failed. Collapsing unknown into either success or failure causes duplicate actions, false completion, and unsafe retries. Typed uncertainty lets the policy inspect, retry safely, reconcile, or ask a human.
A useful verifier has four properties:

Authority: it measures the state that matters, rather than the model’s narration of that state.
Specificity: it corresponds to the user’s postcondition, not merely to an intermediate action.
Freshness: it is taken after the proposed action and identifies the relevant environment version.
Independence: it is difficult for the same proposal to corrupt both the work and the check.

Tests are strong only when they capture the requirement and remain read-only to the agent. Citations are strong only when they support the associated atomic claim and preserve source provenance. A UI banner is strong only when the product defines it as an authoritative outcome; a backend record or transaction receipt is often better.
Verification strength and reversibility jointly determine autonomy. When both are strong, the loop may iterate without frequent intervention. When the verifier is weak but the work is reversible, the agent can draft or stage work for review. When a verifier is weak and an effect is hard to reverse, human approval belongs before the commit point. Chapter 24 owns adversarial controls and Chapter 27 owns operational review queues; this chapter uses that policy boundary to compare applications.
Freshness deserves special treatment. Repositories change between inspection and patch application. Sources are updated or disappear between extraction and publication. Web pages rerender between a snapshot and a click. Bind a proposal to the version it observed: commit hash and file digest, source URL plus retrieval time and content hash, page or element reference plus application state. Re-observe before a consequential commit. This is the application-level form of the approval-to-execution race from Chapter 17.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
section: 21.6 Coding: treat the repository as executable state
text:
21.6 Coding: treat the repository as executable state
Code generation becomes coding agency when the model can inspect and change a repository, execute programs, interpret failures, and iterate. The repository is not a bag of source text. It is a stateful environment containing source, tests, dependency locks, generators, build scripts, migrations, conventions, and version history. The useful output is not a persuasive explanation. It is a minimal patch that satisfies the intended behavior without breaking protected behavior.
The Agent–Computer Interface (ACI) determines what the model can express and observe. A single unrestricted shell can represent almost any operation, but it offers weak intent typing, noisy output, and a large blast radius. A practical coding surface combines a few bounded capabilities:

structural repository mapping and targeted file inspection;
literal, range-based, or patch-based edits with path controls;
search over names and references;
bounded commands for tests, formatting, static analysis, and build steps;
version-control inspection and diff receipts;
concise observations that retain the failing assertion, command, exit status, and relevant location.

A repository map is an orientation layer, not a replacement for retrieval. Paths, module summaries, top-level symbols, dependency edges, and changed-file status help the model choose where to inspect. Complete contents should be loaded on demand. Chapter 15 owns the algorithms for locating relevant code in large repositories; the application concern here is that every retrieved fragment is tied to a repository revision and that edits are applied to the same revision.
Edit format is another capability surface. Whole-file replacement is simple but expands diffs and overwrites concurrent changes. Line numbers are compact but become stale after prior edits. Free-form search-and-replace is readable but ambiguous when the target repeats. Unified diffs express locality and produce familiar review artifacts, yet a production patch applier must handle context drift and parser edge cases. Structured edits can name a file, anchor, expected old text, replacement, and observed file digest. There is no universal winner: measure malformed edits, unintended changed lines, repair turns, and task completion for the model and repository distribution you deploy.
The teaching artifact uses an exact single-occurrence replacement while emitting a unified diff as its receipt. Its model-facing proposal is intentionally small:
# coding_agent.py — the proposal and trace contracts
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Edit:
    path: str
    old: str
    new: str
    reason: str

@dataclass
class TaskResult:
    task_id: str
    resolved: bool = False
    proposals: int = 0
    test_runs: int = 0
    rejected_edits: int = 0
    events: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
The executor, not the model, owns the invariants. It resolves the path against the workspace, refuses test-file edits, requires one exact anchor, parses the resulting Python, writes the file, and returns a reviewable receipt:
# coding_agent.py — the constrained edit seam
def apply_edit(root: Path, edit: Edit) -> str:
    target = safe_path(root, edit.path)
    if not target.is_file():
        raise EditRejected(f"file does not exist: {edit.path}")
    before = target.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    occurrences = before.count(edit.old)
    if occurrences != 1:
        raise EditRejected(f"old text must occur once, found {occurrences}: {edit.path}")
    after = before.replace(edit.old, edit.new, 1)
    try:
        ast.parse(after)
    except SyntaxError as exc:
        raise EditRejected(f"edit creates invalid Python: {exc.msg}") from exc
    target.write_text(after, encoding="utf-8")
    return "".join(
        difflib.unified_diff(
            before.splitlines(keepends=True), after.splitlines(keepends=True),
            fromfile=f"a/{edit.path}", tofile=f"b/{edit.path}",
        )
    )
Syntax validity is only an admission check. The authoritative observation is the repository’s test command, executed after the edit. A failed assertion becomes evidence for the next proposal; it is not an invitation to rewrite the assertion.

sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Agent policy
    participant E as Edit executor
    participant R as Isolated repository
    participant T as Test runner
    A->>R: inspect map and relevant files
    A->>E: propose patch bound to observed text
    E->>E: validate path, anchor, syntax
    E->>R: apply provisional edit
    E->>T: run declared tests
    T-->>A: failing assertion and exit status
    A->>E: propose bounded repair
    E->>R: apply repair
    E->>T: rerun tests
    T-->>E: postcondition passes
    E-->>A: commit-eligible diff receipt





sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Agent policy
    participant E as Edit executor
    participant R as Isolated repository
    participant T as Test runner
    A->>R: inspect map and relevant files
    A->>E: propose patch bound to observed text
    E->>E: validate path, anchor, syntax
    E->>R: apply provisional edit
    E->>T: run declared tests
    T-->>A: failing assertion and exit status
    A->>E: propose bounded repair
    E->>R: apply repair
    E->>T: rerun tests
    T-->>E: postcondition passes
    E-->>A: commit-eligible diff receipt




Figure 21.2: A coding agent earns completion from a post-edit test observation; a plausible patch is only provisional.





The shell, edit, and test loop should have separate policy. Read-only inspection can be broad. Build commands need CPU, memory, time, network, and output limits. Edits need path and file-class controls. Tests need an environment the agent cannot redefine. A command that exits zero is not sufficient if it ran the wrong test target, skipped tests, or reused stale build output. Record the exact command, repository revision, dependency image, resource envelope, and test-selection rule.
Repository-level benchmarks such as SWE-bench made an essential contribution: the environment and executable verifier are part of the task. Their scores are not portable model constants. Dataset filtering, issue ambiguity, test adequacy, repository exposure, scaffold, dependency image, CPU and memory limits, timeout, and retry policy all change the construct. The chapter fixture is not a miniature SWE-bench and its six-of-six result makes no capability claim. Chapter 22 owns statistical comparison and release decisions.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




coding_agent.py complete
197 lines total; fewer than 50 shown
A patch cannot escape the workspace or edit tests, and completion requires tests observed after the edit.

21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
section: 21.7 Deep research: build an evidence ledger before prose
text:
21.7 Deep research: build an evidence ledger before prose
Deep research is not “RAG with more searches.” It is a long-horizon production process whose deliverable is a set of claims, qualifications, and source relationships. Retrieval finds potentially useful material; research decides which questions remain open, which evidence is fit for purpose, how sources conflict, and what the final report may assert. Chapter 15 owns adaptive query and retrieval mechanics. This section owns the application state those mechanics must populate.
Begin with a research contract. State the decision the report will inform, scope, jurisdictions or populations, time boundary, acceptable source classes, required comparisons, output form, and stop condition. Decompose the brief into questions whose answers could change the conclusion. A plan should remain editable because early evidence often reveals that the original decomposition was wrong. Plan revision is progress when it is recorded; silent goal drift is not.
The central artifact is an evidence ledger. One record should contain at least:

an atomic candidate claim;
source identifier, stable locator, title, author or publisher, publication date, and retrieval time;
the exact supporting span or structured result, within copyright and privacy limits;
whether the source supports, contradicts, or merely contextualizes the claim;
source class and known conflicts of interest;
units, population, time range, and methodology for quantitative evidence;
transformations or calculations performed by the agent;
confidence and unresolved questions;
the report locations that consume the record.

This representation prevents a common failure: attaching a relevant citation to a sentence that the cited page does not establish. Citation presence, source quality, and claim entailment are three different checks. A corporate homepage may be authoritative for the company’s current product description and poor evidence for an independent performance comparison. A review may orient the search while the final claim requires the underlying study. Two secondary sources repeating the same primary claim are not independent corroboration.

flowchart LR
    B["Decision-bound brief"] --> Q["Atomic questions"]
    Q --> S["Search and browse observations"]
    S --> E["Source records with provenance"]
    E -->|"supports"| C["Atomic claims"]
    E -->|"contradicts"| C
    E -->|"context only"| C
    C --> M["Methods, units, transformations"]
    M --> D["Draft synthesis"]
    C -->|"unsupported or disputed"| G["Open gaps"]
    G --> Q





flowchart LR
    B["Decision-bound brief"] --> Q["Atomic questions"]
    Q --> S["Search and browse observations"]
    S --> E["Source records with provenance"]
    E -->|"supports"| C["Atomic claims"]
    E -->|"contradicts"| C
    E -->|"context only"| C
    C --> M["Methods, units, transformations"]
    M --> D["Draft synthesis"]
    C -->|"unsupported or disputed"| G["Open gaps"]
    G --> Q




Figure 21.3: The ledger makes report claims traceable to support, contradiction, transformations, and unresolved gaps before synthesis.





The ledger changes the control loop. Search is driven by gaps, contradictions, and decision value rather than by a fixed source count. Before adding another query, ask which unresolved claim it could change. Before drafting a conclusion, ask whether each load-bearing claim has suitable evidence and whether contrary evidence was sought. Before stopping, ask whether marginal search is producing new decision-relevant evidence or merely more redundant sources.
Synthesis should operate from ledger records rather than a large undifferentiated transcript. Draft one claim at a time, attach its evidence IDs, preserve qualifications, and distinguish observation from inference. Run a citation audit in both directions: every material factual claim needs adequate support, and every citation must have a declared purpose. Then perform a contradiction pass and a coverage pass against the original research contract. These are semantic checks, so a second model may help triage them, but it does not convert them into ground truth. High-stakes claims still require expert review of the cited material.
Parallel researchers can reduce latency when questions are independent and sources are read-only. They can also duplicate searches, miss shared contradictions, and hand the synthesizer incompatible definitions. Chapter 20 owns orchestration topologies. At the application layer, every worker must write the same evidence schema, use stable question and source IDs, declare search coverage, and return gaps as well as findings. A single researcher is preferable when the question is tightly coupled, the evidence corpus is small, or cross-source interpretation dominates browsing time.
Test-time effort should be allocated by expected information value. More browsing often improves coverage at first, then produces diminishing returns. Set per-question search budgets, reserve effort for contradiction and citation audits, and stop when new searches fail to change the claim ledger across several rounds. A long report is not evidence that the research loop used its budget well.

21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
section: 21.8 Computer use: prefer semantic controls, then bind action to observation
text:
21.8 Computer use: prefer semantic controls, then bind action to observation
A computer-use agent interacts with applications built for humans. Its core difficulty is grounding: mapping an intent such as “cancel this order” onto the correct control in the correct application state, then determining whether the requested state actually changed. Desktop and web environments add partial observability, dynamic layout, focus, modal dialogs, latency, authentication, and irreversible external effects.
Choose the highest-level reliable interface available:

Domain API or purpose-built tool. It exposes typed arguments and machine-readable receipts. Prefer it when it faithfully implements the user’s task.
DOM or accessibility-tree control. Roles, accessible names, labels, states, and element references preserve interaction semantics while reducing visual noise.
Keyboard commands and application scripting. These can be efficient but depend on focus, mode, and platform conventions.
Pixel perception and coordinate action. Use it for canvas content, remote desktops, visual-only controls, and layout-dependent tasks that lack semantic exposure.

The ordering is a decision rule, not a claim that semantic trees are complete. Custom canvases, unlabeled controls, charts, maps, drag-and-drop, remote-rendered applications, and visual confirmation can require pixels. Chapter 29 teaches VLM grounding, screenshot parsing, and coordinate-based GUI perception. This chapter’s responsibility is to mark that fallback explicitly and to keep coordinate actions behind stronger freshness and confirmation checks.
Accessibility trees are useful because they expose roles, names, values, and states close to how assistive technologies perceive an interface. A control can be addressed as “button named Submit” instead of “the blue rectangle around coordinate 812, 644.” Semantic selection is more resilient to window size and minor layout changes, and the action is easier to audit. It still fails when names are duplicated, semantics are absent, virtualized content is not mounted, or the page is stale.
Treat an element reference as a lease on an observation, not a permanent address. A safe interaction step records the application identity, page or document identity, semantic target, observed state, and freshness token. Immediately before a consequential action, re-resolve the target and compare the material state. Immediately afterward, obtain a new observation and test the user’s postcondition. A click receipt proves only that an input event was dispatched; it does not prove that the business action completed.
Computer-use actions fall into three bands:

Navigate and inspect: reversible movement, search, scroll, open, and read.
Stage: fill fields, select options, prepare a draft, or place items in a cart without committing.
Commit: submit, send, purchase, delete, publish, authorize, or change an external record.

The system should expose the band before execution. Stage work can be inspected and summarized to a human. Commit work requires an explicit postcondition, recovery plan, and authority rule. If the interface cannot reveal what will happen, the agent should stop rather than infer semantics from appearance.
Web content is also untrusted input. Instructions embedded in pages, documents, issue text, or tool output do not acquire authority merely because the agent can read them. Bot-detection challenges and authentication failures are environment states, not puzzles to evade. The application should surface them as typed handoff conditions. Chapter 24 develops prompt-injection defenses, credential isolation, and action authorization; here, the architectural rule is to keep observed content separate from the instruction and authority channels.
Verification should prefer resulting state over UI narration. For a sent message, inspect the durable sent-item identifier. For an order change, retrieve the order record or transaction receipt. For a saved document, reopen it and verify its content and location. When the interface exposes no authoritative state, lower autonomy: stage the work, capture the final screen and intended action, and request takeover.

21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
section: 21.9 Rigor where the verifier is weak
text:
21.9 Rigor where the verifier is weak
Coding enjoys an unusually honest verifier when tests are good. Research and computer use often have weaker or delayed feedback. Data and analytics agents make the contrast sharp: SQL can execute successfully while answering the wrong question, and a chart can render perfectly while mixing units or leaking future information. The remedy is not a generic “critic” prompt. It is to turn analytical assumptions and evidence lineage into application state.
For every computed quantity, retain a chain:
[ . ]
The chain should name schema and snapshot versions, filters, joins, missing-data handling, units, denominators, time zones, aggregation grain, and code or query hashes. A number in the final report points back to this chain. Re-executing the query against the same snapshot should reproduce it; checking the current database state answers a different question and must be labeled as such.
Several failure bands deserve explicit gates:

Units and denominators: distinguish dollars from thousands of dollars, events from users, gross from net, percentages from percentage points, calendar days from business days, and local time from UTC.
Population and selection: state inclusion criteria, missingness, survivorship, and whether a sample represents the target population.
Leakage: prevent future labels, post-outcome fields, duplicate entities, or test-set knowledge from entering features or analysis choices.
Multiplicity and researcher degrees of freedom: record how many outcomes, subgroups, windows, models, and stopping rules were tried. Do not present a selected favorable result as if it were the only analysis.
Statistical assumptions: identify independence, distributional, stationarity, and measurement assumptions; report uncertainty and sensitivity where the decision needs them.
Causality: prediction and association do not establish intervention effects. A causal claim requires a defensible design, temporal ordering, treatment and outcome definitions, confounder reasoning, and domain review.

“Verify against database state” means more than asking the model to run the query twice. Compare row counts and key uniqueness at each join, reconcile aggregates to an independent control total, inspect units and schema metadata, and test known edge cases. If the agent writes back, read the authoritative record after the transaction. The same independent-verifier principle from coding applies, but the oracle must be engineered.
Research agents inherit these requirements whenever they execute code or summarize quantitative studies. Preserve whether a number was quoted, calculated, converted, or inferred. Keep original and converted units. Distinguish a paper’s measured result from the agent’s cross-study comparison. If two sources use different populations or definitions, do not average them merely because the column names look alike.
Scientific-discovery agents extend the pattern rather than replacing it. Hypothesis generation is cheap; experimental validity, instrument calibration, protocol adherence, negative results, and expert vetoes remain the hard verifier. A useful co-scientist can propose hypotheses, search prior work, generate analysis code, and queue experiments. It should not silently choose favorable outcomes, modify a protocol after seeing results, or promote a simulated result as an empirical finding. The durable architecture is an evidence and experiment ledger with human-owned commit points. Frontier examples belong in Appendix C; Chapter 31 treats interaction with physical environments.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
text:
21.10 Escalation follows authority and reversibility
An application’s domain label does not determine its risk. A coding agent formatting a local notebook and a coding agent deploying authentication changes share tools but not authority. A research agent preparing a literature map and one recommending clinical treatment have different evidence and review contracts. A browser agent collecting public product descriptions and one moving money inhabit different consequence classes.
Profile deployments along at least four dimensions:









Profile
Typical behavior
Commit rule
Dominant residual risk




Ambient
Observes context and suggests next steps
User initiates every effect
Privacy, distraction, overreliance


Proactive
Starts bounded work from events or schedules
Auto-commit only for reversible, low-impact effects
Wrong trigger, stale context, action volume


Cyber or privileged technical
Changes code, infrastructure, accounts, or security state
Isolated execution plus explicit authority and independent verification
Capability abuse, lateral movement, destructive error


Regulated or professional
Informs healthcare, legal, finance, employment, or similar decisions
Qualified review and evidence retention before consequential use
Invalid advice, disparate harm, noncompliance



These profiles recur in Chapters 24 and 27, where controls and operations are taught. Here they answer one application-design question: where must the provisional-to-committed transition sit? Do not bury that answer in a generic confidence threshold. A model can be confident and wrong, and risk comes from the effect as well as the probability of error.
Escalation is a typed outcome with a useful packet, not abandonment. Include the original goal, current state and version, actions already taken, proposed next effect, evidence and verifier results, uncertainty, remaining budget, and the exact decision requested from the human. For computer use, preserve the screen or semantic snapshot and identify the target. For research, list unsupported and disputed claims. For coding, provide the diff, test command, failures, and changed files.
Conversational customer service is a useful transfer case. Its observation is dialogue plus account state; its action language is a policy-constrained tool set; its verifier is the resulting account state and policy compliance; its recovery is correction, compensation, or escalation. Benchmarks such as τ-bench made final database state and policy following explicit. The architecture is not a fourth mini-book here: it demonstrates that the same application contract transfers when natural-language interaction is the dominant observation surface.
Persistence and parallelism should preserve, not blur, that packet. A fleet of coding workers needs isolated checkouts and a merge authority. Parallel research workers need shared source and claim identities. A resumed computer-use thread must re-observe the live application rather than replay stale element references. Chapter 20 owns coordination; Chapter 26 owns durable execution, retries, and fleet economics. The application rule is that a restart or handoff invalidates ephemeral observations unless the environment proves otherwise.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
section: 21.11 Choose the smallest architecture that can prove completion
text:
21.11 Choose the smallest architecture that can prove completion
The mature reference architectures are valuable because they expose recurring seams, not because every product needs their maximum autonomy. Start from the postcondition and work backward.

Name authoritative success. If the team cannot state what observation proves completion, keep the system advisory.
Identify the cheapest safe action vocabulary. Prefer a typed domain tool over a shell, a semantic element over a coordinate, and a constrained query over arbitrary code when each can express the task.
Separate staging from commit. Let the agent iterate where errors are reversible; narrow the final effect.
Bind every proposal to observed state. Reject stale patches, source assumptions, and UI targets.
Choose a recovery mechanism before autonomy. Revert, retract, undo, compensate, or escalate must be concrete.
Add adaptive looping only where feedback can improve the next action. A deterministic workflow is better when the steps and branches are already known.








Situation
Smallest suitable architecture




One known transform with a deterministic check
Ordinary function or fixed workflow


Several known tools and predictable branches
Typed workflow with model decisions at narrow seams


Unknown number of inspect–act–verify cycles
Bounded single-agent loop


Independent read-heavy subquestions with a common evidence schema
Parallel workers behind one synthesis contract


Weak verifier plus consequential, irreversible action
Draft or stage only; human commits


No authoritative postcondition
Advisory assistant until one is engineered



Do not add planner, coder, critic, browser, and verifier personas merely to match a reference diagram. A separate verifier is valuable only when it has independent evidence or authority. A second model reading the same incomplete context can increase confidence without increasing truth. Likewise, a large tool surface is not sophistication if a smaller domain action would be easier to validate.
Reference architectures should be tested through ablations of seams, not brand names. For coding, compare repository maps, edit formats, test selection, and repair budgets. For research, compare ledger schemas, contradiction search, source restrictions, and synthesis from ledger versus transcript. For computer use, compare semantic and coordinate actions on tasks that genuinely require each, while holding the goal and verifier fixed. Chapter 22 supplies the evaluation mechanics; the application designer supplies the construct and authoritative environment.






NoteLandscape 2026 (dated)



As of 2026-07-19, coding benchmarks are fragmenting into curated, live-updated, held-out, and longer-horizon variants; scores from SWE-bench, Verified, Live, and Pro-style task sets are not interchangeable. Recent primary reports also show that infrastructure configuration and task defects can move results enough to swamp small leaderboard differences. Deep-research products expose plan review, interruption, connected sources, citation-rich reports, and both single- and multi-agent implementations. Browser and desktop systems increasingly mix accessibility-tree control with visual fallback rather than treating either representation as sufficient.
Verify live: 2026-07-19. Recheck the official SWE-bench repository, SWE-bench Live paper, SWE-bench Pro paper, OpenAI benchmark audit, Anthropic infrastructure study, OpenAI deep research documentation, Anthropic’s research-system report, and Playwright’s accessibility-snapshot documentation before adopting named products, benchmark subsets, or current interface claims.

21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
text:
21.12 Build
The integrated artifact consists of coding_agent.py, the committed six-task fixture, and focused tests. It uses only the Python standard library. From the newbook/ directory, run:
python -m unittest discover -s code/ch21/tests -v
python code/ch21/coding_agent.py --max-proposals 3
The second command prints the complete event report as JSON and exits zero. Read it from aggregates to trajectories:

Confirm tasks = 6 and resolved = 6.
Confirm eight proposals produced seven test runs. A rejected edit must not reach the test runner.
Find minimum-repair. Its first syntactically valid patch fails a test; the second patch is proposed only after that observation and passes.
Find workspace-guard. Its first proposal targets ../escape.py; the executor rejects it before any write, then accepts the repository-local repair.
Inspect each execute event. It contains a unified-diff receipt even though the accepted proposal uses a constrained exact-replacement schema.
Inspect each commit event. It appears only after a post-edit test run passes.

Now predict the effect of reducing the proposal budget, then run:
python code/ch21/coding_agent.py --max-proposals 1
This command intentionally exits with status 2. Four of six tasks resolve. The repair task spends its only proposal on a plausible wrong edit, and the workspace task spends its proposal on an invalid path. The result demonstrates why a budget is semantic: “one proposal” constrains both recovery from execution feedback and recovery from rejected actions.
To save the evidence packet, add --output code/ch21/generated/results.json. Generated output is not required by the tests and should not be treated as source. The committed fixture remains the reproducibility anchor.
The tests enforce six properties:

all default tasks resolve with the expected proposal, test, and rejection counts;
the tight budget exposes the two tasks that need a second proposal;
a path traversal proposal is rejected;
test files are read-only;
an ambiguous replacement is rejected;
the repository map exposes structure rather than full source.

The fixture keeps proposal generation deterministic so the application loop is inspectable. To connect a real model, replace only the scripted proposal adapter: give it the issue, repository map, targeted file observations, and last typed failure; require the Edit schema in return. Preserve the executor, test oracle, event schema, task snapshots, and budgets. Then evaluate repeated trials and uncertainty with Chapter 22 rather than comparing one successful demonstration.

21.12.1 Acceptance checks
The build is complete when the focused tests pass, the default suite exits zero with the exact aggregate counts above, the one-proposal run exits nonzero with four resolved tasks, and no fixture writes outside a temporary directory. Review at least one diff and one failed-test observation manually to confirm that the trace explains why the policy repaired.


21.12.2 Honesty note
This is an application-mechanism fixture, not evidence of model coding ability. Scripted proposals stand in for a model so every reader observes the same rejection and repair. Exact replacement avoids implementing a production-grade patch parser. The subprocess is bounded but is not a security sandbox, which is acceptable only because the repository content is committed and trusted. The tests are tiny and known; a six-of-six result must not be compared with SWE-bench or a product workload.
crumbs: Part V — Building Agents
21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
text:
21.13 What endures, what changes
What endures. Application engineering begins with an observation, proposal, execution, verifier, commit, and recovery contract. Keep provisional work separate from committed effects. Bind actions to the state they observed. Prefer authoritative final-state verification to action receipts or model narration. In coding, treat the repository and test environment as state. In research, build claim-level provenance before synthesis. In computer use, prefer semantic interfaces and verify the external state after action. Lower autonomy as verifiability weakens, reversibility falls, or authority grows.
What changes. Model capability, coding scaffolds, edit formats, benchmark variants, browser drivers, accessibility tooling, visual grounding, deep-research products, multi-agent implementations, and useful test-time budgets will change. Removing the Landscape 2026 box leaves the application contracts and decision rules intact.

21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]

title: 21  Agent Applications: Coding, Deep Research, and Computer Use [S]
text:
21.14 Exercises

Draw the six-part application contract for an incident-response agent. Name the authoritative verifier and recovery path for a log query, a firewall-rule proposal, and an applied rule. Explain why the same agent crosses risk profiles across those steps.
Add a seventh fixture task whose first edit is syntactically valid but changes the wrong function. Predict the event counts, add the failing test and two scripted proposals, then verify the prediction without changing the loop.
Replace exact replacement with a line-range edit that includes an observed file digest. Preserve path containment, read-only tests, syntax validation, and diff receipts. Write a test for stale-digest rejection and compare malformed-edit behavior with the original interface.
Design an evidence-ledger schema for a market analysis containing currency conversion, conflicting market-size estimates, and a forecast. Mark which fields support citation entailment, unit reconciliation, methodology comparison, and the boundary between observed fact and inference.
For a travel-booking browser agent, classify twelve actions as inspect, stage, or commit. For each commit action, name an authoritative postcondition, an observation-freshness check, and an undo or compensation path. Identify where Chapter 29’s pixel perception is genuinely necessary.
Take an analytics question from your work and enumerate units, population, snapshot time, filters, join keys, missingness, multiplicity, and causal assumptions. State which checks can be deterministic and which require domain review.
Design a compute-controlled comparison of semantic accessibility-tree actions and pixel-coordinate actions on a web task set. Hold the task, commit policy, and final-state verifier fixed; predeclare which task slice should favor each representation. Use Chapter 22 to specify repetitions and uncertainty.
