title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
section: 27.6 Fan telemetry into four sources of truth
text:
27.6 Fan telemetry into four sources of truth
No single telemetry channel should answer billing, debugging, alerting, and audit. Their reliability, volume, retention, and mutability needs conflict. Use four channels joined by stable identities:

unsampled metrics and counters answer fleet rate, latency distribution, saturation, error, queue age, and SLO questions;
sampled traces explain selected trajectories and dependency timing;
an unsampled usage ledger settles model, tool, and review cost by tenant, run, attempt, and release;
an append-only audit/effect record establishes policy decisions and authoritative external receipts.

Figure 27.2 shows which channel is authoritative for which question. Export can fail independently; a tracing outage must not erase billing or effect truth.

flowchart LR
    RUN["Agent journey<br/>run · attempt · release · tenant surrogate"] --> METRIC[("Unsampled metrics<br/>rates · latency · SLO")]
    RUN --> TRACE[("Sampled traces<br/>trajectory · dependency timing")]
    RUN --> USAGE[("Unsampled usage ledger<br/>tokens · tools · review · cost")]
    RUN --> AUDIT[("Append-only effect audit<br/>policy · approval · receipt")]
    METRIC -. "exemplar / run ID" .-> TRACE
    TRACE -. "diagnostic attribution" .-> USAGE
    AUDIT -. "authoritative outcome" .-> METRIC
    USAGE -. "invoice reconciliation" .-> METRIC





flowchart LR
    RUN["Agent journey<br/>run · attempt · release · tenant surrogate"] --> METRIC[("Unsampled metrics<br/>rates · latency · SLO")]
    RUN --> TRACE[("Sampled traces<br/>trajectory · dependency timing")]
    RUN --> USAGE[("Unsampled usage ledger<br/>tokens · tools · review · cost")]
    RUN --> AUDIT[("Append-only effect audit<br/>policy · approval · receipt")]
    METRIC -. "exemplar / run ID" .-> TRACE
    TRACE -. "diagnostic attribution" .-> USAGE
    AUDIT -. "authoritative outcome" .-> METRIC
    USAGE -. "invoice reconciliation" .-> METRIC




Figure 27.2: Which operational channel is authoritative for rates, diagnosis, cost, and consequential effects?





Metrics aggregate. A counter such as agent_journeys_total{release, outcome_class} is cheap and complete, but it cannot reconstruct one path. Histograms represent TTFT, time per tool, total journey latency, cost, steps, and approval wait without a label for every run. Include bounded dimensions that drive decisions—environment, release, region, task family, autonomy tier, outcome class—not every property available in context.
Sampling controls trace volume. Head sampling decides before observing the trace and can miss rare failures. Tail sampling decides after enough spans arrive and can retain errors, high latency, new releases, safety decisions, or unusual cost. Keep an unbiased probability sample alongside targeted retention so population estimates remain possible. Record the sampling policy; a trace store overrepresenting failures is valuable for debugging but cannot directly estimate fleet failure rate.
The usage ledger is unsampled and append-oriented. Provider usage may arrive late or be adjusted. Record observed units, price-schedule version, currency, provider account, cache class, and parent journey, then reconcile totals against provider invoices. The reference artifact sums app.cost on spans only as a diagnostic demonstration. It explicitly names that result diagnostic_trace_cost; losing a sampled span must not change the bill.
The audit channel is also unsampled but narrower. It records proposed and authorized action, policy version, approval evidence, provider receipt, and outcome. Access should be more restricted than ordinary traces, because consequential records often contain identifiers and decision context. Immutability and independent anchoring belong to Chapter 24.
Replay is a diagnostic mode, not a second production execution. Reconstruct model inputs from retained, authorized content; substitute recorded model and read-tool results where the question is deterministic code; run write tools behind a no-effect adapter; and use the Chapter 26 ledger if an integration test intentionally exercises a sandbox effect. A “replay” that resends a customer email or refund is a new incident.
Reconciliation jobs should detect missing joins: an effect receipt without a run, metered usage without a tenant, a completed journey without an outcome, a trace naming an unknown bundle, or a review decision without an action digest. The operator console can then say whether evidence is absent or the underlying event is absent—a distinction dashboards often blur.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
section: 27.7 Score live behavior asynchronously and slice drift
text:
27.7 Score live behavior asynchronously and slice drift
Offline tasks are controlled and reproducible. Production traffic supplies distribution, dependency, and interaction reality. Use both without putting an expensive, fallible judge in the request path.
Sample eligible completed runs asynchronously. Redact or tokenize content before export, attach the exact rubric and judge versions, and score only when the authoritative outcome is ready. A deterministic grader should inspect state, schema, policy, citations, or receipts before any model judge. The calibrated judge from Chapter 22 handles remaining open-ended qualities. Store component scores and diagnostic reasons, not only one average.
The sample rate is a capacity and detection decision, not a ritual percentage. Rare severe outcomes need targeted or complete monitoring; common quality questions may use a stable probability sample. Oversample a new release, low-confidence runs, high-cost outliers, policy reviews, new task families, and critical tenants, while retaining weights or a separate representative sample for population estimates. The canary-power arithmetic remains in Chapters 22 and 26.
Drift means the live distribution or relationship has changed relative to a reference. Monitor several kinds:

input drift: task family, language, document size, retrieval source, modality, or risk mix changes;
outcome drift: success, groundedness, correction, reversal, or abandonment changes;
score drift: calibrated grader distributions change under a pinned judge;
trajectory drift: steps, tool choice, retries, loop rate, approval requests, or fallback changes;
dependency drift: tool latency/error, provider refusal, corpus freshness, or index coverage changes.

A difference is not automatically harmful. A product launch can create intentional input drift. A score shift can come from a judge update rather than the agent. Compare control and candidate under the same interval, slice by release and tenant, preserve reference windows, and annotate known events. When a detector fires, inspect raw counts, sampling, grader health, and authoritative outcomes before assigning cause.
Fleet averages hide concentrated harm. Suppose tenant A’s mean score improves from 0.9 to 1.0 while tenant B falls from 0.9 to 0.4. The fleet mean drops from 0.9 to 0.7 and may stay above a coarse threshold; tenant B has suffered a 0.5 regression. The artifact’s drift_by_tenant compares per-tenant means and emits only drops above the declared threshold. Production detectors should add uncertainty, minimum counts, multiple-testing discipline, seasonality, and robust distribution metrics.
Thumbs-up rate is not a standalone quality metric. Users self-select whether to rate; response propensity changes with sentiment, interface, and cohort. Combine it with invocation-to-outcome funnels, corrections, reversals, sampled adjudication, and exposure. Chapter 28 owns product reliance and value measurement.
The feedback-to-dataset flywheel is governed promotion: triage a trace, remove or tokenize private data, verify the failure and intended behavior, deduplicate, assign provenance, have a human approve, then add a versioned task or training example. Chapter 23’s data and policy controls apply. Automatically training on every negative reaction creates poisoning, feedback-loop, and deletion problems.
Quality is a metric; safety is a guardrail. A slight quality regression can consume a declared error budget. An unauthorized cross-tenant disclosure or unapproved irreversible effect is an invariant violation and triggers containment regardless of the monthly average.

27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
section: 27.8 Page on error-budget burn, not a green average
text:
27.8 Page on error-budget burn, not a green average
A service-level indicator (SLI) is a measured property of service behavior. A service-level objective (SLO) is a target for that indicator over a window. The error budget is the permitted amount of behavior outside the target. For a 99.5 percent good-journey SLO, the allowed bad-event fraction is 0.5 percent.
Choose indicators at the journey boundary:








User promise
Good event
SLI




useful completion
authoritative task success and grounded response
good completed journeys / eligible journeys


effect integrity
exactly the intended number of authorized effects
journeys with exact effect cardinality / effectful journeys


responsiveness
first useful streamed token within threshold
compliant interactive journeys / interactive journeys


bounded cost
successful journey within task-family cost ceiling
cost-compliant successes / successful journeys


workable oversight
risk review completed before expiry and action remains current
valid reviews / review-required actions



“API availability” remains useful for a component, but it does not substitute for task correctness. Define eligibility and exclusions before measurement. A user cancellation, policy denial, upstream outage, and agent failure may each deserve a different class, yet exclusions should not make genuine user pain disappear. Publish both SLO accounting and total outcome distribution.
For observed good events (g) among (n), target (S), observed bad fraction is (1-g/n) and allowed bad fraction is (1-S). The burn rate is

B=\frac{1-g/n}{1-S}.

At g=9{,}655, n=10{,}000, and S=0.995, burn is 6.9. A burn of 1 consumes exactly the available budget over the SLO window if sustained. A burn above 1 exhausts early. If fraction r of the budget remains in a W-day window, a constant-burn approximation gives

T_{\text{exhaust}}=\frac{Wr}{B}.

With W=30, r=0.95, and B=6.9, this is about 4.1 days.
Figure 27.3 makes urgency legible. The same apparently small failure percentage has different consequences because the promised budget is narrow.






Figure 27.3: Error-budget remaining falls linearly under constant burn rates of 1, 6.9, and 14.4 times. The 6.9-times curve reaches zero near day 4.3 and the 14.4-times curve near day 2.1, while one-times lasts the full 30 days.



Multiwindow, multi-burn-rate alerting balances speed and precision. A fast page can require a high burn over both a long and short confirmation window; a slower page uses a lower burn; sustained mild burn becomes a ticket. The Google SRE Workbook gives a principled derivation and example thresholds. The artifact uses 14.4 times for read-only plus rollout freeze, 6 times for mandatory review plus a page, and 1 time for a ticket. These are teaching values, not universal policy.
Low traffic complicates ratios. One failure can produce enormous burn with little evidence, while a rare safety failure may be too important to wait for volume. Use longer windows, event counts, Bayesian or exact interval context, synthetic probes, and targeted invariant monitoring. State which rule is SLO-budgeted and which is zero tolerance.
Cost per successful task divides total journey cost—including failures—by successful journeys. If failed runs vanish from the numerator, the metric rewards systems for failing early. Track distribution and task mix, not only a mean. Chapter 26 owns the underlying cost ledger; this chapter pages when the user-facing cost objective is threatened.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
section: 27.9 Let evidence lower autonomy and let chaos test the claim
text:
27.9 Let evidence lower autonomy and let chaos test the claim
An error-budget policy is executable governance. Before the bad day, map evidence to automatic and human actions. One example:








Condition
Immediate platform action
Human action




burn below 1
normal declared autonomy
routine review


sustained 1–6
stop rollout expansion
open owned ticket and inspect slices


burn 6–14.4
require review for write actions
page operator; compare release/control


burn at least 14.4
read-only mode; freeze rollout
page incident lead; prepare rollback


safety invariant violated
stop new effects regardless of burn
declare security/safety incident



Self-throttling reduces blast radius before a person understands cause. It can lower step or concurrency budgets, disable optional tools, route to a simpler verified workflow, require human approval, or stop new effects while preserving read-only service. The fallback must have a smaller explicit contract. It must not route around a refusal or discard authorization.
The artifact implements this as a pure decision function so the policy is reviewable and testable. At 6.9 times, it returns require_review_and_page; a safety flag at 0.1 times still returns stop_new_effects. Safety is not an error budget to spend.
Chaos engineering tests whether these controls survive real failure. Begin with a measurable steady-state hypothesis, inject one realistic fault under a bounded blast radius, observe user-facing and invariant outcomes, and abort on declared conditions. It is not random production breakage.
Useful agent drills include:

kill a worker after provider acceptance and assert the Chapter 26 effect count remains one;
inject overload and connection resets, then assert retry and currency budgets cap amplification;
delay a tool beyond the propagated deadline and assert stale actions cannot execute;
corrupt or load an old checkpoint and assert quarantine or compatible migration;
make the trace exporter unavailable and assert requests continue while a telemetry-loss counter fires;
fill the review queue and assert expiry prevents old approval from releasing a changed action;
revoke the effect tool or kill-switch control plane and verify the independent enforcement fallback.

A chaos card names the hypothesis, steady-state indicators, target cohort, fault, start and stop conditions, maximum duration, expected alerts, owner, rollback, and evidence retained. Start in a disposable environment, then a non-effectful or tiny production cohort when justified. Chapter 32’s failure game consumes this skill but does not repeat the incident tabletop taught here.

27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
section: 27.10 Run the incident through both kinds of rollback
text:
27.10 Run the incident through both kinds of rollback
An incident begins when observed behavior threatens users or an invariant, not when the root cause is known. A workable lifecycle is detection, triage and declaration, containment, diagnosis and eradication, recovery, then learning. Roles scale with severity: one incident commander coordinates; an operations lead changes the system; a communications lead keeps stakeholders current; subject experts investigate without each becoming commander.
Figure 27.4 separates containment from recovery and technical from business rollback.

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DETECT: alert, report, or audit mismatch
    DETECT --> DECLARE: classify severity and assign commander
    DECLARE --> CONTAIN: stop new admission/effects, preserve evidence
    CONTAIN --> DIAGNOSE: compare release, tenant, tool, and trajectory
    DIAGNOSE --> TECHNICAL: revert or repair bundle
    DIAGNOSE --> BUSINESS: reconcile executed consequences
    TECHNICAL --> RECOVER
    BUSINESS --> RECOVER
    RECOVER --> VALIDATE: SLI, invariant, and authoritative state checks
    VALIDATE --> RAMP: controlled restoration
    RAMP --> LEARN: blameless postmortem + regression case
    LEARN --> [*]





stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DETECT: alert, report, or audit mismatch
    DETECT --> DECLARE: classify severity and assign commander
    DECLARE --> CONTAIN: stop new admission/effects, preserve evidence
    CONTAIN --> DIAGNOSE: compare release, tenant, tool, and trajectory
    DIAGNOSE --> TECHNICAL: revert or repair bundle
    DIAGNOSE --> BUSINESS: reconcile executed consequences
    TECHNICAL --> RECOVER
    BUSINESS --> RECOVER
    RECOVER --> VALIDATE: SLI, invariant, and authoritative state checks
    VALIDATE --> RAMP: controlled restoration
    RAMP --> LEARN: blameless postmortem + regression case
    LEARN --> [*]




Figure 27.4: What happens in what order when an agent incident has already created external effects?





The kill switch must have defined semantics. “Disable agent” can mean stop new journeys, cancel active model calls, prevent new tool invocations, revoke effect credentials, drain queues, or place tasks in review. The strong containment path stops new admissions and new consequential effects while preserving receipts and read-only diagnosis. It cannot retract a sent message, erase a disclosure, or reverse an already-settled transfer.
That limitation creates two rollbacks:









Rollback
Changes
Examples
Evidence needed




technical
future system behavior
point traffic to last-known-good bundle, revoke tool, restore policy, rebuild poisoned index
release hashes, control-plane events, health and eval evidence


business
consequences already produced
void or credit transaction, correct record, notify affected user, rotate exposed secret, arrange return
effect receipts, affected-object query, owner, compensation result



Incident response cannot wait for perfect diagnosis. Contain based on plausible blast radius, preserve state, and keep a timestamped decision log. Compare candidate versus control, tenant and task slices, provider and tool health, recent bundles, corpus changes, approval behavior, and audit receipts. Avoid replaying hostile input through production effects during investigation.
Mean time to detect is often the hidden failure. Probabilistic quality defects may first appear as user corrections days after release. Asynchronous outcome scoring, drift slices, authoritative-effect reconciliation, and golden probes shorten that gap. Track time from first harmful event—not first alert—to detection, containment, last harmful effect, technical recovery, and business recovery.
Recovery is controlled restoration, not “pods are green.” Verify the user-facing SLI, critical slices, effect invariant, queue state, data consistency, and control health. Ramp with the Chapter 26 delivery path. Retain a comparison cohort when safe and communicate remaining uncertainty.
A blameless postmortem explains the conditions and decisions that made the incident possible without confusing blamelessness with lack of accountability. Include impact, timeline, detection gap, contributing technical and organizational factors, what went well, where controls failed, technical and business recovery, and actions with owners and verification. Promote a reviewed incident case into Chapter 22’s golden regression set. If the incident exposed a missing runtime invariant, also add a deterministic or chaos test. “Be more careful” is not a corrective action.

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
section: 27.11 Operate human review as a queue, not a checkbox
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
text:
27.12 Make telemetry private by construction
Telemetry is a second data product. It often outlives application state, reaches more operators and vendors, and copies prompts, retrieved documents, tool arguments, model output, user IDs, secrets, and decisions into indexes optimized for search. A tracing feature can therefore become the breach.
Adopt content off by default. Emit operation name, timing, model/deployment, token counts, status, bounded task class, release, and pseudonymous join keys without prompt or response bodies. Enable sampled content only for approved environments, cohorts, purposes, roles, and retention windows. Prefer hashes, classifications, lengths, and references to a governed content store over raw duplication.
Redact before span creation, not only in the backend UI. Exporters can fan out to several systems; network buffers and collector logs may already contain the secret before server-side masking. Secret-bearing keys—authorization, API key, access token, cookie, password—are unconditionally removed even when content capture is allowed. Scan string values for common bearer patterns. Treat model-generated output as capable of echoing a secret.
The reference sanitizer enforces three small rules: secret-named fields become [REDACTED], raw tenant ID becomes a short one-way surrogate, and GenAI input/output content becomes [CONTENT_DISABLED] unless capture is explicitly enabled. It also removes bearer values from error messages. The test plants a token in both an authorization attribute and a normal string; neither survives.
Production redaction needs structured parsing, allowlists, validated tokenization, secret scanning, and tests over every instrumentation path. Regex is a last layer, not a complete privacy system. Keep a telemetry data inventory, access control, purpose, residency, retention, deletion path, export list, and breach-response owner. Audit who queries raw-content projects.
“Zero data retention” is a provider contract term, not an end-to-end property. Verify which endpoint, account tier, abuse or safety exception, logging surface, feature, and region it covers. It does not erase your gateway, trace backend, crash dump, evaluation dataset, support export, browser recording, or effect audit. Record the contract revision and test actual integrations.
Prompts and traces may be personal data when they identify or relate to a person. Applicable duties depend on jurisdiction, role, purpose, and sector. Engineering supports counsel and privacy owners by documenting lawful purpose, minimization, access, retention, residency, processors, security, data-subject request reach, deletion lineage, and holds. Chapter 18 owns propagation to memories, embeddings, adapters, caches, and derived learning artifacts. This section owns the operational job that finds and processes telemetry copies.
Privacy-enhancing technologies solve different problems:

differential privacy bounds how much one record can influence a released statistic or trained mechanism under a stated adjacency relation and privacy budget; it does not hide raw inputs from the machine computing the mechanism;
federated or on-device processing keeps raw examples decentralized and aggregates updates or results; updates can still leak information and require secure aggregation, clipping, authentication, and sometimes differential privacy;
confidential computing protects data in use from parts of the host infrastructure through hardware-backed isolated execution and attestation; it does not make application code, prompts, outputs, or authorized recipients trustworthy.

Choose a PET from the threat model. Pair it with minimization, access control, and deletion rather than using it as a privacy label. The model-asset privacy attacks and differential-privacy limitations introduced in Chapter 24 still apply.






NoteLandscape 2026 (dated)



Verify live: 2026-07-19. Appendix C owner: telemetry retention, ZDR, privacy law, and PET deployment support. Provider retention terms, content-capture defaults, regional telemetry storage, self-hosting architectures, masking features, GDPR guidance, and confidential-accelerator support are fast-moving and contract-specific. For example, current Langfuse self-hosting documentation describes its present storage architecture and deployment options, while its masking documentation exposes configurable failure behavior that must be reviewed rather than assumed. Maintain a counsel-reviewed jurisdiction and vendor matrix; never infer production privacy posture from this chapter’s dated examples.

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
text:
27.13 Build
The integrated build has two parts: execute the console, then run the chapter’s single incident tabletop. From newbook/, run:
python -m pytest tests/test_ch27_ops.py -q
python code/ch27/ops_console.py
python code/ch27/render_burn.py \
  --plot assets/figures/ch27-budget-burn.svg
The fixture reports:
spans                         5
traces                        2
links                         1
diagnostic trace cost         0.013
success-and-grounded SLI      0.50   (four-run teaching subset)
exactly-one-effect SLI        0.75   (four-run teaching subset)
p95 TTFT                      400 ms
cost per successful task      0.028
30-day fleet burn             6.9×   (9,655 / 10,000 good; 99.5% target)
days to exhaustion            4.1    (95% budget remaining)
runtime action                require_review_and_page
rubber-stamp signal           true   (99% approve; 0.2 s median)
The four-record subset exists to make SLI ownership inspectable; it is not the 10,000-event fleet window used for the burn calculation. One run succeeds and is grounded, one appears successful but is ungrounded and emits two effects, and another fails without an effect. HTTP status is irrelevant to these outcome labels.
Now write three SLOs for a support agent:

at least 99.5 percent of eligible journeys are authoritatively successful and grounded over 30 days;
100 percent of effectful journeys have exactly the authorized effect cardinality, treated as an invariant rather than burnable budget;
95 percent of interactive journeys stream a first useful token within the product threshold, with an accompanying cost-per-successful-task ceiling by task family.

Write the error-budget policy before the incident. Use the table from the control section, name the feature flag and independent effect-credential revocation path, assign the on-call and business-effect owners, and state which SLO windows and slices determine restoration.

27.13.1 The incident tabletop
At 14:00, the facilitator reveals a canary drift alert: outputs shaped like data exfiltration appear for one tenant. The general task-success chart is green. A new retrieval connector was enabled at 13:30. At 14:05, audit reconciliation finds two non-allowlisted URL proposals; the Chapter 24 PEP denied both. At 14:12, one older tool path with direct network credentials is discovered outside that PEP. Its trace exporter is also missing 40 percent of spans.
Run the response in real time:

Detect and declare. Start the incident clock at the first harmful or suspicious output, not the alert. Assign commander, operations, communications, security, and business-effect roles.
Contain. Stop new external effects, revoke the old tool credential independently of tracing, freeze the canary, preserve audit and usage ledgers, and keep safe read-only service if policy permits.
Scope. Query authoritative egress and effect receipts by tool, tenant, release, connector revision, and time. Treat missing traces as missing diagnostic evidence, not absence of effects.
Recover technically. Point traffic to the last-known-good bundle, remove the bypass path, rebuild the connector corpus if poisoned, and pass the security regression plus release gate.
Recover the business. Identify any data actually disclosed, rotate affected secrets, contact owners under the incident plan, and record remediation. Denied proposals need different handling from executed disclosure.
Validate and ramp. Confirm the PEP is unavoidable, reconcile every attempted effect, restore trace health, run the golden case, and ramp through a bounded canary.
Learn. Produce the Appendix B postmortem, calculate MTTD, time to stop new effects, budget consumed, and last harmful effect. Add a permanent test proving no tool path can bypass the PEP and a chaos drill in which telemetry disappears during effect reconciliation.

Success is not naming the poisoned connector. It is demonstrating that containment did not depend on that diagnosis, that missing sampled traces did not hide authoritative effect evidence, and that both technical and business rollback received an owner.

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
text:
27.14 What endures, what changes
What endures. Instrument journeys, not only endpoints. Use bounded spans and linked traces for durable runs. Join unsampled metrics, sampled traces, an unsampled usage ledger, and an append-only effect record rather than forcing one store to be every truth. Score live outcomes asynchronously, preserve representative samples, and slice drift. Define journey-level SLOs and page on budget burn. Let rising burn reduce autonomy while invariant violations stop effects immediately. Practice hypothesis-driven chaos. Contain before perfect diagnosis, distinguish technical from business rollback, and convert incidents into permanent evidence. Measure human queues for discrimination and delay. Minimize and redact telemetry before export.
What changes. GenAI semantic-convention fields, observability vendors, judge models, drift libraries, provider retention contracts, privacy rules, confidential hardware, and dashboard APIs will change. Appendix C records those dated facts. Removing both Landscape boxes leaves the operating model and its equations intact.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
text:
27.15 Exercises

Extend drift_by_tenant with minimum sample counts and confidence intervals. Construct a fleet in which the weighted average improves while one high-risk language slice regresses; specify the alert and the action it should trigger.
Compute burn, remaining budget, and days to exhaustion for three scripted patterns: a brief total outage, a sustained 2-times burn, and intermittent 14.4-times spikes. Design multiwindow rules that page on the first and third without paging on harmless low-volume noise.
Export the artifact’s OTel spans to a local collector and one current self-hosted GenAI observability backend. Pin every package and mapping version, reproduce the two linked traces, and prove content capture remains off in storage—not just in the UI.
Write and run a chaos drill against Chapter 26: kill the worker in the effect gap, disable the trace exporter, and delay the approval signal past expiry. State the steady-state hypothesis, blast radius, abort condition, expected alerts, and authoritative post-run assertions.
Given a week of approval events, compute queue age, arrival/service rate, approval, override, abandonment, expiry, and post-approval defects by risk class. Decide which class should automate, which needs better evidence, and where staffing—not reviewer behavior—is the bottleneck.
Audit a supplied span fixture containing a bearer token in tool arguments, raw tenant ID, full prompt on an error span, a secret echoed in model output, and an unbounded URL label. Extend sanitize_attributes and tests while retaining enough surrogate identity for incident joins.
Defend the four-channel telemetry fan-out against a proposal to derive invoices and effect audit entirely from sampled traces. Name the sampling, exporter, mutation, retention, cardinality, and access-control failures the proposal creates.
Repeat the tabletop with both the feature-flag service and normal trace backend unavailable. Design an independent kill path, identify which evidence remains authoritative, and add concrete postmortem actions with owners, deadlines, and verification tests.

title: 27  Operating Agents: Observability, SLOs, Incidents, and HITL/Privacy Operations [S]
text:
27.16 Notes and sources

OpenTelemetry’s trace concepts define spans, context, and links; the dedicated GenAI semantic-conventions repository is the current moving source for GenAI mappings.
Beyer et al., Site Reliability Engineering and the SRE Workbook, supply the SLI, SLO, error-budget, incident, and postmortem foundations. The Workbook’s alerting chapter derives burn-rate and multiwindow alerting.
The Principles of Chaos Engineering supply the steady-state hypothesis, realistic-variable, and minimized-blast-radius frame used by the drills.
Langfuse’s current data model, self-hosting, and data-masking pages are one maintained implementation example, not the chapter’s mechanism.
NIST’s Privacy Engineering Program frames privacy as measurable systems engineering; NIST SP 800-226 is the maintained technical reference for differential privacy.
McMahan et al., “Communication-Efficient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data”, introduce the federated-averaging setting referenced in the PET literacy section.
Dwork and Roth, The Algorithmic Foundations of Differential Privacy, provide the formal differential-privacy foundation.
Applicable privacy and data-protection obligations are jurisdiction- and sector-dependent. Use Appendix C’s dated, counsel-reviewed supplement rather than treating this engineering chapter as legal advice.

Not required: prior product-management, accessibility, organization-design, or management-system training. This chapter is an engineering treatment, not legal, employment, procurement, or professional-practice advice.

28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
section: 28.3 Design trust through legible state and receipts
text:
28.3 Design trust through legible state and receipts
An expense-triage agent reaches launch review with green technical dashboards. Its task benchmark improved, latency meets the objective, effect authorization is enforced, and the review queue stays within its service target. The meeting approves expansion in eleven minutes. Six weeks later, finance cannot show that reimbursements close faster. New reviewers accept bad categories much more often than experienced reviewers. Screen-reader users abandon the streaming review flow. The product owner assumed the risk team would request recertification after a prompt change; the risk team assumed the platform would do it. Nobody owned the whole decision.
This is not one missing metric. It is a missing socio-technical system: the product, its users and affected people, the humans who review or repair it, the teams who change it, and the management process that decides whether its evidence still justifies its authority. Chapter 27 keeps the service operating and runs queues and incidents. This chapter asks a different question: should this product exist in this form, for these people, under this organization, at this autonomy level?
Treat a launch argument as three nested rings. The user ring asks whether a person can form an accurate model of what the agent is doing and intervene. The product ring asks whether use causes a worthwhile outcome compared with a simpler baseline. The organization ring asks whether named owners can maintain the claim through changes. A release is not ready when one ring is green; it is ready only when their evidence supports the same bounded decision.

flowchart LR
    U["User evidence<br/>legibility · accessibility<br/>appropriate reliance"] --> P["Product evidence<br/>causal outcome · funnel<br/>unit economics"]
    P --> O["Organization evidence<br/>owner · risk treatment<br/>recertification"]
    O --> D{"Launch decision"}
    D -->|"all conditions pass"| SHIP["Ship bounded authority"]
    D -->|"some conditions fail"| LIMIT["Constrain, repair, and re-evaluate"]
    D -->|"value or control fails"| HOLD["Hold or retire"]
    U -. "cannot be averaged away" .-> D
    P -. "must beat a baseline" .-> D





flowchart LR
    U["User evidence<br/>legibility · accessibility<br/>appropriate reliance"] --> P["Product evidence<br/>causal outcome · funnel<br/>unit economics"]
    P --> O["Organization evidence<br/>owner · risk treatment<br/>recertification"]
    O --> D{"Launch decision"}
    D -->|"all conditions pass"| SHIP["Ship bounded authority"]
    D -->|"some conditions fail"| LIMIT["Constrain, repair, and re-evaluate"]
    D -->|"value or control fails"| HOLD["Hold or retire"]
    U -. "cannot be averaged away" .-> D
    P -. "must beat a baseline" .-> D




Figure 28.1: Which three evidence rings must agree before an agent receives more authority?





The invariant in Figure 28.1 is conjunctive: product value cannot compensate for an excluded cohort, and policy completeness cannot compensate for no useful outcome.

28.3.1 Concern boundaries are product architecture
An agent product needs more than a chat surface. Separate at least six concerns: goal elicitation, planning, execution, evidence, intervention, and outcome confirmation. Goal elicitation turns an underspecified request into a bounded objective and names what must not happen. Planning exposes consequential interpretation before effects. Execution invokes models and tools under the Chapter 17 harness. Evidence distinguishes source facts, model inferences, unresolved assumptions, and effect receipts. Intervention lets a person correct, pause, deny, or narrow authority. Outcome confirmation checks the external state that mattered.
This separation is the product counterpart of mixed-initiative interaction. The person and automation can each take initiative, but initiative should move according to expected value, uncertainty, cost of interruption, and consequence—not because the agent always asks or always acts. A low-consequence, reversible formatting choice may proceed silently. A novel payee, uncertain policy interpretation, or irreversible submission should surface a decision point with the exact delta.
Before an action, show a plan diff: what the agent intends to change, from which current state to which proposed state, with affected objects and permissions. Afterward, show a receipt: the authoritative system, operation identity, committed result, timestamp, and reconciliation status. A prose message saying “done” is not a receipt. Nor should a product expose private chain-of-thought as an explanation. Give users the decision-relevant evidence—sources, assumptions, uncertainty, rule or tool result, and action record—without claiming that a generated rationale faithfully reveals internal computation. Chapter 25 owns that distinction.
Use stable epistemic labels. “Source fact,” “user-provided,” “system state,” “model inference,” “estimate,” and “unresolved” should retain the same meaning across screens and modalities. Calibrated numeric probabilities are useful only when validated for the task and slice. Otherwise, concrete statements such as “two required receipts are missing” are more actionable than ornamental confidence. A refusal should name the boundary and a safe next step; an uncertainty should identify what new evidence could resolve it.
Personalization raises both usefulness and risk. Make remembered preferences inspectable, scoped, editable, exportable when appropriate, and resettable. Distinguish a temporary session choice from a durable profile and an organizational policy. Never infer a sensitive preference merely because it predicts clicks. Reset must remove the preference from every product surface that consumes it, while Chapter 18 owns deletion lineage through derived memory artifacts.

28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
text:
28.4 What you will build






TipOne integrated launch-review artifact



You will build launch_metrics.py around one candidate expense-report triage agent. A compact fixture expands into a 500-row reviewer decision log and a 500-row usage ledger. The artifact computes the reliance matrix by cohort, the invocation-to-outcome funnel, cost per realized outcome, a review-time break-even point, an explicitly bounded energy intensity, and four predeclared launch conditions. It attaches those results to one AIMS risk-register entry and returns SHIP or HOLD_FULL_LAUNCH.
The fixture is synthetic so the decision is reproducible. Its intended tension is realistic: the candidate beats the conventional workflow on cost, yet fails overall appropriate reliance, cohort overreliance, and assistive-technology completion. The permitted next step is therefore a read-only pilot for experienced reviewers—not a vote to ignore the failing slices.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
text:
28.5 Measure appropriate reliance, not engagement
Trust is a person’s attitude that an agent will help achieve a goal under uncertainty. Reliance is observable behavior: accepting, rejecting, checking, delegating, or intervening. A product cannot directly optimize “trust” as a scalar without inviting manipulation. It can design information and control so that reliance tracks demonstrated capability.
The target is appropriate reliance, not maximal acceptance. For a set of recommendations with externally established correctness, classify each decision into four cells:



Recommendation
Person accepts
Person rejects




correct
correct accept, (N_{CA})
correct reject, (N_{CR})


wrong
wrong accept, (N_{WA})
wrong reject, (N_{WR})



Define

R_{appropriate}=\frac{N_{CA}+N_{WR}}{N}, \qquad
R_{over}=\frac{N_{WA}}{N_{WA}+N_{WR}}, \qquad
R_{under}=\frac{N_{CR}}{N_{CA}+N_{CR}}.

The first rate measures agreement with correctness. The second asks how often a person accepts when the agent is wrong; the third asks how often useful recommendations are rejected. Report all three. A single “agreement with AI” number makes correct acceptance and harmful wrong acceptance look alike. Likewise, acceptance, daily active use, time in product, and satisfaction can rise while judgment worsens.
Correctness needs an outcome-grounded label. Reviewer agreement alone is circular: if every reviewer accepts the same bad recommendation, agreement is high and reliance is poor. Use authoritative downstream state, independently adjudicated cases, or a delayed outcome whose limitations are recorded. Confirmed catches are especially informative: did a rejection prevent an actual defect, or merely create rework?
The artifact keeps the four cells intact rather than reducing them too early:
# launch_metrics.py — one reliance slice
def _reliance_rates(rows):
    rows = list(rows)
    counts = Counter(row["quadrant"] for row in rows)
    correct = counts["correct_accept"] + counts["correct_reject"]
    wrong = counts["wrong_accept"] + counts["wrong_reject"]
    catches = sum(row["confirmed_catch"] for row in rows)
    return {
        "n": len(rows),
        "quadrants": {name: counts[name] for name in QUADRANTS},
        "appropriate_rate": round(
            (counts["correct_accept"] + counts["wrong_reject"]) / len(rows), 4
        ),
        "overreliance_rate": round(counts["wrong_accept"] / wrong, 4),
        "underreliance_rate": round(counts["correct_reject"] / correct, 4),
        "confirmed_catch_rate": round(catches / counts["wrong_reject"], 4),
    }








Figure 28.2: Where does the aggregate reviewer population rely appropriately, and where does it fail?





The aggregate appropriate rate in Figure 28.2 is (441/500=0.882). More importantly, the new-reviewer slice accepts 25 of 30 wrong recommendations: an overreliance rate of (0.833). An average can hide exactly the population that a new rollout creates. Slice by experience, language, assistive technology, task novelty, consequence, time pressure, and any protected or operational group justified by the context. Predeclare minimum sample handling; do not publish unstable rankings of individual reviewers.
Interventions have trade-offs. Showing sources and exact action deltas can improve discrimination. Requiring a person to make an initial judgment before seeing the recommendation, request evidence, or explain a choice can reduce overreliance, but also adds effort and can reduce satisfaction. Warnings shown on every case become wallpaper. Confidence displays can anchor users when calibration is poor. Test interventions on all four cells, decision time, comprehension, accessibility, downstream defects, and distributional effects. The best interface is not the one users like most in isolation; it is the one that helps the relevant people accept good assistance and catch bad assistance at a sustainable cost.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




reviewer-decision ledger → cohort reliance report
43 analysis lines; 18 shown
Every decision occupies one of four exhaustive cells, and a passing aggregate cannot erase a failing cohort gate.

28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
section: 28.6 Make accessibility part of system correctness
text:
28.6 Make accessibility part of system correctness
Accessibility is not a compliance pass added after model quality. It is whether people with varied sensory, motor, cognitive, language, and situational needs can perceive state, understand consequences, operate controls, recover from error, and obtain the same outcome. For an agent, inaccessible state is also a control defect: a person cannot supervise a plan or stop an action they cannot perceive.
Start with the ordinary web and software baseline: semantic structure; keyboard operation; visible focus; sufficient contrast; text alternatives; captions and transcripts; labels and instructions; predictable navigation; zoom and reflow; error identification; and compatibility with assistive technologies. Then test the interaction patterns agents add:

Streaming output: do not move focus on every token or flood a live region. Announce meaningful completion states and offer pause, replay, or a non-streaming view.
Plans and tool calls: represent state in semantic text, not only animation, color, indentation, or a canvas. A collapsed detail must expose whether an effect is pending.
Approval: focus the exact proposed change, its evidence, and distinct approve, deny, modify, and escalate controls. Preserve enough time to decide; refresh stale state before effect.
Voice and multimodality: provide text alternatives, captions, transcripts, and a path that does not require speech, hearing, precise pointing, or camera use.
Generated interface elements: constrain them to a tested component system. A model should not invent unlabeled controls or change keyboard order at runtime.
Errors and uncertainty: identify the field or claim, state what happened, preserve entered work, and give a correction path without requiring memory of a vanished message.

Conformance to a published accessibility standard is a floor, not evidence that the agent task works. Combine automated checks, manual keyboard and screen-reader testing, zoom and reflow tests, cognitive walkthroughs, and representative task studies. Automated scanners catch only classes of detectable violations. Include disabled people and assistive-technology users in discovery and evaluation, compensate expertise, and do not make one participant speak for a population.
Measure the same funnel and outcome by access mode. In the fixture, 85 assistive-technology users invoke the agent but only 65 complete the recommendation step, an adjacent conversion of (65/85=0.765). The predeclared floor is (0.85). Shipping because the overall completion count is acceptable would treat exclusion as a rounding error. The correct engineering response is to diagnose the interaction, repair it, and repeat the task—not lower the cohort gate after looking at the result.
Language and culture require the same seriousness. Translation quality, reading level, names, addresses, dates, currencies, formality, and policy concepts can change both comprehension and model behavior. Evaluate the end-to-end task with people who use the language in context. A fluent response is not necessarily an accurate or culturally suitable action. Give users control over language and locale; do not silently infer them from sensitive proxies.
Accessibility ownership must survive procurement. If a vendor supplies the model or interface, the product team still needs acceptance criteria, test access, remediation terms, change notification, and a fallback. A vendor conformance report is evidence about a named version and scope, not a transferable guarantee for your prompts, generated content, wrapper, tools, or workflow.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
text:
28.7 Discover value before adding autonomy
Agents are attractive when the path to an outcome is variable, information-rich, and partly judgmental. Those same properties make them expensive and difficult to evaluate. Begin with the least agentic baseline capable of producing value: better instructions, search, a form, a deterministic rule, a conventional workflow, or a single model call. If the baseline solves the problem, added autonomy is complexity without value.
Write a product hypothesis in causal language:

For a named population performing a named job, this bounded agent intervention will improve a measurable outcome relative to a named baseline, without violating reliance, accessibility, safety, labor, or cost conditions.

“Users want an AI assistant” is not a hypothesis. Neither are benchmark score, tokens generated, tool calls, chat sessions, or minutes spent. A support agent may aim to increase correctly resolved cases without repeat contact. A coding agent may reduce accepted-change lead time without increasing escaped defects. An expense agent may reduce time to correctly settled reimbursement without increasing improper categorization.
Use qualitative discovery before automation: observe the job, artifacts, workarounds, handoffs, exceptions, and sources of truth. Interview affected people as well as the purchaser and operator. Map who benefits, who does extra work, who absorbs errors, who can refuse, and who can appeal. The apparent “manual bottleneck” may be a necessary negotiation, duty separation, or missing upstream data rather than an opportunity to make a model decide faster.
Prototype the information and decision flow before the autonomous loop. A concierge test, Wizard-of-Oz study, read-only copilot, or offline replay can reveal whether recommendations are actionable and which context is missing. Instrument the baseline first. Without its outcome rate, time, defect, labor, and cost, the launch review cannot know whether the agent improved anything.
Declare kill criteria before the pilot. Each condition needs a metric, population or slice, threshold, measurement window, owner, and response. Include value and non-negotiable constraints. For example: hold write authority if appropriate reliance is below 0.90, if any adequately sampled cohort accepts more than 25 percent of wrong recommendations, if assistive-technology completion is below 0.85, or if cost per realized outcome exceeds the conventional workflow. A threshold set after viewing results is an explanation, not a gate.
Offline evaluation remains necessary but insufficient. Chapter 22 establishes task quality, slice performance, judge validity, uncertainty, and powered comparisons. Product discovery asks whether invoking the system changes a real outcome. Use randomized experiments where treatment assignment and interference assumptions are credible. Otherwise use a defensible quasi-experiment, phased rollout, matched comparison, interrupted time series, or carefully bounded before/after study, and state the confounders. Never attribute every post-launch change to the agent.
Selection effects matter. Early adopters may have easier tasks or more enthusiasm. People invoke assistance selectively, so “outcome among invoked cases” does not estimate “effect if offered” without assumptions. Novelty can temporarily inflate engagement and diligence; later, reviewers may automate their attention. Track assigned, exposed, invoked, completed, and retained populations, and preserve a contemporaneous baseline long enough to see learning and fatigue.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
text:
28.8 Follow the invocation-to-outcome funnel
A product dashboard often begins at invocation because that is where the agent emits an event. The causal product story begins earlier and ends later. Define a nested funnel appropriate to the job:

eligible or exposed: the person and task could use the intervention;
invoked: the person or policy actually starts it;
completed: the agent returns a usable candidate;
accepted or edited: the candidate enters the human or system workflow;
outcome realized: authoritative downstream evidence confirms the intended result;
retained: the outcome or repeated use remains useful after a meaningful interval.

For counts (n_0,,n_K), adjacent conversion is (c_k=n_k/n_{k-1}), while end-to-end conversion is (n_K/n_0). Keep both. End-to-end value can fall even when model completion improves because invocation, review, integration, or downstream realization worsens.

flowchart LR
    E["Exposed<br/>500"] -->|"−40"| I["Invoked<br/>460"]
    I -->|"−40"| C["Completed<br/>420"]
    C -->|"−40"| A["Accepted or edited<br/>380"]
    A -->|"−35"| O["Outcome realized<br/>345"]
    O -->|"−50"| R["Retained<br/>295"]
    AT["Assistive-technology slice<br/>65 / 85 complete"] -. "largest adjacent access loss" .-> C





flowchart LR
    E["Exposed<br/>500"] -->|"−40"| I["Invoked<br/>460"]
    I -->|"−40"| C["Completed<br/>420"]
    C -->|"−40"| A["Accepted or edited<br/>380"]
    A -->|"−35"| O["Outcome realized<br/>345"]
    O -->|"−50"| R["Retained<br/>295"]
    AT["Assistive-technology slice<br/>65 / 85 complete"] -. "largest adjacent access loss" .-> C




Figure 28.3: Where does the expense-agent pilot lose people between exposure and durable value?





Figure 28.3 prevents an inference that 460 invocations equal 460 outcomes. Every stage needs an event definition, identity join, time window, and source of truth. A generated answer is completion; it is not a resolved case. A proposed expense category is not a settled reimbursement. Chapter 27’s usage and effect ledgers provide operational records; this chapter maps them to product outcomes.
Instrument edits, rejection, rework, escalation, delayed reversal, appeal, and downstream harm. Edits are not simply failures: they may be the intended joint-work pattern. But copying the recommendation into another system can look like acceptance even when the user corrects it later. Join to authoritative state and define attribution windows.
Review labor is a hidden funnel stage and a hidden cost. Count reading, evidence retrieval, correction, escalation, queue wait, training, quality sampling, and downstream repair. Separate queue wait from active decision time; Chapter 27 owns staffing and queue mechanics. Here, the question is whether that labor creates enough discrimination and value to justify the product design. If almost every case receives a costly approval, compare the system with a deterministic policy that automatically handles the safe subset.
Experiment at the unit where treatment is assigned. A user-level assignment avoids a person switching interfaces, but team members may share outputs. A case-level assignment increases power but can leak learning across conditions. Organizational rollouts have few clusters and strong time trends. Predeclare the primary outcome, guardrails, assignment unit, sample-size logic, stopping rule, and novelty window. Use Chapter 22 for interval estimates and multiple comparisons rather than manufacturing significance inside a launch dashboard.
Selection into invocation should itself become a metric. Compare exposed non-invokers with invokers, record why tasks are ineligible, and sample abandoned paths. When the hardest cases avoid the tool, high success among invoked cases may be true and irrelevant. When only novices invoke it, the same aggregate may conceal a dependence risk. Product telemetry is not a substitute for the assignment and population diagram.









Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




usage ledger → overall and cohort funnel
31 analysis lines; 0 shown
Counts never increase between nested stages, and every aggregate funnel retains an accessibility slice.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
text:
28.9 Price outcomes, labor, and energy
Return on investment begins with a counterfactual: what would the organization and affected people spend, receive, and risk without this product? Use the conventional workflow as the baseline, not zero. The relevant denominator is a realized, quality-qualified outcome.
For a measurement window, define

C_{outcome}=\frac{C_{inference}+C_{retrieval}+C_{review}+C_{remediation}+C_{platform}}{N_{qualified\ outcomes}}.

The numerator includes failed and abandoned attempts attributable to the population. Per conversation pricing can hide multi-call loops; per-token pricing can hide human review; infrastructure allocation can hide idle capacity; labor estimates can hide interruptions and training. Chapter 26 owns the metered journey ledger. This chapter adds product labor and divides by the outcome.
The fixture prices 500 exposures at 0.09 machine cost units each, 31,000 review seconds at 36 units per hour, and 34 wrong accepts at 4 remediation units each. Total cost is 491 units for 345 realized outcomes, or 1.4232 per outcome, below the 1.60 baseline. That is a real pass—and still insufficient to launch because three other conditions fail.
Review time has a useful break-even threshold. Let (C_b) be baseline cost per outcome, (N_o) realized outcomes, (C_{nr}) all non-review cost, (r_h) reviewer cost per second, and (N_r) reviewed cases. The maximum average review duration compatible with the baseline is

t_{max}=\frac{C_bN_o-C_{nr}}{r_hN_r}.

The fixture’s average is 62.0 seconds and break-even is 74.2 seconds. This threshold helps a team see how interface changes or harder cohorts affect economics. It is not a target to rush people toward. Shorter decisions can increase wrong acceptance; evaluate time with reliance and outcome.
Outcome-based commercial pricing can align a vendor with a customer only when “outcome” is attributable, quality-qualified, deduplicated, reversible when later invalidated, and protected against gaming. Specify eligibility, attribution window, dispute process, exclusions, evidence source, and who pays for retries, escalations, human labor, and harms. A vendor should not be paid for deflecting a case that returns unresolved. Nor should a customer demand an outcome price while withholding the authoritative label.
Economic value is distributed. Record whose time is saved, whose workload grows, who bears errors, and whether a faster process transfers unpaid correction to users or reviewers. Include training, accessibility remediation, governance, procurement, security, incident recovery, and exit costs. A narrow departmental ROI can be negative for the organization or public.
Energy intensity needs the same denominator and an explicit measurement boundary:

E_{outcome}=\frac{E_{accelerator}+E_{host}+E_{idle\ allocation}+E_{facility}+E_{network?}}{N_{qualified\ outcomes}}.

The question mark is deliberate: different measurements include different components. State whether energy is measured or estimated, average or marginal, which hardware and region, allocation of idle capacity, power-usage effectiveness, request shape, model and precision, cache behavior, retries, network, and user devices. Do not compare two published numbers with different boundaries. Carbon and water additionally require location and time-sensitive factors; model development and hardware life-cycle impacts are different scopes from serving.
The artifact reports a synthetic allocated serving value of 0.2609 watt-hours per realized outcome and explicitly excludes user devices. It is useful for regression tests within one fixed boundary, not a claim about a vendor or a universal agent query. The highest-leverage optimization is often product-level: avoid valueless invocations, use a smaller sufficient workflow, cache safely, reduce retries, and increase qualified outcomes—not merely shorten one prompt.
Professional responsibility constrains the business case. Engineers should disclose material limitations, uncertainty, conflicts, affected populations, and evidence gaps; protect confidentiality; avoid deceptive claims; and prioritize public and user welfare. Refuse a launch narrative that relabels a failing slice as “edge cases” without analysis. Escalation paths and protected dissent are system controls, not cultural luxuries.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
text:
28.10 Organize for staged autonomy
An agent product crosses application, model, data, platform, security, risk, accessibility, domain, operations, and legal boundaries. A committee containing all of them does not automatically create ownership. Assign one stream-aligned product team end-to-end responsibility for the user job, outcome, evaluation, interaction, and release. Give it a paved road from an AI platform team: gateway, model catalog, identity, policy enforcement, evaluation infrastructure, tracing, durable execution, and release evidence. Use enabling specialists to help teams acquire accessibility, safety, data, and domain capabilities rather than becoming permanent ticket queues.
Centralize mechanisms that must be consistent; keep product judgments near the work. The platform can enforce identity, immutable bundle versions, effect policy, and required evidence fields. It cannot decide the semantic cost of a wrong medical summary or whether expense reviewers understand a policy exception. A risk function sets minimum governance, independently challenges high-consequence claims, and can block; it should not become the unnamed owner of product outcomes.
Build-versus-buy is a responsibility allocation, not a binary architecture choice. Compare strategic differentiation, data and residency, customization, latency, scale, reliability, portability, observability, security controls, accessibility evidence, model-change notice, evaluation access, incident duties, price exposure, staffing, and exit cost. For every purchased layer, record:








Responsibility
Vendor evidence
Product-team duty




model behavior
version/change notice, evaluation access, known limits
task and slice evaluation in the actual scaffold


data handling
contract, subprocessors, retention and region controls
minimization, configuration, lineage, deletion, verification


availability
service objectives and incident channel
degraded product contract and business continuity


accessibility
scoped conformance evidence and remediation process
end-to-end task testing in the wrapper


security
control reports, vulnerability and incident terms
threat model, least privilege, effect enforcement


lifecycle
deprecation and export terms
migration rehearsal, portable data and prompts, exit owner



You can outsource operation; you cannot outsource accountability for how the integrated product is used.
A golden path is the supported route from prototype to production with sensible defaults, examples, templates, controls, and observable evidence. It should make the safe path the fast path: registered system identity, approved model alias, evaluation suite, policy-bound tools, accessible components, trace and cost schema, launch packet, and rollback. Allow exceptions through an explicit review with an owner and expiry. Measure golden-path adoption, time to first evaluated prototype, lead time to bounded production, exception age, duplicated infrastructure, and platform support load. A path nobody uses is documentation, not a platform product.
Stage autonomy by evidence and consequence:









Stage
Authority
Evidence to advance
Reversion condition




observe
offline replay; no user effect
representative task value and failure taxonomy
corpus or population no longer representative


advise
read-only recommendation
comprehension, accessibility, reliance, outcome lift
failing slice or misunderstood scope


act with approval
proposed effect; explicit human authorization
reviewer discrimination, reversible receipts, stable economics
rubber stamping, stale state, or review debt


bounded autonomy
allowlisted low-consequence effects
outcome SLO, invariant controls, recertified risk treatment
burn, drift, invariant breach, or material change


broader autonomy
larger scope with independent oversight
sustained evidence across novelty, cohorts, and failures
any gate outside approved residual risk



Advancement is not a maturity badge. Different task classes can remain at different stages indefinitely. Reversion must be technically cheap and organizationally legitimate. An agency budget from Chapter 1 caps authority; good product evidence can spend more of that budget, but cannot erase an irreversible consequence or a weak control.
Track review debt: decisions awaiting independent challenge, expiring exceptions, controls without owners, untested recertification triggers, stale model cards, unsupported custom infrastructure, and launch conditions temporarily waived. Like technical debt, it accumulates interest as staff and system context change. Give every item principal, owner, due date, compensating control, and repayment test. Do not turn “risk accepted” into an immortal status.
Also track human capability. Heavy assistance can deskill reviewers, narrow situational awareness, and make fallback impossible. Rotate work, sample unassisted performance when ethical, rehearse degraded modes, provide feedback on caught and missed cases, and design career paths that do not reduce domain experts to exception handlers. Monitor workload, interruption, emotional burden, exposure to harmful content, decision pressure, appeal conflict, and control over pace. Chapter 27 measures the queue; organizational leadership owns whether the job is sustainable and whether incentives reward careful dissent rather than approval volume.
crumbs: Part VII — Production, Operations, and Organizations
28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]

title: 28  Agent Products, People, and Organizations [S]
section: 28.11 Operate AIMS through every material change
text:
28.11 Operate AIMS through every material change
An AI management system (AIMS) is the repeatable organizational machinery for policy, objectives, roles, resources, risk treatment, operation, evidence, audit, corrective action, and improvement. It is not a document folder and not a one-time launch council. Chapter 25 established governed safety claims and regulatory interfaces. Here, the organization turns them into an operating loop.
Use the NIST AI Risk Management Framework functions as a useful risk lifecycle: Govern establishes culture, policy, roles, accountability, and oversight across Map, Measure, and Manage. Map establishes context, intended purpose, affected people, dependencies, impacts, and risk tolerance. Measure selects and interprets evidence. Manage prioritizes treatment, decides whether objectives are achieved, accepts bounded residual risk through authorized roles, and monitors the decision. These functions are iterative, not a checklist sequence.
Wrap that risk work in plan-do-check-act. Plan policy, objectives, scope, risk method, competence, and controls. Do the controlled development and operation. Check performance, incidents, audits, complaints, accessibility, reliance, outcomes, and control effectiveness. Act on nonconformities, root causes, changed context, and opportunities to improve. Management review supplies resources and resolves cross-team accountability; it does not rubber-stamp a packet.
Begin with an inventory entry that can answer: system and owner; intended and prohibited uses; users and affected parties; autonomy and effects; models, prompts, tools, data, vendors, regions, and interfaces; outcome and guardrail metrics; risk tier and rationale; approvals; evidence bundle; deployment population; monitoring; incident and appeal path; and recertification triggers. Inventory identity must join the release bundle and operational telemetry. A spreadsheet with no release hash cannot tell which governed system ran.
Risk tier determines a minimum review path. Consequence, affected scale, reversibility, vulnerability, rights impact, data sensitivity, novelty, autonomy, external exposure, and uncertainty are common inputs. A product can raise its review rigor; it cannot lower a required tier by calling an effect a “recommendation.” Document the rationale and independent challenger. Keep the full rubric in the organization’s governance standard rather than embedding another ad hoc scorer in application code.
A risk-register entry should state hazard, cause or scenario, affected parties, consequence, likelihood or uncertainty, existing controls, control evidence, residual risk, treatment, owner, due date, and status. Connect hazards to product conditions. “Overreliance” becomes a measurable scenario: new reviewers accept wrong categories; the control is an evidence-first review design; the evidence is the four-cell cohort matrix; the gate is a maximum wrong-accept rate. “Accessibility” becomes a task-completion scenario, not a generic promise.
Every material change passes through the loop in Figure 28.4. Model aliases, system prompts, tool schemas or descriptions, retrieval corpora, data policy, write scope, deployment region, affected population, vendor, interface, and risk controls can invalidate old evidence. Define triggers before change, resolve them against an immutable bundle diff, and require the appropriate delta evaluation. A typo need not rerun every study, but the owner must justify the affected evidence. Periodic recertification catches drift that no discrete release declares.

flowchart LR
    subgraph G["GOVERN — policy · roles · competence · accountability · resources"]
        I["Inventory + immutable bundle"] --> M["MAP<br/>purpose · people · context · impact"]
        M --> E["MEASURE<br/>value · reliance · access · safety · cost"]
        E --> R["MANAGE<br/>treat risk · assign owner · record residual"]
        R --> L{"Launch review"}
        L -->|"pass bounded conditions"| O["Operate at approved autonomy"]
        L -->|"condition, hold, or retire"| C["Repair or reduce scope"]
        C --> I
        O --> S["Outcomes · complaints · audits<br/>incidents · workforce evidence"]
        S -->|"monitoring"| E
        S -->|"material change or periodic review"| I
    end





flowchart LR
    subgraph G["GOVERN — policy · roles · competence · accountability · resources"]
        I["Inventory + immutable bundle"] --> M["MAP<br/>purpose · people · context · impact"]
        M --> E["MEASURE<br/>value · reliance · access · safety · cost"]
        E --> R["MANAGE<br/>treat risk · assign owner · record residual"]
        R --> L{"Launch review"}
        L -->|"pass bounded conditions"| O["Operate at approved autonomy"]
        L -->|"condition, hold, or retire"| C["Repair or reduce scope"]
        C --> I
        O --> S["Outcomes · complaints · audits<br/>incidents · workforce evidence"]
        S -->|"monitoring"| E
        S -->|"material change or periodic review"| I
    end




Figure 28.4: How does every material product change return through risk evidence and a launch decision?





The launch review is a decision record, not a presentation. It names scope, evidence period and population, baseline, unresolved uncertainty, failed and passed conditions, residual risks, accountable owner, independent reviewer, decision, permitted autonomy, expiry, rollback, monitoring, and recertification triggers. Conditions should be machine-checkable where possible and human-judged where necessary. The code evaluates four quantitative gates but does not pretend to automate risk tier, residual-risk acceptance, professional judgment, or accessibility diagnosis.
# launch_metrics.py — exact gate-evaluation core
limits = fixture["kill_criteria"]
max_cohort = max(
    item["overreliance_rate"] for item in reliance["by_cohort"].values()
)
assistive_completion = funnel["by_cohort"]["assistive_technology"][
    "conversion_from_previous"
]["completed"]
checks = (
    ("appropriate_reliance", reliance["overall"]["appropriate_rate"],
     limits["minimum_appropriate_rate"], ">="),
    ("cohort_overreliance", max_cohort,
     limits["maximum_cohort_overreliance"], "<="),
    ("assistive_completion", assistive_completion,
     limits["minimum_assistive_completion"], ">="),
    ("cost_per_outcome", economics["cost_per_outcome"],
     economics["baseline_cost_per_outcome"], "<="),
)
return [
    {"gate": name, "observed": observed, "threshold": threshold,
     "passed": observed >= threshold if operator == ">=" else observed <= threshold}
    for name, observed, threshold, operator in checks
]
When a condition fails, do not average it into an overall readiness score. Choose among repair and repeat, narrow population, lower autonomy, add a time-bound compensating control, continue a non-effectful pilot, or retire. Record who can approve each choice. The fixture permits read-only use only for experienced reviewers while the team repairs the interface and repeats cohort evaluation. That is a conditioned product decision, not an operational incident response.
Internal audit asks whether the management system is implemented and effective: samples can be traced from inventory to bundle, evidence, decision, operation, and recertification; owners exist; exceptions expire; and corrective actions close. Management review asks whether objectives, resources, competence, performance, complaints, audits, changes, residual risks, and opportunities require a policy or investment change. Both need independence proportionate to consequence.






NoteLandscape 2026 (dated)



Verify live: 2026-07-19. Appendix C owner: accessibility law and standards, AI management frameworks, commercial pricing, capability benchmarks, and inference environmental accounting. WCAG 2.2 is the W3C-recommended web accessibility target; the European Accessibility Act has applied to covered products and services since 2025-06-28. In April 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice extended the Title II web/mobile rule deadlines to 2027-04-26 for larger public entities and 2028-04-26 for smaller entities and special district governments. Sector, jurisdiction, entity, and product scope require counsel.
NIST AI RMF 1.0 is under revision; its current Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage Core and Playbook remain voluntary risk-management resources. ISO/IEC 42001:2023 is the current certifiable AIMS standard. Certification scope and a risk framework are not proof that a particular product is safe or valuable.
Current examples of outcome-based agent pricing include vendor offerings such as Sierra and Intercom; definitions and terms change. GDPval is one current attempt to evaluate economically valuable work products, but product ROI still requires deployment outcomes. Published inference-energy estimates, including Google’s production measurement and Mistral’s life-cycle work, use differing workloads and boundaries. Verify the current method before comparison.











Artifact state
New code
Invariant now verified




reliance + funnel + economics + risk entry → launch decision
201 nonblank artifact lines; gate core shown
A passing economic gate cannot compensate for failed reliance or accessibility, and any material change has a named recertification path.
